text: [PERF] Result persistence (trades delete+insert, equity_curve JSONB serialize, metrics upsert) MUST scale with trade/close count — NOT candle count — and stay within a bounded share of end-to-end time on the fast engine; a test doubling candles at fixed trade count MUST show persist time constant within ±10%.
rationale: When the engine is 1000× faster, persistence can become the dominant stage; no item budgets persist cost or asserts it scales with trades rather than candles.

text: [PERF] The in-memory equity_curve (one point per close per #68) MUST be bounded in length to ≈ close count (not candle count), and its FULL-JSONB serialization + LTTB downsample cost MUST be budgeted so a trade-heavy long run cannot blow RSS or persist time; a test MUST assert curve length == close count and serialization stays within budget.
rationale: A scan-heavy long backtest emits an equity point on every close; unbounded accumulation threatens both RSS (#30) and persist latency. No item bounds equity_curve size or its serialization cost.

text: [OBS] Beyond recording a per-stage breakdown (#39), an explicit per-stage BUDGET (kline-load %, engine %, drill %, persist %) MUST be asserted in CI so a single-stage regression that stays under the total wall-time gate still fails; a test MUST attribute and gate each stage independently.
rationale: #39 only records timings; a stage can regress badly while another improves, passing the total-time gate (#36). Per-stage budget gates catch hidden regressions.

text: [OBS] A committed profile artifact (cProfile/py-spy) MUST re-confirm the dominant hotspot after Phase 4 (njit), Phase 5 (columnar), AND Phase 6 (fast-path/sweep) — not only after Phase 3 — so each subsequent optimization is re-targeted at the then-current bottleneck.
rationale: #37 requires a profile only before Phase 4; each accelerating phase shifts the hotspot, so the next phase needs a fresh profile to avoid optimizing a no-longer-dominant cost.

text: [PERF] A documented capacity envelope (max symbols × days ÷ interval that completes within budget) MUST exist, and a single-run PRE-FLIGHT cost estimate (≈ candles × scans) MUST warn or reject runs provably unable to finish under the 120s cap rather than letting them hit the timeout-kill.
rationale: sweep_estimate exists for sweeps but there is no single-run pre-flight; without a capacity envelope + estimate, oversized runs silently burn 120s and get killed instead of failing fast.

text: [PERF] When an accelerated path (numba/DuckDB/Parquet) fails mid-run and degrades (#155), the degraded pure-Python/Postgres path MUST still complete within the 120s cap on the canonical fixture AND MUST NOT re-execute already-completed scans/candles; a fault-injection perf test MUST assert both.
rationale: #155 asserts the degraded path is correct but not that it still fits the latency cap or that mid-run fallback avoids redoing finished work — both are scalability risks of in-run degradation.

text: [PERF] All run-scoped allocations (SoA arrays, position book, in-process 1m drill cache) MUST be released on completion, timeout-kill, AND cancel, so a long-lived server process shows stable RSS across many heterogeneous runs (bounded by documented cache ceilings); a soak test of N varied runs MUST assert no monotonic RSS growth.
rationale: #20 only asserts the rerun LRU doesn't grow RSS; the broader server reality — RSS stability across many varied runs including timed-out/cancelled ones — is untested and is where real leaks surface.

text: [PERF] Beyond torn-read safety (#103), shared hot/LRU cache access MUST be read-mostly / sharded / lock-free on the read path so _MAX_CONCURRENT concurrent runs do not serialize on a cache lock; a 3-concurrent-run test MUST assert aggregate throughput ≥ ~2.5× a single run (near-linear, not lock-bound).
rationale: #103 guarantees correctness under concurrency but a coarse cache lock could serialize the 3 runs, erasing the concurrency benefit — a perf-scalability concern distinct from correctness.

text: [CACHE] The in-process 1m drill cache (#23/#104) MUST have its OWN explicit byte/entry ceiling with eviction, separate from the 5m _load_klines LRU, so a drill-heavy long run cannot grow it unbounded; a test MUST assert it stays ≤ the configured ceiling and evicts oldest bars under pressure.
rationale: #23 says the 1m cache is 'bounded/cleared per session' but specifies no explicit ceiling+eviction independent of the 5m LRU; a drill-heavy 90-day run can accumulate many 1m bars and blow RSS.

text: [CACHE] Cold gap-fill fetches (per-gap-run #95) and parallel 1m drill fetches (#22) MUST share a rate-limit-aware concurrency semaphore so parallel fetching maximizes throughput WITHOUT tripping Bybit rate limits/backoff that would blow the cold budget; a test MUST assert fetch concurrency ≤ the configured cap and that backoff is bounded.
rationale: #5 bounds cold request COUNT and #22 parallelizes drill fetches, but nothing caps concurrent exchange calls against Bybit rate limits — unbounded parallel fetch can trigger throttling that inflates cold wall-time.

text: [TEST] The frozen Phase-0 baseline (#38) and every phase's optimized measurement MUST be captured on the SAME hardware/OS/Python/env, with those facts stamped into the benchmark artifact, so the ≥100× ratio is apples-to-apples and not a cross-machine artifact.
rationale: #38 asserts a 100× headline vs a frozen baseline but doesn't require baseline and optimized numbers to come from identical hardware; a cross-machine ratio is meaningless and could mask a real regression.

text: [PERF] The scaling-law tests (#6/#7/#12/#13) MUST be validated at ≥2 additional points of the config matrix — a coarse-interval point (e.g. 4h) and a wide point (e.g. 200 symbols / 365 days) — each with its own latency budget, proving linear scaling holds across the surface, not only at 90d×50sym×5m.
rationale: The doubling tests prove relative scaling at one fixture point; absolute budgets and scaling validation across interval/symbol-count/length extremes (where memory and seeding behave differently) are unrepresented.

text: [DEP] The @njit kernel MUST be compiled nogil=True so the executor-thread concurrent runs achieve true CPU parallelism; the pure-Python fallback is GIL-bound (concurrent runs serialize) and that degradation MUST be documented and reflected in the concurrent-run latency budget, verified by a test comparing concurrent throughput JIT vs fallback.
rationale: Concurrent-run scalability (#103/#26) silently depends on the kernel releasing the GIL; without nogil the 3 executor threads serialize, and the fallback's GIL-bound concurrency penalty is unstated.

text: [STORAGE] Building the Parquet/Feather read cache for the first time (cold columnar build) MUST have a bounded build-time and disk-footprint budget (footprint ≈ working-set size, no small-file sprawl per #108), and that one-time build cost MUST be excluded from / amortized out of the steady-state warm latency targets; a test MUST assert footprint and build time stay within budget.
rationale: #157 covers rebuild correctness but not the cost of building the columnar cache; an unbudgeted first-build could itself blow the run budget or sprawl disk, undermining the warm-path targets.

text: [ENGINE] The hot per-candle loop (especially the pure-Python SoA fallback) MUST allocate no per-candle Python objects (verified by a tracemalloc/allocation-counter delta ≈ 0 across the bar loop), and the timed run MAY disable/freeze GC around the kernel so GC pauses do not perturb the latency target; a test MUST assert allocation-per-candle ≈ 0 and bound GC-induced variance.
rationale: #31 eliminates per-row dicts at bulk-load but not per-candle allocations inside the simulation loop (RC-10's candles_at_time/list copies); high allocation rate triggers GC pauses that destabilize the sub-10s latency target and the fallback's ≥100k evals/sec floor.

text: Add a golden fixture for direction='reverse' (straight vs reverse paired oracles) asserting the split-semantics are preserved EXACTLY: the executed position side is flipped via determine_side(direction, 'reverse'), while signal_sides and the signed max_price_drift check are evaluated on the RAW PRE-reverse signal direction, and max_same_direction is counted on the POST-reverse trade side — so the SoA/numba refactor cannot collapse the pre/post-reverse direction usages.
rationale: engine L644/667/739/920 use the signal direction at three different reverse stages; 'direction' is a documented Execution config field but no item in the 208 pins its flip semantics. A refactor that uniformly applies (or omits) the reverse flip would change selection and PnL sign while still passing the straight-only canonical oracle.

text: Add F1-regime-ACTIVE golden fixtures (separate from the regime-disabled no-op) freezing oracles for each enabled gate: regime_filter_enabled with gate_session skipping signals inside a blocked/allowed hour window, gate_btc_vol skipping on a btc_vol min/max threshold breach, and routing_regime routing a signal to trend/none — asserting each ACTIVELY filters/routes and that toggling the feature off restores the byte-identical 5m path.
rationale: engine L524-528/L605-622 run the live F1 gates, but the 208 only assert the regime-DISABLED no-op (72/156/177) and the MR cohort (178). The regime/session/btc-vol ACTIVE branches have zero frozen oracle, so a refactor could silently neuter or alter them undetected.

text: Pin the scan PROCESSING ORDER: scans MUST be iterated in the exact order produced by a STABLE sort keyed on each scan's first-signal signal_time (engine L211), including the stable tie-break for scans sharing an identical first-signal timestamp; the SoA/global-timeline/parallel refactor MUST reproduce this iteration order bit-for-bit because wallet sizing, cycle re-anchor, smart-drawdown re-arm, and the adaptive blacklist are all path-dependent on it — verified by a fixture with equal-timestamp scans that would diverge under any reordering.
rationale: 79/80/185/206 pin equity-curve order, blacklist point-in-time, and dict/set determinism, but none pin the top-level scan iteration order or its stable tie-break. A global sorted-unique timeline (Phase 3) could re-derive scan order with a different tie-break and shift every path-dependent latch.

text: The Buy&Hold BTC baseline (_attach_buy_hold fetching BTC/USDT klines on a SEPARATE path from the symbol loaders) MUST route through the same sealed-manifest/LRU cache so a warm rerun still issues 0 Bybit HTTP calls (otherwise it violates the warm-rerun 0-call guarantee), MUST keep buy_hold_return_pct/buy_hold_final_value/excess_return within the <1% oracle tolerance, and MUST null those keys (never abort _persist_results) on fetch failure — verified by a warm-rerun call-count assertion plus a fetch-failure test.
rationale: backtest_service L1331-1381 fetches BTC independently; req 3 asserts 'exactly 0 Bybit HTTP calls' on warm rerun but the buy&hold BTC pull is uncounted and could break it, and no item pins the buy_hold_*/excess_return metrics (part of the ~45 keys) or the fetch-failure null path.

text: The Phase-1 bounded per-symbol kline span (req 19) MUST be proven to NEVER increase signals_no_kline versus a full-range load: the bounded window MUST always include each signal's entry-fill bar (first open_time >= signal_time) AND the full max-hold/close-rule evaluation window, so no trade the full-range oracle opened is dropped as 'no kline'. A test loads the same fixture with bounded vs unbounded spans and asserts identical signals_no_kline AND an identical opened-trade set.
rationale: req 19 bounds the load to [min(signal)-lookback, max(signal)+max_hold] but nothing guarantees the bound is wide enough to retain the next-bar-open fill and the hold window; an off-by-one tightening would raise signals_no_kline (engine L855/L885) and silently change trade SELECTION while leaving the canonical oracle untouched.

text: The equity_curve construction MUST be reproduced exactly: seed a starting_capital anchor point at the first scan's first signal_time, append a point on every close, apply the final STABLE sort keyed (ts is not None, ts) (None-first), and append the terminal final_equity==wallet_balance point ONLY when it differs from the last point by > 1e-9 — verified by hashing the ordered curve including the seed anchor and the conditional terminal dedup.
rationale: engine L218-222 (seed), L327-329 (None-first stable sort), L339-343 (>1e-9 terminal dedup) are specific path-dependent constructions feeding Sharpe/Sortino/max-dd; 68/79 pin 'point on every close' and 'order preserved' but not the seed anchor, the None-first sort key, or the dedup threshold, any of which a reorder could perturb.

text: Add explicit searchsorted/merge-walk BOUNDARY-equivalence fixtures beyond the randomized property test: empty kline array, single-candle array, signal_time strictly before the first candle, and signal_time at/after the last candle — asserting side='left' index == len maps to signals_no_kline+SKIP (no fabricated fill from a stale close), side='right'-1 == -1 maps to 'no prior mark' identically to the linear-scan no-break case, and no path raises IndexError.
rationale: req 62/193 cover next-bar semantics and a randomized property test, but randomized timelines need not deterministically hit the empty/single/out-of-range array ends where np.searchsorted returns 0 or len; these boundaries are exactly where an off-by-one fabricates a fill or throws.

text: A run (or sweep combo) terminated by the 120s hard wall-clock cap (_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, distinct from the cooperative threading.Event cancel of req 208) MUST leave the backtest_runs row in 'failed' with an actionable timeout message, release its _MAX_CONCURRENT slot, and leave the shared in-process LRU / Arrow hot cache / sealed manifest UNCORRUPTED (no torn or partially-inserted entry from the interrupted load) — verified by a test that forces a mid-load timeout and asserts a subsequent run reads byte-identical klines and the slot count is restored.
rationale: req 103/208 cover cache concurrency safety and cooperative cancel, but the HARD-timeout kill is a different mechanism; interrupting a load that is mid-insert into the new shared cache could leave a torn entry or leak a concurrency slot, breaking the next run with no parity test guarding the failure path.

text: Pin the MR concurrent-position cap: on a mean_reversion route the engine MUST gate new entries on mr_max_trades (default 2) counted against ALL currently-open positions, NOT the generic max_trades (default 999) — verified by a fixture where the (mr_max_trades+1)th MR signal within a scan is filtered while the same config under trend would admit it.
rationale: engine L619-622 enforces mr_max_trades as the concurrent cap (an IR6 parity fix that prevents wild MR over-trading); req 178 adds an MR cohort fixture but does not explicitly assert the mr_max_trades concurrent cap vs the generic max_trades, a distinct and easily-regressed admission rule.

text: Pin the inter-scan evaluation-window candle MEMBERSHIP: the merge-walk replacing _evaluate_candles_until MUST evaluate exactly the same set of bars in the half-open interval [current_time, next_scan_time) (same first and last bar, same inclusive/exclusive boundary handling) as the current window scan, so no liq/TP/SL/equity/time rule fires one bar early or late — verified by a fixture with an exit level touched on the boundary bar between two adjacent scans.
rationale: the RC-1 fix replaces a 'continue-not-break' full window scan with pointer/searchsorted bounds; an off-by-one on the half-open [current_time, next_scan_time) boundary would shift which bar an exit fires on. 6/13 pin perf scaling and 63 pins mark-seeding identity, but the per-window evaluated-bar SET boundary is not pinned.

text: progress_pct MUST remain monotonic non-decreasing from 0 to a terminal 100 across the warmup band (_WARMUP_BAND=10%), Phase A, and Phase B on the optimized path even after the cache fix makes warm-up near-instant — verified by a test capturing the on_progress callback sequence and asserting it never decreases and ends at 100, so a stage collapsing to ~0ms cannot produce a stalled or backward progress bar.
rationale: backtest_service L815-832 bands progress across warm-up + two passes; the Phase-1 cache fix makes warm-up near-instant, a downstream effect on the progress UI that the 208 do not guard, and a non-monotonic or stuck bar is a user-visible regression even when results are correct.

text: Concurrent cold loads of the same (symbol, interval, range) MUST coalesce into a single in-flight fetch (single-flight/request-coalescing), so a K-combo parallel sweep on a cold cache issues 1 Bybit fetch for a shared range, not K; verified by a test launching ≥4 concurrent loads of the same uncached range against a mocked client and asserting fetch call_count == 1.
rationale: Phase 2/6 introduce concurrent sweep combos and asyncio.gather. Reqs 5/17/186 only bound fetch counts for SEQUENTIAL reruns. Without a single-flight guard, N concurrent combos stampede the same uncached range before the first populates the cache/manifest, multiplying cold Bybit calls and directly undermining the 'sealed day fetched exactly once' guarantee under the new parallelism.

text: A day MUST be sealed ONLY after a SUCCESSFUL (HTTP 200, non-error, non-throttled) exchange response; a failed/partial/timeout/429 fetch MUST leave the day unsealed for retry, store whatever partial rows it got idempotently, apply bounded backoff/retry on transient/429 errors, and surface an actionable error (with symbol+range) if the range cannot be completed within budget — never silently seal or corrupt the manifest on failure.
rationale: Seal rule 86 says merely 'an exchange response has been fetched', which is ambiguous about success. If a transient outage/429 returns empty or partial and that counts as 'fetched' once day.end<=frontier, the day is permanently sealed as incomplete (data loss). The 200→1000 page-size bump (req 18) also increases per-request payload and rate-limit pressure. Failure-path sealing semantics and backoff are unspecified across all 208 items.

text: A day carrying a recorded transient/structural gap (negative-cache TTL entry) that reaches the completion frontier before its TTL heals MUST be re-verified against the exchange exactly once before being permanently sealed as known-empty, so a real day with real candles is never frozen empty by the seal-forever rule racing the heal-TTL.
rationale: Reqs 92/93 establish 'record gaps as known-empty' and 'TTL for transient gaps' as independent mechanisms, but their INTERACTION is a data-correctness hazard: sealing freezes forever (req 86) while the TTL heals lazily. A transient gap recorded near the live edge can cross the frontier and be sealed-as-empty before the heal ever runs. The re-verify-before-permanent-seal rule is not covered.

text: The completion frontier MUST be computed from a UTC clock with an explicit safety lag (e.g. frontier = floor((now_utc − skew_margin)/T)*T) so a fast/skewed host clock can never prematurely seal the still-forming live-edge day; verified by a test that advances a simulated clock with skew and asserts the current/forming day remains unsealed and refreshable.
rationale: Reqs 83/84/88 define the frontier formula and timestamp-based live-edge detection but assume a correct clock. Premature sealing (host clock ahead of exchange time) permanently freezes the forming day with partial candles — the exact failure mode the whole design exists to prevent. No requirement guards clock skew.

text: All day bucketing (day_utc manifest key), frontier math, lifecycle comparisons, and Parquet hive year/month partitioning MUST be UTC-only with zero naive-local-datetime/DST leakage on the Windows host; verified by a test that runs identical bucketing under multiple TZ/DST settings (and with the open_time-may-be-datetime-OR-epoch input) and asserts identical day keys and partition paths.
rationale: The host is Windows (local TZ), open_time may be datetime OR epoch (RC/req 64), and the manifest, frontier, and partition layout all key on calendar days. A single naive-local datetime leak shifts a candle into the wrong day/month partition or mis-buckets the frontier. tz-safety of the new day-keyed surfaces is not asserted anywhere.

text: Seal/materialization MUST be crash-consistent with the manifest 'sealed=true' commit performed LAST (after Postgres rows AND the Parquet/Feather partition are durably written), and the columnar read path MUST fall back to the Postgres SOR for any day the manifest reports sealed but whose Parquet partition is missing/unreadable — so a crash between writes never yields a sealed-but-data-missing read; verified by a test that kills materialization mid-way and asserts the next read is correct (via fallback) and never returns short data.
rationale: Postgres is write-of-record and Parquet is a derived cache (req 113), but nothing orders the multi-store seal nor handles 'manifest says sealed, Parquet not yet written' (a guaranteed race once sealing and columnar materialization are separate steps). Req 109 only covers single-file atomic rename, not the cross-store ordering or the missing-partition read fallback.

text: The per-day manifest seal write MUST be a race-safe idempotent upsert (ON CONFLICT) so concurrent runs/sweep combos sealing the same (symbol, interval, day_utc) converge with no error and no duplicate/divergent row; verified by a concurrency test sealing the same day from ≥3 concurrent writers under _MAX_CONCURRENT and asserting a single consistent sealed row.
rationale: Req 103 covers thread-safety of the in-process LRU/hot cache, but the sealed-manifest is a Postgres table written concurrently by up to _MAX_CONCURRENT=3 runs plus parallel sweep combos. A non-idempotent seal INSERT/UPDATE under concurrency can deadlock, error, or write divergent gap_count/sha. The DB-level seal-write concurrency contract is uncovered.

text: Incrementally sealing days within a not-yet-complete month MUST correctly extend that month's immutable Parquet partition (atomic read-modify-write of the month file, or multiple atomic part files per month) without data loss and without a concurrent reader ever observing a partial/duplicated month; verified by a test that seals days across a month boundary one run at a time and asserts the month partition row set equals the Postgres SOR after each seal.
rationale: Reqs 107/108 mandate month-granularity hive partitions and 109 mandates atomic writes, but Parquet files are IMMUTABLE — you cannot append a newly-sealed day in place. Days seal incrementally as the frontier advances, so each new seal within a current month requires rewriting/adding a part file. This incremental-append-to-immutable strategy (and its atomicity/no-loss guarantee) is unspecified.

text: content_sha256 MUST be computed over a CANONICAL representation (rows sorted by open_time with a fixed column order and fixed numeric serialization), so a hash recomputed from a Postgres rebuild equals the hash from the original paginated fetch; verified by a test that ingests a day via paginated fetch and via a Postgres-rebuild and asserts identical content_sha256 (no spurious hash-mismatch refetch).
rationale: Req 118 stores content_sha256 and refetches only on hash mismatch, but does not pin HOW the hash is computed. If it hashes raw paginated response bytes (page order/whitespace-dependent) instead of canonical sorted rows, every Postgres-rebuild or columnar re-materialization produces a different hash → false integrity-mismatch → exactly the perpetual refetch the design is eliminating. Canonical-serialization is the prerequisite that makes req 118 safe.

text: The derived columnar read (Parquet/Feather/Arrow) MUST be integrity-verified against the manifest content_sha256 (or the Postgres SOR) at rebuild time and on a read-time integrity check, and a mismatch/corruption MUST invalidate+rebuild that partition rather than return corrupt klines; verified by a test that bit-corrupts a Parquet file and asserts the read self-heals to SOR-identical data instead of silently mis-reading.
rationale: Req 115 asserts the columnar path is byte-identical at TEST time and 157 covers manual clear/rebuild, but nothing detects RUNTIME corruption (bit-rot, a partial write that survived rename, a half-rebuilt file) of the derived layer. Because golden tests run against a clean fixture, silent production corruption of the derived cache would pass tests yet return wrong klines. The derived layer needs an integrity check tied to the stored sha.

text: Every derived columnar file (Parquet/Feather) MUST embed a schema/format version, and a version or Arrow-schema mismatch on read MUST invalidate+rebuild from Postgres rather than mis-read; verified by a test that writes a file with an older/incompatible schema tag and asserts the new reader rebuilds it instead of returning wrong/garbled columns.
rationale: Reqs 123/153/160 cover import-guarding and rolling app code back/forward against the v58 SCHEMA and old RESULT rows, but the DERIVED cache files persist on disk across deploys. A forward roll after a column/dtype change in the columnar layout would read stale-schema files. Without an embedded version + rebuild-on-mismatch, a code upgrade silently mis-reads previously-written Parquet/Feather.

text: A derived coarse-timeframe bar (15m/1h/4h) MUST be treated as complete/sealed if and only if ALL of its constituent sealed fine (5m) base bars exist and are sealed; the forming coarse bar (whose final fine bars are past the frontier) MUST never be cached or sealed; verified by a test that a 4h bar whose last hour is still forming is excluded from the sealed coarse set and recomputed on the next run.
rationale: Reqs 116/117 mandate deriving coarse TFs from the fine base and assert value-parity vs native coarse, but never state the coarse COMPLETENESS/seal rule. Since the engine supports simulation_interval up to 4h, a coarse bar derived from a partially-formed final hour would be treated as final — re-introducing the live-edge-staleness bug at the coarse layer. The all-fine-bars-sealed rule is the coarse analogue of the frontier and is uncovered.

text: When both exchange-native coarse rows AND derive-from-fine are available for a coarse interval, the read path MUST use ONE documented authoritative source deterministically, and a coarse-interval (15m/1h/4h) backtest MUST NOT silently switch data source between runs; verified by a test asserting a 4h backtest returns identical klines (and identical trades) whether or not native 4h rows happen to be cached.
rationale: Req 116 says 'store ONE base granularity, derive coarse', but the EXISTING kline_cache already holds separately-fetched native coarse rows. If the read path nondeterministically prefers native-4h on one run and derived-from-5m on another (which can differ at boundaries, hence the '<within tolerance>' hedge in 117), a coarse backtest's results change between runs with no config change — a parity regression. The source-of-truth precedence for coarse intervals is unspecified.

text: symbol_lifecycle (listing_time/delist_time) MUST be populated and refreshed from exchange instrument info, and when a symbol's listing/delist is UNKNOWN/NULL the loader MUST fetch-and-discover (treating the range as potentially live) rather than negatively-caching/skipping it as pre-listing; verified by a test that a symbol with no lifecycle row is still fetched and not auto-skipped as empty.
rationale: Req 89 persists the lifecycle table and 90 negatively-caches pre-listing/post-delist ranges, but neither defines the lifecycle DATA SOURCE/refresh nor the UNKNOWN-lifecycle behavior. If an unpopulated (NULL) lifecycle is treated as 'not yet listed', the negcache predicate (req 91: day_overlaps[listing,delist]) would skip ALL real candles for any symbol missing a lifecycle row — silent data loss masquerading as a complete cache.

text: The v58 backfill MUST distinguish a genuinely-incomplete interior day (a true structural hole confirmed within [listing,delist]) from a complete-but-short day: incomplete interior days MUST be recorded with gap_count>0 and marked known-empty for those missing sub-ranges (count-free, never a perpetual refetch candidate), NOT silently sealed as full; verified by a test seeding an interior day with a real hole and asserting post-migration it is sealed-with-gap_count and triggers zero refetch.
rationale: Req 130 backfills sealed=true for every day with end<frontier within lifecycle, and req 87 makes count irrelevant to fetching — but together they would seal a day that has a genuine recoverable hole as 'complete', and reqs 92/132 only cover RUNTIME re-verified gaps, not the one-time backfill. The backfill must capture gap_count for real holes so they become known-empty (immutable closed candles) rather than being indistinguishable from full days.

text: The durable derived columnar store (Parquet/Feather/mmap) MUST have a bounded disk budget with a retention/GC policy that evicts regenerable files (LRU-by-access) and disk-usage observability, and cleanup MUST never touch the Postgres SOR; verified by a test that exceeding the budget evicts least-recently-used partitions and a subsequent read transparently rebuilds them from Postgres.
rationale: Reqs 30/102 bound process RSS and the in-process LRU, but nothing bounds or GCs the ON-DISK derived cache. A long-lived deployment running sweeps over many symbols/intervals accumulates Parquet/Feather for every range ever backtested, growing disk unboundedly. Since these files are explicitly regenerable from Postgres (req 113), they are safe to evict — but the budget, retention policy, and disk observability are absent.

text: GET /backtest-cache/status gap/readiness computation MUST consult the sealed manifest so a sealed-short day (e.g. a 144/288 listing day) is reported READY and excluded from symbols_with_gaps; verified by a test asserting a symbol whose only 'gap' is a sealed-short day shows ready=true and symbols_with_gaps does not count it.
rationale: Req 162 pins the CacheStatusResponse SHAPE (additive-only) but not its SEMANTICS. The status endpoint today derives symbols_with_gaps/ready from the same count-based get_coverage_gaps that causes the re-download bug. Once sealing exists, a sealed-short day must stop counting as a gap — otherwise the UI shows perpetual 'not ready'/'has gaps' for legitimately-complete symbols, a user-visible regression distinct from the response shape.

text: A cache-efficiency REGRESSION GATE (separate from the wall-time/RSS gate) MUST run each phase's PR and FAIL on regression of Bybit kline call count or kline-table SELECT count on the canonical fixture: a sealed day must stay at 0 refetch and a warm rerun must stay at 0 kline SELECTs; the gate asserts these counters do not increase phase-over-phase.
rationale: Req 36 gates only end-to-end wall-time (>5%) and peak RSS (>10%). A refactor (e.g. an LRU key change, a manifest-gate bypass, a lost single-flight) could silently reintroduce refetches/SELECTs while wall-time still passes on a warm machine. The cache-call-count and DB-query-count are the direct invariants of the whole Phase-1 cache fix and need their own non-regression gate, not just a timing gate.

text: Every production backtest/sweep run (not only the benchmark harness) MUST emit structured cache statistics — LRU/hot-cache hit/miss counts, days sealed this run, negative-cache skips, Bybit kline calls, and bytes read from the columnar layer — so a real-world cache regression is attributable without re-running the benchmark; verified by a test asserting these fields are present in the run's structured log/metric output.
rationale: Req 35 reports these only inside the committed benchmark harness and req 39 covers per-STAGE timing, not cache hit/miss/seal/negcache counts. In production, a degraded cache (e.g. a symbol whose lifecycle is misconfigured and silently refetches) would be invisible until someone runs the benchmark. Per-run cache-stat emission is the operational observability hook that makes cache degradations diagnosable in situ.

text: Migration v58 MUST acquire a Postgres advisory lock (or equivalent serialization) before applying DDL+backfill so that concurrent app-instance/worker startups (multiple uvicorn workers, or an old+new pod overlap during a rolling deploy) cannot run v58 simultaneously; verified by a test that spawns two concurrent migration runners and asserts the DDL and seal backfill execute exactly once with no duplicate-column/partition/constraint error.
rationale: CLAUDE.md states migrations auto-apply on app startup; with _MAX_CONCURRENT and multi-worker/rolling deploys, two processes can race v58. None of items 120-134 address concurrent execution — they assume a single serial runner.

text: The v58 seal backfill MUST NOT cause the app's readiness/liveness probe to fail during boot: it MUST either complete within a bounded wall-clock budget OR run deferred/asynchronously so a large kline_cache_coverage does not delay readiness past the orchestrator's probe timeout (pod kill); verified by a test asserting the app reports ready within a defined N seconds even when the coverage table is large.
rationale: Item 133 bounds the backfill to a statement budget to avoid DB locks, but does not address the deploy-orchestrator killing a booting pod whose startup migration runs long — a distinct operational failure mode.

text: numba/pyarrow/duckdb MUST be declared so that core `pip install` SUCCEEDS on a target Python (e.g. 3.14.3) that has NO prebuilt wheel for one of them — i.e. as optional extras or environment-marker-gated, NOT unconditional hard pins — so an unbuildable/missing wheel cannot fail the entire backend install; verified by a CI job that installs the core package WITHOUT the accel extra and asserts backend.main imports and the canonical backtest runs pure-Python.
rationale: Direct tension with item 138 (which mandates them in [project].dependencies with >= floors). Discovery §10/D5 warns Python 3.14.3+numpy 2.4.4 may have no numba wheel; a hard dependency would make `pip install` fail outright, leaving the backend undeployable. Install-time optionality is unaddressed.

text: Adding numba/pyarrow/duckdb MUST NOT force a numpy (or pandas) downgrade below the floor required by existing transitive users; the resolved environment MUST be mutually compatible; verified by a test that parses the resolved/locked versions and asserts numpy simultaneously satisfies numba's pin and pandas' floor.
rationale: Discovery §10 notes numpy 2.4.4 arrives transitively via pandas and numba pins numpy tightly. A resolver-induced numpy downgrade could silently break pandas/other code. No item covers cross-dependency version compatibility.

text: A committed, hash-pinned lockfile MUST capture the exact numba/llvmlite/pyarrow/duckdb (and transitive) versions so the deploy target resolves byte-identical packages to CI; verified by a CI check that the lockfile is in sync with pyproject floors and that llvmlite resolves to a single pinned version.
rationale: Item 138 sets >= floors and 146 checks wheels, but neither pins exact transitive versions. llvmlite is the fragile link; floor-only resolution can drift between CI and prod, producing a different (possibly source-built/incompatible) llvmlite.

text: CI MUST assert prebuilt wheels for numba+llvmlite+pyarrow+duckdb exist for the EXACT production base image's libc (e.g. musl/Alpine if used, not just generic manylinux); if the prod image is musl-based and a wheel is unavailable, the build MUST fail OR the accel extra MUST be omitted from that image with the pure-Python path verified; tested by a containerized install on the prod base image.
rationale: Item 146 checks OS/arch/Python generically but musl/Alpine commonly lacks numba/llvmlite wheels, forcing a source build that fails. The specific deploy container libc is the real risk surface and is unaddressed.

text: Because container __pycache__ is ephemeral (numba cache=True yields no cross-deploy benefit), the boot-time JIT warm-up MUST be explicitly invoked in the prod startup sequence on a path that (a) never delays readiness and (b) on warm-up failure logs and DISABLES the JIT (falls back) rather than crashing boot; verified by a test simulating warm-up raising and asserting boot completes with JIT disabled and the run still produces oracle-identical output.
rationale: Items 143/144 cover compile-latency-out-of-deadline and read-only cache dir, but not that in containers the persisted cache is useless so boot warm-up is the ONLY protection — and that warm-up must be non-blocking and crash-safe.

text: The system MUST define how symbol_lifecycle.listing_time/delist_time are populated and MUST treat an absent/NULL row as listing=-inf / delist=+inf (fetch-everything), NEVER as 'out of life → seal as known-empty'; verified by a test with an EMPTY symbol_lifecycle table asserting normal fetch/seal behavior is unchanged and no day is falsely negative-cached.
rationale: Item 129 creates symbol_lifecycle and items 89-91 consult it in the fetch predicate, but nothing populates it. On a fresh migration the table is empty; without defined NULL semantics the negcache predicate (91) could suppress all legitimate fetches.

text: The v58 backfill MUST seal a (symbol,interval,day) ONLY when a coverage row already exists for it (an exchange response was previously stored); it MUST NOT fabricate seals for days with no coverage row, so a never-fetched true hole remains fetchable; verified by a test asserting a day with no coverage row stays unsealed/fetchable after migration while a fetched-but-short day is sealed.
rationale: Item 86 defines 'sealed iff end<=frontier AND fetched', and 130 seals days in range, but the backfill must honor the 'has been fetched' precondition — otherwise a genuine never-fetched gap would be permanently sealed as complete, hiding real missing data.

text: A backfilled sealed day (sealed without a fresh refetch) MUST carry NULL/sentinel content_sha256, and the integrity escape-hatch (item 118) MUST treat NULL sha as 'no integrity check available' — never as a hash-mismatch that triggers refetch and never raising; verified by a test sealing a historic day with NULL sha and asserting zero refetch and no error on the integrity path.
rationale: Item 118 allows refetch only on hash-mismatch, but backfilled historic seals have no sha (we don't refetch them). Undefined NULL-sha handling could either crash the integrity check or trigger mass refetches of exactly the days the seal was meant to protect.

text: The v58 backfill MUST populate first_open_ts/last_open_ts/candle_count from existing kline_cache_coverage/kline_cache data and set listing_snapped=false and delisted from symbol_lifecycle (default false) rather than leaving fact columns NULL where the value is already known; verified by a post-backfill test asserting fact columns match the source coverage rows.
rationale: Items 128/130 add the columns and seal the flag but do not specify populating the provenance/fact columns from data already in coverage, leaving the manifest partially blank and degrading later gap/integrity logic.

text: Rolling the application back to a pre-v58 build whose migration runner inspects schema_version MUST NOT refuse to start or attempt a downgrade when it observes schema_version=58 (> its expected 57); verified by a test running the legacy migration runner/boot path against a v58 DB and asserting it no-ops on version and serves normally.
rationale: Item 153 covers legacy code tolerating additive columns/tables, but not the schema_version guard itself — old code that asserts version==expected (or <=) could crash-loop against a forward-migrated DB during a rollback.

text: A single master kill-switch env var (e.g. BACKTEST_SAFE_MODE/BACKTEST_LEGACY_MODE) MUST force ALL optimization flags (sealed-manifest gate, batched loaders, SoA, numba, Parquet/DuckDB, fast-path, parallel sweep) off in one operation so an operator can revert every accelerated path without redeploy or flipping seven vars; verified by a test asserting the master switch routes engine+cache+storage+sweep to the legacy path and reproduces the golden master byte-for-byte.
rationale: Items 147-151 give per-optimization flags, but emergency prod rollback needs one atomic operator action. Flipping seven independent vars under incident pressure is error-prone and is not covered.

text: Each backtest run MUST persist (in its run/result row) the resolved set of optimization flags actually in effect plus the engine git SHA, so a stored result's numbers are reproducible and a prod incident can identify which code path produced them; verified by a test asserting the persisted run records the flag snapshot and SHA, and that two runs with different flag sets are distinguishable.
rationale: Item 183 stamps SHA/config-hash into oracle snapshots (test-time) and 35/39 are bench/stage timing, but live runs do not record which flags/SHA were active — without it, a divergent prod result cannot be attributed to a path or reproduced.

text: The running service (not just the bench harness) MUST expose a runtime counter of Bybit kline calls and sealed-day refetch ATTEMPTS per run with a documented alert threshold, so a re-emergence of the re-download bug is detectable/alertable in production; verified by a test asserting the counter increments on a (mocked) sealed-day refetch and is zero on a warm rerun.
rationale: Item 35 reports Bybit call count inside the committed bench harness only. The core defect (closed days re-downloaded) needs a production runtime signal with alerting, not just a CI fixture, to catch regressions after deploy.

text: Each run MUST record a data-provenance breakdown of which storage tier served kline loads (Postgres / Parquet / Arrow-hot / mmap-Feather) and the per-tier hit ratio, so a latency or correctness incident can be traced to the serving tier; verified by a test asserting the provenance breakdown is emitted and its counts sum to the total load count.
rationale: Item 39 records per-stage timing and 164 records degradation events, but neither attributes loads to a concrete storage tier — essential for diagnosing a stale-read or slow-tier incident in the multi-tier (PG/Parquet/Arrow/mmap) read path.

text: Because kline_cache_service is shared with the live scanner/trading path, the sealed-manifest fetch gate MUST preserve the LIVE path's current-day freshness and coverage semantics (the forming day still refreshed for the live scanner/auto-trade), verified by a test exercising the LIVE (non-backtest) fetch path and asserting current-day data is refreshed while sealed days are skipped.
rationale: The optimization targets backtest, but kline_cache_service is shared with scanner_service/auto-trade/reconciler. A sealed-manifest gate tuned for backtest could starve live trading of fresh current-day candles — a downstream regression no item guards.

text: Adding optimization/infra flags to the config MUST NOT change the sweep config_hash for a logically-identical strategy config, so pre-existing sweep_results rows stay matchable under ON CONFLICT(sweep_id, config_hash) and warm cache hits survive the deploy; verified by a test asserting a config's hash is unchanged when only optimization/infra flags differ from a pre-upgrade config.
rationale: Items 76/207 assert parallel==serial idempotency but not hash STABILITY across the upgrade. If new flags enter the hashed payload, every prior sweep row becomes a miss and dedup/crash-resume idempotency silently breaks after deploy.

text: POST /backtest/compare MUST render correctly when comparing a pre-optimization result row (missing new optional metric keys) against a new-engine result row, supplying defaults for absent keys without error; verified by a contract test comparing an old-shape result against a new-shape result and asserting both columns populate.
rationale: Item 160 covers reading/rendering a single old row, but the compare endpoint cross-references TWO rows of potentially different schema versions — a distinct null-handling path that can throw when one side lacks new keys.

text: store_klines auto-creating a monthly partition for a >6mo-old or future range MUST be safe under _MAX_CONCURRENT concurrent writers (CREATE … PARTITION IF NOT EXISTS plus catch duplicate/overlap, or an advisory lock), so two simultaneous runs writing the same missing partition do not error; verified by a concurrent-write test asserting no partition-collision exception and that both writes land.
rationale: Item 137 requires auto-create/fall-through but not concurrency safety. With up to 3 concurrent runs, two can race to create the same partition; PG raises on duplicate/overlapping partition, aborting one run.

text: The Parquet/Feather derived cache MUST enforce a documented disk/retention budget with eviction of old partitions, and a full or unwritable cache volume MUST degrade to the Postgres read path (log + continue) rather than failing the run; verified by a test simulating a full/unwritable cache dir and asserting the run completes correctly from Postgres.
rationale: Item 157 covers manual clear/rebuild, but not bounded growth or graceful behavior when the cache disk fills — an unbounded derived cache or a full volume could crash runs in prod.

text: After a crash mid atomic temp-write+rename (item 109), orphaned temp files MUST be ignored by readers (never partially read) and reaped by a cleanup pass so they cannot accumulate; verified by a test that plants a leftover temp file and asserts reads ignore it and a cleanup pass removes it.
rationale: Item 109 guarantees atomic writes but not recovery from a crash BETWEEN temp-write and rename — orphaned partials must be provably non-readable and garbage-collected, an operational gap.

text: Each Feather/Parquet/Arrow artifact MUST carry a cache-format/schema version stamp; a new code version with a bumped stamp MUST ignore and regenerate artifacts written by an older format so a deploy never mmap-reads an incompatible old-format file; verified by a test that writes an old-stamp artifact and asserts the new code regenerates rather than mis-reading it.
rationale: Items 112/158 invalidate the forming-day mmap but not artifacts whose on-disk FORMAT changed across a deploy. A zero-deserialization mmap of an old-format Feather after an upgrade is a silent corruption risk.

text: A post-migration/post-deploy canary smoke test MUST run a tiny canonical backtest end-to-end against the migrated DB (mocked exchange), assert it completes, reconciles (Σ trade.pnl == final_equity − starting_capital), and returns a present metrics.total_trades — gating the deploy as healthy before traffic; verified by the smoke test existing in the deploy pipeline and failing on a broken v58 migration or missing column.
rationale: Unit fixtures (166-208) validate logic, but no end-to-end post-deploy gate exercises the freshly-migrated prod DB. A migration/partition/column fault would otherwise surface only on first user run.

text: A v58 dry-run/pre-flight mode MUST report, WITHOUT writing, how many days would be sealed, how many sealed ranges fall in kline_cache_default (no dedicated partition), and any symbol_lifecycle coverage gaps, so a risky migration is caught before apply; verified by a test asserting the dry-run produces these counts and performs zero writes.
rationale: Items 122/135-137 require partition handling for >6mo ranges, but offer no way to assess blast radius before committing v58 on a large prod history. A pre-flight report is a standard migration-safety control that is missing.

text: Backtest endpoints MUST NOT read the new v58 columns until schema_version=58 is confirmed applied; if v58 failed and rolled back to 57 (item 134), the app MUST still boot and serve the legacy path rather than crash-loop on missing columns; verified by a test running the service at schema_version=57 and asserting backtests execute on the legacy path.
rationale: Item 134 guarantees a clean rollback to 57 on failure, and 155 covers in-run degradation, but not that the SERVICE remains bootable and functional at 57 (new code expecting v58 columns could crash-loop after a failed migration).

text: Before the Parquet/DuckDB read path is trusted in prod, a shadow/dark-read mode MUST read from BOTH Postgres and the columnar cache and log any byte-divergence WITHOUT affecting the run's returned result (Postgres remains authoritative), enabling progressive rollout; verified by a test asserting shadow mode compares both tiers, emits a divergence metric, and returns the Postgres result.
rationale: Item 149 is a binary kill-switch and 115 is a CI parity test, but neither provides an in-prod progressive-validation step. Shadow-read catches real-data divergence the columnar path would otherwise expose only after it is already authoritative.

text: Backfilled sealed days MUST receive a sentinel/NULL fetched_at and be provably exempt from the unsealed-gap TTL refetch (item 93), so the one-time seal of historic days is never re-aged into a refetch; verified by a test advancing the clock far past any TTL and asserting zero refetch of backfilled sealed days.
rationale: Item 93 restricts TTL to unsealed/transient days in general, but does not pin the backfill case: backfilled seals get no real fetched_at, and an unspecified default could make the TTL logic treat them as stale and refetch the entire sealed history.

text: The entire golden + cache test suite MUST run hermetically with the Bybit client fully mocked and outbound network blocked, plus an explicit guard test asserting zero real HTTP egress occurs during the suite run (any live call fails the build).
rationale: None of the 208 items guarantee suite-wide network isolation; 17/186 mock the client only for specific fetch-count tests. Without a no-egress guarantee the golden/benchmark suite is non-deterministic and unsafe in CI, and a regressed cache gate could silently hit Bybit and pass.

text: The canonical 90d×50sym benchmark fixture's input data (kline candles + scheduled-scan signals) MUST be materialized deterministically from a committed seed/recorded snapshot checked into the repo, NOT pulled from a live/prod DB, so the perf and parity suites are reproducible and offline-runnable in CI; a test MUST assert the fixture loads with zero DB/exchange dependency.
rationale: Req 35 says 'fixed fixture, fixed seed' but never pins the DATA provenance. Without committed/generated fixture data the entire benchmark + golden battery cannot run hermetically or reproducibly on CI, undermining every parity and regression gate built on it.

text: A manifest-vs-store integrity guard MUST detect when a day marked sealed in the manifest returns fewer rows than its stored candle_count on read (rows deleted, partition dropped, store/manifest skew) and MUST raise an integrity error or trigger the content_sha256 refetch escape hatch — NEVER silently backtest on truncated klines; verified by a test that deletes rows behind a sealed manifest entry and asserts the run fails-loud or refetches.
rationale: Req 98 makes the manifest the single source of truth for completeness and the loader stops issuing COUNT(*), but nothing reconciles the manifest against the actual stored rows. Making the manifest authoritative creates a new silent-data-loss failure mode if the two drift.

text: A test MUST assert the content_sha256 integrity escape hatch works both ways: a sealed day whose recomputed content hash no longer matches the stored hash IS refetched and re-sealed, while a sealed day with a matching hash is NEVER refetched (call_count==0).
rationale: Req 118 defines the hash-mismatch-only refetch behavior but the TEST list (186-192) never exercises it. The escape hatch is the only sanctioned way a sealed day can be refetched, so its correctness (and its non-triggering on matching hashes) must be explicitly proven.

text: A test MUST assert pre-listing and post-delist day ranges produce Bybit mock call_count==0 (never fetched), AND that a symbol listing mid-window loads correctly from its listing day forward with no fetch of out-of-life days.
rationale: Reqs 90/91 define negative caching as behavior and 19 bounds the load span, but no TEST item asserts the out-of-life ranges are actually never hit (call_count==0). This is the load-bounding payoff and a classic place for a regression to silently re-introduce wasted fetches.

text: A frontier exact-boundary test MUST assert off-by-one correctness at the seal edge: a candle with open_time == frontier−T is classified sealed/immutable (memoized, not refetched) while a candle with open_time == frontier is classified forming (refreshed), with the boundary frozen as an oracle.
rationale: Reqs 83/84/88 define frontier = floor(now/T)*T and timestamp-based live-edge detection, but no test pins the exact == frontier / == frontier−T boundary. Off-by-one at the seal boundary would either freeze live data or perpetually refetch the last sealed day.

text: A test MUST assert that a run exceeding the 120s _TIMEOUT_SECONDS hard cap transitions to status='failed' with NO partial backtest_trades, backtest_results, or equity_curve persisted (clean state), distinct from the cooperative threading.Event cancel path.
rationale: Req 208 covers cooperative cancel only; the hard wall-clock timeout is a different code path (the run is killed, not asked to stop). A timeout that leaves half-written trades/results would corrupt the run record and mislead the frontend — the after-failure state must be proven clean.

text: A test MUST assert that a Bybit fetch error or network failure during cold gap-fill (ensure_coverage) surfaces as a run failure or a documented explicit partial-data warning, and does NOT let the engine proceed silently on a truncated/incomplete kline set.
rationale: The during-failure path for the fetch layer is unspecified. With the cache gate trusting the manifest, a fetch error that returns partial data could produce a wrong-but-green backtest. QA must pin that incomplete fetches fail loud rather than silently degrade result correctness.

text: A test MUST assert that when the numba/DuckDB/Parquet accelerated path throws MID-run, the engine restarts that run from a clean state on the pure-Python/Postgres path (not continue-from-failure-point), so the persisted result can never be a spliced hybrid of accelerated + fallback partial outputs; the recovered result MUST be byte-identical to the oracle.
rationale: Req 155 says degrade 'within the same run' but does not forbid mid-stream splicing. Continuing fallback from the failure scan would mix two engines' partial states (latches, equity) and silently violate parity. The all-or-nothing restart is the testable safety property.

text: The float64 SoA/numba tolerance policy MUST be validated across the CI OS/arch matrix: the Decimal-exact byte-identical gate runs ONLY on the 5m no-drill path, and a test MUST assert the float64 paths stay within the defined epsilon (<1% cap) on every CI target (x86/ARM, differing SIMD/BLAS), OR the oracle is regenerated per-arch — so a cross-arch last-bit difference does not false-fail the bit-identical gate.
rationale: Req 199 defines per-path tolerance but assumes a single frozen oracle. numba/numpy reductions vary by SIMD width and arch; the oracle is frozen on one machine. Without explicit cross-arch handling, the byte-identical CI guard (198) will either false-fail or mask real drift when CI hardware differs from the dev box.

text: The ≥90% line AND branch coverage gate MUST be extended to enumerate the NEW modules introduced by the optimization (SoA engine, numba kernel wrapper, sealed-manifest service, Parquet/DuckDB read layer, parallel-sweep orchestrator), not just backtest_engine.py / backtest_service.py / kline_cache_service.py.
rationale: Req 200 names only the 3 legacy files. New logic will live in new modules whose coverage is unmeasured by that gate, so the most novel and highest-risk code (numba kernel, manifest predicates, parquet reads) could ship with near-zero test coverage while the gate stays green.

text: CI MUST include a dedicated 'fallback lane' that runs the FULL golden suite a second time with numba, pyarrow, and duckdb force-absent (simulated ImportError), so every import-guarded except branch and pure-Python/Postgres fallback path is actually executed and counted toward coverage — not merely presence-tested on a few cases.
rationale: Reqs 139/140/196 test fallback on specific cases, but in normal CI the deps are installed, leaving the fallback branches unexecuted across the rest of the suite. The fallback path is a primary supported configuration (Phase 3 must meet budget without numba) and must get equal whole-suite exercise.

text: A test MUST assert migration v58 is safe under concurrent application startup: when two app instances boot simultaneously and both attempt v58, an advisory lock (or atomic schema_version compare-and-set) prevents double-application, deadlock, or partial apply, leaving exactly one v58 applied.
rationale: Migrations auto-apply on startup and the system runs multiple workers (_MAX_CONCURRENT context). Version-int collisions have already happened twice. Concurrent boots racing the v58 DDL/backfill is an unhandled operational failure mode not covered by the idempotency tests (which assume sequential re-runs).

text: A crash-resume completeness test MUST kill a sweep mid-run, restart it, and assert it skips already-written (sweep_id, config_hash) combos, completes the remaining combos, and produces a final ranked set IDENTICAL to an uninterrupted sweep — beyond just write-statement idempotency.
rationale: Req 207 asserts write_result ON CONFLICT idempotency but not end-to-end resume correctness. A resumed sweep must yield the same ranked output as if never interrupted; partial-resume that drops combos or mis-ranks would silently corrupt optimizer results.

text: A concurrent-run isolation test MUST assert that two simultaneous backtests with DIFFERENT configs sharing the process-level kline LRU and 1m-drill cache do not cross-contaminate per-run mutable engine state (adaptive-blacklist closed_trades, cycle_start_equity, smart_drawdown_fired, funding dedupe) — shared caches return immutable kline data only, and each run's result equals its standalone oracle.
rationale: Req 103 covers torn-read/race safety of the shared cache (memory correctness) but not LOGICAL isolation of per-run path-dependent state. With _MAX_CONCURRENT=3 and shared process caches, a leak of one run's trade history into another's adaptive blacklist would silently break parity in a way single-run tests never catch.

text: Add zero-trade and degenerate-input golden fixtures with frozen oracles: (a) a scan where every signal is filtered out (zero trades), (b) a zero-symbol window, (c) a symbol with no klines in the window, (d) a single-candle window — each asserting metrics.total_trades is present-and-0 (not absent), the run renders as a real result (not the 'no trades simulated' fallback), and reconciliation holds trivially.
rationale: Req 202 asserts total_trades present on a zero-trade run but no fixture actually PRODUCES one with a frozen full-result oracle, and degenerate inputs (empty window, no-kline symbol) are untested. These boundary inputs are exactly where the SoA timeline/searchsorted refactor is most likely to throw or emit a malformed empty result.

text: Add a direction='reverse' golden fixture with a frozen stored-snapshot oracle, proving the optimized engine preserves the signal-side flip identically to the legacy engine.
rationale: direction (straight/reverse) is a documented config-surface field that inverts which side each signal trades, but no fixture in the battery freezes it. Given the project's prior 93% sell-bias incident, side/direction parity is safety-critical and must be pinned, not assumed covered by the generic filter-chain test.

text: Add a funding fixture in which a SHORT position RECEIVES funding (negative funding_paid increasing PnL) and assert the three-way reconciliation Σ trade.pnl == net_profit == final_equity − starting_capital holds with the negative funding term, freezing the sign convention in the oracle.
rationale: Reqs 55/66 state longs-pay/shorts-receive and include funding_paid in reconciliation, but no concrete frozen fixture exercises the shorts-RECEIVE (negative) sign through the recon identity. A sign error in the funding term would still satisfy net_profit==final−start while breaking the per-trade-sum cross-check — exactly the bug the new cross-check exists to catch.

text: Add a frozen fixture that pins engine behavior when a drilled 5m bar has PARTIAL 1m sub-bar coverage (e.g. 3 of 5 sub-bars present): the chosen rule (treat as 'has window' and proceed on available sub-bars, OR treat as incomplete and fall back to 5m per the full-book rule) MUST be explicit and oracle-frozen, and asserted non-optimistic.
rationale: Req 53 defines fallback only for the all-or-nothing case (a symbol has a 1m window or it doesn't). Partial intra-bar 1m coverage is a real exchange condition the per-bar narrow-window fetcher (106) can produce, and undefined behavior there is a parity gap that could leak look-ahead or an optimistic fill.

text: The benchmark regression gate MUST compare against committed, version-tracked baseline numbers and MUST normalize for CI-host variance by using engine-only CPU time and/or an IN-RUN measured ratio (re-measure the frozen pre-optimization baseline in the same CI process for the ≥100× claim) rather than absolute wall-seconds versus a dev-frozen number; the perf test MUST be marked 'slow', deterministic, and skip cleanly off the canonical runner.
rationale: Reqs 36/38/201 assert absolute thresholds and a 100× speedup vs a baseline frozen on dev hardware. Comparing dev-frozen seconds to CI-host seconds is apples-to-oranges and guarantees flaky false-fails or false-passes. The gate's methodology must be self-normalizing to be a trustworthy regression signal.

text: A feature-flag combination parity matrix test MUST exercise a representative subset of flag interactions (all-on, all-off, each-singly-on, plus mixed states such as SoA-on + numba-off + parquet-on, manifest-on + fastpath-off) and assert each combination both produces oracle-identical results on the canonical path and does not crash.
rationale: Reqs 147/156 cover each flag on/off and all-off==oracle, but not INTERACTIONS among the ~7 independently toggleable optimizations. Real deploys run partial flag sets (staged rollout, 152); an untested combination where two flags interact badly is a likely production failure the per-flag tests miss.

text: A sweep memory-stability test MUST run a long sweep (≥50–100 combos) and assert peak process RSS stays flat within a bound across combos (no monotonic growth), proving the LRU/Arrow hot-cache eviction and per-session 1m-drill-cache clearing prevent accumulation across the sweep workload.
rationale: Req 30 bounds RSS for a single canonical run and 102 requires the LRU be bounded, but neither tests the sweep workload where the cache is hit repeatedly across many combos — the scenario most prone to an eviction-policy leak that exhausts memory only after dozens of iterations.

text: A guard/test MUST assert the Phase-6 parallel path parallelizes ONLY the outer independent sweep/config loop, and that the per-candle Σ uPnL reduction over the open book is NEVER prange-parallelized — preserving the deterministic float summation order required by the no-fastmath/deterministic-order rule.
rationale: Req 65 forbids fastmath and requires identical float summation order, but a prange over positions in the equity reduction would reorder/associate the float sum even without fastmath, silently shifting which equity threshold fires. The Phase-6 prange introduction creates this specific hazard, which 65 does not explicitly fence off.

text: A contract test MUST assert POST /backtest/compare correctly diffs a PRE-optimization stored result row against a POST-optimization result row (old row may have NULL new/optional metric and manifest fields) without error and renders all shared ~45 metric keys.
rationale: Req 160 covers old rows being readable individually, but the compare endpoint specifically joins/diffs two results and is a downstream feature that will be fed mixed-vintage rows in practice. A schema-skew crash or mis-diff in compare is a realistic regression not covered by single-row read tests.

text: A determinism test MUST assert that sweep rank assignment is stable under out-of-order combo completion (asyncio.gather / ProcessPool): equal-metric ties resolve by a deterministic key (config_hash or combo index), so the final ranked set does not depend on which combo finishes first.
rationale: Parallelizing the sweep (25-29, 207) makes completion order nondeterministic. If the set-based rank UPDATE breaks metric ties by arrival/insertion order, identical sweeps yield different rankings run-to-run — a parallelism-induced nondeterminism that serial==parallel equivalence tests can miss if they happen to tie-break the same way by luck.

text: Each persisted backtest_run/result MUST record an optimization fingerprint (engine git SHA + the active optimization-flag set) as additive nullable fields, so any post-hoc parity discrepancy can be traced to the exact code version and flag combination that produced it; verified by a test asserting the fingerprint is stamped and that old rows with a NULL fingerprint still load.
rationale: The prompt's after/triage angle and the staged-flag rollout (152) mean different runs will be produced by different engine/flag combinations. Without per-run provenance, debugging a reported parity drift means guessing which flags were live — an operational/observability gap that additive nullable columns close cheaply while preserving frontend back-compat.

text: A direct UNIT-level differential harness MUST diff the numba/SoA reimplementations of the trading_rules.py kernels (sizing, TP/SL price, slippage, liquidation price, fee, trailing, breakeven) against the current pure-Python helpers over a randomized input grid (entry price, side, leverage, qty_step, min_qty, fee_rate_pct, slippage_bps) and assert bit-identical outputs — independent of the full engine — because RC-11 reimplements these shared helpers in nopython and a drift there would silently corrupt every trade.
rationale: trading_rules.py is the named SSOT shared by live+backtest; existing parity tests assert at the engine/fixture level only. No requirement tests the reimplemented kernels in isolation, where a single formula divergence (the actual RC-11 risk) is cheapest to catch and hardest to see through full-engine fixtures.

text: TP/SL barrier-PRICE derivation from take_profit_pct/stop_loss_pct MUST be reproduced exactly (the existing ROI-on-margin-at-leverage basis with Buy/Sell sign), so e.g. a 150% TP / 100% SL at leverage 20 yields bit-identical barrier prices pre/post-optimization — frozen by an oracle that varies leverage and side.
rationale: Req #60 pins qty sizing and #41 pins SL-vs-TP-vs-liq precedence, but NO requirement pins how the configured percentages become actual barrier prices. If the numba kernel rederives barriers, a wrong basis (price-% vs ROI-%) flips every exit without touching selection.

text: The SL-clamp that pulls a configured stop inside the liquidation band MUST be preserved with identical direction and boundary, and a test MUST assert a normal SL-configured position still clamps (never liquidates) post-refactor — paired with the SL-omitted liquidation fixture (#181).
rationale: Discovery §8 states the SL-clamp is the exact reason close_reason='liquidation' is unreachable in normal configs. #59/#181 exploit this but nothing PINS the clamp as an invariant; a kernel rewrite that drops it would make liquidations fire in normal configs — a silent, large parity break.

text: The liquidation-PRICE formula (derived from entry, side, leverage, and the maintenance-margin assumption in trading_rules) MUST be bit-identical pre/post-optimization, verified by a leverage×side differential grid, so both the liquidation trigger and the 'SL wins only if closer than the liq price' comparison (#41) operate on the same price.
rationale: #41 references the liquidation price as a comparison operand and #58 pins liq wallet accounting, but the derivation of the liq price itself is never pinned. A float64 reimplementation of the maintenance-margin math can shift the band and flip both the trigger and the SL/liq tie-break.

text: Entry and exit slippage application MUST be preserved exactly: slippage_bps applied in the ADVERSE direction (Buy entry fills higher / Sell entry fills lower; reversed on exit) to the fill price used for PnL, while sizing continues to use the un-slipped mark (#60/#61) — a fixture MUST freeze the slipped fill prices and assert both the bps basis and the sign.
rationale: slippage_bps(2) is in the config surface and #58/#60 only say sizing/liq are un-slipped; the direction and basis of slippage on the actual entry/exit fill price (which directly moves every non-liq PnL) is not pinned anywhere.

text: Trade fee computation MUST be preserved: entry_fee and exit_fee both = notional(qty × fill_price) × fee_rate_pct/100 at the same rate on each side, verified by a fixture that freezes both fee legs and asserts the notional basis (not margin-based, not one-sided).
rationale: #66 ties trade.pnl = pnl − exit_fee − entry_fee − funding but never pins the fee BASIS/rate symmetry. A reimplementation could compute fee on margin instead of notional, or apply the rate once, and still satisfy #66's identity arithmetic while diverging from the oracle.

text: Special-close fee/slippage treatment MUST be explicitly oracle-frozen for BOTH liquidation (no exit fee, no slippage per #58) AND backtest_end force-close (pin whether the terminal close at each symbol's last-close charges exit fee + slippage or closes raw), and every such close MUST still satisfy the three-way reconciliation (#66).
rationale: #68/#180 pin that force-close happens and emits an equity point, but not whether the terminal close incurs fee/slippage. This is an unpinned money term that changes final_equity and every terminal trade's pnl.

text: The adaptive-blacklist incremental counter MUST honor the lookback_hours=48 SLIDING window (evicting trades whose close_time < T−lookback at scan time T) while remaining O(1) amortized, AND reproduce the legacy recompute-from-history win/total exactly — a test MUST construct closed trades straddling the 48h boundary and assert the windowed-incremental result equals the legacy full-scan result point-in-time.
rationale: Direct CONFLICT between req #11 ('incremental per-symbol win/total counter, O(1), cost independent of accumulated closed-trade count' = lifetime tally) and req #80 ('lookback_hours=48 ... point-in-time'). A naive lifetime counter cannot honor a 48h window; the reconciliation of these two is unspecified and is a genuine parity landmine the optimization introduces.

text: The per-unit uPnL formula (qty × (mark − equity_ref_entry) × side_sign) and the open-book Σ uPnL reduction MUST iterate open positions in their legacy INSERTION/open order (not symbol-index or sorted order naturally produced by the SoA timeline), pinned by a multi-position fixture whose summation reorder would flip an equity-threshold decision.
rationale: #65 requires a 'deterministic and identical' summation order and #315 forbids prange on the reduction, but neither pins WHICH order. The SoA refactor's natural iteration is by global symbol/timeline index; if that replaces open-position insertion order, float non-associativity can flip a borderline EQUITY_DROP/RISE crossing.

text: Intra-scan signal admission under max_trades MUST apply the exact legacy tie-break when abs(score) values are equal (pin the existing deterministic key — e.g. original signal-list index), so SoA/dict re-keying cannot change WHICH equal-score signals are admitted — verified by a fixture with tied scores exceeding max_trades.
rationale: #69 pins abs(score) ranking and #237 pins INTER-scan order, but the INTRA-scan tie-break among equal-abs-score signals (which decides admission when ties exceed max_trades) is unpinned. #206 forbids iteration-order leakage generally but does not freeze the specific equal-score selection.

text: On a mean_reversion route, sizing MUST use mr_capital_pct and mr_leverage (NOT capital_pct/leverage) and mr_tight_stop_pct MUST override stop_loss_pct, with qty and barrier bit-identical to legacy — a paired trend-vs-MR fixture freezes that the same signal sizes and stops differently under each route.
rationale: #243 pins the mr_max_trades cap and #178 has an MR golden fixture, but no requirement asserts the MR path substitutes mr_capital_pct/mr_leverage/mr_tight_stop_pct into the sizing and stop kernels — exactly the SSOT helpers being reimplemented.

text: MR entry-gating math MUST be reproduced exactly: the mr_min_edge_pct edge check, the mr_target_capture_pct target computed off the mr_mean_period/mr_mean_interval mean, and the mr_extreme_min_abs_score gate — frozen so the SoA refactor cannot shift which MR signals qualify or their resulting target/stop prices.
rationale: #82 only pins the mr_long_ack bypass and #178 freezes one MR oracle; the specific MR decision math (edge/target/extreme gates) that determines MR selection and barriers is not enumerated as an invariant.

text: MFE/MAE MUST be computed from 5m bar high/low extremes (not 1m sub-bar extremes) even on the drilled path, consistent with equity_ref staying on 5m (#50) — a drill-on vs drill-off fixture MUST assert identical MFE/MAE while only fill PRICE differs.
rationale: #67 pins MFE/MAE updated every bar before exit checks, and #52 pins that drill changes only fill price; but the granularity of MFE/MAE under drill (5m vs 1m extremes) is unspecified. If drill switches excursions to 1m, recorded MFE/MAE diverge even though selection is identical.

text: The max_price_drift check for STRAIGHT direction MUST preserve its exact signed/adverse semantics and basis (drift = (entry_fill − signal_price)/signal_price, rejected only when adverse beyond threshold), independent of the reverse case — frozen by favorable-vs-adverse drift fixtures that admit one and reject the other.
rationale: #235 pins the drift sign ONLY for direction=reverse (evaluated on the pre-reverse signal). The straight-direction signed drift formula/threshold-side is the base case and is not independently pinned; the shared-scan refactor that de-dupes the drift+entry scans (#194) could shift its sign basis.

text: max_signal_age_minutes filtering MUST use the exact legacy age basis (scan/current_time − signal_time, in minutes, with the existing inclusive/exclusive boundary) so no signal is admitted or dropped one minute early/late post-refactor — frozen by a boundary-age fixture sitting exactly on the threshold.
rationale: The 17-step chain order is pinned (#69) but the age computation BASIS and boundary inclusivity are not. A refactor that recomputes age off a different time anchor (bar open_time vs scan_time) changes selection at the boundary.

text: If EquityPoint.drawdown_pct is engine-populated, its per-point running-peak computation MUST reproduce the legacy value exactly, asserted by hashing the ORDERED drawdown_pct sequence (not only the equity sequence of #185), since the stored curve drives path-dependent metrics.
rationale: #79/#185 hash the equity sequence, but EquityPoint carries an optional drawdown_pct field. If that field is computed in-engine, its running-peak logic is a separate parity surface not covered by the equity-only hash.

text: The Buy&Hold baseline MUST reproduce the legacy computation exactly — buy_hold_return_pct anchored on BTC/USDT first-vs-last close over the documented anchor timestamps and interval, yielding bit-identical buy_hold_final_value/excess_return — frozen in the oracle, distinct from #238's cache-routing/null-on-failure concern.
rationale: #238 only covers the buy&hold FETCH path (cache routing, <1% tolerance, null-on-failure). The actual return COMPUTATION anchors (which timestamps/interval define first and last close) are not pinned, and the SoA/timeline change could shift the anchor candles.

text: With instrument_info empty (the no-op guarantee, #72), sizing MUST apply the exact legacy default qty_step/min_qty fallback (documented default / no-flooring behavior) so qty is bit-identical to the pure 5m path — a dedicated test MUST assert the instrument_info-absent sizing default is unchanged.
rationale: #60 pins sizing WHEN qty_step/min_qty are present (from instrument_info); #72 guarantees byte-identity when instrument_info is EMPTY. The default rounding/min-qty applied in the absent case is the bridge between them and is not explicitly pinned.

text: Degenerate metric values MUST be frozen in oracles for edge baskets: zero-trade (win_rate, profit_factor, Sharpe, Sortino, expectancy at their legacy 0/null/inf-guarded sentinels), all-wins (profit_factor inf-guard per #204), and all-losses — so the SoA/metrics path cannot silently change a divide-by-zero sentinel.
rationale: #308 freezes that total_trades is present-and-0 for zero-trade runs, but does not freeze the DERIVED metric sentinels (profit_factor with zero losses, Sharpe with zero/one return). These divide-by-zero edges are exactly where a reimplementation diverges undetected.

text: target_goal_type='trade_count' LIFETIME early-stop MUST halt at the exact legacy granularity/boundary (pin whether the run stops mid-scan the instant signals_entered hits the goal, or only after the current scan's signals finish) so the optimized engine opens neither one trade too many nor too few — frozen by a fixture whose goal is reached mid-scan.
rationale: #173 asserts the lifetime early-stop halts the run, but not the GRANULARITY of the halt. Mid-scan vs end-of-scan stop changes the final trade count by 1+ when the goal lands inside a multi-signal scan — a path-dependent edge the parallel/SoA refactor could shift.

text: Funding charged on a position's OPENING and CLOSING bars MUST follow the exact legacy boundary inclusivity (whether a position opened or closed exactly on a 0/8/16h boundary pays/receives that period), enforced via the (date,hour) dedupe, so neither the finer drill granularity (#55) nor the SoA bar-walk adds or drops a boundary charge — frozen by a boundary-straddling fixture.
rationale: #55 pins funding dedupe and #310 pins the short-receives sign, but the entry/exit-bar boundary inclusivity (does the open/close bar's boundary count?) is unpinned. A merge-walk that evaluates the boundary bar differently at open/close edges silently changes funding_paid.

text: A liquidation trade's recorded pnl MUST equal the legacy value (−locked_margin via the separate branch, with no exit fee and no slippage per #58) AND MUST be included in the Σ trade.pnl == final_equity − starting_capital reconciliation — the SL-omitted liquidation fixture (#181) MUST assert BOTH the exact pnl value and that the three-way identity still holds with the liquidation term.
rationale: #181 freezes a liquidation oracle and #58 pins liq wallet mechanics, but no requirement asserts the liquidation trade's pnl VALUE feeds the reconciliation identity. A reimplementation that records liq pnl differently (or omits it from the sum) breaks #66 only on the rare liq path.
