text: sizing_capital for the FIRST trade of a new cycle immediately after a cycle-terminating equity close MUST re-anchor exactly as legacy (cycle_start_equity zeroed → next non-skipped scan re-anchors to totalAvailableBalance per #45), so that first post-termination entry is sized bit-identically — frozen by a fixture chaining an EQUITY_DROP close into a next-scan entry.
rationale: #45 pins cycle termination/re-anchor and #61 pins path-dependent sizing_capital, but the INTERACTION (sizing the very next entry after a cycle-terminating close) is an unpinned path-dependent seam where the running-wallet mark and re-anchor must compose correctly.

text: When 1m entry drill refines entry_base_price (#51), entry slippage MUST be applied with the same direction/bps as the 5m path and neither dropped nor double-applied, so the drilled fill PnL stays non-optimistic versus the always-LTF oracle — frozen by a drill fixture comparing the slipped drilled fill against the 5m slipped fill.
rationale: #51 pins that entry drill is price-only and #74 requires non-optimistic <1% on drill configs, but the COMPOSITION of slippage with the drilled price is unspecified. Applying slippage to the 5m price then re-applying to the drilled price (double-count) or skipping it (optimistic) both pass coarse checks but break parity/non-optimism.

text: Every Decimal QUANTIZATION site in the legacy engine — qty floor-to-qty_step (truncation), price/tick rounding for barriers and fills, and pnl/fee/funding rounding to their decimal places — MUST be reproduced by the float64/numba path with the SAME rounding MODE (floor vs ROUND_HALF_UP vs ROUND_HALF_EVEN) and precision, enumerated site-by-site, so recorded money values are byte-identical to the Decimal oracle.
rationale: The current engine is Decimal-based; #64 only guarantees float64 doesn't flip threshold DECISIONS, and #199 mandates Decimal-exact byte-identity on the 5m path WITHOUT specifying HOW float64 reproduces it. The mechanism is per-site quantization: a numpy round-half-even where legacy used Decimal ROUND_HALF_UP flips last-cent values in trades/equity, failing the byte-identical gate. This rounding-mode mapping is the core unaddressed mechanism behind the entire bit-identical guarantee.

text: A mutation/canary test MUST inject a deliberate engine defect (e.g. flip SL/TP precedence, off-by-one on the entry-fill bar, drop the funding dedupe) and assert the golden-master gate turns RED — proving the parity suite actually has teeth and is not vacuously passing.
rationale: Nothing in the 318 items proves the bit-identical gate can FAIL; a gate that only ever passes gives false confidence across 7 refactor phases.

text: The fixture battery MUST collectively exercise EVERY close_reason enum value (take_profit, stop_loss, liquidation, equity_drop, equity_drop_smart, close_on_profit, equity_rise, trailing_profit, breakeven_timeout, max_duration, mr_time_stop, backtest_end) with an explicit assertion that the union of close_reasons observed across the suite covers the full enum — so no exit branch ships untested.
rationale: Individual close-rule fixtures exist (#166-181) but nothing asserts the SET is exhaustive; a new/renamed close_reason could slip through uncovered.

text: The oracle-diff helper MUST assert deep equality on ALL 19 BacktestTrade fields + strategy_kind (entry/exit time, entry/exit price, qty, side, leverage, pnl, fees, funding_paid, close_reason, mfe, mae, …), and a meta-test MUST verify the comparator fails when any single field is perturbed — so silent drift in mfe/mae/close_reason/funding is caught, not just pnl.
rationale: #182 says capture full result but no requirement proves the comparator inspects every field or that omitting one field is detected.

text: The Phase-0 stored oracle files MUST be hash-pinned and a CI test MUST assert their content hash is byte-identical from Phase 0 through Phase 6 (regenerable ONLY via the reviewed regen script with an engine-SHA bump), so an optimization phase cannot quietly re-freeze the oracle to its own (wrong) output.
rationale: #183 tracks a regen script but nothing forbids a later phase from regenerating the oracle to mask a regression — the oracle must be immutable across the optimization.

text: config_hash MUST be deterministic across process restarts and PYTHONHASHSEED randomization (sorted keys, fixed serialization, no reliance on dict/set ordering); a test MUST assert the same logical config hashes identically across two fresh interpreters, since ON CONFLICT(sweep_id, config_hash) crash-resume and warm-cache matching depend on it.
rationale: #282/#306 assume config_hash stability but no requirement pins it against Python hash randomization — a non-deterministic hash silently breaks crash-resume idempotency.

text: A SoA cross-symbol fixture with MISALIGNED/GAPPED timelines (symbol A has a candle at T, symbol B has a hole at T; staggered listing days) MUST assert the global-timeline merge-walk advances each symbol's pointer independently and values portfolio uPnL using each symbol's last-close≤T — byte-identical to the legacy per-symbol linear scan.
rationale: The single biggest SoA parity risk (RC-1/RC-2 merge-walk) is only tested on randomized aligned timelines (#193); real cross-symbol gaps/misalignment are the landmine and are untested.

text: A basket-flatten fixture (EQUITY_DROP/close_on_profit/EQUITY_RISE closing multiple positions in one tick) MUST assert each position emits its OWN equity_curve point in a deterministic, oracle-frozen close ORDER, with a defined tie-break when several close at the identical timestamp — so path-dependent metrics (max_dd/Sharpe) are reproducible under SoA/parallel reordering.
rationale: #79/#185 pin curve order as a sequence but nothing pins the intra-tick ordering of simultaneous basket closes, which the refactor can reorder.

text: After SoA build, the kline arrays (int64 epoch + float64 OHLC) MUST be made read-only (numpy writeable=False or equivalent) so accelerated/nogil kernels and concurrent sweep combos sharing them cannot mutate shared state; a test MUST assert an attempted write raises and that concurrent combos observe identical arrays.
rationale: #307 covers per-run mutable state isolation but nothing enforces that the SHARED immutable kline SoA is actually immutable — a nogil/prange write would corrupt every concurrent run.

text: After persistence, a test MUST READ BACK the stored backtest_trades and backtest_results from the DB and assert Σ stored_trade.pnl == stored final_equity − starting_capital within Decimal tolerance — proving the float64→NUMERIC Decimal coercion (_num) does not break the three-way reconciliation on the persisted (not just in-memory) values.
rationale: #66/#184 reconcile in-memory engine output; the Decimal coercion at persist (backtest_service.py:1449) can introduce rounding that only manifests on DB read-back.

text: A test MUST assert a COLD run and a subsequent WARM run of the same config produce byte-identical trades, ordered equity_curve, and all metrics — proving the sealed-manifest/LRU/columnar cache changes WHERE data comes from but never WHAT the engine decides.
rationale: Cache fix has many call-count tests but none asserts cold-vs-warm result identity — the core guarantee that caching is output-neutral is unverified end-to-end.

text: The Phase-1 bounded per-symbol kline span MUST be widened to include EVERY indicator lookback in effect (adaptive_blacklist lookback_hours=48, mr_mean_period×mr_mean_interval, btc_vol lookback, regime EMA distance window), and a test MUST assert bounded-span results are byte-identical to full-range for a config exercising all those lookbacks (no truncated-warmup divergence).
rationale: #19/#239 bound the span to signal±hold but omit indicator warmup windows; a too-tight bound silently changes MR/regime/blacklist decisions.

text: All AUXILIARY kline paths — Buy&Hold BTC (#238), btc_vol regime filter BTC data, and MR mean-reference klines at mr_mean_interval — MUST route through the sealed-manifest/LRU cache; a warm-rerun test MUST assert 0 Bybit calls across ALL of these auxiliary paths, not only the symbol loaders.
rationale: #238 covers Buy&Hold BTC only; the btc_vol filter and MR mean-interval are separate fetch paths that can each violate the warm-rerun 0-call guarantee.

text: Degenerate-metric fixtures MUST assert finite-guard correctness: a zero-variance equity curve yields a defined (not NaN) Sharpe/Sortino, an all-wins run yields a defined profit_factor (Inf coerced per policy), an all-losses run yields 0, and each still persists without aborting _persist_results — with the chosen sentinel/coercion frozen in the oracle.
rationale: #204 guards Inf/NaN generically but no fixture pins the actual degenerate metric VALUES (zero-variance Sharpe, Inf profit_factor) the new engine must reproduce.

text: A parallel-sweep fault-isolation test MUST make ONE combo raise mid-execution under asyncio.gather/ProcessPool and assert the remaining combos still complete and persist, the failed combo is recorded as failed (not silently dropped), and the final ranked set is identical to a serial run with the same one failure.
rationale: #76/#207/#306 cover equivalence and crash-resume but not a single-combo in-flight failure poisoning its siblings in the same gather batch.

text: Golden fixtures with FROZEN stored oracles MUST exist for non-5m simulation_interval values (15m, 1h, 4h), asserting the optimized engine is byte-identical to the legacy engine at each coarse interval (distinct from the canonical 5m path), since interval changes bar membership, funding-boundary alignment, and drill granularity.
rationale: Every parity gate targets 5m no-drill; #258 only checks coarse data-source identity, not full engine parity at coarse intervals — a real config surface (simulation_interval 15m/1h/4h).

text: A cross-partition read test MUST request a window spanning a monthly Parquet partition boundary (and the kline_cache_default >6mo case) and assert the stitched, ordered, de-duplicated row set is byte-identical to the Postgres SOR read — no rows dropped, duplicated, or misordered at the seam.
rationale: #107/#108/#253 cover layout and incremental seal but nothing tests that a read SPANNING two month partitions reassembles correctly in open_time order.

text: A capital-exhaustion fixture (margin runs out mid-scan so lower-ranked signals are rejected) MUST freeze an oracle proving the rejection set is path-dependent on scan/abs(score) order and that rejected signals never appear as trades nor perturb reconciliation — preserved bit-for-bit under SoA/parallel refactor.
rationale: Rejection accounting (qty<min_qty, required_margin>available) is in the config surface but no fixture pins the path-dependent capital-exhaustion ordering that scan reordering could change.

text: The benchmark harness's OWN instrumentation MUST be unit-tested: inject a mocked Bybit client and assert the harness's Bybit-call counter, Postgres-query counter, and stage timers report the true counts/durations — so the cache-efficiency and perf regression gates measure reality, not a broken meter.
rationale: #35/#263 rely on the harness's counters but nothing verifies the counters themselves are correct; a miscounting meter makes every downstream gate blind.

text: Coverage from the main lane and the force-deps-absent fallback lane (#304) MUST be MERGED so the ≥90% line+branch gate counts BOTH the import-present and import-guarded ImportError branches; a CI step MUST assert combined coverage, not per-lane coverage that each miss the other's branches.
rationale: #200/#303/#304 define coverage and a fallback lane separately, but unless coverage is merged the import-guard except-branches and JIT paths can each fall below 90% while each lane looks green.

text: The 120s _TIMEOUT_SECONDS cap MUST be enforced over the WHOLE pipeline (kline load + engine + 1m drill + persist), and a test MUST force a timeout DURING _persist_results and assert the run ends status='failed' with NO partial backtest_trades/results/equity_curve rows committed (clean state), distinct from an engine-phase timeout (#299).
rationale: #299 covers engine-phase timeout; a timeout during the persist/drill stages is a different code path that could leave half-written rows.

text: A race test MUST fire the 120s timeout-kill and the engine's natural completion (and a cooperative cancel) near-simultaneously and assert the backtest_runs row resolves to exactly ONE terminal state with no invalid transition (e.g. completed→failed) and the _MAX_CONCURRENT slot is released exactly once.
rationale: #242/#299 cover timeout and cancel individually but not the concurrent timeout-vs-finish race that could double-release a slot or land an inconsistent status.

text: A multi-boundary funding fixture MUST hold a position across N consecutive 0/8/16h boundaries and assert exactly N funding charges (correct sign per side), AND a funding_rate_model='none' fixture MUST assert zero funding — both frozen in the oracle, complementing the single-boundary short-receives case (#310).
rationale: #55/#310 cover one boundary and short-receives; multi-boundary accrual count and the 'none' model (a real config value) are untested and easy to off-by-one in a refactor.

text: Isolated golden fixtures MUST pin slippage and fee mechanics: slippage_bps applied to entry and exit fill price (NOT to qty/sizing mark), fee_rate_pct charged on notional (qty×price) for both legs, and liquidation applying NEITHER exit slippage NOR exit fee — each asserted against a stored oracle separate from the liquidation-reachability fixture.
rationale: #58/#60 mention slippage/fee/liquidation accounting in prose but there is no dedicated fixture isolating slippage-on-price and fee-on-notional parity, which the SoA float refactor can perturb.

text: Per-FILTER-STEP golden fixtures MUST individually pin confidence_filter, min_score, signal_sides, max_signal_age_minutes, and existing-position dedup — each with a paired admit/reject oracle proving the step filters identically — beyond the single chain-ordering fixture (#69).
rationale: #69 proves chain ORDER is preserved but not that each individual filter still admits/rejects the same signals; a step could be neutralized without changing order.

text: A test MUST assert the LTTB-downsampled equity_curve returned by GET preserves the first point, the last point, and the global max-drawdown trough point, so the rendered curve can never hide the maximum-drawdown extreme even after downsampling — while the manifest hash still covers the FULL pre-downsample JSONB.
rationale: #78/#203 require hashing the full JSONB but nothing guarantees the DOWNSAMPLED GET view preserves the drawdown extreme — a downsample that drops the trough misrepresents the result to the UI.

text: A drill fixture MUST pin the case where TP and SL touch in DIFFERENT 1m sub-bars of the same coarse bar: the chronologically-FIRST sub-bar's level fires (distinct from the same-sub-bar SL-first tie-break of #49), and a fixture MUST also pin liquidation touching in a sub-bar BEFORE SL/TP — both frozen and asserted non-optimistic.
rationale: #48/#49 cover first-touched and same-sub-bar tie-break, but the different-sub-bar chronology and intra-sub-bar liq-precedence are separate resolution paths not explicitly fixtured.

text: A test MUST assert all engine/service warnings (max_same_sector_not_enforced, signals_no_kline summary, partial-data/degradation warnings) survive into the persisted result.warnings[] array and render through GET — additive-only, never dropped or reordered by the refactor — so operational signals reach the UI.
rationale: #70/#205 assert the no-op warning is emitted but nothing asserts warnings PERSIST end-to-end into the stored result.warnings contract the frontend consumes.

text: A test MUST reproduce a stored result from its persisted optimization fingerprint (engine git SHA + flag set, #278/#318): re-running the engine at that SHA with that flag set on the same fixture yields the stored numbers — closing the loop that the fingerprint is sufficient to reproduce, not merely recorded.
rationale: #278/#318 stamp the fingerprint but nothing verifies it is ACTIONABLE — that the recorded SHA+flags actually reproduce the stored result, which is the point of provenance.

text: An LRU eviction-correctness test MUST evict an entry then re-request the same (symbol,interval,range) and assert it RELOADS byte-identical from Postgres/columnar (never served as cached-empty or stale), proving eviction degrades to a correct miss rather than poisoning subsequent reads.
rationale: #20/#102/#228 bound the LRU and assert eviction-under-pressure but none asserts a re-request AFTER eviction reloads correctly — an eviction bug could return empty/stale.

text: An operational saturation test MUST run _MAX_CONCURRENT concurrent runs each issuing the batched ANY($1) loader + sweep workers and assert the asyncpg pool is not exhausted/deadlocked (bounded acquisition, no starvation) and each run still completes within budget — the connection-pool dimension of concurrency, distinct from CPU/thread budgets (#211/#212).
rationale: #211/#212 bound CPU/thread oversubscription but nothing tests DB connection-pool exhaustion under concurrent batched loads + sweeps, a classic operational failure.

text: Define a peak-RSS ceiling for the WIDE capacity point (e.g. 200 symbols × 365 days × 5m, whose SoA klines alone ≈ 1 GB and exceed the 50-symbol ≤1 GB ceiling of #30), and the single-run pre-flight estimator (#224) MUST gate on projected working set (≈ Σ symbol-span-candles × 48 B + position book + bounded hot cache) against that ceiling — not only the 120 s time cap. A run that would exceed it MUST be either rejected pre-flight with an actionable message OR executed via bounded symbol-batched/streamed loading, and MUST NEVER OOM-kill the long-lived server process; tests assert (a) an over-large run is rejected or batched and (b) process RSS stays under the ceiling on the wide fixture.
rationale: #30 fixes the RSS ceiling only for the 50-symbol canonical case and #231 gives the wide point a LATENCY budget but no MEMORY budget; #224's pre-flight gates on time only. The wide working set provably exceeds 1 GB, so without a memory-aware pre-flight + reject-or-batch contract a large run silently OOM-kills the shared FastAPI/live-trading process.

text: The CPU-bound engine (especially the GIL-bound pure-Python fallback) executing in the backtest threadpool MUST NOT starve the shared FastAPI asyncio event loop or the in-process live scanner/auto-trade coroutines: it MUST periodically release the GIL (nogil JIT), yield cooperatively between candle-chunks, or run under process isolation, bounded so that p95 latency of a concurrent live-path/API probe degrades ≤ a defined factor while a backtest/sweep saturates a core; a test asserts a probe coroutine's latency stays within bound during a CPU-heavy backtest.
rationale: kline_cache_service and the engine share the process with live trading (#281 covers data FRESHNESS, not CPU contention). #211 bounds backtest-vs-backtest latency but nothing bounds the live-trading/API latency impact of a GIL-holding pure-Python backtest in the threadpool — a real-money-sensitive downstream effect.

text: Parallel cold kline/signal loads across sweep combos and concurrent runs MUST bound their Postgres read concurrency to a configured fraction of the asyncpg pool (a DB-pool semaphore distinct from the Bybit rate-limit semaphore of #229 and the request-coalescing of #246), so a wide cold sweep cannot exhaust the connection pool and starve the live trading path's DB access; a test launching a wide cold sweep asserts in-flight Postgres reads ≤ the configured cap and the pool never fully saturates.
rationale: #229 caps the EXCHANGE concurrency and #246 coalesces identical ranges, but DIFFERENT ranges loaded in parallel across combos can exhaust the asyncpg pool — a shared resource with the live path. No requirement bounds Postgres read concurrency.

text: Loading the scan_source signals and per-scan ScanContexts MUST execute a bounded number of Postgres queries independent of scan count (batched range/ANY query), mirroring the kline ANY($1) rule (#16); a test on a ~2,160-scan fixture asserts the signal/scan-context load issues O(1) queries (not O(scans)) and its load latency stays constant as scan count doubles at fixed kline data.
rationale: #16 eliminates the N+1 on KLINES only. The canonical fixture has ~2,160 scheduled scans; if signals/ScanContexts are loaded per-scan that is an O(scans) round-trip storm before the engine even starts, uncovered by any current requirement.

text: compute_all_metrics (Sharpe/Sortino/max_drawdown/profit_factor over the ordered equity_curve) MUST be O(curve_length + trades) with no super-linear pass (e.g. no O(n²) running-peak max-drawdown) and MUST remain a bounded share of end-to-end wall-time on a trade-heavy run; a test doubling close count asserts metrics-compute time grows ≤ ~2× (linear) and never dominates the optimized engine.
rationale: #220 bounds PERSISTENCE and #221 bounds equity_curve LENGTH/serialization, but the metric COMPUTATION cost (path-dependent Sharpe/Sortino/drawdown over the ordered curve) is unbounded — a naive O(n²) drawdown on a long trade-heavy curve would silently re-dominate the now-fast engine.

text: The global sorted-unique timeline and per-symbol SoA arrays MUST be constructed exactly once per run and SHARED between both passes of the 2-phase drilldown path (portfolio-equity pass and drill/fine pass) — never rebuilt per scan or per phase; a counter test asserts the timeline/SoA build is invoked once for a 2-pass drill-enabled run (not twice), so enabling drilldown does not double engine setup cost.
rationale: #218 memoizes SoA conversion ACROSS sweep combos and #6 bounds per-scan setup, but neither pins that the GLOBAL timeline build and SoA arrays are reused across the two passes WITHIN a single drill-enabled run; a naive 2-phase loop could pay the O(total_candles·log) build twice.

text: The in-process 1m drill cache (#23/#104/#228) MUST be shared across all combos of a sweep and across reruns within a session (keyed only by (symbol, bar_open_epoch), never by combo/config), so the same drilled 5m bar fetches its 1m sub-bars exactly once for the entire sweep; a test runs a K-combo sweep that drills the same bar in every combo and asserts the 1m fetch call_count == 1, not K.
rationale: #23 asserts a warm RERUN issues 0 1m calls and #228 gives it a ceiling, but neither requires the drill cache be SHARED across sweep combos — without sharing, a K-combo sweep re-pulls the same 1m sub-bars up to K times, re-amplifying the drill-fetch cost the optimization is meant to kill.

text: If the never-resetting merge-walk pointer variant is used for price location, it MUST handle a NON-monotonic query sequence (a query whose target time precedes the previous query — possible across out-of-order intra-scan signal_times) by falling back to np.searchsorted O(log N) for that query rather than rescanning O(N) or resetting the pointer to 0; a microbenchmark with deliberately non-monotonic queries asserts per-query cost stays ≤ O(log N) and the result is bit-identical to the linear-scan oracle.
rationale: #8/#63 permit a merge-walk O(1) pointer 'or' searchsorted, but the merge-walk's O(1) guarantee silently assumes monotonically-increasing query times. Intra-scan signals need not be time-sorted, so a non-monotonic mark/entry query could degrade the pointer path to O(N) or force a reset — an uncovered perf-correctness edge.

text: Beyond numba/OMP/OpenBLAS (#212), the DuckDB (PRAGMA threads / SET threads) and Polars (POLARS_MAX_THREADS) thread pools MUST be explicitly pinned and coordinated with sweep concurrency so columnar-read parallelism × sweep parallelism × numba threads never oversubscribe cpu_count; a test asserts the configured DuckDB/Polars thread caps are applied at connection/import setup.
rationale: #212 pins numba and BLAS/OpenMP pools but omits the Phase-5 columnar engines' OWN thread pools (DuckDB and Polars both default to all cores). A DuckDB scan multiplying by sweep concurrency and numba threads re-introduces the exact nested-oversubscription #212 was meant to prevent.

text: Sweep parallelism MUST use dynamic/work-stealing dispatch (a shared queue of combos pulled by workers), not static pre-chunking, so a minority of drill-heavy/slow combos cannot serialize the tail; a test with deliberately heterogeneous combo costs (mix of fast no-drill and slow drill-heavy configs) asserts wall-time ≈ total_work/concurrency + O(1) setup and that tail worker-idle time stays below a defined bound.
rationale: #29 asserts speedup only on the HOMOGENEOUS canonical combo. Real sweeps vary combo cost by 10×+ (drill-on vs off, P depth), and static chunking leaves one worker holding all slow combos — the achieved speedup collapses to the slowest-chunk time. No requirement addresses load balancing / stragglers.

text: For CPU-bound combos the sweep speedup targets (#25/#29) MUST be achieved via TRUE parallelism (nogil-JIT threads OR a ProcessPool); the asyncio.gather+threads path on the GIL-bound pure-Python fallback (notably win32, where ProcessPool is constrained) yields ≈no speedup and MUST be either routed to a ProcessPool or have its degraded (≈serial) sweep budget explicitly documented and asserted; a test compares measured sweep speedup for the JIT-nogil path vs the pure-Python path and asserts the configured path meets its stated full-or-degraded budget.
rationale: #25 asserts near-linear win32 asyncio.gather sweep speedup UNCONDITIONALLY, but #34/#232 establish the fallback is GIL-bound and pure-Python+threads gives no parallelism. This unresolved tension means #25's budget is unmeetable on the fallback unless a ProcessPool is used or the degradation is budgeted — distinct from #232 (concurrent independent runs).

text: When an accelerated path (numba/DuckDB/Parquet) fails and the run restarts clean on the pure-Python/Postgres path (#301), the COMBINED wall-time of the failed attempt + full fallback rerun MUST still fit the 120 s cap: accelerated-path health MUST be validated fail-fast (at boot/warmup/first-combo, before deep execution) and the failed attempt's allocations freed before the fallback runs (no double-RSS); a fault-injection test asserts a failure injected mid-warmup completes the fallback within the 120 s cap AND under the RSS ceiling.
rationale: #155/#225/#301 require a clean fallback rerun but none bound the COMBINED time of (partial accelerated attempt + whole-engine pure-Python rerun). If numba fails 60 s deep and the fallback needs another 60 s, total exceeds the cap; only fail-fast detection + freed allocations make the restart fit budget and memory.

text: Define and gate a latency budget for a CROSS-PROCESS warm rerun (cold in-process LRU/hot cache but warm mmap'd Feather V2 + sealed manifest), distinct from the same-process < 2 s warm rerun (#3): it pays OS page-in but zero deserialization and zero exchange/Postgres kline work and MUST complete within a defined bound (e.g. < 5 s) issuing 0 Bybit calls and 0 kline SELECTs; a test in a FRESH process asserts the budget and the zero-IO counters.
rationale: #3's <2 s warm guarantee is explicitly same-process-session; #32/#112 establish the mmap'd Feather path exists for cross-process reruns but give it no LATENCY target. Server restart, a new sweep worker, or a separate process is a real warm scenario with its own (page-in-bound) budget that is currently unspecified.

text: Every stated latency budget (#1/#2/#3/#215, etc.) MUST specify the statistic and load context it is measured under — median/p95 over N≥3 iterations, solo vs under _MAX_CONCURRENT concurrent runs — and the production SLO MUST be the p95-under-concurrency figure, not the solo best case; additionally the benchmark gate MUST enforce an ABSOLUTE floor versus the FROZEN Phase-0 baseline so that many individually-sub-5% phase-over-phase deltas (#36) cannot ratchet into a large cumulative regression.
rationale: All budgets are bare thresholds ('warm < 10 s') with no percentile or concurrency qualifier, making them ambiguous/untestable; #213 sets median-of-N only for the GATE. And #36's phase-over-phase 5% gate against a MOVING baseline permits monotonic ratcheting that an absolute floor vs the frozen Phase-0 number (#38/#312) must catch.

text: When a sweep's working set exceeds the in-process hot/LRU cache budget, eviction MUST be whole-(symbol,interval,range)-entry granular and working-set-aware (retain the active combo's frames, evict least-recently-used WHOLE entries — never partial slices), so it degrades gracefully instead of thrashing; a test with a cache budget smaller than the sweep working set asserts re-load count grows sub-linearly (bounded re-loads, no per-combo full reload) and wall-time degrades gracefully rather than catastrophically.
rationale: #102/#228 require that eviction EXISTS, and #217 asserts zero re-load only under the explicit 'fits-in-RAM' assumption. The over-budget case (large symbol set, long sweep) is unaddressed: naive slice-granular eviction can thrash, turning a warm sweep back into a re-load-bound one.

text: The benchmark/scaling battery MUST include a high concurrent-position-count fixture (deep simultaneous open book, large P) plus a P-doubling variant, so the O(P) trailing-profit (#10), incremental adaptive-blacklist (#11), and per-candle Σ-uPnL reduction costs are actually MEASURED (assert trailing + equity-reduction time grows ≤ ~2× as P doubles), not merely asserted asymptotically on a low-P canonical fixture.
rationale: #10/#11 state O(P) / O(1) per-position claims, but the canonical fixture (#1) is symbol-breadth-oriented, not depth-oriented; without a deliberately deep-book fixture the quadratic-to-linear trailing/reduction wins are never exercised and a regression to O(P²) would pass every existing gate.

text: The seal decision MUST be a monotonic ratchet: once a (symbol,interval,day) is sealed it MUST NEVER be un-sealed, even if a later read recomputes the completion frontier to an EARLIER value because the host wall-clock stepped backward (NTP correction, VM clock skew, manual change); verified by a test that seals a day, regresses the simulated clock below that day's end, reruns, and asserts the day stays sealed and is not refetched.
rationale: Frontier is recomputed at read time (req 84) from now_utc; a backward clock step would reclassify a sealed day as forming and re-open the re-download bug. No existing item pins seal as a one-way ratchet against clock regression — req 249 only guards premature forward sealing.

text: A day MUST be sealed only after a fetch performed at or after the day's completion frontier confirms its terminal candles exist (last_open_ts == day_end − T for a full day, or the listing/structural-gap exception): a fetch that happened BEFORE the day finished forming MUST NOT seal it; verified by a test where an early (pre-frontier) fetch stores partial rows, the day stays unsealed, and only a post-frontier fetch seals it with the final candle present.
rationale: Reqs 86/247 require day.end<=frontier AND a successful fetch, but neither forbids sealing on the strength of an OLD pre-completion fetch — that would permanently seal a day missing its final candles, an immutable data-loss bug distinct from the success/throttle guard.

text: A delisted symbol (delist_time < frontier) MUST have ALL its in-life days sealed and MUST incur ZERO ongoing live-edge refresh on subsequent runs (a dead symbol has no forming candle); verified by a test that, after delist, asserts a warm rerun issues 0 Bybit calls for that symbol while a still-listed symbol's forming day is still refreshed.
rationale: The forming-day refresh rule (req 88) keys on open_time>=frontier, but a delisted symbol's last real day sits below frontier forever — without an explicit 'delisted => no live edge' rule the loader may keep probing the exchange for a non-existent forming candle on every run.

text: instrument_info (qty_step, min_qty, tick_size) and the symbol_lifecycle exchange lookups MUST be cached with a TTL / sealed snapshot so a warm rerun issues 0 Bybit HTTP calls for them; the zero-call warm-rerun guarantee (req 3) MUST hold for the FULL run including these auxiliary fetches, not just the kline loaders; verified by a warm-rerun assertion that total Bybit call_count==0 across kline + instrument_info + lifecycle paths.
rationale: Reqs 3/238 cover kline loaders and the Buy&Hold BTC path, but instrument_info and lifecycle are separate Bybit calls that, if fetched fresh each run, silently violate the warm-rerun 0-call guarantee — an uncaptured re-download path.

text: instrument_info used for sizing/qty-step flooring MUST be treated as a documented accepted divergence (current-snapshot, not point-in-time historical): the optimization MUST ship the SAME snapshot the legacy engine used (so parity holds vs the oracle) and MUST NOT silently substitute a different/fresher snapshot that would change qty flooring; verified by a test that pins the snapshot and asserts bit-identical qty across legacy and optimized engines.
rationale: Req 82 enumerates accepted divergences (mr_long_ack, cohort, staleness) but omits instrument_info, which directly affects bit-identical qty (req 60). If the new loader fetches a fresher snapshot it breaks the golden master without touching business logic.

text: Each persisted backtest result MUST record a DATA-PROVENANCE fingerprint (e.g. the sealed-manifest content_sha256 set or a coverage-version hash over the loaded (symbol,interval,range) days) in addition to the engine SHA + flag set (reqs 278/318), so that if the cache is later corrected (hash-mismatch refetch, exchange restatement) a stored result's exact input data is identifiable and a silent numbers-changed-on-rerun is detectable; verified by a test asserting the data fingerprint is stamped and that two runs over differing underlying klines get distinguishable fingerprints.
rationale: Engine-SHA + flag fingerprint (318) makes CODE reproducible but not DATA reproducible. Sealed days can legitimately change via the content_sha256 escape hatch (118) or restatement — without a data fingerprint a result's numbers become irreproducible and the change undetectable.

text: store_klines MUST refuse to MUTATE a sealed (symbol,interval,day): a write introducing a NOVEL candle (open_time not already present) into a sealed day MUST be rejected/logged (not silently absorbed by ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING) so the immutable content_sha256 cannot drift out of sync with stored rows; verified by a test that attempts to insert a new candle into a sealed day and asserts it is refused and content_sha256 stays valid.
rationale: ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (req 114) protects against duplicate keys but NOT against a genuinely new open_time landing in a sealed day (e.g. exchange backfill, live scanner overlap) — which would make stored rows disagree with the sealed content hash. No item guards sealed-day immutability on the WRITE side.

text: There MUST be a documented, tested admin/operational path to FORCE re-verification (content_sha256 recompute + conditional refetch) of a sealed day or range, for the exchange-backfill / historical-restatement case where seal-forever would otherwise freeze stale data; it MUST be explicit/manual (never on candle_count) and MUST re-seal with the corrected hash; verified by a test that flags a range for re-verify, detects a hash change, refetches, and re-seals.
rationale: Req 118 allows refetch ONLY on hash-mismatch, but provides no mechanism to TRIGGER a recompute of an already-sealed day (the loader trusts sealed and never reads rows). Without an operator escape hatch, a real exchange restatement of closed candles is uncorrectable.

text: A whitelisted/requested symbol that does not exist on the exchange (typo, never-listed, fully-delisted-and-purged) MUST be negatively cached at the SYMBOL level after a bounded discovery attempt, so repeated runs do not re-issue a failing Bybit lookup every time; verified by a test that requests a non-existent symbol across N reruns and asserts the exchange is queried at most once (then served from the symbol-level negative cache) and the run completes with that symbol skipped.
rationale: Negative caching (reqs 90/272) covers pre-listing/post-delist DAY ranges for KNOWN symbols, but a symbol with no listing row that the exchange rejects (req 259 says 'fetch-and-discover') would be re-probed every run — an unbounded repeated-failure path with no symbol-level memo.

text: kline_cache_default (the unpruned catch-all partition) MUST stay empty under steady-state writes: store_klines MUST auto-create the needed monthly partition for current/forward AND >6mo-old dates so rows never silently accumulate in the default partition, and a regression test MUST assert that after writing candles spanning a future month boundary and a >6mo-old month, row_count(kline_cache_default)==0 and each landed in its dedicated partition.
rationale: Reqs 135-137/284 cover READING >6mo-old ranges and concurrent partition creation, but none asserts the default partition stays EMPTY going forward as wall-clock advances past the migration-38 ±6mo window — silent accumulation in the unpruned default reintroduces full-scan slowness.

text: When symbol_lifecycle is EMPTY at v58 backfill time (lifecycle populated later), the backfill MUST still seal every complete historic day (treating absent lifecycle as listing=−inf/delist=+inf per req 272) rather than skipping sealing for lack of [listing,delist] bounds; verified by a test running the v58 backfill against a populated kline_cache_coverage but an EMPTY symbol_lifecycle and asserting complete days are sealed (not left unsealed pending lifecycle).
rationale: Req 130 seals days 'within [listing,delist]' and req 273 requires a coverage row to exist, but neither resolves the migration-ordering hazard where lifecycle is empty at backfill time — a naive AND-overlap predicate would seal NOTHING, leaving the re-download bug live until lifecycle is backfilled.

text: The sealed-manifest fields MUST EXTEND the existing kline_cache_coverage row in place — the manifest day key reusing the existing (symbol,interval,date) PK and candle_count reusing the existing column — with NO parallel/shadow manifest key that could diverge from coverage; verified by a schema test asserting the seal columns live on kline_cache_coverage keyed by its existing PK and that there is no second source of candle_count.
rationale: Reqs 85/128 say the manifest is keyed (symbol,interval,day_utc) with a candle_count fact, while discovery says coverage already has PK(symbol,interval,date)+candle_count. If implemented as a separate table/key the two candle_counts can skew — pinning in-place extension prevents a manifest-vs-coverage split-brain.

text: The in-process LRU/hot-cache forming-day EXCLUSION MUST be re-evaluated against the live frontier on EACH access (not frozen at first insert), so that a day which was the forming/live-edge day earlier in a long-lived session automatically becomes memoizable once it crosses the frontier mid-session (UTC midnight rollover) — with no restart; verified by a test that advances the simulated clock past a day boundary within one session and asserts the now-sealed day flips from always-fresh-read to memoized.
rationale: Reqs 100/158 forbid memoizing the forming day and require invalidation, and req 84 recomputes the frontier per run — but none covers the WITHIN-session rollover where today's excluded day becomes sealed; a frozen exclusion flag would keep re-reading a now-immutable day forever in a long server process.

text: The Parquet/Feather retention GC (req 261) MUST NOT evict/delete a partition file that is currently open or mmap'd by an in-flight run: eviction MUST be guarded by an in-use refcount (or skip-if-open), so a concurrent reader never hits SIGBUS/IOError from a file deleted mid-read; verified by a test that triggers GC while a run holds a partition open and asserts the file is retained until released and the reader completes correctly.
rationale: Req 261 defines LRU-by-access eviction and req 285 covers a full/unwritable volume, but neither protects an ACTIVE mmap reader from having its file evicted underneath it — a real crash (SIGBUS on zero-copy mmap) under _MAX_CONCURRENT runs + GC.

text: Fine→coarse derivation (req 116) MUST produce BIT-IDENTICAL OHLCV bars regardless of which engine performs the bucketing (DuckDB time_bucket vs Polars group_by_dynamic vs pandas/numpy fallback): all MUST use left-closed, UTC-aligned interval boundaries with identical open/high/low/close/volume aggregation; verified by a cross-backend test asserting all available resamplers agree byte-for-byte on a sample range.
rationale: Req 117 asserts derived==exchange-native within tolerance and req 258 picks one authoritative source, but neither asserts the DERIVATION ITSELF is identical across the import-guarded backends (DuckDB/Polars/pandas) — a left-vs-right-closed or tz-alignment mismatch between fallback backends would silently shift coarse bars and break parity when a wheel is absent.

text: The fine→coarse resample result for a given (symbol, coarse_interval, range) MUST be memoized per session so a sweep varying simulation_interval (or repeated coarse-TF reruns) derives each coarse series ONCE, not per combo; verified by a test asserting the resample routine is invoked once per (symbol,coarse_interval,range) across a K-combo coarse-interval sweep.
rationale: Reqs 217/218 memoize kline-load and the raw→SoA conversion, but the fine→coarse derivation (req 116) is a distinct compute step not covered — a coarse-interval sweep would re-bucket the 5m base for every combo without an explicit resample memo.

text: date_range_start/date_range_end (and any tz-aware/naive datetime input at the API/MCP boundary) MUST be normalized to UTC before window-bounding, day-bucketing, and sealed-day selection, so a non-UTC or naive input cannot shift the requested window off the UTC day grid and cause spurious gap/seal mismatches; verified by a test passing tz-aware and naive equivalents of the same instant and asserting identical loaded klines, day keys, and sealed-day set.
rationale: Req 250 pins UTC-only for INTERNAL bucketing/frontier/partition math, but does not cover NORMALIZATION of the user-supplied date_range at ingress — a naive/local datetime entering the window math would misalign with the UTC manifest grid (Windows-host DST hazard called out in discovery).

text: DELETE /backtest/{id} (and run cleanup) MUST remove only run-scoped rows (backtest_runs/results/trades/equity_curve for that id) and MUST NOT delete, unseal, or evict any shared kline_cache, kline_cache_coverage/manifest, Parquet/Feather, or lifecycle data; verified by a test that deletes a run and asserts the sealed manifest, klines, and columnar cache are byte-identical afterward and a subsequent run still issues 0 sealed-day refetches.
rationale: The shared read cache is now the source of truth for 'never refetch'. No existing item guards the DELETE path from cascading into shared cache/manifest state — an over-broad delete would silently reintroduce cold re-downloads for every other run.

text: The single-run pre-flight cost estimate (req 224) MUST additionally estimate COLD-FETCH network cost — count unsealed/missing candles in the requested range via the manifest and reject/warn when ceil(missing/1000) Bybit pages provably cannot complete within the 120s cap — directing the user to warm the cache first, distinct from the compute-side candles×scans estimate; verified by a test that a large cold range is flagged as network-infeasible pre-flight (no partial run that hits the timeout-kill).
rationale: Req 224's pre-flight is compute-bound (candles×scans); req 5 bounds cold requests but does not pre-flight-reject. A 200-sym×365d COLD run can blow the 120s cap on network alone before the engine runs — needs a manifest-driven cold-fetch feasibility gate separate from the compute estimate.

text: POST /backtest-cache/warmup MUST be manifest-aware and idempotent: warming a range MUST incrementally seal completed days, re-warming an already-sealed range MUST issue 0 Bybit calls, it MUST be safe to run concurrently with backtests (single-flight per req 246), populate symbol_lifecycle, and report PER-SYMBOL success/failure (not all-or-nothing); verified by a test asserting a second warmup of the same range is a no-op (0 calls) and a 3-of-50 failure surfaces per-symbol status with the 47 successes sealed.
rationale: The warmup endpoint is in scope (discovery §4) and is the intended pre-warm path for the new cold-fetch gate, but no requirement specifies its manifest interaction, idempotency, concurrency-safety, or partial-failure reporting — leaving the operator-facing warm path undefined.

text: The refreshable (non-memoized, always-fresh) set MUST be EXACTLY the forming candle(s) at open_time >= frontier — i.e. at most the single forming UTC day per (symbol,interval) — and the legacy coarse 'date >= today−1' two-day band MUST NOT widen it; verified by a test asserting only the forming day is re-read while yesterday's now-sealed day is memoized, so the optimized path neither re-seals history nor under-refreshes the live edge.
rationale: The findings use BOTH 'date >= today-1' (parity guarantee text) and the precise frontier rule (open_time>=frontier) — an ambiguity. Pinning the refreshable set to exactly the frontier-forming day prevents the loose today-1 band from silently re-reading a sealed day (perf) or, if mis-narrowed, freezing the live edge.

text: A discontinuous symbol lifecycle (symbol delisted then the same symbol string re-listed for a new contract) MUST NOT keep a post-delist range frozen as negative-cached-empty when the exchange begins returning candles again: the loader MUST detect unexpected candles in a known-empty range and trigger re-discovery/refresh of lifecycle rather than serving empty; verified by a test that seeds a delist+negative-cache, then simulates re-listing, and asserts the new candles are discovered (not permanently skipped).
rationale: Reqs 90/259/272 treat listing/delist as a single monotonic interval; a reused symbol string (perp relist) violates that and the seal-forever negative cache would permanently hide the relisted contract's real candles — an unhandled lifecycle-discontinuity correctness hole.

text: Migration v58 MUST decouple the fast transactional DDL (ADD COLUMN/CREATE TABLE that advances schema_version, idempotent, sub-second) from the long sealed-day SEAL BACKFILL (idempotent, resumable, tracked by its OWN completion marker outside the schema_version bump); a test MUST assert schema_version reaches 58 immediately after DDL while the backfill can run/resume separately, because the existing per-migration single conn.transaction() + in-band schema_version UPDATE (async_persistence.py:1629-1639) cannot hold a chunked/deferred multi-commit backfill.
rationale: Reqs 133 (chunked/set-based backfill) and 266 (deferred so readiness isn't blocked) directly contradict req 134 (schema_version advanced only after DDL+backfill succeed in one transaction) given the actual runner runs each migration in ONE transaction. No requirement resolves this; without decoupling, the migration is either non-atomic or boot-blocking.

text: The v58 seal backfill MUST NOT hold the global migration advisory lock pg_advisory_lock(8675309) — held for the entire _run_migrations loop (async_persistence.py:1607-1641) — long enough to trip the 30s lock_timeout (line 1599) on a second concurrently-booting instance; a test MUST start a long backfill and assert a concurrent migration runner does NOT fail with a lock_timeout/crash-loop.
rationale: Req 265 only requires the advisory lock to PREVENT double-apply. The opposite hazard — a long backfill STARVING the global lock so other rolling-deploy instances time out at 30s and crash-loop — is uncovered and is a direct consequence of the existing single-lock-for-whole-loop runner.

text: Because the migration runner RAISES RuntimeError when stored schema_version > app's max supported version (async_persistence.py:1617-1621), rolling the application BINARY back below v58 hard-fails at boot; the supported rollback path MUST therefore be the master kill-switch (keep v58 code, all optimizations off) and a test MUST assert that either (a) binary-downgrade-below-v58 is documented as forbidden, or (b) the legacy runner is patched to no-op on a newer schema — reqs 153/276/290 currently ASSUME a graceful no-op the code does not perform.
rationale: Reqs 153, 276, and 290 all claim rolling back to pre-v58 code runs/serves fine against a v58 DB. The actual runner raises 'Database schema vN is newer than this application supports' and refuses to start — so those requirements test against behavior the code contradicts; the real rollback story (kill-switch, not downgrade) is unstated.

text: Applying the FULL _MIGRATIONS list from an EMPTY database (0→58) MUST produce a schema byte-identical to an in-place 57→58 upgrade, and the v58 backfill MUST be a no-op (zero coverage rows to seal) on a fresh DB without error; verified by a fresh-DB migrate test that diffs the resulting schema against an upgraded-DB schema and asserts the backfill completes cleanly on empty tables.
rationale: Every existing v58 migration requirement (120-134) tests v58 in ISOLATION on a v57 DB. New-environment/disaster-recovery deploys run 0→58 from scratch; nothing asserts the chain composes or that the data-dependent seal backfill tolerates an empty DB.

text: The v58 seal-backfill UPDATE on the SHARED kline_cache_coverage table MUST NOT deadlock with, or block beyond a bounded wait, the live scanner/auto-trade path's concurrent GREATEST coverage upserts (use short lock_timeout + chunked commits and/or SKIP LOCKED), verified by a test that runs the backfill while issuing concurrent live-style coverage upserts and asserts no deadlock and that live-write latency stays within a defined budget.
rationale: kline_cache_coverage is shared with the live trading path (discovery §, req 281). Reqs 133/266 bound the backfill's own runtime but say nothing about CONTENTION with concurrent live writers during a rolling deploy — a real deadlock/lock-wait hazard on the shared table.

text: A kline_cache_coverage row whose new v58 'sealed' column is NULL (written by old code in the window between DDL and backfill completion, or after a rollback to legacy code) MUST be treated as UNSEALED/refetchable — never as sealed=true and never as an error/exception; verified by a test inserting a coverage row with sealed IS NULL and asserting the count-free predicate classifies it as fetchable.
rationale: Reqs 273/274 cover backfilled-but-NULL-sha and never-fetched rows, but not the transient/rollback case where 'sealed' itself is NULL on a row that DOES have coverage. A NULL-sealed defaulting to true would freeze a forming day empty; defaulting to an exception would crash the loader. The tri-state must be pinned.

text: During a rolling deploy or rollback, the LEGACY coverage/kline upsert path (which has no knowledge of the v58 columns and omits them) MUST NOT null-out or clobber sealed/first_open_ts/last_open_ts/gap_count/content_sha256/fetched_at on existing rows; verified by a test that runs a legacy-shaped INSERT...ON CONFLICT upsert against a v58 row already carrying manifest values and asserts those columns survive unchanged.
rationale: Old+new pods coexist during a rolling deploy and after a code rollback. Req 161 covers persistence idempotency for the NEW code; nothing asserts the OLD code's column-omitting upserts preserve the additive v58 manifest columns (Postgres keeps omitted columns on UPDATE, but a SELECT-all/re-INSERT pattern could overwrite — must be tested).

text: Because kline_cache_service is shared with the live scanner/auto-trade path, the sealed-manifest fetch GATE MUST be independently toggleable for the backtest path versus the live path (two flags, not one), so enabling the backtest optimization cannot alter live current-day/coverage fetch behavior; verified by a test that enables the backtest gate and asserts the live fetch path's behavior is byte-identical to pre-deploy.
rationale: Req 281 asserts live freshness is PRESERVED when the gate is on, and req 150 toggles the gate globally. Neither provides INDEPENDENT rollout control so the backtest path can ship the cache fix dark/staged while the live path stays on the legacy code path — essential given the shared service's blast radius.

text: numba/pyarrow/duckdb MUST be imported LAZILY (at first accelerated-path use, behind the flag), NOT at backend.main / module import time, so (a) backend import time stays within a bounded budget and (b) a slow or partially-broken heavy wheel cannot delay or block process boot; verified by a test asserting backend.main imports successfully WITHOUT importing numba/pyarrow/duckdb and within a defined time budget.
rationale: Req 139 asserts a missing wheel doesn't CRASH import; nothing bounds the import-TIME cost or requires deferral. numba import alone can add ~1s+ and pulls llvmlite; eager top-level imports tax every boot (including pure-Python deploys) and widen the boot-failure surface.

text: The project MUST resolve working prebuilt wheels (or a verified pure-Python fallback) for numba/llvmlite/pyarrow/duckdb on the WINDOWS dev/CI host (win32, where ProcessPool is unavailable per RC-13), verified by a CI lane on windows-latest that runs pip install + the full golden suite green.
rationale: Req 146/270 cover the Linux/prod-image libc and manylinux/musl targets, but the actual development and one CI host is win32 (discovery: Python 3.14.3 on Windows; RC-13 notes supports_process_pool()==False on win32). No requirement guarantees the accel stack installs/falls back cleanly on the dev/CI OS where the work is actually run.

text: The boot-time numba JIT warm-up MUST run under a bounded wall-clock WATCHDOG so a warm-up that HANGS (not merely raises) disables the JIT and lets boot proceed within a defined deadline; verified by a test that simulates a warm-up blocking past the watchdog and asserts boot completes with JIT disabled and the run still produces oracle-identical output.
rationale: Req 271 covers warm-up RAISING an exception (caught → disable). A compile that BLOCKS/hangs (LLVM stall, lock contention on a read-only cache dir) is a different failure mode that an except-block does not catch; without a timeout it stalls boot indefinitely and fails the readiness probe.

text: The optimization-flag resolver MUST validate inter-flag COHERENCE at boot (e.g. numba-on requires SoA-on; the vectorized fast-path requires SoA-on; columnar-read-on is independent) and either auto-normalize to the nearest safe coherent set (logging the change) or fail-fast with an actionable error — it MUST never execute an incoherent combination; verified by a test feeding contradictory flag combinations and asserting normalization or fail-fast.
rationale: Req 147 makes each phase independently toggleable and req 313 tests representative COMBINATIONS for parity, but nothing requires the resolver to REJECT/normalize logically-impossible combos (numba kernel with SoA off has no array layout to JIT). An unvalidated incoherent combo could silently mis-route or crash mid-run.

text: The active optimization-flag set MUST be resolved ONCE and frozen for the entire duration of a run (and a whole sweep), so an env-var/config change mid-run cannot splice two code paths into a single persisted result; verified by a test that mutates the flag env between phases of one run and asserts the run used the snapshot captured at start.
rationale: Req 278/318 persist the flag snapshot for provenance, but nothing guarantees flags are IMMUTABLE across the run. A mid-run flip (operator toggling during an incident) could produce a hybrid accelerated+legacy result that silently violates parity and is misattributed by the stamped snapshot.

text: An unset, empty, or malformed optimization-flag environment value MUST resolve to the SAFE path (legacy/pure-Python for kill-switches; documented staged default otherwise) and NEVER to an accelerated path by accident of parsing; verified by a test passing garbage/empty/whitespace flag values and asserting legacy routing plus oracle-identical output.
rationale: Req 148/152/277 define kill-switches and documented defaults, but no requirement pins fail-SAFE parsing semantics. A truthy-coercion bug (e.g. 'false'/'0'/'' parsing to True) would silently arm an accelerated path on a host meant to run safe-mode — the highest-risk possible default.

text: POST /backtest-cache/warmup MUST populate AND seal the v58 manifest for the closed days it fetches (not merely fill kline_cache rows), so an operator pre-warm before a large sweep yields zero refetch on the subsequent run; its response contract MUST stay additive-only; verified by a test asserting post-warmup days are sealed and a following backtest issues 0 Bybit kline calls for that range.
rationale: Reqs 162/262 cover GET /backtest-cache/status consulting the manifest, but the WARMUP endpoint — the explicit operational pre-fetch tool — is never required to write the sealed manifest. If warmup fills kline_cache without sealing, the pre-warm is wasted and the first sweep still pays cold detection.

text: The post-migration deploy canary (req 288) MUST additionally exercise the LIVE (non-backtest) kline fetch through the shared kline_cache_service against the v58 schema and assert current-day refresh still succeeds, because the shared service — not just the backtest path — is what a broken v58/manifest gate would break in production trading; verified by the canary including a live-path fetch assertion that fails on a regression.
rationale: Req 288's canary only runs a backtest end-to-end. The shared kline_cache_service also serves live scanning/auto-trade; a v58 or manifest-gate defect could break live current-day fetching while the backtest canary stays green. The canary must cover the widest-blast-radius path.

text: Beyond the one-time boot self-check LOG (req 164), the running service MUST expose a QUERYABLE runtime status (a health endpoint field or dedicated route) reporting active-vs-degraded optimizations, the applied schema_version, and the engine git SHA, so the deploy pipeline can gate/verify the rollout and an incident can be triaged without scraping logs; verified by a contract test on the status payload shape and values.
rationale: Req 164 emits a boot log/metric; req 278/318 stamp provenance into stored RESULTS. Neither gives a live, pollable endpoint the orchestrator/operator can hit to confirm which optimizations a running pod actually has active and at what schema/SHA — the standard deploy-verification and incident-triage handle.

text: A v58 DDL or seal-backfill FAILURE MUST surface as a deploy-gating, alertable signal (non-zero migration exit/health-failed/metric), distinct from the app's graceful in-run degradation, so a deploy where the migration silently failed and the optimization never applied is DETECTED rather than masked by the pure-Python fallback still serving traffic; verified by a test asserting a failing v58 emits the gating/alert signal even though the app continues to boot on the legacy path.
rationale: Reqs 155/164/225 make runtime fallbacks graceful and observable, and req 165 surfaces a migration error — but the combination creates a trap: graceful degradation can MASK a failed migration so the deploy looks healthy while shipping zero optimization. The migration outcome must be independently deploy-gating and loud.

text: The additive backtest_runs/backtest_results FINGERPRINT columns required by reqs 278/318 (optimization-flag snapshot, engine git SHA, schema_version-in-effect) MUST be added by the SAME idempotent v58 migration using ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS with constant defaults and remain NULL-tolerant for pre-existing rows; verified by a post-migration schema-introspection test plus an old-row (NULL-fingerprint) load test.
rationale: Reqs 278/318 assert the fingerprint DATA is stamped and that old NULL rows still load, but no requirement ties the underlying COLUMNS to the idempotent v58 DDL contract (120/126/154). Without it, the fingerprint columns could be added by an ad-hoc non-idempotent path that breaks re-run safety or the additive-only guarantee.

text: Any index v58 creates (e.g. on the (symbol,interval,day_utc) manifest key or symbol_lifecycle) MUST be created CONCURRENTLY outside the migration's single transaction, or be justified as on a provably-small/new table, so v58 takes no long ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on a large existing kline_cache_coverage at boot; verified by a test asserting v58 acquires no long blocking lock against a representative-size coverage table.
rationale: The runner wraps each migration in one transaction (async_persistence.py:1629) — CREATE INDEX there takes a blocking lock and CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY is illegal inside a transaction. No requirement addresses index-creation locking; on a large coverage table this stalls boot/readiness, separate from the backfill-runtime reqs (133/266).

text: A tested, documented ROLLBACK DRILL MUST exercise the full operational revert sequence end-to-end — flip the master kill-switch (req 277) → assert golden-master byte-identical parity on the legacy path → confirm the v58 schema is RETAINED (additive, not downgraded) → assert reads remain byte-identical — so the revert procedure is proven runnable, not merely assumed; verified by a rollback-drill integration test.
rationale: Reqs 153/156/277 assert the master switch and all-flags-off reproduce the golden master, but no requirement ties them into a single executed REVERT runbook (switch → verify parity → retain schema → verify reads). Operationally, a rollback is a sequence; an untested sequence is an untested rollback.

text: The accel dependencies (numba/llvmlite/pyarrow/duckdb) MUST be installable as a SEPARABLE image layer / optional extra with a documented image-size budget, so the base pure-Python deploy image stays lean and a cold container pull does not inflate deploy time; verified by a CI check on the core image's size built WITHOUT the accel extra and asserting the accel layer is additive/optional.
rationale: Req 267 makes the extras optional for INSTALL SUCCESS and req 270 checks wheel availability, but nothing budgets the IMAGE-SIZE / cold-pull-time impact of adding numba+llvmlite+pyarrow+duckdb, nor requires layering them so pure-Python deploy targets aren't forced to ship the heavy binary stack.

text: The sealed-manifest 'range already complete' gate (reqs 97/98) MUST resolve seal state for ALL requested symbols in a single batched `WHERE symbol = ANY($1) AND interval = $2 AND day_utc BETWEEN …` query (bucketed in Python), exactly like the kline ANY($1) loader (req 16), so the completeness check itself is O(1) queries independent of symbol count; a test on an N-symbol fully-sealed range MUST assert the manifest lookup issues exactly 1 query and zero per-symbol round-trips.
rationale: Req 16 batches the kline load and reqs 97/98 mandate a manifest gate, but nothing requires the manifest LOOKUP to also be batched — a naive per-symbol seal check reintroduces the exact O(N) query fan-out the kline batching eliminated.

text: A still-forming candle (open_time >= the write-time completion frontier) MUST be CLIPPED before store_klines so it is never persisted to kline_cache; because store_klines stays ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (reqs 77/114), persisting a forming candle would freeze its incomplete OHLC and the later seal refetch could never correct it — a test MUST store a forming candle, advance the frontier, refetch the now-closed final candle, and assert the stored row holds the FINAL (not stale forming) values and the sealed day's content_sha256 reflects the final candle.
rationale: ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (mandated by 77/114) is only safe if no incomplete candle is ever written; a forming candle persisted by any path would be frozen by DO-NOTHING and then immortalized by the seal — a silent data-corruption hazard the existing clipping req (119) doesn't call out.

text: When a sealed day is re-verified and refetched via the content_sha256 mismatch escape hatch (req 296) or the manual force-reverify path (req 397), EVERY in-process artifact overlapping that day — the _load_klines LRU, the Arrow/Polars hot frame, any mmap'd Feather slice, and the in-session derived-coarse memo (req 405) — MUST be invalidated so a long-lived process never continues to serve the pre-correction klines; a test MUST warm all caches, force a content-changing sealed-day refetch, and assert the next read returns corrected data from every tier.
rationale: Req 158 only invalidates the FORMING day and 372 covers ordinary eviction; nothing invalidates a SEALED day's memoized copies after an integrity-driven correction, so a session that already cached the wrong bytes serves stale data indefinitely.

text: A single documented epoch UNIT (Bybit-native milliseconds) MUST be used for open_time across kline_cache, the manifest first_open_ts/last_open_ts/fetched_at columns, Parquet/Feather files, the Arrow hot frame, and the int64 SoA arrays, with NO seconds/milliseconds mixing; a test MUST assert every storage tier round-trips the identical int64 epoch-ms value (catching a 1000× unit error) and that the Arrow schema stores open_time as int64 and OHLCV as float64 (not Arrow timestamp/decimal logical types) so the SoA view is zero-copy with no per-element cast.
rationale: Req 64 only requires accepting datetime-OR-epoch input; it never pins the epoch UNIT, yet introducing Parquet/Arrow/SoA layers multiplies the surface for an s-vs-ms (1000×) mismatch that would silently corrupt every timestamp comparison and the zero-copy SoA view.

text: Symbol identifiers MUST be sanitized/encoded for the Parquet hive partition path so a '/' or other filesystem-reserved character in a symbol string cannot create spurious nested directories or path collisions, using a documented reversible encoding; a test MUST assert symbols containing reserved characters partition to a single safe `symbol=<encoded>` directory and read back to the exact original symbol.
rationale: Req 107 specifies `symbol=<SYM>` hive layout but the specs reference symbols like 'BTC/USDT'; an unsanitized '/' silently splits the partition into nested dirs, breaking pruning and round-tripping the symbol incorrectly.

text: A backtest window covering BOTH sealed days (served from the columnar Parquet/Arrow tier) AND the still-forming day (served only from Postgres) MUST stitch the two tiers into one strictly-ascending, de-duplicated kline series byte-identical to a pure-Postgres read; a test MUST request a window straddling the seal/forming boundary and assert the columnar+Postgres stitched result equals the Postgres SOR read with no dropped, duplicated, or misordered candle at the seam.
rationale: Req 359 only covers Parquet↔Parquet and the >6mo default-partition seam; the routine case — sealed columnar history plus the Postgres-only forming tail in one window — is a distinct stitch seam that must be proven byte-identical.

text: On the Windows host, atomically extending a month's immutable Parquet/Feather partition (req 253) MUST NOT rely on rename-over-an-open-file (win32 raises a sharing violation when a concurrent reader holds the file mmap'd/open); the writer MUST use a versioned part-file + atomic pointer/manifest swap (or equivalent) so an in-flight reader is never blocked and never sees ENOENT/partial data — verified by a test that holds a partition open for read while a writer seals an additional day into that month and asserts both the write and the concurrent read succeed.
rationale: Reqs 109/253/403 assume POSIX atomic-rename semantics, but the discovery doc pins a Windows host where rename-over an mmap'd/open Feather file fails — the cross-process mmap reuse (112/386) and month-extension (253) collide on win32 file locking unless a versioned-pointer scheme is used.

text: The v58 manifest timestamp columns (first_open_ts, last_open_ts, fetched_at) MUST be typed wide enough for epoch-millisecond values (BIGINT or timestamptz, NEVER INT4, which overflows at ~2.1e9 well below current epoch-ms ~1.7e12), content_sha256 MUST be sized for a full 32-byte/64-hex digest, and candle_count's type MUST accommodate the densest manifested interval — verified by a schema-introspection test asserting each column's type/width cannot overflow on a max-density day.
rationale: Req 128 asserts the columns are 'present and correctly typed' but never pins the integer width; an INT4 epoch-ms column silently overflows today, so the overflow/width hazard needs an explicit gate beyond mere presence.

text: The Phase-1 bounded per-symbol span (reqs 19/239/354) MUST be snapped OUTWARD to whole UTC-day boundaries for all manifest/coverage/seal lookups (a sub-day span still consults whole-day seal state and never triggers a partial-day fetch that would break the whole-day seal model), while the engine still consumes only candles inside the precise [min(signal)−lookback, max(signal)+hold] window; a test MUST assert a sub-day-spanning request reads whole sealed days from cache (0 refetch) yet the engine's evaluated candle set is byte-identical to the unbounded-load oracle.
rationale: The bounded-span perf optimization (19) produces arbitrary sub-day windows, but the manifest/seal model is day-granular (83-87) — without explicit outward day-snapping a sub-day request could fetch a partial day and violate the seal-once invariant or miss a sealed day entirely.

text: The forming/live-edge UTC day MUST be served exclusively from Postgres and MUST NEVER be written to any durable columnar artifact (Parquet/Feather/on-disk Arrow); only sealed (immutable) days are materialized to the columnar tier, so no cross-process reader can serve a stale forming day from a file — verified by a test asserting that after a run touching the forming day, no Parquet/Feather partition contains a candle with open_time >= the frontier.
rationale: Req 112 invalidates the mmap for the forming day but never forbids WRITING the forming day to columnar in the first place; the clean immutability boundary is 'sealed→columnar, forming→Postgres-only', which must be stated so a writer never persists a mutable bar to an immutable file.

text: A day sealed in Phase 1 (Postgres + manifest only, before the Parquet/DuckDB tier exists) MUST remain valid and readable after Phase 5 ships — read from Postgres (req 251 fallback) until its columnar partition is lazily built, with NO mass re-seal or forced re-materialization on the Phase-5 deploy — and the content_sha256 later computed from the Parquet rebuild MUST equal the Phase-1 sha computed from Postgres rows (canonical representation, req 254); a test MUST seal a day Postgres-only, build its Parquet later, and assert read-equality and sha-equality with zero re-seal.
rationale: The locked plan seals in Phase 1 but adds Parquet in Phase 5; nothing guarantees Phase-1-sealed days (which have a manifest sha but no Parquet) stay valid and hash-consistent once the columnar tier arrives — a phase-seam data-consistency gap that could trigger a spurious mass refetch on the Phase-5 deploy.

text: In a multi-worker deployment (N uvicorn processes) each process holds its OWN in-process LRU/Arrow hot cache, so the per-process cache budget (reqs 102/216/30) MUST be set such that Σ(N workers × per-process cache) ≤ the host RAM budget — OR the hot cache MUST be backed by mmap'd/shared memory so N workers do not N× resident kline RAM; a test MUST assert the documented aggregate-across-workers ceiling holds and that adding a worker does not multiply resident kline bytes beyond the shared-memory path's bound.
rationale: Reqs 30/102/216 bound cache memory per process and _MAX_CONCURRENT is within one process, but a multi-worker uvicorn deploy multiplies the in-process caches by worker count with no aggregate ceiling — an OOM risk on the long-lived server the existing per-process budgets don't capture.

text: The cache-efficiency regression gate (req 263) MUST additionally track columnar BYTES-READ and row-groups/partitions scanned on the canonical fixture and FAIL on a phase-over-phase increase, so a broken Parquet predicate/partition pushdown (silently reading whole files instead of the requested date range) is caught even when Bybit-call and Postgres-SELECT counts are unchanged.
rationale: Req 263 gates only Bybit calls and Postgres SELECTs; a pushdown regression in the DuckDB/Parquet path (reading entire partitions) inflates IO and latency while leaving those two counters at 0, so it slips past the existing gate undetected.

text: The batched `symbol = ANY($1)` kline load (req 16) MUST bucket rows into per-symbol arrays that are strictly ascending by open_time and byte-identical to what a per-symbol `WHERE symbol = $1 ORDER BY open_time` query would yield (no cross-symbol interleave, no reordering), because the SoA build and never-resetting merge-walk pointers (req 63) assume per-symbol monotonic order; a test MUST diff the bucketed batched-load arrays against single-symbol-query arrays for every symbol.
rationale: Req 16 says rows are 'bucketed in Python' but never asserts the bucketed order equals the per-symbol ordered read; the SoA/merge-walk correctness silently depends on strict per-symbol ascending order, which a multi-symbol ANY($1) result does not guarantee without an explicit sort/bucket contract.

text: A negative-cached / known-empty sealed day (pre-listing, post-delist, or confirmed structural gap) MUST be served from the manifest with ZERO Bybit calls AND ZERO kline-table SELECTs (there are no rows to read), returning an empty candle set the loader/engine handle gracefully (the symbol-day contributes to signals_no_kline, never raising); a test MUST assert a fully negative-cached range issues 0 fetches and 0 kline SELECTs and the run completes.
rationale: Reqs 90/91/297 establish that negative-cached ranges are never FETCHED, but don't pin the READ-path behavior — a known-empty day should also skip the Postgres SELECT entirely (nothing to read) and produce a graceful empty result, not an error or a wasted query.

text: A derived coarse bar (15m/1h/4h) whose constituent fine (5m) window contains a known-empty/structural-gap sub-bar MUST follow a documented, oracle-frozen aggregation rule (e.g. aggregate over present fine bars vs emit no coarse bar) and MUST match exchange-native coarse candles within the parity tolerance for that gap case — verified by a fixture seeding a fine-level gap and asserting the derived coarse bar equals the frozen oracle and is non-divergent from the native coarse bar.
rationale: Req 257 governs coarse SEALING completeness and 404 governs cross-backend agreement on clean ranges, but neither pins how derivation behaves when the fine base has a real structural hole — a subtle parity edge where derived-from-partial-fine can diverge from the exchange's native coarse bar.

text: On process restart during a deploy, backtest_runs rows left in 'running'/'pending' by the killed process MUST be reconciled to a terminal state on boot (marked 'failed' with an actionable 'interrupted by restart' message, or requeued) — never stuck 'running' forever — and the in-process _MAX_CONCURRENT slot counter MUST reset cleanly so a deploy mid-run cannot permanently leak a concurrency slot; verified by a test that seeds a 'running' row, simulates a restart, and asserts the row is reconciled and 3 slots are free.
rationale: _MAX_CONCURRENT is an in-process counter and run state lives in Postgres; a deploy SIGKILL/restart mid-run leaves orphaned 'running' rows and (across the persisted/in-memory boundary) a stuck UI. Existing reqs 242/299/364 only cover timeout-kill and the completion/cancel race within a single live process, not the boot-after-restart reconciliation that every rolling deploy triggers.

text: On SIGTERM / graceful shutdown during a deploy, an in-flight backtest or sweep MUST either complete within the orchestrator's termination grace period or be cooperatively cancelled (threading.Event) and persisted to a clean terminal state ('cancelled'/'failed') with its slot released and NO partial backtest_trades/results/equity_curve committed; verified by a shutdown-during-run test asserting a clean terminal row and no half-written persistence.
rationale: Deploys send SIGTERM before SIGKILL; the cooperative-cancel path (req 208) and timeout clean-state (req 363) exist, but nothing wires graceful-shutdown draining of an in-flight run, so a routine deploy can leave a spliced/partial result or a leaked slot.

text: A documented, tested admin procedure MUST exist to RESET seal state in place — set sealed=false and clear the manifest fact columns (first_open_ts/last_open_ts/gap_count/listing_snapped/content_sha256/fetched_at) for a symbol/interval/range or globally — WITHOUT dropping the v58 columns or downgrading the schema, so a discovered seal-correctness bug or a buggy backfill can be remediated in place (next run re-fetches and re-seals via the count-free predicate); verified by a test that resets a sealed range and asserts the subsequent run refetches and re-seals it correctly.
rationale: Reqs 296/397 only cover sha-mismatch / explicit single-day re-verify. There is no blanket in-place un-seal remediation path; without one, the only fix for a systemic backfill bug is a manual SQL surgery or a destructive schema action, which the additive forward-only design (154/432) otherwise forbids.

text: A kill-switch rollback of the columnar read layer (req 149) MUST leave previously-written Parquet/Feather/Arrow artifacts on disk UNTOUCHED (not deleted), and a subsequent re-enable MUST transparently REUSE the still-valid (format-version-matching, sha/SOR-verified) artifacts rather than forcing a full cold rebuild; verified by a disable→re-enable cycle test asserting zero rebuilds of unchanged, format-current partitions.
rationale: Reqs 287 (regenerate on format bump) and 432 (rollback drill parity) don't cover the rollback→re-enable round-trip. An operator who flips the kill-switch to investigate and flips it back should not pay a multi-minute cold columnar rebuild, and the rollback must not orphan/garbage the cache.

text: When the v58 backfill runs against an EMPTY/partial symbol_lifecycle and cannot determine whether a complete-but-short historic day (e.g. 144/288) is a listing-boundary day or a structural hole, it MUST seal it count-free with gap_count recording the shortfall (never leaving it a perpetual refetch candidate), and a later symbol_lifecycle population MUST be able to reclassify listing_snapped WITHOUT un-sealing it; verified by a test that backfills an ambiguous short day with empty lifecycle, asserts it is sealed-with-gap_count and zero-refetch, then populates lifecycle and asserts no un-seal/refetch.
rationale: Reqs 260 (distinguish incomplete-interior vs complete-short), 400 (empty-lifecycle still seal complete days), and 272/273 collide on the short-day case: without lifecycle bounds the listing-vs-hole classification is undecidable at backfill time, and no requirement specifies the conservative, count-free, reclassify-without-unseal resolution.
