text: The v58 backfill MUST correctly handle pre-existing coarse-interval (15m/1h/4h) rows already in kline_cache_coverage from the legacy per-timeframe fetch path — sealing complete coarse days as immutable fetched facts (keyed by their existing (symbol,interval,date) PK) so the transition to derive-coarse-from-fine (req 116) neither orphans nor triggers refetch of already-stored coarse data, and the read path's single-authoritative-source rule (req 258) governs which is used; verified by a test that seals a legacy 1h coverage row and asserts zero refetch on rerun.
rationale: simulation_interval ∈ {5m,15m,1h,4h} means coarse coverage rows exist in production; reqs 116/258 define the go-forward derive model but no requirement says what the v58 backfill does with the EXISTING coarse rows, risking either orphaning real data or a full coarse re-download on first post-deploy run.

text: store_klines auto-creating a monthly Postgres partition for a >6-month-old (or future) range MUST use the IDENTICAL UTC-month RANGE(open_time) boundary alignment as migration-38's partition scheme, so a backfilled/old sealed range's partition cannot overlap or gap against existing partitions; verified by a test that creates a partition for a >6mo-old month and asserts its bounds align exactly with the migration-38 monthly grid and no overlap/range error occurs.
rationale: Req 284 only catches the overlap exception under concurrency and req 122/137 only require a partition to 'exist'; the positive alignment-correctness invariant (matching migration-38's UTC-month bounds) that prevents the overlap in the first place is unspecified, and a misaligned auto-created partition would fail every write into that month.

text: A released migration version's DDL/backfill definition MUST be frozen once shipped: a CI guard (recorded checksum/snapshot of each migration ≤58 in _MIGRATIONS) MUST fail the build if a previously-released migration's statements are edited in place, forcing corrections to ship as a NEW version (v59+); verified by a test that mutating v58's recorded definition trips the guard.
rationale: Migrations apply exactly once per DB (schema_version gate); editing v58 after some environments have applied it silently diverges schemas across the fleet. Discovery flags that version-int collisions have already happened twice — an immutability guard closes the adjacent 'edited-after-release' failure mode, which no current requirement addresses.

text: A backtest run that arrives BEFORE the deferred boot-time numba JIT warm-up has completed (the window created by deferring warm-up so it doesn't block readiness, req 271) MUST either await a bounded warm-up or route to the pure-Python fallback for that run — it MUST NEVER pay an unbounded cold-compile inside the 120s cap; verified by a test that issues a run immediately post-boot (warm-up still in progress) and asserts it completes within budget via await-bounded-or-fallback.
rationale: Req 214 proves warm-at-boot is wired and the first cold run fits the cap, but req 271 explicitly DEFERS warm-up to protect readiness — creating a race window where an early run hits a not-yet-warm kernel. Neither requirement covers a run landing during an incomplete deferred warm-up.

text: The tightly-coupled JIT dependencies numba and llvmlite MUST carry an UPPER version ceiling in pyproject (not only the >= floor of req 138), so a fresh dependency resolution on a rebuild cannot pull a major bump that breaks the nopython kernel or the numba/llvmlite/numpy ABI; verified by a test asserting the numba and llvmlite specifiers are upper-bounded and mutually compatible.
rationale: Req 138 mandates only `>=` floors and req 269 pins exact versions via a lockfile, but any environment that resolves from pyproject without the lockfile (or regenerates it) can grab an incompatible numba major. numba↔llvmlite↔numpy coupling is exactly the kind that needs a ceiling, not just a floor.

text: The new optimization/infra flags MUST NOT be added to the agent- and API-facing request contracts — neither BacktestCreateRequest nor the MCP tool parameter schemas (backtest_run, sweep_run, optimize_config) — so existing API clients and MCP agents are unaffected and config_hash inputs are unchanged; verified by a schema-snapshot test asserting the request/tool parameter sets are byte-identical to pre-optimization and contain none of the optimization flag names.
rationale: Reqs 147/282 establish that flags are env/infra and config_hash is stable, but no requirement protects the actual REQUEST/TOOL SCHEMA shape. If infra flags leaked into BacktestCreateRequest or an MCP tool, every agent/integration contract and the MCP param surface (which discovery says is 1:1 with the API) would silently change.

text: The optimizer's PROPOSED AutoTradeConfig (optimize_config / config_current output that a human applies to a live scheduled scan) MUST contain only strategy/business config fields and MUST exclude all backtest engine optimization/infra flags, so an operator applying a proposal cannot accidentally set SoA/numba/Parquet/sweep-parallelism flags onto the live trading path; verified by a test asserting the proposed-config payload carries no infra-flag keys.
rationale: The optimizer proposes configs a human applies to live scans (per the MCP tool contract). If infra flags rode along in the proposal, applying it would mutate live-path engine behavior — a cross-contamination of the backtest-only optimization surface into production trading that no current requirement forbids.

text: An UNRECOGNIZED or misspelled optimization-flag environment-variable NAME (e.g. BACKTEST_DISABLE_NUMBAA) MUST be detected and surfaced as a boot-time warning/structured signal (typo guard against the known flag-name set), so an operator's intended kill-switch that is misspelled does not silently fail to take effect; verified by a test setting a near-miss flag name and asserting a warning is emitted and the real flag retains its safe default.
rationale: Req 425 covers a malformed VALUE of a known flag, but a misspelled flag NAME is the more dangerous operational failure: the operator believes they disabled numba/Parquet during an incident, but the accelerated path is still live and silently producing the results they were trying to avoid.

text: A deploy-time fleet-consistency check MUST be able to verify that all running instances report the SAME active-vs-degraded optimization-flag set AND the same engine git SHA via the queryable status route (req 428), so a partial or misconfigured rollout where pods diverge (one with SoA on, another off) is detected before it produces inconsistent results for the same logical config; verified by a test that divergent instance status payloads are flagged as a rollout inconsistency.
rationale: Flags are resolved per-instance from env (req 424), and results are fingerprinted per-run (318/430), but nothing checks FLEET-level uniformity. In a multi-pod/rolling deploy, two instances on different flag sets silently yield different numbers for the same config — the per-instance status of req 428 detects this only if something actually compares across instances.

text: A rolling-deploy OVERLAP integration test MUST run an old-code (v57/legacy, count-based coverage + legacy ON CONFLICT upserts) instance and a new-code (v58, sealed-manifest gate) instance CONCURRENTLY against one v58-migrated DB and assert: no errors on either path, legacy upserts do not null/clobber manifest columns (req 418) nor un-seal days, the new instance's reads stay byte-identical to its standalone oracle, and both serve traffic — proving the deploy overlap window is safe, not just the unit-level column-preservation.
rationale: Reqs 305 (concurrent startup migration), 418 (legacy upsert vs v58 row, unit), 153 (old code on v58 schema), and 281 (live path current-day freshness) each cover a slice, but no requirement exercises the actual rolling-deploy steady state where both code paths are LIVE simultaneously against the shared kline_cache_service/coverage table — the highest-risk real-world deployment moment.

text: A CI meta-test MUST guard against pytest-asyncio strict-mode silent skips: it asserts the parity+cache+sweep suites collect at least an expected floor count of tests, that zero tests are silently skipped/errored for a missing @pytest.mark.asyncio marker, and that no parity/golden test is marked skip/xfail without an explicit allowlist reason — so a marker omission (which strict mode turns into a silent no-run) can never make the golden gate report green while testing nothing.
rationale: Discovery §1 states async tests missing the asyncio marker silently error/skip under strict mode; a silently-skipped golden/cache parity test is worse than a failing one because it looks passing, and nothing in the 433 items guards collection integrity.

text: A meta-test MUST pin (hash) the default keyword values of the duplicated _config/_signal/_klines fixture builders so that an accidental edit or naive consolidation that shifts a builder default — which would silently re-baseline every oracle derived from it — fails CI; the test asserts the builder defaults are byte-identical to a committed snapshot, complementing the regen-script discipline (#183) which only forbids hand-editing the stored oracle files, not the builders that feed them.
rationale: Discovery §7 warns the builders are duplicated with DIFFERENT defaults and must be extended in place, never consolidated, because a default shift silently moves frozen golden values; #183 protects the snapshots but nothing protects the builder defaults that generate them.

text: The full-scale canonical 90d×50sym×5m drilldown-OFF benchmark fixture (#1) MUST carry its OWN frozen result fingerprint (content hash over ordered trades + ordered equity_curve + all ~45 metrics) asserted byte-identical across Phases 0–6, distinct from the micro golden fixtures, so the large-scale perf fixture doubles as a large-scale bit-identical parity gate; a phase MUST NOT achieve a speedup by silently changing results at scale, and accumulation/scan-ordering/seeding bugs that micro-fixtures cannot surface are caught — verified by a test that fails if the large-run fingerprint deviates from the Phase-0 baseline.
rationale: #73 freezes a small canonical 5m no-drill config and #38 asserts only the speed ratio; neither guarantees the LARGE 90d×50sym run (2160 scans, many carried positions) is bit-identical, which is exactly where O(scans×N) seeding and stable-sort reordering regressions manifest.

text: Every performance/benchmark/scaling fixture (#1,#2,#6,#7,#12,#13,#231,#389) MUST, within the SAME timed execution, also assert the three-way reconciliation Σtrade.pnl == final_equity − starting_capital and that metrics.total_trades is present and non-null, so the perf/regression gate can never be satisfied by a fast-but-incorrect path; a test MUST confirm a deliberately broken-but-fast engine fails the perf fixture on the reconciliation assertion, not merely on wall-time.
rationale: Perf gates that measure only wall-time/RSS can be 'passed' by a path that skips work and produces wrong numbers; tying every perf fixture to reconciliation + total_trades makes speed and correctness inseparable in one assertion.

text: A mutation/canary BATTERY MUST inject a documented set of distinct engine defects — at minimum: SL/TP intrabar precedence flip, entry-fill-bar off-by-one, funding (date,hour) dedupe removal, entry/exit slippage sign flip, equity-rule cascade reorder, one filter-chain step skipped, qty floor→round-half-up, and a merge-walk pointer reset across scans — and assert the golden-master gate turns RED for EACH mutation independently, proving the suite detects every defect CLASS rather than a single example.
rationale: #344 injects only one example defect ('e.g. flip SL/TP precedence'); a one-mutation canary proves the gate has teeth for one bug but not that it covers the precedence, off-by-one, funding, slippage-sign, cascade-order, filter, rounding, and pointer-reset failure modes the refactor actually risks.

text: A randomized PROPERTY test over many synthetic (coarse-bar, 1m-sub-bar) configurations MUST assert a two-sided sandwich for every drilled trade: drilled PnL ≤ the always-LTF oracle (non-optimistic) AND drilled PnL ≥ the coarse pessimistic-resolution bound (no worse than the un-drilled SL-first coarse result), so the drill resolution is provably bracketed across the input space rather than checked on a single both-levels-in-bar fixture.
rationale: #49 asserts non-optimism only on a single fixture and only the upper bound; a randomized two-sided property test proves the drill is bounded BOTH ways (never more optimistic than LTF, never more pessimistic than coarse) across the whole sub-bar arrangement space, catching tie-break/ordering bugs single fixtures miss.

text: A test MUST cross-check that the boot/runtime optimization self-check report (#164/#428) MATCHES the path actually executed, not merely that a log/metric was emitted: with numba (or duckdb/pyarrow) force-absent, the report MUST say that optimization is degraded AND a probe backtest MUST be proven to have run the pure-Python/Postgres path (e.g. via an execution-path counter or sentinel), so the active-vs-degraded status can never falsely report an optimization as active when it silently fell back.
rationale: #164/#428 only require that a self-check signal is emitted/exposed; an optimistically-wrong self-check ('numba active' while it actually fell back) would mask a degraded prod deploy — the report must be verified consistent with the real executed path.

text: A single 'kitchen-sink' golden fixture MUST exercise liquidation (no exit fee/slippage) + a SHORT receiving negative funding + end-of-backtest force-close (backtest_end) + a basket-flatten equity close all within one run, and assert the three-way reconciliation Σtrade.pnl == net_profit == final_equity − starting_capital holds with ALL special-case terms summed together, catching cross-term sign/rounding accumulation that single-term fixtures (#310/#325/#340) cannot.
rationale: Each special PnL term (liquidation −locked_margin, negative funding, raw/charged force-close, basket flatten) is reconciled in isolation, but their SUM with mixed signs and differing fee/slippage treatment is where Decimal→float64 accumulation drift would silently break the identity — only a combined fixture exercises it.

text: A test MUST assert the paginated GET /backtest/{id}/trades endpoint returns trades in a deterministic order that matches the in-memory/oracle trade sequence under the optimized engine (delete-before-insert + cursor pagination), so the SoA/parallel/sweep refactor cannot scramble the persisted trade ordering the UI renders or cause a cursor page to skip/duplicate a trade across the page boundary.
rationale: Trades are delete-before-insert and the GET is cursor-paginated; the SoA/parallel refactor changes how trades are produced/ordered, and nothing in the list asserts the persisted+paginated trade ORDER stays stable and gap-free for the frontend.

text: A guard test MUST assert the parity/cache/golden suite never connects to a production database: it fails fast if DATABASE_URL (or the resolved test DSN) points at a non-test/prod host, requiring an isolated/ephemeral test DB, complementing the no-real-HTTP-egress guard (#293) so prod-scan copy workflows cannot accidentally run the destructive delete-before-insert/migration tests against live data.
rationale: #293 blocks network egress but not DB writes; the copy-prod-scans workflow puts prod DSNs within reach, and the suite performs migrations + delete-before-insert + store_klines writes that must never touch a production Postgres.

text: A golden fixture MUST pin and freeze the backtest engine's exact treatment of a PAUSE_TRADING action/close-rule (asserting whether it is an intentional no-op like max_same_sector, or actually halts new entries while still evaluating closes) so the legacy behavior is preserved bit-identically, and PAUSE_TRADING MUST be added to the close_reason/action enum-coverage assertion (#345 currently omits it).
rationale: CLAUDE.md's recent DB-constraint fix lists PAUSE_TRADING alongside EQUITY_DROP_PCT_SMART and TRAILING_PROFIT, but #345's close_reason enumeration omits pause_trading, leaving its backtest semantics unpinned and a refactor free to silently change or drop it.

text: Because the bit-identical golden suite runs on EVERY phase PR (#73/#198), its own wall-time MUST be bounded and asserted (e.g. micro-fixtures run on the pure synchronous engine seam per discovery §7, full golden battery completes under a documented per-PR budget on the canonical CI runner), so the parity gate stays cheap enough to actually run every phase and cannot be quietly marked 'slow'/optional as fixtures accumulate.
rationale: A bit-identical gate is only effective if it runs on every phase PR; if the fixture battery balloons and the suite slows, teams mark it slow/skip it — the suite must have an enforced runtime budget using the fast pure-engine seam discovery §7 identifies.

text: A config-surface completeness meta-test MUST enumerate every config field in the §3 surface (execution, trade-decision, all 17 filter steps, close rules, adaptive-blacklist, F1 regime/session, F2 mean-reversion, F3 cohort) and assert each is exercised by at least one golden fixture, failing CI when any field (e.g. fill_to_max_trades, max_signal_age_minutes, mr_extreme_min_abs_score, funding_rate_model) has no covering fixture — a backstop that the battery actually spans the no-regress config surface.
rationale: Individual fixtures cover many fields, but nothing systematically proves EVERY config field has at least one parity fixture; a newly added or long-untested field can slip through with zero coverage, which a field→fixture mapping meta-test prevents.

text: The benchmark/perf REGRESSION gate (#36/#213/#312) MUST also run on the force-deps-absent (numba/pyarrow/duckdb simulated-ImportError) fallback lane, gating the pure-Python/Postgres path's engine-CPU-time and wall-time against its OWN committed frozen baseline, so a regression in the safety-net fallback path (which must stay under the 120s cap and within the degradation budget of #225/#385) is detected — not only the accelerated path.
rationale: #304 runs the GOLDEN (correctness) suite in the fallback lane but no perf gate; the pure-Python path is the mandated degradation target (#34/#142/#225/#385) and a silent fallback-perf regression would blow the 120s degradation budget undetected.

text: The ENGINE's time arithmetic — funding (date,hour) boundary dedupe, max_signal_age_minutes age basis, max_trade_duration_hours, breakeven_timeout_hours, and mr_time_stop_minutes — MUST be proven host-timezone/DST independent by running a golden fixture under multiple host TZ settings (e.g. UTC, a positive-offset zone, and a DST-observing zone) and asserting byte-identical trades and close_reasons, distinct from the storage/bucketing UTC requirements (#250/#406) which cover day-keys and partitions, not the engine's in-loop minute/hour/boundary math.
rationale: #250/#406 enforce UTC for day bucketing and Parquet partitions but not the engine's funding-boundary and age/duration deltas; if any naive-local datetime leaks into the (date,hour) funding key or a minute/hour delta on the Windows host, trades diverge under a non-UTC TZ — an engine-side parity edge no current test exercises.

text: A test MUST assert config_hash is INJECTIVE over all decision-relevant config fields: varying each decision field individually (TP/SL, leverage, every filter threshold, MR params, close rules, direction, execution_mode) MUST change the hash, so two logically-distinct strategy configs can never collide and silently drop a combo under ON CONFLICT(sweep_id, config_hash); complements #348 (cross-interpreter determinism) and #282 (infra-flags must NOT change the hash) by guarding the opposite failure — a decision-field collision.
rationale: #348 proves the hash is stable and #282 proves infra flags don't perturb it, but neither proves the hash actually DISTINGUISHES two different strategy configs; a collision would make a sweep's ON CONFLICT(sweep_id, config_hash) silently overwrite/skip a legitimate combo, losing results with no error.

text: A test MUST assert the per-stage timing breakdown (#39/#222) is EXHAUSTIVE — the sum of all reported stage durations (warmup band, kline load, engine sim, 1m drill, persist) equals total end-to-end wall-time within a small epsilon — so no latency can hide in an unmeasured gap between stages and the per-stage budget gates of #222 cannot be evaded by a regression landing in unattributed time.
rationale: #39 records stages and #222 budgets each, but nothing asserts the stages SUM to the whole; a regression in uninstrumented glue code (between stages) would pass every per-stage budget while degrading total wall-time, defeating the attribution guarantee.

text: A position MUST be eligible (or ineligible) to exit in its OWN entry-fill bar exactly as the legacy engine: pin whether intrabar liq/TP/SL evaluated on the entry bar's high/low can close a position in the SAME bar it filled (entry = next-bar-open at that bar's open), so the SoA/searchsorted refactor neither adds a same-bar exit the legacy engine skipped nor drops one it took — frozen by a fixture whose entry bar's [low,high] range spans the TP/SL barrier.
rationale: #62 pins the entry fill bar and #244 pins the inter-scan window membership, but nothing pins the half-open boundary AT the entry bar — whether the fill bar itself is in the exit-evaluation set. A one-bar shift here changes whether a fast in-and-out trade exists at all, cascading the whole equity curve.

text: The tolerance policy MUST split DISCRETE from CONTINUOUS fields: discrete outcomes — the set of positions opened (symbol + entry bar), side, close_reason, the exit BAR index, total trade count, and the filter selection set — MUST be BIT-IDENTICAL between the Decimal oracle and every float64/numba path, while ONLY continuous money/price fields (entry/exit price, pnl, fees, funding, mfe/mae, equity) may differ within the <1% epsilon; a test MUST assert a float64 run never changes any discrete outcome versus the oracle even when prices differ in the last bits.
rationale: #74 says 'per-trade fields and every summary metric must deviate <1%', but close_reason/side/count/selection are discrete — '<1%' is meaningless for them. The real invariant is that epsilon may move PRICES but must never change WHICH trades exist or WHICH bar they exit in.

text: float64 rounding MUST NOT flip any barrier touch/no-touch decision nor the 'SL wins only if closer than liquidation' distance comparison near a threshold: a fixture placing a bar's high/low and a TP/SL/liq price within epsilon of equality MUST resolve touched-vs-untouched and liq-vs-SL precedence identically to the Decimal oracle (same `>=` vs `>` boundary and same equality tie-break), so an exit can never fire one bar early/late from a last-bit rounding difference.
rationale: #41 defines the liq-vs-SL distance rule and #64 says float64 must not flip a threshold, but neither has a dedicated near-equality fixture proving the touch boundary and the distance-tie are rounding-stable. This is the mechanism by which D (accumulation) would actually corrupt results.

text: Cumulative float64 error MUST stay below the smallest recorded-money quantization granularity at the DEEPEST fixture (the wide capacity point — e.g. 200 symbols × 365 days, thousands of closed trades, deep concurrent book), not only at 90d×50sym: a depth-scaling parity test MUST assert running-wallet, Σ-uPnL, and per-trade pnl stay within epsilon of the Decimal oracle as run depth grows, proving accumulation drift never reaches a quantization-flip on long runs.
rationale: #343 enumerates per-site rounding modes and #64 forbids threshold flips, but neither bounds ACCUMULATED float error at scale. Drift grows with trade count / book depth; a quantization flip may only surface on a long run, never on the small canonical fixture the parity gate uses.

text: The non-optimistic guarantee MUST be stated with epsilon precision: on AMBIGUOUS bars (drill / same-bar multi-touch) the fast/float64 PnL MUST be ≤ the always-LTF Decimal oracle (strictly non-optimistic), while on NON-ambiguous bars it MUST be within ±epsilon of the oracle (benign rounding allowed in either direction) — so the gate catches a real optimistic shortcut without false-failing when benign last-bit rounding lands a hair above the oracle.
rationale: #49/#74 assert 'PnL ≤ oracle' generally, but on non-ambiguous bars float64 can legitimately round a hair ABOVE Decimal; a strict ≤ would false-fail. The strict-≤ must be scoped to ambiguous bars only.

text: A bar-by-bar STATEFUL-TRAJECTORY differential harness MUST diff the legacy and SoA/numba engines on the evolving mutable latches at EVERY bar (not only final trades): cycle_start_equity, smart_drawdown_fired, breakeven-mutated TP, trailing peak, the funding (date,hour) dedupe structure, adaptive-blacklist win/total counters, and running sizing_capital — and the numba funding-dedupe representation MUST yield charge/no-charge decisions identical to the Python set, so a divergent state-update order that happens to produce identical final trades on one fixture is still caught.
rationale: #319 diffs PURE helper functions and #195 diffs FINAL output, but neither diffs the intermediate state trajectory. Two different latch-update orders can agree on one fixture's output and diverge on another; only a per-bar state diff has teeth against that.

text: searchsorted/merge-walk price location MUST reproduce the legacy linear scan when a symbol's array contains DUPLICATE open_time values (raw data duplication or a partition-seam merge residue): side='right'-1 MUST select the LAST candle at the matched epoch for the 'last close ≤ T' mark and side='left' MUST select the FIRST candle at the matched epoch for the entry fill, byte-identical to the legacy first/last linear match — frozen by a duplicate-timestamp fixture.
rationale: #193/#241 cover randomized and boundary searchsorted cases (empty/single/before/after) but not DUPLICATE epochs within one symbol's array, where searchsorted's left/right index diverges from a linear first/last-match scan. #359 de-dups at the read seam but residual dups can still reach the engine.

text: On the DRILLED path, trailing-profit peak tracking and BREAKEVEN_TIMEOUT activation MUST still use 5m bar extremes (consistent with equity_ref and MFE/MAE remaining 5m under the entry-only drill rule), so toggling drilldown_enabled cannot change trailing activation timing, the ratcheted peak value, or the breakeven-mutation trigger bar — verified by a drill-on vs drill-off fixture asserting identical trailing/breakeven firing while only fill PRICE differs.
rationale: #331 pins MFE/MAE to 5m on the drilled path and #56/#57 pin the trailing/breakeven state machines, but no requirement pins that trailing-peak and breakeven activation specifically stay on 5m extremes when drilling — a gap that would let the drill path silently shift trailing/breakeven timing.

text: When MULTIPLE INDEPENDENT positions (different symbols) each hit their OWN liquidation/TP/SL inside the SAME global-timeline bar, the engine MUST record those individual closes in the legacy insertion/iteration order (not symbol-index or SoA-sorted order), because each close mutates the running wallet that feeds the next position's sizing and emits its own ordered equity_curve point — pinned by a multi-symbol same-bar multi-exit fixture whose reorder would change a later trade's sizing or the curve order.
rationale: #350 covers basket-flatten (one EQUITY rule closing many) and #327 covers the Σ-uPnL iteration order, but neither covers many INDEPENDENT barrier exits coinciding in one bar, whose recording order affects wallet-fed sizing and curve ordering for path-dependent metrics.

text: NaN/None/hole OHLC handling MUST be identical between the Decimal and float64 paths: a kline row carrying missing/NaN values MUST NOT let a float64 comparison silently return False and skip a touch the legacy path took (nor fabricate one), and an interior data hole MUST be treated identically — frozen by a fixture with a NaN/None bar inside an open position's life asserting no exit is added or dropped versus the oracle.
rationale: #349 covers gapped/misaligned timelines (a symbol missing a bar at T) but not a present bar whose OHLC is NaN/None. In float64, NaN comparisons silently evaluate False, which can flip a TP/SL touch decision relative to Decimal/Python — a parity landmine with no current guard.

text: Sharpe/Sortino return-periodization MUST be reproduced exactly under the int64-epoch time representation: the equity_curve→returns derivation, the inter-point time-delta basis, and the annualization constant MUST yield Sharpe/Sortino bit-identical (within epsilon) to the legacy datetime-based computation — verified by a fixture with irregular close spacing that would change the metric if the epoch-delta basis shifted.
rationale: #79/#185 assert the equity_curve ORDER drives path-dependent metrics, but if Sharpe/Sortino infer a period from timestamp deltas, the datetime→int64-epoch conversion must reproduce those deltas exactly. The periodization basis itself is unpinned.

text: The pure simulation ENGINE MUST contain ZERO wall-clock reads — every temporal decision derives solely from scan_time and kline open_time epochs (the completion frontier's now() is the only legitimate wall-clock use and lives in the cache layer, never the engine) — verified by a test that runs the same backtest under two widely different mocked system clocks and asserts byte-identical engine output (trades + ordered equity_curve).
rationale: #206 asserts repeatability and #348 config-hash determinism, but neither isolates the engine from system-clock leakage. A stray now()/utcnow() in a max_signal_age or age path would make a run today vs next week diverge — the cache legitimately reads now(), so the boundary must be explicitly tested.

text: Every recorded numeric value MUST survive the float→NUMERIC/JSONB→float persistence round-trip losslessly (or via a pinned Decimal quantization), so reloaded equity_curve points and trade fields equal the in-memory values byte-for-byte, the FULL-JSONB equity_curve manifest hash is STABLE across a store→reload cycle, and the three-way reconciliation holds on the RELOADED values — a truncation that keeps Σpnl reconciling by luck while individual points drift MUST fail this test.
rationale: #352 reads back and re-checks the SUM, and #78/#203 hash the full JSONB, but no requirement asserts each INDIVIDUAL value round-trips losslessly. A per-field truncation can preserve the reconciled sum while corrupting the ordered-curve hash and individual points.

text: When a SINGLE scan contains MULTIPLE signals for the SAME not-yet-open symbol, the engine MUST admit exactly the legacy-chosen one (pin the deterministic rule — highest abs(score) with the existing index tie-break, or first-in-list) so SoA/dict re-keying cannot change WHICH same-symbol duplicate becomes the trade — frozen by a fixture with two same-symbol signals of differing score within one scan.
rationale: #69 covers existing-symbol dedup against OPEN positions and #328 covers tied scores across DIFFERENT symbols under max_trades, but neither covers two signals for the SAME un-opened symbol in one scan — a selection edge a dict-keyed refactor could silently reorder.

text: Each persisted result MUST record WHICH numeric kernel actually produced its numbers (Decimal-legacy / float64-pure-Python / float64-numba / vectorized-fast-path), and when a run DEGRADES mid-flight (numba→fallback per the fault-injection rule) the recorded kernel MUST be the one that produced the PERSISTED numbers, not the attempted one; a test MUST assert the recorded kernel matches the executed path and that the alternate kernel reproduces those numbers within the <1% tolerance — closing the 'same config yields different numbers depending on whether numba was available' non-determinism.
rationale: #278/#318/#371 record and reproduce from engine SHA + flag SET, but the flag set is the ATTEMPTED config; a mid-run numba→pure-Python degrade can change the serving kernel. Without recording the actual kernel, a stored result is not reproducible and the Decimal-vs-float regime that produced it is ambiguous.

text: The single-run pre-flight cost/feasibility gate (compute and cold-fetch estimators) MUST NOT reject, or silently batch-degrade, any config the LEGACY engine would have completed within budget: a parity-of-acceptance test MUST assert the runnable-config surface does not shrink — every config the golden battery proves legacy completed is still accepted (not pre-flight-rejected) by the optimized path.
rationale: #224/#408 add pre-flight reject/warn gates for infeasible runs, but nothing guards against FALSE rejections that shrink the set of configs users could previously run. A too-aggressive estimator is a silent behavioral regression.

text: A single matrix GATE MUST run the ENTIRE fixture battery through EVERY available path — Decimal-legacy, float64-SoA-pure-Python, float64-numba, the vectorized fast-path (on its eligible subset), and BOTH the Postgres and columnar read layers — asserting the DISCRETE outcome set is bit-identical across ALL paths and continuous values are within epsilon, so a divergence in any single kernel×read-path combination fails the build, not only the pairwise Python-vs-numba (#195), fast-path-vs-sequential (#197), and flags-off (#156) checks.
rationale: The existing parity checks are pairwise/scattered; nothing enforces the full kernel × read-path × fixture cross-product against the discrete/continuous policy. A divergence unique to (numba × columnar-read) on a specific fixture could slip every existing pairwise gate.

text: A NON-SKIPPED scan that admits ZERO signals (every signal filtered out, but skip_if_positions_open did NOT fire) MUST still re-arm smart_drawdown_fired and re-anchor cycle_start_equity exactly as a non-skipped scan that admits trades — the refactor MUST NOT collapse 'admitted nothing' into the 'skipped' anchor-preserving branch — frozen by a fixture chaining an all-filtered scan between two trading scans and asserting the re-arm/re-anchor still occurs.
rationale: #44/#45 pin re-arm/re-anchor on 'non-skipped' scans and #71 pins the skip branch preserving the anchor, but the boundary case 'ran but admitted zero' is unspecified. A refactor could treat zero-admitted as skipped, breaking the smart-drawdown one-shot re-arm cadence.

text: The large benchmark fixture (90d×50sym) MUST carry its OWN frozen parity oracle (or an in-run Decimal-vs-float cross-check on that identical fixture), so the perf/benchmark lane validates RESULT CORRECTNESS at scale: a 'fast but wrong' optimization whose divergence only manifests at benchmark depth (accumulation drift, merge-walk pointer non-reset across thousands of scans, cross-scan latch leakage) MUST fail the benchmark gate rather than pass because the small golden fixtures never exercise that depth.
rationale: #198 runs the golden suite (small hand-crafted fixtures) and #201/#312 gate PERFORMANCE on the large fixture, but the large fixture's RESULT is never parity-checked. Scale-only bugs (the exact O(scans×N_total)/quadratic-seeding regions being refactored) are invisible to small-fixture parity.

text: Cold gap-fill MUST paginate to FULL coverage of each contiguous gap-run within a single ensure_coverage invocation — the _MAX_PAGES=5 cap MUST be raised/removed or the fetch MUST loop until last_fetched_open_time >= gap_run_end — so a cold span larger than _MAX_PAGES×page_size completes in one run instead of only filling the most-recent ~1000-candle tail and leaving interior candles perpetually missing; a test cold-fetches a >5000-candle (>~17-day 5m) span against a paginating mock and asserts full interior coverage in one invocation with request count == ceil(missing/1000).
rationale: RC-4's verifier caveat (findings) states _MAX_PAGES(5)×_PAGE_SIZE means spans >~1000 candles 're-download only the most recent ~1000 candles … it never converges.' Req #18 raises page size and #5 bounds request count, but NEITHER removes the 5-page-per-call ceiling, so a 90-day cold fetch (~25,920 candles/symbol = ~26 pages) cannot complete in one ensure_coverage call — a critical cold-throughput/correctness gap not closed by the existing list.

text: The cold 5m per-symbol gap-fill across N symbols MUST positively ACHIEVE concurrency (not merely stay under the rate-limit cap of #229): it MUST fan out via the shared semaphore-bounded asyncio.gather so wall-time ≈ ceil(N/cap)×per-symbol-fetch; a test with mocked latency-bearing fetches asserts measured cold-load wall-time is sub-linear in N (≈ceil(N/cap)×t), distinct from #229 which only asserts in-flight count ≤ cap and could be satisfied by serial fetching.
rationale: #229 bounds concurrency from ABOVE (≤ cap) and #22 covers 1m drill only; a loader that fetches 5m gaps serially still satisfies #229. The positive cross-symbol wall-time speedup for the COLD 5m path — which dominates cold wall-time for a fresh 50-symbol window — has no gate.

text: The in-process hot/LRU cache MUST serve a sub-range request from an already-cached SUPER-range (range-containment hit, zero-copy slice) without any Bybit fetch or Postgres read; a test loads [t1,t2] then requests [t1',t2']⊂[t1,t2] (different per-run bounded spans from #19) and asserts 0 Bybit + 0 kline SELECTs and byte-identical klines.
rationale: #99 keys the LRU by EXACT (symbol,interval,start,end); the bounded per-symbol span (#19) varies run-to-run with each config's min/max signal_time, so exact-key lookups would MISS even though the candles are already resident — defeating warm-rerun and sweep reuse. Range-containment is a distinct, common, untested perf behavior.

text: Two SEPARATE backtest run invocations over an identical (symbols, interval, window) but DIFFERENT strategy config (e.g. tweaked take_profit_pct/stop_loss_pct/filters) MUST share the kline LRU/hot cache so the second issues 0 Bybit calls and 0 kline Postgres reads — only the engine re-runs; a test asserts this 'change a param and rerun' path is fully warm on data because the cache key excludes strategy params.
rationale: #3 covers same-config rerun and #217 covers within-ONE-sweep combos 2..K, but the everyday interactive loop — run, eyeball results, change TP/SL, run again as a fresh invocation — is the highest-frequency real-world pattern and is unasserted; it validates that the cache key is correctly data-scoped (not config-scoped).

text: The sweep orchestrator MUST pre-load klines and build the shared read-only SoA arrays + global sorted-unique timeline ONCE before fanning out combos (not lazily inside combo-1 with the rest blocked on single-flight), so all workers begin compute simultaneously against shared immutable arrays; a counter test on a K-combo sweep asserts kline-load == 1, SoA-build == 1, AND global-timeline-build == 1 for the whole sweep, and a timing test asserts combos do not serialize behind first-combo materialization.
rationale: #217/#218 assert per-combo LOAD/CONVERT count==0 for combos 2..K and #379 asserts build-once within a 2-pass run, but none requires the timeline to be built once ACROSS a sweep nor that materialization precedes fan-out — single-flight (#246) would technically satisfy the counters while still serializing every worker behind combo-1's cold load, wasting the parallelism.

text: Total drill-down cost MUST scale linearly with the NUMBER of bars actually drilled (each O(sub-bars)), with non-drilled bars staying O(1) via the coarse High/Low pre-filter; a fixture that raises the drilled-bar fraction by tightening stops (more ambiguous bars per #46) MUST show drill time growing linearly with drilled-bar count — not super-linearly — and total engine wall-time MUST degrade gracefully as p→1 rather than collapsing toward the cold full-history budget.
rationale: The findings' explicit OPEN QUESTION ('how tight are typical stops … decides Phase effort split') means drill cost is governed by p (fraction of bars needing LTF resolution). #2 fixes a single drill-on budget and #21 bounds over-fetch ratio, but there is NO scaling law tying drill cost to drilled-bar count, so a tight-stop strategy could blow the latency budget undetected.

text: A symbol-count-doubling scaling test (fixed window, fixed scans, fixed interval) MUST show engine + kline-load + timeline-build time grows ≤ ~2× (linear in symbol count), proving no super-linear cross-symbol term in the global-timeline construction, SoA build, or per-candle portfolio Σ-uPnL reduction.
rationale: #6 varies history, #13 varies scans, #12 is candles×scans, and #231 sets a wide-point (200-symbol) BUDGET — but no test isolates the SYMBOL dimension with a doubling ratio. The cross-sectional portfolio engine is the one place an accidental O(symbols²) term (e.g. reduction over the full universe instead of the open book) could hide.

text: backtest_trades persistence (delete-before-insert) MUST use a single batched write (executemany / COPY) so a run producing T trades issues O(1) insert round-trips, not O(T); a test doubling trade count at fixed candle count asserts the insert round-trip count stays constant and total persist time scales with trade VOLUME, not per-row latency.
rationale: #220 requires persist time to scale with trade count and #161 fixes delete-before-insert IDEMPOTENCY, but neither pins the round-trip count to O(1). On the now-fast engine a trade-heavy run could make per-row INSERT RTTs the dominant wall-time term, re-creating an N+1 bottleneck on the write side analogous to the read-side ANY($1) fix (#16).

text: A COLD-COMPUTE latency budget (empty in-process LRU/hot cache, JIT pre-warmed, exchange + Postgres served locally/mocked so network and wire IO are excluded) MUST be defined and gated for the canonical fixture (e.g. < 30 s), distinct from the network-inclusive 120 s worst case (#214) and the warm targets (#1/#2), so a regression in the load→SoA→engine→persist COMPUTE path is caught and not masked by network variance.
rationale: Existing budgets are either warm (#1/#2/#3) or network-bound worst-case (#5/#214). There is no gated target for the compute path with IO factored out, yet that is exactly the quantity Phases 3–6 are optimizing; without it, a compute regression can hide behind noisy cold network time.

text: Every production backtest/sweep run (not just the benchmark harness) MUST emit engine THROUGHPUT as a structured metric — total candle-evaluations and candle-evals/sec (or engine-only CPU time) — so an engine-level perf regression is detectable in production without re-running the benchmark; a test asserts the field is present and reflects the true candle-eval count on a mocked run.
rationale: #264 emits CACHE statistics and #14/#15 are bench-only throughput TARGETS, but no per-run prod metric captures engine speed. A real-world slowdown (e.g. JIT silently fell back to pure-Python in prod per #164) would be invisible until someone manually benchmarks; throughput is the directly diagnostic signal.

text: A soak test of N sequential heterogeneous runs MUST assert warm-run LATENCY stays stable (no monotonic increase) over the long-lived process — manifest/coverage-table growth, hot-cache fragmentation, and per-run allocation churn MUST NOT degrade per-run latency across the process lifetime — distinct from the RSS-stability soak of #226.
rationale: #226 asserts no monotonic RSS growth but says nothing about LATENCY drift; a process can hold flat RSS while slowing down (cache eviction thrash, growing manifest scans, GC pressure). For a server that stays up for days serving many runs, latency stability is a separate, testable SLO.

text: The sealed-manifest completeness/seal-check query MUST be index-backed so its latency is O(requested-range days) and INDEPENDENT of total manifest/coverage row count; a test against a large (multi-year, many-symbol) manifest asserts seal-check latency stays constant as total manifest size grows.
rationale: #98 makes the manifest the SSOT and #431 governs index-CREATION locking, but neither asserts that the per-run seal CHECK latency is decoupled from accumulated history. As kline_cache_coverage grows over months/years, an unindexed or table-scan seal check would slowly inflate every run's setup cost — a creeping scalability regression.

text: The per-phase benchmark regression gate MUST run and INDEPENDENTLY gate every headline scenario — drill-off warm (#1), drill-on warm (#2), cold worst-case (#214), and the 100-combo sweep (#215) — so a regression isolated to the drill path or the sweep path is caught even when the drill-off canonical run stays green.
rationale: #36 phrases the gate against 'the canonical fixture' (singular, drill-off). A change that regresses only the drill or sweep orchestration (e.g. lost gather concurrency, timeline rebuilt per combo) would pass a drill-off-only gate. Each headline latency budget that exists in the list needs its own regression gate, not just a definition.

text: GET /backtest-cache/status MUST compute readiness via a bounded, index-backed manifest query (O(requested symbols), not a full per-day coverage scan) so its latency is independent of total coverage-table size; a test asserts status-endpoint latency stays constant as historical coverage rows grow.
rationale: #262 makes status CORRECT against the manifest (sealed-short days reported ready) but not FAST; the UI polls this endpoint, and a per-day scan over a large coverage table would make it slow precisely when history is large — a user-facing latency regression with no gate.

text: The GET-time equity_curve LTTB downsample MUST be O(stored-curve-length) and bounded so the results endpoint stays responsive for trade-heavy runs with large stored curves; a test asserts GET latency scales linearly with curve length and stays under a defined bound.
rationale: #221 budgets the PERSIST-time serialization/LTTB cost and #368 covers LTTB CORRECTNESS, but the LTTB pass runs again on every GET against the full stored JSONB; a basket-flatten-heavy run (one point per close, #350) yields a large curve, and an unbounded GET-time downsample would degrade the results page load.

text: The global sorted-unique timeline build MUST be O(total_candles) in time and memory-bounded even in the worst case of fully-misaligned per-symbol timestamps (near-zero shared timestamps → unique-point count approaching Σ candles); a deliberately misaligned/staggered-listing fixture asserts timeline construction time and memory stay within the linear bound and under the RSS ceiling (#30/#374).
rationale: #349 asserts CORRECTNESS of the merge-walk over misaligned timelines, but not the PERF/MEMORY of building the union timeline itself. With staggered listings and gaps, the global timeline can balloon toward Σcandles unique entries; an unbounded or super-linear sort/dedup there would breach the memory ceiling and setup-time budget exactly on the realistic ragged-data case.

text: The single-run pre-flight cost estimator (#224/#408) MUST be CALIBRATED against measured runs — its compute estimate (≈candles×scans) and cold-fetch estimate (≈ceil(missing/1000) pages) MUST bracket actual within a documented factor (e.g. ±50%) so it neither spuriously rejects feasible runs nor admits runs that then hit the 120 s timeout-kill; a test compares estimated vs actual on the canonical and wide fixtures and asserts the estimate brackets reality.
rationale: #224/#374/#408 REQUIRE an estimator and gate but never require it to be ACCURATE. An uncalibrated estimator is worse than none — it either blocks valid runs (false reject) or lets doomed runs consume a 120 s slot before being killed (the exact failure mode the estimator exists to prevent).

text: A conftest/autouse fixture MUST reset ALL process-global mutable caches (the _load_klines LRU, the in-process 1m drill cache, the Arrow/SoA hot cache, the raw→SoA conversion memo, and the derived-coarse resample memo) before AND after every test, and a meta-test MUST run the full golden+cache suite under randomized test order (e.g. pytest-randomly with ≥2 seeds) and assert byte-identical results and identical Bybit/Postgres call-count assertions — so a stale cross-test cache entry can never make a '0 Bybit calls' / parity test pass or fail based on execution order.
rationale: The cache fix introduces several module-level global caches that persist across backtests within one process; the test suite runs hundreds of backtests in one interpreter. Without an enforced reset, test A poisons test B (e.g. a warm entry makes B's cold '0-call' assertion pass spuriously, or a different-config rerun's LRU hit changes parity). No existing requirement (307 is runtime cross-run isolation; 314 is RSS) addresses test-suite-level cache hygiene or order-independence, which is the difference between a meaningful green gate and a vacuous one.

text: Every golden/parity test MUST HARD-FAIL (not skip, xfail, or vacuously pass) when its stored-oracle artifact is missing, empty, or unreadable, and a pre-suite guard test MUST assert that every expected oracle file referenced by the battery exists and is non-empty before any comparison runs; a meta-test MUST verify that deleting/truncating an oracle turns the corresponding golden test RED rather than green.
rationale: A comparator that treats an absent oracle as 'nothing to compare' silently converts the strongest gate into a no-op — exactly the failure mode that lets an optimization phase ship a regression behind a green CI. Requirement 465 guards pytest-marker silent skips and a collection floor, and 347 hash-pins oracle CONTENT, but neither guarantees the test fails when the oracle FILE is absent at compare time.

text: The ≥90% line+branch coverage gate MUST explicitly include backtest_metrics.py (compute_all_metrics: Sharpe/Sortino/max-drawdown/profit_factor/expectancy/buy&hold) and trading_rules.py (the shared SSOT for sizing/TP-SL/slippage/liquidation/fees/trailing/breakeven that RC-11 reimplements in nopython), not only backtest_engine.py / backtest_service.py / kline_cache_service.py (req 200) and the new SoA/numba/manifest/columnar/sweep modules (req 303).
rationale: These two files are the most parity-critical numeric surfaces — metrics drive the path-dependent ratios the optimizer ranks on, and trading_rules is the SSOT being re-expressed in numba — yet both are absent from the enumerated coverage-gate module lists (200/303). A drift in an untested metric or rule helper would corrupt every result while coverage still reports green.

text: The open-book Σ uPnL portfolio-equity reduction MUST use a summation algorithm that reproduces the legacy left-to-right sequential Python sum bit-for-bit (NOT numpy's pairwise/tree summation, which reorders adds even with fastmath disabled), and a fixture placing the basket equity within float epsilon of an EQUITY_DROP/RISE/close_on_profit threshold MUST assert the reduction order does not flip which equity rule fires versus the Decimal oracle.
rationale: Requirement 65 forbids fastmath and demands deterministic order, but np.sum / np.add.reduce use pairwise summation that associates floats differently from the legacy sequential loop — a distinct reordering not implied by 'no fastmath'. On a basket-equity threshold this can flip a close decision (a discrete outcome), violating the byte-identical guarantee on portfolio configs. This must be pinned and tested at the threshold boundary.

text: A meta-test MUST calibrate the oracle COMPARATOR's sensitivity for continuous money/price fields: injecting a synthetic deviation just BELOW the defined epsilon (<1% cap) MUST pass and a deviation just ABOVE it MUST fail, and injecting any change to a DISCRETE field (trade count, side, close_reason, exit-bar index, opened-position set) MUST fail at zero tolerance — proving the tolerance is neither so loose it masks a real 1% regression nor so tight it false-fails on benign last-bit rounding.
rationale: Requirement 346 proves the comparator fails on ANY field perturbation and 486/483 define the epsilon/discrete-vs-continuous policy, but nothing tests the comparator AT the epsilon boundary. An incorrectly implemented tolerance (wrong epsilon, abs-vs-relative confusion, applied to a discrete field) would pass 346 yet silently admit a sub-epsilon-rounded 1.5% regression or false-fail on a 0.0001% benign diff.

text: The drill-down sub-bar interval selected for EACH coarse simulation_interval (which fine interval 15m/1h/4h drills into — e.g. 4h→1m vs 4h→5m) MUST be explicitly pinned and oracle-frozen, with a fixture per non-5m coarse interval asserting the optimized engine drills at the identical sub-granularity as legacy and produces byte-identical intrabar fills.
rationale: Requirement 358 freezes coarse-interval runs with drill OFF, and 46-49 pin 5m→1m drill mechanics, but the drill SUB-INTERVAL chosen for each coarse base (and whether it is always 1m or interval-dependent) is unpinned. The SoA/columnar refactor that derives coarse bars from a fine base (req 116) is exactly where the drill granularity mapping could silently change, altering which sub-bar resolves a both-levels-in-bar ambiguity.

text: The async/parallel non-determinism-prone tests (sweep equivalence #207, fault-isolation #357, crash-resume #306, rank-stability #317, single-flight coalescing #246, semaphore-bound concurrency) MUST be executed N≥10 times in a dedicated CI flakiness lane (optionally under randomized task-scheduling jitter) and assert byte-identical pass/results on every iteration, so a timing- or completion-order-dependent flake cannot intermittently green-light a real ordering bug.
rationale: These tests depend on asyncio.gather / ProcessPool completion order, which is inherently nondeterministic; a single pass (as 317 specifies) can succeed by luck. A repeat-N flakiness gate is the standard QA control for concurrency tests and is absent — without it, an order-dependent rank or persistence bug surfaces only intermittently in production sweeps.

text: Every numeric tolerance/epsilon constant used by the parity gates (the <1% continuous-field epsilon, the Decimal reconciliation tolerance, the progress-overhead %, the per-stage budget %s, the benchmark relative-delta threshold and absolute floor) MUST be defined as named committed constants and hash-pinned by a meta-test (like the builder-default pin #466 and oracle pin #347), so a phase cannot quietly LOOSEN a threshold to make its own regression pass.
rationale: 347 prevents re-freezing the oracle and 466 pins fixture-builder defaults, but the TOLERANCES that decide pass/fail are themselves editable knobs. Widening epsilon from 1% to 5%, or the perf delta from 5% to 25%, is a one-line change that defeats every gate while leaving oracles and builders untouched — it must be guarded by the same can't-edit-without-review discipline.

text: A single side×outcome sign-convention golden fixture MUST exercise all four quadrants (winning-long, losing-long, winning-short, losing-short) plus a liquidation and a funding-receive in one frozen oracle, asserting the sign of every money/excursion field per quadrant: pnl signed correctly, entry_fee/exit_fee always positive, funding_paid sign per side, mfe ≥ 0 and mae ≤ 0 (in the documented convention), so a SoA/numba helper that drops or flips a side_sign is caught regardless of magnitude tolerance.
rationale: Sign errors are the classic regression when re-expressing PnL/excursion math over signed SoA arrays, and they can stay within a <1% magnitude tolerance check while being catastrophically wrong on the opposite-direction trade. Existing fixtures (310 short-receives-funding, 346 field perturbation) don't systematically assert the full side×outcome sign matrix in one oracle, leaving e.g. losing-short pnl-sign or short-MAE convention unpinned.

text: The EXACT TEXT of every operational warning string the engine/service emits (max_same_sector_not_enforced, the signals_no_kline summary, partial-data / degradation / fallback warnings) MUST be frozen in an oracle and asserted byte-identical post-refactor, because ops dashboards and the frontend may key on these literals — verified by a test that fails if a warning string is reworded, not merely dropped or reordered (which #370/#205 already cover).
rationale: Requirements 205 (warning emitted) and 370 (warning survives persistence and ordering) protect presence and order but not the literal text. A refactor that rephrases 'max_same_sector_not_enforced' or the signals_no_kline summary breaks any consumer matching the string, a silent operational regression invisible to magnitude/parity gates.

text: The canonical 90d×50sym benchmark fixture input (klines + scheduled-scan signals) MUST be REGENERABLE byte-identically from a compact committed deterministic generator/seed (not a large committed binary blob), the regeneration MUST be covered by a test asserting it reproduces the exact committed fixture, and the on-disk committed fixture footprint MUST stay within a documented repo-size budget (compressed) — so the perf/parity suites are offline-reproducible (#294) without bloating the repository.
rationale: Requirement 294 mandates a deterministic, offline, committed fixture but leaves open whether it is a compact regenerable seed or a large recorded blob. A ~1.3M-row recorded snapshot would bloat the repo and become un-reviewable; a generator-from-seed keeps it auditable and lets reviewers verify the data, but only if a test proves the generator reproduces the committed bytes and a size budget is enforced.

text: The oracle-regeneration script (#183) MUST itself be covered by an end-to-end test that: runs the script, then runs the full golden gate against the freshly produced oracles and asserts it passes (the script yields self-consistent oracles), and asserts the script REFUSES to overwrite oracles unless the engine git SHA stamped in each artifact is updated/bumped — so the regen path cannot silently emit an oracle that disagrees with the engine or skip the SHA-provenance stamp.
rationale: 183 describes the regen script's required properties (stamp SHA, forbid hand-edits) but no test exercises the script. An un-tested regen path can drift (produce oracles the gate rejects, or stamp a stale SHA), and a future maintainer regenerating after a legitimate change needs proof the script enforces the SHA-bump discipline rather than silently re-freezing wrong output.

text: A cooperative-cancel OR 120s timeout firing DURING the Phase-B 1m drill-down fetch (mid asyncio.gather of per-bar 1m windows) MUST release the drill-fetch concurrency semaphore, leave the in-process 1m drill cache with no torn/partial bar entry, and persist NO 1m data to kline_cache/coverage; a test MUST force a cancel/timeout inside the drill fetch and assert the next run reads byte-identical klines, the semaphore permit count is fully restored, and the 1m cache contains only complete bars.
rationale: Requirements 208/242 cover cancel/timeout cleanup for the engine and the 5m LRU, but the Phase-B 1m drill path is a separate in-flight fetch (bounded gather + in-memory-only 1m cache, never store_klines). A cancel mid-gather is a distinct cleanup site that could leak a semaphore permit (degrading all later drills) or cache a half-fetched bar — neither is covered.

text: Re-running a run_id that previously FAILED or TIMED OUT (after the cause is fixed) MUST produce a clean, correct result with NO orphan rows from the failed attempt: delete-before-insert MUST purge any partial backtest_trades and equity_curve, and the results upsert ON CONFLICT(run_id) MUST fully overwrite — verified by a test that fails a run, retries the same run_id to success, and asserts the persisted trades/results/equity_curve equal the standalone oracle with zero leftover rows.
rationale: Requirement 161 proves re-persisting a SUCCESSFUL run is idempotent and 242/299/363 assert a failed run leaves clean state, but the fail→fix→retry-same-id transition (the common operator workflow) is untested. If the failed attempt left partial trades or a stale equity_curve and the retry's delete-before-insert keyed differently, the rerun could splice old+new rows into a corrupt, non-reconciling result.

text: The three-way reconciliation cross-check (Σ trade.pnl == net_profit == final_equity − starting_capital) MUST accumulate Σ trade.pnl in Decimal/exact arithmetic over the RECORDED (already-quantized) per-trade values — never in float64 — and a trade-heavy fixture (thousands of small-pnl closes) MUST assert that a float accumulation would drift past tolerance while the Decimal accumulation reconciles exactly, so the CHECK ITSELF cannot false-pass or false-fail from its own rounding.
rationale: 66/184/352 mandate the reconciliation 'within a Decimal tolerance' but don't pin that the SUM be accumulated exactly. If the cross-check sums float pnls, accumulation error on a long deep run can exceed the very tolerance it's testing — masking a real fee/funding drift or false-failing a correct run. The check must be exact over quantized recorded money to have teeth at depth.

text: A general NO-LOOK-AHEAD guard test MUST mutate every candle strictly AFTER the current decision point (zero/NaN/scramble future OHLC beyond each scan_time and beyond each bar under evaluation) and assert ZERO change to trades, close_reasons, fills, and the ordered equity_curve versus the unmutated oracle — proving the SoA global-timeline + searchsorted + never-resetting merge-walk pointers never read a future index for any sizing/mark/entry/exit/equity/filter decision, beyond the entry-next-bar-open check of #62.
rationale: The global sorted timeline with shared per-symbol pointers is the single most likely place for future-bar leakage to sneak in (a pointer landing one index ahead, a searchsorted side error, a mark seeded from a not-yet-closed candle). 62 guards only the entry-fill bar; a comprehensive future-mutation invariant is the canonical, decisive test that the refactor preserves causality across ALL rules — and look-ahead is the cardinal backtest correctness sin.

text: The per-run structured stats record (cache hit/miss + sealed/negative-cache counts #264, storage-tier provenance #280, candle-eval throughput #510, per-stage timing #39, Bybit/Postgres call counts, active-flag+SHA fingerprint #278/#318) MUST be pinned by a SCHEMA-SNAPSHOT contract test asserting the full field set, names, and types — so dropping, renaming, or retyping any observability field fails CI, exactly like the request-schema snapshot guard (#460).
rationale: Requirements 264/280/510 each assert an individual field is PRESENT with a true value, but no test pins the FULL record shape, so a refactor can silently drop or rename a field that a prod dashboard/alert depends on (e.g. the sealed-day-refetch counter of #279) without any gate noticing. Observability payloads need the same drift-protection contract test the request/result contracts already have.

text: A degenerate-excursion golden fixture MUST freeze MFE/MAE for the boundary trades the bulk fixtures miss: a same-bar entry==exit trade (entry next-bar-open and exit in that same bar), a trade that is NEVER adverse (MAE at its documented floor, e.g. 0), and a trade that is NEVER favorable (MFE at its floor), asserting the per-bar-before-exit MFE/MAE update rule (#67) yields the legacy values for each — so a refactor cannot alter the excursion sentinel on a one-bar or monotonic trade.
rationale: 67/331/346 cover MFE/MAE update timing and comparator sensitivity but not the degenerate excursion cases where the high/low-before-exit logic has only one bar (entry==exit) or never crosses into profit/loss. These are precisely the off-by-one / initialization edges (does the entry bar count toward MFE/MAE? what's the floor for a monotonic trade?) most likely to drift in the SoA rewrite and least likely to be exercised by ordinary multi-bar fixtures.

text: When more than _MAX_CONCURRENT runs are submitted, the surplus run MUST either queue with a bounded observable wait or be rejected with an actionable capacity message, and the 120s _TIMEOUT_SECONDS clock MUST start at execution-start (slot acquisition) NEVER at submission, so queue wait never consumes a run's compute budget; verified by a test that submits 4 runs and asserts the 4th's 120s budget excludes its queue wait and no run is timeout-killed for time spent queued.
rationale: Existing reqs cover the slot counter reset (#450), the worker/thread CPU budget (#211), and pool exhaustion (#373) but never define what happens to the (_MAX_CONCURRENT+1)th request nor whether queue wait counts against the 120s cap — a real risk that a queued run is killed before it ever computes.

text: The ThreadPoolExecutor backing run_in_executor for the synchronous engine MUST have an explicitly-configured max_workers >= _MAX_CONCURRENT (so runs never queue at the executor below the concurrency limit) coordinated with the pinned numba/BLAS thread caps so executor_workers x per-run kernel threads <= the CPU budget; verified by a test asserting the executor pool size is explicitly set, >= _MAX_CONCURRENT, and does not oversubscribe cores.
rationale: #211/#212 pin numba/OMP/BLAS pools abstractly, but the FastAPI run_in_executor pool (default min(32,cpu+4)) that actually hosts the sync engine is unaddressed — if left default it can both oversubscribe (executor x numba threads) and silently cap concurrency below _MAX_CONCURRENT.

text: A heaviest-config benchmark fixture (drilldown ON + every close rule armed + regime + mean-reversion + a deep concurrent open book) MUST carry its own committed warm-latency and peak-RSS budget and run in the per-phase regression gate, so the latency/memory targets are proven on the most expensive engine path and not only on the light canonical drill-off/drill-on fixtures.
rationale: #1 is drill-off, #2 is drill-on, #389 is high-P only — none combines ALL features into the worst-case per-candle work path, so the headline budgets could pass on a cheap config while the heaviest real config silently blows the cap.

text: A peak-RSS ceiling MUST be defined and gated for the canonical drilldown-ON run (the #2 path), explicitly budgeting the in-process 1m drill cache + fine SoA arrays on top of the 5m working set, distinct from the drill-OFF <=1GB ceiling (#30); verified by an RSS test on the drill-on fixture.
rationale: #30 bounds the drill-OFF run and #216 bounds sweeps, but no single-run RSS ceiling accounts for the additional 1m drill cache + fine arrays that drilldown-ON allocates, so enabling drilldown could breach memory undetected.

text: A COLD drilldown-ON latency budget (1m sub-bars not yet cached, JIT warm) MUST be defined and gated on the canonical fixture, distinct from the warm drill-on <20s (#2) and the drill-off cold-compute budget (#509), since cold drill adds bounded narrow-window 1m fetches; verified by a cold-drill test.
rationale: The cold first-run drill path (1m cache empty) has no latency budget — #2 is warm, #509/#214 are drill-off/network-cold — leaving the common post-deploy first-drill-run latency ungated.

text: The cold (first-run) fetch of instrument_info (qty_step/min_qty/tick_size) and symbol_lifecycle across N symbols MUST be a single batched call or semaphore-bounded concurrent fan-out (not N serial awaits), with wall-time ~ ceil(N/cap)xper-fetch; verified by a test asserting cold instrument_info/lifecycle load latency is sub-linear in symbol count.
rationale: #393 only guarantees 0 calls on a WARM rerun; the COLD instrument_info/lifecycle fetch latency is unaddressed and, if serial over 50 symbols, adds seconds of fixed setup before the engine even starts.

text: The Buy&Hold BTC baseline kline fetch/compute MUST be issued concurrently with the engine pass (or served from the warm cache before it), adding no serial latency to the run; verified by a test asserting end-to-end wall-time with B&H enabled ~ wall-time with it disabled (it overlaps) and that a cold B&H fetch does not serialize ahead of the engine.
rationale: #238/#335/#355 cover B&H cache-routing, parity, and warm 0-calls, but never require it to be OFF the critical path — a cold serial BTC fetch+compute before/after the engine would add avoidable latency to every run.

text: The single-run pre-flight cost/feasibility check (#224/#408) MUST execute BEFORE acquiring a _MAX_CONCURRENT slot (a rejected oversized run consumes no slot) and MUST itself complete within a small bounded budget (e.g. <100ms, manifest-index-backed, independent of history size); verified by a test asserting a rejected run never occupied a slot and the check latency stays flat as coverage/manifest rows grow.
rationale: #224/#408/#517 cover the estimate's accuracy but not its placement (before slot acquisition) or its own latency — a slow or post-slot pre-flight would either burn a concurrency slot on a doomed run or add measurable per-run overhead.

text: The 120s hard cap MUST be configurable per run-class so the WIDE capacity point (e.g. 200sym x 365d, #374) is either assigned a higher explicit cap or rejected pre-flight, while the canonical class keeps the <=120s default; verified by a test asserting the cap is configurable and the wide fixture either completes under its assigned cap or is pre-flight-rejected with an actionable message, and is NEVER silently timeout-killed mid-run.
rationale: Discovery explicitly says the cap may be raised, and #374 mandates the wide point be supported via batched loading — but a fixed 120s cap would kill a legitimately large run mid-flight; the cap's configurability/per-class semantics are unspecified.

text: For coarse-interval (15m/1h/4h) runs the one-time fine->coarse resample (#405) MUST be O(fine_candles) and bounded so a coarse-interval run's end-to-end wall-time is <= the equivalent 5m run on the same window (the resample cost must not outweigh the fewer simulation bars); verified by a test gating the resample stage budget and asserting coarse runs are not slower than the 5m run on the same window.
rationale: #405 memoizes the resample once-per-session and #404 covers its parity, but there is no throughput budget ensuring the derive-from-fine cost doesn't make a 4h run (which has 48x fewer sim bars) paradoxically slower than 5m.

text: The serial portion of a sweep (shared kline pre-load + SoA build + global-timeline build, #505) MUST be a bounded fraction of total sweep wall-time (e.g. <15% on the canonical 100-combo sweep) so parallel speedup is not Amdahl-capped below min(M,concurrency); verified by a test measuring the serial-setup fraction and asserting it stays under the documented bound.
rationale: #505 requires the shared setup to run once before fan-out (inherently serial) and #29 sets a speedup ratio, but nothing bounds the serial-setup fraction — a heavy serial pre-load would Amdahl-cap the achievable parallel speedup regardless of core count.

text: A K-combo sweep MUST surface completed-combo results progressively (each combo persisted and queryable the moment it finishes, not all-or-nothing at sweep end) with a bounded time-to-first-result ~ shared-setup + one combo; verified by a test asserting the first combo's result is observable well before the full sweep completes.
rationale: #306 covers crash-resume correctness but not the throughput/UX requirement that combos become observable as they complete with a gated time-to-first-result — important so a long sweep isn't an opaque multi-minute black box.

text: Every production run/sweep-combo MUST emit an alertable structured signal when its end-to-end wall-time exceeds its pre-flight-estimated envelope (or a configured soft-SLO) — distinct from the 120s hard-kill and the Bybit-call counter (#279) — so gradual real-world latency regressions are caught before they reach the hard cap; verified by a test asserting the breach signal fires on a deliberately-slow mocked run and stays silent on a nominal run.
rationale: #279 alerts on sealed-day refetch and #510 emits throughput, but there is no production latency-SLO breach signal — a slow drift toward the 120s cap would be invisible until runs actually start timing out.

text: The per-stage timing breakdown (#39/#481) MUST be persisted as additive nullable fields on the backtest result row (not only logged), so a latency incident on a STORED run is diagnosable without re-running; verified by a test asserting the stored result carries the stage breakdown and that pre-existing rows with NULL stage-timings still load.
rationale: #39/#481 require recording/attributing per-stage timings but only as logs/in-run metrics; persisting them on the result row (additively, NULL-tolerant for old rows) is what makes a post-hoc latency triage of a specific stored run possible.

text: After a 120s timeout-kill or a cooperative cancel, the NEXT run on the same long-lived process MUST meet its nominal latency budget (no residual slowdown from un-freed allocations, partially-inserted cache entries, or hot-cache fragmentation); verified by a test that runs a killed/cancelled run immediately followed by a clean run and asserts the clean run's wall-time is within the nominal budget.
rationale: #242/#226 assert post-kill CORRECTNESS (uncorrupted cache, released RSS) but not that the next run's LATENCY is unaffected — leftover fragmentation or half-evicted entries could degrade the subsequent run without corrupting data.

text: Before fan-out, the sweep orchestrator MUST estimate shared-klines + concurrency x per-combo working set against the RSS ceiling and AUTO-THROTTLE effective concurrency (or reject) when it would exceed RAM, rather than OOM-killing mid-sweep; verified by a test with a constrained ceiling asserting concurrency is reduced and peak RSS stays under the ceiling.
rationale: #216 asserts RSS stays flat across K and #374 covers single-run pre-flight, but no requirement makes the sweep proactively reduce its OWN concurrency under a memory ceiling — a wide sweep at high concurrency could OOM the long-lived server before any per-combo bound engages.

text: The persist stage (trades delete + batched insert, equity_curve full-JSONB serialize + LTTB, metrics upsert) MUST complete within a defined ABSOLUTE budget on a trade-heavy canonical run (e.g. <3s), not merely a bounded share (#220); verified by a high-trade-count fixture asserting persist wall-time stays under the absolute budget.
rationale: #220 only bounds persist as a relative SHARE of end-to-end time, which on a very fast engine could still be seconds of absolute latency; an absolute persist budget on a trade-heavy run is needed so persistence can't dominate a sub-10s target.

text: The int64-epoch and float64-OHLC SoA arrays MUST be C-contiguous (asserted via numpy .flags) so the numba/nogil kernel gets zero-copy cache-local views, and the one-time per-(symbol,interval,range) SoA build MUST complete within a defined absolute build-time budget on the canonical fixture; verified by tests asserting array contiguity and the build-time budget.
rationale: #437 mandates int64/float64 zero-copy types but not memory CONTIGUITY (non-contiguous arrays force numba copies and wreck cache locality), and #218 memoizes the build but sets no absolute time budget for the build itself.

text: The candle-eval throughput floors (>=100k/sec pure-Python #14, >=5M/sec njit #15) MUST also be asserted on the HEAVY config path (all close rules + deep book, where per-candle work is larger) with its own documented lower floor, so a config-light measurement cannot mask a heavy-path throughput regression; verified by a candle-evals/sec test on the heavy fixture.
rationale: #14/#15 set throughput floors without pinning the config under test — measuring them on a trivial single-rule config would report a flattering number while the realistic heavy path (more per-candle branches) regresses undetected.

text: The three in-process cache tiers (5m _load_klines LRU #102, 1m drill cache #228, Arrow/SoA hot cache #217) MUST be governed by a SINGLE unified process-wide memory budget with coordinated eviction, so they cannot each independently grow to their own ceiling and collectively breach the process RSS ceiling; verified by a test asserting the summed resident cache bytes stay under the unified budget under combined 5m+1m+hot pressure.
rationale: #102, #228, and #217 each cap a tier independently, but three independent ceilings sum to more than the #30/#374 process RSS ceiling allows — a unified cross-tier budget is needed so the caches don't collectively OOM.

text: A degenerate run (zero admitted signals, zero symbols, or an empty window) MUST short-circuit to near-zero wall-time (no full SoA build / timeline construction for no data) while still returning a real result with metrics.total_trades present-and-0 (#308); verified by a test asserting a zero-work run completes under a small fixed budget (e.g. <100ms warm).
rationale: #308 covers the CORRECTNESS of degenerate runs (present-and-0, real result not fallback) but not that they short-circuit cheaply — without a fast path a no-work run could still pay full SoA/timeline setup cost.

text: The CPU-bound Python-side work in the async loader — bucketing ANY($1) rows into per-symbol arrays and building the SoA/global timeline — MUST run off the asyncio event loop (in the executor or chunked with cooperative yields) so a large cold load cannot block the shared FastAPI loop and the live scanner/auto-trade coroutines; verified by a test asserting a probe coroutine's latency stays within bound while a large kline load + SoA build is in progress.
rationale: #375 isolates the ENGINE (which runs in the threadpool) but the loader's Python-side row-bucketing and SoA/timeline construction typically execute in the async service method — if not offloaded, a wide cold load blocks the event loop and starves live trading coroutines, a gap #375 does not cover.

text: The funding-amount BASIS must be pinned bit-identically: funding_paid = notional × funding_rate_fixed_pct/8h-period, where the notional price basis (entry_base_price vs current mark vs un-slipped vs slipped, and qty×price vs locked_margin×leverage) MUST match the legacy engine exactly — a fixture holding a position across a funding boundary MUST freeze the precise funding magnitude (not just its sign/timing/dedupe), because reqs 55/310/339/365/487 pin WHEN/SIGN/COUNT/dedupe but never the price basis of the charge, and req 319's kernel differential harness omits funding entirely.
rationale: Funding basis price is the one funding sub-fact no requirement constrains; a refactor reimplementing funding in the SoA/numba kernel could silently change qty×entry vs qty×mark and stay within <1% on short holds while breaking the three-way reconciliation on long holds.

text: target_goal_type='profit_pct' early-stop granularity and basis MUST be pinned exactly as legacy (analog to req 338 for trade_count): pin WHICH equity the profit_pct target is measured against (realized net_profit_pct on wallet vs basket equity including open uPnL vs cycle equity), and WHEN it is evaluated (after each close / per-scan / per-candle), and whether the run halts mid-scan the instant the goal is hit or only after the current scan's signals finish — frozen by a fixture whose profit_pct goal is reached mid-scan, so the optimized engine opens neither one trade too many nor too few.
