rationale: Req 174 only adds a profit_pct fixture and 338 pins ONLY the trade_count granularity; the profit_pct evaluation basis/timing is an unpinned LIFETIME-early-stop surface that directly changes which is the last admitted trade.

text: Intra-scan SEQUENTIAL sizing depletion MUST be reproduced bit-identically: as each position opens within ONE scan, its locked_margin is added to Σ locked_margin and its carried uPnL updates sizing_capital for the NEXT signal in the SAME scan, so the qty of the k-th admitted signal reflects the (k−1) positions already opened in that scan — verified by a multi-signal single-scan fixture where same-scan ordering changes a later signal's qty, not merely whether it is rejected (req 360 covers only mid-scan REJECTION; req 61 covers cross-scan sizing).
rationale: sizing_capital = wallet + carried uPnL − Σ locked_margin and Σ locked_margin includes positions opened earlier in the same scan; the SoA refactor must preserve this within-scan running-balance latch or every multi-signal scan's later qty drifts.

text: backtest_end force-close ORDERING MUST be deterministic and oracle-frozen: when multiple positions remain open at end-of-run, pin the exact order they are flattened (insertion/open order vs symbol-index vs SoA-timeline order) and the resulting per-close equity_curve point order, since running-wallet and path-dependent metrics depend on it — a distinct mechanism from basket-flatten equity closes (req 350) and same-bar liq/TP/SL multi-exit (req 490), neither of which covers the terminal force-close path of req 68.
rationale: Req 68 pins THAT every position closes at backtest_end with reason backtest_end and emits a point, but not the ORDER; under SoA/parallel reordering the terminal multi-close order (and thus the equity-curve tail driving max_dd/Sharpe) could change.

text: The max_same_direction cap COUNT SCOPE MUST be pinned bit-identically: specify whether the cap is counted against the currently-open book, per-scan admissions only, or lifetime trades, evaluated on the POST-reverse trade side (req 235) — frozen by a paired admit/reject fixture where the (cap+1)-th same-direction signal is filtered, so the SoA/dict re-keying cannot change which signals the directional cap rejects.
rationale: Req 235 pins that max_same_direction is counted on the post-reverse side but leaves the count SCOPE unspecified; scope (open-book vs per-scan vs lifetime) is the decision-relevant part that determines which signals are dropped.

text: Per-scan (scan_entered, the max_trades counter) and LIFETIME (signals_entered, the target_goal counter) increment semantics MUST be pinned: a fixture MUST assert exactly which dispositions increment each counter — existing-symbol-deduped signals, margin/balance-rejected signals, and qty<min_qty-rejected signals — so the optimized engine's max_trades admission and target_goal early-stop fire at the identical signal, independent of dict/SoA iteration order.
rationale: target_goal LIFETIME early-stop (req 173/338) and per-scan max_trades both hinge on whether a non-opened signal still 'counts'; this counter-increment rule is path-dependent and currently unpinned (req 360 only asserts rejected signals don't appear as trades/perturb reconciliation, not their effect on the counters).

text: The batched scan_source signal + ScanContext loader (the O(1) load of req 377) MUST return signals BYTE-IDENTICAL to the legacy per-scan load — every field (symbol, side, score, confidence, signal_time, signal_price) preserved, grouped into byte-identical scans, in the identical stable first-signal-time scan order (req 237) — a parity analog of req 447 (which only covers kline ANY($1) bucketing); verified by diffing batched-load signals/scans against the legacy loader's output.
rationale: Req 377 is PERF-only (asserts query count) and 447 covers klines, but no requirement asserts the batched signal load is decision-identical; a reordered/dropped signal field or scan-grouping change would silently alter selection and scan-processing order.

text: The <1% relative tolerance MUST define an ABSOLUTE-floor fallback for near-zero oracle values: when an oracle field is at or near zero (pnl≈0, funding_paid=0 under funding_rate_model='none', mfe/mae≈0, excess_return≈0), the comparator MUST switch to an absolute epsilon (documented per-field) so a benign last-bit float difference on a ~0 field does not explode to a >100% relative deviation and false-fail the gate — verified by a fixture with legitimately-zero money fields asserting the gate passes under the absolute floor.
rationale: Reqs 74/199/483/486 define relative <1% and discrete/continuous split but never address the divide-by-near-zero denominator; without an absolute floor the tolerance test is numerically undefined exactly where money fields are zero.

text: The in-memory TRADE-LIST order as consumed by compute_all_metrics MUST be deterministic and oracle-matched for any order-dependent metric (e.g. max_consecutive_wins/losses, streak/run metrics): this trade-sequence order is distinct from equity_curve order (req 185) and the persisted/GET order (req 473), and a fixture MUST assert that reordering the trades list fed to compute_all_metrics changes such a metric, then assert the optimized engine feeds the legacy-identical order.
rationale: compute_all_metrics(trades, equity_curve, config) consumes the trades LIST; if any of the ~45 metrics computes consecutive-streaks over that list, SoA/parallel reordering of the trade list would change the metric even though the trade SET and equity_curve order are preserved.

text: The regime CLASSIFIER computation (the trend/ranging/volatile label derived from regime_volatile_atr and regime_trend_ema_dist_pct over the per-scan-time-built ScanContext) MUST be reproduced bit-identically by the refactor, with a regime-ACTIVE oracle freezing the classifier's deterministic output — distinct from req 236 which freezes the GATES/routing that CONSUME the label but not the label-producing math itself.
rationale: Because the backtest builds a fresh ScanContext per scan_time, the regime classifier runs inside the optimized path; routing parity (236) is only as good as the classifier input, which is currently unpinned.

text: The max_price_drift check MUST use the UN-slipped entry_base_price (the next-bar open used for sizing per req 60/61), not the slippage-adjusted fill, as the entry_fill term in drift=(entry_fill−signal_price)/signal_price — frozen by a fixture where a signal sits within slippage_bps of the drift threshold so a slipped-vs-un-slipped basis would flip admit/reject — so de-duplicating the entry-bar and drift scans (reqs 9/194) and the SoA refactor cannot shift which signals the drift filter rejects.
rationale: Req 332 pins drift semantics using the ambiguous term 'entry_fill'; whether drift uses the slipped or un-slipped price is decision-relevant near the threshold and must be resolved explicitly to keep the filter selection bit-identical.

text: The NON-OPTIMISTIC guarantee MUST be scoped explicitly to money/PnL/equity fields (drilled/fast-path money ≤ always-LTF oracle on ambiguous bars), while DERIVED RATIO metrics (Sharpe, Sortino, win_rate, profit_factor, expectancy) are bounded by ±epsilon in EITHER direction — because a strictly-more-pessimistic PnL can move a ratio up or down, so applying a one-sided ≤ check to ratio metrics would false-fail; a test MUST assert the gate enforces ≤ on money fields and two-sided epsilon on ratios.
rationale: Reqs 74/486 state non-optimistic on PnL but the gate must not be naively applied to ratio metrics where pessimism is non-monotonic; without this scoping a correct pessimistic drill could fail the non-optimistic assertion on Sharpe.

text: MFE/MAE per-side excursion extremes MUST be pinned bit-identically: MFE/MAE select the favorable/adverse bar extreme by side (long: MFE from bar high, MAE from bar low; short: MFE from bar low, MAE from bar high), updated every bar before exit checks (req 67) and from 5m extremes even when drilled (req 331) — a side-paired fixture MUST assert the long and short excursion-extreme selection is byte-identical to the oracle so the SoA kernel cannot transpose the high/low-by-side mapping.
rationale: Reqs 67/331/346 pin WHEN (every bar), GRANULARITY (5m), and deep-equality, but not the side-dependent high-vs-low selection formula; a transposed mapping in the reimplemented kernel would silently drift mfe/mae within deep-equality only if the comparator catches it, and the formula itself is unpinned.

text: Every input signal's DISPOSITION must reconcile to the oracle: each signal maps to exactly one outcome (admitted-as-trade / filtered-at-step-S / signals_no_kline / qty-undersized / margin-rejected), and the optimized engine's full per-signal disposition map (and any surfaced filter-funnel counts in summary/warnings) MUST equal the oracle's — a stronger invariant than 'admitted set matches' (req 69) that pins WHY each signal was dropped, so a refactor that admits the same trades but via a different rejection step (changing diagnostic counts/warnings) is caught.
rationale: Req 69 pins the admitted SET and chain ORDER and 370 pins that warnings survive, but not that EACH signal's rejection step/disposition is identical; the per-signal funnel feeds operator-facing counts and is a latent parity surface.

text: The position-age REFERENCE POINT and threshold INCLUSIVITY for the three hold-duration close rules — max_trade_duration_hours, breakeven_timeout_hours, mr_time_stop_minutes — MUST be pinned bit-identically (the age clock starts at the exact legacy reference: entry-fill bar open_time vs fill time vs scan_time, and the fire condition uses the legacy inclusive/exclusive boundary), verified by a boundary-straddling fixture per rule so a position fires on the identical bar — the duration-rule analog of max_signal_age (req 333).
rationale: Reqs 57/169/170/178 pin time-rule ORDER and freeze fixture values, and 479 pins TZ-independence, but no requirement pins the age-clock reference point + inclusivity for the hold-duration rules the way 333 does for signal age; an off-by-one in the age basis fires the close one bar early/late.

text: A per-phase CI regression gate MUST assert the NON-OPTIMISTIC inequality Σ(drilled/fast-path trade.pnl) ≤ Σ(always-LTF Decimal-oracle trade.pnl) on the drill and fast-path fixtures at EVERY phase (wired alongside the bit-identical gate of reqs 73/198), so a phase cannot introduce an optimistic shortcut that still lands within the <1% band — converting the property check of req 470 into a standing phase-over-phase gate.
rationale: Req 470 is a one-off property test and 74 states the guarantee, but no requirement wires a non-optimistic gate to run on every phase PR the way 198 wires the bit-identical gate; an optimistic regression under <1% would otherwise pass.

text: During the in-progress-backfill window (schema_version already advanced to 58 per req 412 but the deferred seal-backfill not yet complete) the runtime MUST treat every not-yet-sealed historic day as UNSEALED/refetchable (count-free predicate, correct-but-slower) and MUST NEVER raise, falsely report a range complete, or falsely seal; a separate backfill-completion marker (not schema_version) MUST gate any 'fully optimized / cache-complete' status, and the backfill MUST auto-resume idempotently to completion across reboots — verified by a test that boots with schema=58 + a half-finished backfill, asserts backtests run correctly on the partially-sealed manifest, and that a subsequent boot completes the remaining seals.
rationale: Req 412 mandates decoupling fast DDL from the long resumable backfill and a completion marker, but nothing pins the RUNTIME behavior nor auto-resume DURING the window where schema=58 yet seals are incomplete — the exact state a rolling deploy lingers in. A bug here either errors or (worse) treats unsealed days as sealed.

text: A backtest_results/backtest_runs row WRITTEN by new (v58) code — carrying extra optional metric keys in the result JSONB and populated fingerprint columns (engine SHA / flag-set / schema-in-effect, reqs 278/318/430) — MUST render correctly under rolled-back OLD code: the legacy SELECT projects only known columns and the legacy result renderer ignores unknown JSONB metric keys without error; verified by a test that persists a new-shape row, then loads+renders it through the legacy code path and GET endpoints.
rationale: Reqs 160/283/316 only cover the forward direction (new code reading OLD/NULL-field rows). The REVERSE — old code reading rows new code produced during the pre-rollback window — is the actual rollback hazard and is untested; an old renderer iterating metric keys or a strict column projection could break post-rollback.

text: Each spawned sweep ProcessPool worker process MUST warm the numba JIT in its pool initializer (alongside the once-shipped snapshot of req 27/RC-14) so combo-1 on every worker pays zero cold-compile inside the per-run/120s budget; a test MUST assert the worker initializer invokes JIT warm-up and that the first combo dispatched to a fresh worker does not incur compile latency.
rationale: Reqs 33/143/271 warm the JIT only in the MAIN process at boot. On the Linux prod speedup path each ProcessPool worker is a SEPARATE interpreter that re-imports and would JIT-compile on its own first combo — an unbudgeted 1–10s cold compile per worker that the main-process warm-up cannot cover.

text: Thread-pool sizing for numba (NUMBA_NUM_THREADS), BLAS/OpenMP (OMP/OPENBLAS), DuckDB (PRAGMA threads) and Polars (POLARS_MAX_THREADS), and the sweep concurrency cap, MUST be derived from the CONTAINER cgroup CPU quota (or an explicit config), NOT os.cpu_count()/host core count — which a container sees as the host's full core count and would oversubscribe; a test MUST assert that under a simulated cgroup CPU limit the effective Σ thread caps track the cgroup quota, not the host.
rationale: Reqs 212/382 explicitly bound nested parallelism to 'cpu_count', but in a containerized deploy os.cpu_count() returns HOST cores, not the cgroup limit — a classic oversubscription bug that would blow the concurrency/latency budgets on the real deploy target. This corrects the assumption baked into 212/382.

text: The v58 seal-backfill's per-day seal decision MUST use the IDENTICAL completion-frontier computation as the runtime loader — same per-(symbol,interval) floor(now_utc − skew_margin)/T*T including the skew lag of req 249 — so no day can be sealed-by-backfill yet classified forming-at-runtime (or the reverse); verified by a test asserting the backfill and the runtime frontier helper are the same code path and agree on the seal/forming boundary day for a fixed clock.
rationale: Req 130 seals days with end<frontier and req 249 mandates a runtime skew_margin, but nothing ties the BACKFILL's frontier math to the RUNTIME's. If they diverge (e.g. backfill omits skew_margin), a day sealed by the migration could be treated as still-forming by the loader — inconsistent seal state straight out of the migration.

text: The v58 ADD COLUMN statements on the hot, live-shared kline_cache_coverage MUST acquire their ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock under a bounded lock_timeout with retry/backoff so the DDL cannot sit in (or block behind) the live scanner/auto-trade coverage-upsert lock queue beyond a defined budget during a deploy; verified by a test that holds a concurrent coverage-write transaction and asserts the v58 DDL times out/retries rather than indefinitely blocking live writes.
rationale: Req 127 ensures the constant-default ADD COLUMN avoids a table rewrite and 431 handles index CONCURRENTLY and 416 handles the backfill UPDATE — but the ADD COLUMN's brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on a HOT shared table (queuing with live trading writes) is a distinct deploy hazard with no bounded-lock_timeout requirement.

text: In a multi-worker / multi-pod deploy the DEFERRED seal-backfill (req 412) MUST be elected to run on exactly ONE worker via advisory lock or atomic compare-and-set on the backfill-completion marker, with the other workers neither blocking on it nor launching duplicate backfills; verified by a test spawning ≥3 concurrent post-DDL boots and asserting the backfill body executes once while all workers reach readiness.
rationale: Req 265 covers advisory-lock protection of the in-transaction DDL, but the backfill is explicitly DECOUPLED and async (412) — a different mechanism that N uvicorn workers would each try to start. Nothing currently elects a single backfill runner for the deferred phase.

text: This entire optimization MUST ship as an EXPAND-ONLY (parallel-change) migration: v58 only ADDs columns/tables (no DROP/RENAME/RETYPE) so pre-v58 and v58 code run correctly side-by-side throughout the rolling-deploy window, and any eventual contraction (removing legacy count-based coverage columns/paths) is DEFERRED to a separate future migration applied only after all pre-v58 pods have drained; verified by a CI guard asserting the v58 statement set contains zero destructive DDL and that the documented deploy strategy is expand-then-(later)-contract.
rationale: Req 154 asserts v58 'only adds' and 464 tests overlap, but the DEPLOYMENT-STRATEGY contract (expand-only now, contraction deferred post-drain) is not stated as a requirement — it is the discipline that makes the rolling-deploy/rollback window provably safe and prevents a future cleanup from shipping a breaking contraction prematurely.

text: Deploy promotion MUST be gated on a PRODUCTION post-deploy verification (not only unit tests): the first real warm canonical rerun after the v58 deploy MUST be observed via the per-run Bybit sealed-day-refetch counter (req 279) to issue 0 sealed-day refetches, and the deploy automation MUST fail/alert if any sealed-day refetch occurs — catching a real-world re-emergence of the re-download bug in prod; verified by a test that the promotion hook reads the counter and blocks/alerts on a non-zero sealed-day refetch.
rationale: Req 279 only asserts the counter increments on a MOCKED refetch in a test, and 429 covers migration-FAILURE alerting. Neither makes the actual headline bug-fix (0 refetch on a real warm rerun) a post-deploy production gate, so a subtle seal/frontier mismatch that passes CI but refetches in prod would ship undetected.

text: symbol_lifecycle population/refresh from exchange instrument info MUST run as a SEPARATE idempotent, non-blocking background/startup task that never delays the v58 migration or the readiness/liveness probe; while the table is empty or partially populated the loader MUST behave correctly (absent row ⇒ listing=−inf/delist=+inf, fetch-everything, per req 272) with only reduced negative-cache benefit; verified by a test asserting readiness is reached before lifecycle population completes and behavior is correct mid-population.
rationale: Reqs 259/272/393/400 cover lifecycle semantics and that empty=fetch-everything, but none requires population to be a non-blocking background task decoupled from migration/readiness — leaving open a deploy where lifecycle discovery (many exchange calls) blocks boot or the readiness probe and triggers pod kills.

text: Because the optimization ships across a multi-week, multi-PR phased rollout where unrelated parallel branches have historically grabbed the same migration int (discovery §9: collisions happened twice), the v58 (and any subsequent v59+) version int MUST be re-verified as the next free, non-colliding, strictly-increasing int at EACH phase's merge, and CI MUST fail on any duplicate or out-of-order version introduced by ANY merge into the _MIGRATIONS list — not just at a single point in time; verified by a CI guard that detects a duplicate/regressed version int.
rationale: Req 124 asserts no duplicate v58 at one snapshot, but the phased rollout spans weeks during which other branches merge; the across-time collision risk that the discovery doc explicitly flags ('twice before') needs a standing CI guard, not a one-shot assertion.

text: Per-process and aggregate cache budgets and the RSS ceilings (reqs 30/216/374/445) MUST be derived from the CONTAINER memory LIMIT (cgroup), not host RAM: Σ(N workers × per-process hot/LRU/1m-drill cache) plus SoA/position-book working set MUST stay under the cgroup memory limit so the orchestrator never OOM-kills the long-lived process; a test MUST assert the effective cache/RSS ceiling tracks a simulated cgroup memory limit (below host RAM) and that adding a worker does not push projected resident bytes past it.
rationale: Req 445 sizes caches against 'the host RAM budget', but a container is OOM-killed at its cgroup memory LIMIT (typically far below host RAM). Tying budgets to host RAM rather than the cgroup limit invites orchestrator OOM-kills on the real deploy target — corrects the assumption in 445/30/216/374.

text: Each optimization phase MUST be independently rollback-able by flipping ONLY its own flag, without reverting earlier phases AND without forfeiting earlier phases' realized speedups: a test MUST disable phase-N's flag while phases 1..N-1 stay enabled and assert both oracle-identical results AND that the retained engine throughput/wall-time still reflects phases 1..N-1 (e.g. numba off still keeps the SoA/searchsorted gain, columnar off still keeps batched-loader gains).
rationale: Reqs 147/313 test per-flag toggling for CORRECTNESS and 142 tests numba-off meeting budget, but none asserts that rolling back a later phase PRESERVES the PERFORMANCE of earlier phases — the operational expectation when an operator disables one misbehaving optimization without a full revert.

text: The durable columnar cache directory (Parquet/Feather/Arrow) MUST be operator-configurable via env var and validated at boot for existence, writability, and a minimum free-space budget on the deploy target's mounted volume; on failure the read path MUST degrade to Postgres with a structured warning and the boot self-check (req 164) MUST report the columnar tier as degraded — never crash boot or silently write to an unintended path; verified by a test pointing the cache dir at an unwritable/insufficient-space location and asserting boot completes, reads serve from Postgres, and the degraded status is reported.
rationale: Req 285 covers RUNTIME degradation on a full/unwritable cache dir, but not BOOT-time validation, env-configurability of the path (essential for a container volume mount), or a free-space precheck — so a misconfigured volume mount on the deploy target would be discovered only mid-run rather than flagged as degraded at startup.

text: The cross-process warm-rerun 0-Bybit-call guarantee (#386) MUST be explicitly reconciled with the in-memory-only 1m drill cache (#104/#105/#380): because 1m sub-bars are never persisted to any durable tier, a drill-enabled config rerun in a FRESH process would re-issue 1m Bybit fetches and silently violate #386. The system MUST either (a) scope #386's 0-call guarantee to exclude 1m drill fetches as a documented accepted divergence, OR (b) persist sealed (immutable, past-frontier) 1m drilled sub-bars to a durable drill-specific store SEPARATE from the 5m coverage table so cross-process drill reruns also issue 0 calls; verified by a fresh-process drill-config rerun test asserting the chosen contract (0 calls, or the documented-and-bounded 1m-only call count) and that 5m coverage is never poisoned either way.
rationale: #386 asserts a fresh-process warm rerun issues 0 Bybit calls served from mmap'd Feather + manifest, but #104/#105 mandate the 1m drill cache be in-memory-only and never persisted — so a drill config's cross-process rerun necessarily refetches 1m, contradicting #386. No requirement resolves this tension; it must be pinned and tested or it will surface as a real-world warm-rerun cache miss on every drill config.

text: Evicting an entry from the in-process Arrow/SoA hot cache or _load_klines LRU (#102/#388) MUST NOT free or invalidate a zero-copy array/slice VIEW still held by an in-flight sweep combo or concurrent run: eviction removes only the dict entry, while the underlying numpy/Arrow buffer stays alive via refcount until every borrowing view drops, so a combo mid-computation can never observe freed/garbage memory or a torn buffer; verified by a test that holds a zero-copy slice of a cached frame, forces eviction of that entry under memory pressure, and asserts the held slice still reads byte-identical data and no segfault/use-after-free occurs.
rationale: #403 covers the on-DISK analog (GC must not delete an mmap'd file out from under a reader, SIGBUS safety) and #388 covers whole-entry eviction reload behavior, but nothing covers the IN-MEMORY zero-copy view: dict-evicting an Arrow/numpy frame while a combo holds a zero-copy slice is a use-after-free hazard the SoA + shared-snapshot design (#27/#217) makes likely. This is a distinct memory-safety guarantee.

text: Runtime sealing of newly-closed days during a cold run MUST batch the manifest seal upserts into O(1) round-trips (executemany / single INSERT…ON CONFLICT over all sealed (symbol,interval,day) tuples), so a cold 90d×50sym run issuing ~4,500 day-seals performs a bounded constant number of seal-write statements, NOT one round-trip per day; verified by a test that cold-runs an N-day×M-symbol fixture against a counting DB wrapper and asserts seal-write statement count is O(1) (or O(symbols)) and independent of day count.
rationale: #133 mandates the one-time v58 BACKFILL be set-based, and #508 mandates batched trade inserts, but neither covers the RUNTIME seal-write path that fires on every cold backtest as days close. Without an explicit batching requirement, a naive per-day seal upsert reintroduces O(N×M) round-trips on exactly the cold path the optimization targets.

text: GET /backtest-cache/status `ready` MUST be true when every CLOSED/sealed day in the requested range is present and ONLY the forming live-edge day (open_time >= frontier) is incomplete — the forming day's inherent, expected incompleteness MUST NOT cause the range to report ready=false or count toward symbols_with_gaps; verified by a test requesting a range ending at the current forming day and asserting ready=true with that symbol absent from symbols_with_gaps, distinct from the sealed-short-listing-day readiness case (#262).
rationale: #262 covers a sealed-short listing day being reported ready, and #514 covers status latency, but neither pins the most common operational case: a range whose only 'incompleteness' is today's still-forming candle. If the forming edge is treated as a gap, every up-to-now backtest's cache shows perpetually not-ready, breaking the warmup/readiness UX the manifest is meant to fix.

text: When a CLOSED day (day.end < frontier, within [listing,delist]) already has kline_cache rows in Postgres but carries NO manifest seal (sealed IS NULL — e.g. written by old code in the DDL→backfill window, or after a manifest-write failure), the loader MUST LAZILY SEAL it from the existing Postgres rows WITHOUT any Bybit refetch (the runtime equivalent of the v58 backfill), so a missing manifest row can never force re-downloading data already present in Postgres; verified by a test seeding Postgres klines for a closed day with sealed IS NULL and asserting the next run seals it with zero Bybit calls and zero subsequent refetch.
rationale: #417 only requires that a sealed-NULL row be TREATED as refetchable (correctness), and #273 governs only the backfill, leaving an efficiency hole: at runtime, a closed day with Postgres data but no manifest seal would be re-evaluated and re-fetched from the exchange even though the data already exists locally — directly violating the never-redownload-a-closed-day goal. Lazy-seal-from-Postgres closes it.

text: POST /backtest-cache/warmup MUST execute the IDENTICAL completion-frontier seal gate, forming-candle clip (#435), and seal-only-after-post-frontier-terminal-candle rule (#391) as the backtest cold-fetch path via SHARED code — so a warmup launched moments before UTC midnight can never seal a still-forming day nor persist a forming candle that the backtest path would have clipped; verified by a test running warmup against a simulated near-midnight clock and asserting the forming day remains unsealed/refreshable and no forming candle is persisted, byte-identical to what the backtest fetch path would produce.
rationale: #409/#426 require warmup to seal closed days idempotently and report per-symbol status, but neither requires warmup to share the frontier/clip/seal RULES with the backtest fetch path. A divergent warmup implementation could seal the live edge or persist a forming candle (which ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING then freezes forever per #435), corrupting the manifest through the operator-facing pre-warm path.

text: A requested backtest window whose end extends to or beyond `now` MUST clamp to the available/forming edge: genuinely-future days (open_time >= now, beyond the completion frontier) MUST incur 0 Bybit calls, 0 kline SELECTs, MUST NOT be classified as coverage gaps, and MUST NOT be sealed or negative-cached; the run proceeds on the available candles up to the forming edge; verified by a test requesting a window ending one week in the future and asserting zero fetch/zero gap for the future portion and a clean run on the present-up-to-now data.
rationale: The frontier math (#83/#249) defines sealing for past/forming days but no requirement covers a window whose end is in the FUTURE (a common user error or an open-ended range). Without clamping, future days could be flagged as perpetual gaps (the very RC-3 class of bug) and hammer the exchange for candles that do not yet exist.

text: Two processes concurrently materializing the SAME monthly Parquet/Feather partition while sealing DISJOINT sets of days MUST converge to a partition containing the UNION of both processes' sealed days — the read-modify-write that extends a month (#253) MUST merge against the current durable partition state, never blind last-writer-wins-overwrite that would drop the peer's just-sealed days; verified by a concurrency test where process A seals days 1–10 and process B seals days 11–20 of the same month and the final partition's row set equals the Postgres SOR for all 20 days.
rationale: #253 covers SINGLE-run sequential month extension, #440 covers concurrent READ during a write (Windows sharing), and #284 covers Postgres partition CREATE races — but none covers two processes WRITING the same month partition with different sealed days. The versioned-part-file + atomic-pointer-swap (#440) is last-writer-wins by construction, so without a merge requirement a concurrent sweep could silently lose sealed days from a month.

text: The cold gap-fill path MUST NOT hold a pooled asyncpg connection across the (slow, network-bound) Bybit HTTP await: the connection is released before the exchange fetch and re-acquired only to store the returned rows, so a slow or backing-off exchange fetch cannot pin a Postgres connection and starve the shared live-trading/scanner pool; verified by a test with a mocked latency-bearing Bybit fetch asserting zero pooled connections are held during the fetch window while stores still succeed.
rationale: #376 caps the NUMBER of concurrent Postgres reads (a DB-pool semaphore), but does not forbid HOLDING a connection across the HTTP round-trip. A naive `async with pool.acquire() as conn:` wrapping fetch+store would pin a connection for the full multi-second exchange latency, and since kline_cache_service is shared with the live path (#281), a cold sweep could exhaust the pool and stall live trading.

text: A single batched `symbol = ANY($1)` kline load whose symbols span MIXED tiers — some fully-sealed (served from Parquet/Arrow columnar) and some with a still-forming tail (served from Postgres) — MUST route each symbol independently to its correct tier and assemble per-symbol arrays byte-identical to a uniform pure-Postgres read, with no symbol served from the wrong tier and no cross-symbol interleave; verified by a test loading a symbol set deliberately split across sealed-only and forming-tail symbols in one batched call and diffing each symbol's array against its single-symbol Postgres-order oracle.
rationale: #439 covers ONE symbol's window straddling the seal/forming boundary in time, and #447 covers per-symbol bucketing ORDER within a batched Postgres read — but neither covers the integration case where a single batched load must fan out across DIFFERENT storage tiers per symbol simultaneously. The columnar read path (#110) and the forming-day Postgres path (#443) intersect here, and a routing bug would serve a forming symbol from a stale columnar file.

text: A range request that PARTIALLY overlaps (not strict containment, #503) an already-cached (symbol,interval,range) entry MUST return byte-identical klines for the overlap with NO torn, duplicated, or misordered candle at the boundary — at minimum by a correct cache-miss reload of the requested span, and the chosen key/merge strategy (reload vs range-merge) MUST be documented; verified by a test that caches [t1,t3], then requests [t2,t4] (t2<t3<t4), and asserts the returned series is strictly ascending, de-duplicated, and equals the Postgres SOR read.
rationale: #503 covers the strict sub-range CONTAINMENT hit, but partially-overlapping spans arise naturally when date_range or symbol sets differ across runs while the LRU key is (symbol,interval,start,end). No requirement pins correctness for the partial-overlap case, where a buggy range-merge could splice two slices into a torn/duplicated seam feeding the SoA build.

text: On a sealed day carrying gap_count > 0 (a real interior structural hole recorded known-empty per #260), the loader MUST serve the PRESENT candles and issue ZERO Bybit fetches and ZERO kline SELECTs for the recorded known-empty sub-ranges (count-free, never a perpetual refetch), returning a candle set the engine consumes gracefully (the missing sub-range contributes to signals_no_kline where applicable, never raising); verified by a test seeding a sealed-with-gap day and asserting the read issues 0 fetch for the hole, returns exactly the present candles, and the run completes.
rationale: #448 covers a FULLY negative-cached (entirely empty) day and #260 covers the BACKFILL recording of gap_count, but neither pins the LOADER's runtime behavior on a PARTIALLY-present sealed day (some real candles + a recorded hole). This is the common 'exchange outage mid-day' case; without the count-free guarantee the recorded hole could re-trigger the RC-3 refetch loop on every run.

text: The read path MUST resolve a sealed (symbol,interval,day) through a SINGLE documented, deterministic tier precedence — in-process Arrow/SoA hot frame → mmap'd Feather V2 → on-disk Parquet → Postgres SOR — short-circuiting at the first hit, so the serving tier is a pure function of cache-warmth state and a warmer tier ALWAYS shadows a colder one (never a nondeterministic race between tiers that could return divergent representations); verified by a test that warms tiers in layers and asserts each warmer tier deterministically shadows the colder ones and the provenance record (#280) matches the precedence.
rationale: #32 enumerates the three cache layers and #251 covers Parquet-missing→Postgres fallback, and #280 RECORDS which tier served — but no requirement pins the deterministic PRECEDENCE ORDER when multiple tiers are simultaneously warm. Without a fixed short-circuit order, two runs could serve the same sealed day from different tiers, undermining the byte-identical-across-tiers guarantees (#115/#359) and making provenance non-reproducible.

text: signal_time values from scan_source MUST be normalized to the IDENTICAL epoch unit (Bybit-native milliseconds, #437) and UTC basis as kline open_time BEFORE any searchsorted entry-fill / mark lookup (#62/#63), so a unit mismatch (seconds vs ms) or timezone offset in the stored signal timestamps can never shift which candle is selected as the entry-fill bar or the 'last close ≤ T' mark; verified by a test that feeds signals whose raw timestamps differ in unit/tz from the kline epochs and asserts byte-identical entry-fill bar selection, signals_no_kline count, and trade set versus the legacy engine.
rationale: #437 pins kline open_time uniformity across storage tiers and #406 normalizes the date_range API boundary, but neither covers the JOIN KEY between signal_time (from a separate scan_source table) and kline open_time. The searchsorted entry-fill (#62) is exact only if both operands share unit and tz; a silent seconds/ms or naive-local mismatch would shift every entry bar — a data-alignment cause with a direct parity effect.

text: Every columnar (Parquet/DuckDB/Polars/Arrow) read of a sealed day MUST verify the returned row count equals the manifest candle_count minus recorded gap_count, and on ANY shortfall MUST fall back to the Postgres SOR for that day rather than backtest on a truncated slice — guarding against a silent predicate/partition-pushdown bug that returns fewer rows WITHOUT corrupting the file; verified by a test that injects a pushdown/predicate defect causing the columnar read to drop interior rows and asserts the loader detects the count shortfall and serves Postgres-identical full data.
rationale: #295 covers Postgres-tier truncation (rows deleted behind a sealed manifest) and #255 covers Parquet BIT-CORRUPTION (sha mismatch), but neither covers a structurally-valid Parquet file that a buggy predicate/partition pushdown reads SHORT (the file is fine; the query returns a truncated slice). A row-count-vs-manifest cross-check at read time is the only guard against silently simulating on a partial columnar slice.

text: Each symbol MUST be mapped to a contiguous int code ONCE at SoA build, and the per-candle hot loop + position book MUST key on these int codes (never Python string hashing/equality or dict-of-symbol lookups inside the bar loop); a test MUST assert the kernel/inner loop receives int-code arrays and that a profiler/allocation probe shows zero per-candle str/dict operations, so symbol identity comparison cost is O(1) and does not scale with symbol-name length or symbol count.
rationale: Repeated symbol-string hashing/equality in the per-candle portfolio reduction is a silent super-linear constant; #31/#234 forbid per-candle allocation generally but never pin the categorical-encoding mechanism that makes symbol identity free in the hot path.

text: All S scan start anchors MUST be bound to global-timeline indices with a SINGLE vectorized np.searchsorted(timeline, sorted_scan_times) computed once before the scan loop, NOT a per-scan bisect; a test MUST assert the anchor binding issues one vectorized call and that doubling scan count S at fixed kline data grows binding cost ~2x (linear), not S*log(T), so the per-scan O(log T) anchor lookup is amortized out of the loop.
rationale: #13/#377 eliminate O(scans*N) setup and batch signal loads, but the specific scan_time->timeline-index binding can still be S separate bisects; batching it is a distinct, measurable setup-cost elimination.

text: TP, SL, and liquidation barrier PRICES MUST be derived ONCE at position open and cached on the position record, then reused unchanged every candle of the position's life (only breakeven-mutation rewrites TP); a counter test MUST assert barrier-price derivation (the trading_rules helpers) is invoked once per opened position, NOT once per candle evaluated, so a 1000-candle-long position pays barrier math once.
rationale: #320/#322 pin barrier-price PARITY but never that the (relatively expensive, Decimal-in-legacy) derivation is hoisted out of the per-candle loop; recomputing barriers per bar is an O(candles) waste the SoA refactor could accidentally preserve.

text: The sizing mark price ('last close <= current_time') is CONSTANT within a single scan (current_time is fixed for that scan), so it MUST be resolved once per scan and reused for every signal admitted in that scan's sequential sizing loop, NOT re-looked-up per signal; a test MUST assert the within-scan mark lookup count == 1 per scan regardless of how many signals the scan admits.
rationale: #61 pins the mark's path-dependent VALUE and #560 pins sequential depletion, but neither prevents an O(signals-per-scan) redundant mark re-lookup that the merge-walk refactor could introduce.

text: The open-position book MUST be backed by preallocated fixed-capacity typed arrays/struct-of-arrays (capacity >= the max simultaneously-open positions implied by max_trades / mr_max_trades and carried cycles) so opening and closing a position performs zero heap allocation in the hot loop; a test MUST assert zero per-open/per-close allocation (tracemalloc delta ~0) and that capacity exhaustion is handled by a documented grow-once or bounded policy, not per-open reallocation.
rationale: #234 asserts no per-CANDLE allocation but the per-OPEN/per-CLOSE position-book churn (Python dicts/objects per position) is a separate allocation source that degrades trade-heavy runs and perturbs GC.

text: The sweep ProcessPool (and the engine's run_in_executor ThreadPoolExecutor) MUST be a process-lifetime singleton reused across successive sweeps/runs, NOT spawned-and-torn-down per sweep, so repeated worker-spawn cost (high on win32 spawn-start) and per-worker JIT warm-up are paid once per process; a test MUST assert two sequential sweeps reuse the same worker PIDs (no re-spawn, no re-warm) and that the second sweep's time-to-first-result excludes spawn+compile latency. A cancelled/timed-out sweep MUST return its workers to the idle pool (not leak or destroy them).
rationale: #576 warms JIT in the worker initializer but assumes pool lifecycle; if the pool is recreated per sweep, every sweep re-pays spawn+JIT-warm, defeating the steady-state targets (#219) and inflating time-to-first-result (#547).

text: ProcessPool sweep workers MUST access the shared read-only kline/SoA arrays + global timeline via shared memory (multiprocessing.shared_memory / mmap), NOT per-worker pickled copies, so aggregate process-tree RSS for a C-worker sweep stays ~ base_snapshot + C*small-per-combo-working-set and does NOT scale with C*full-kline-bytes; a test MUST assert peak RSS across the worker tree is flat as worker count rises at fixed data (distinct from #27's IPC-bytes claim and #445's uvicorn-worker claim).
rationale: #27 bounds IPC/pickling bytes per combo and #445 covers uvicorn workers, but neither prevents each sweep ProcessPool worker from holding its OWN resident copy of the (~75MB+) SoA arrays, which on a wide fixture multiplies RSS by worker count and OOM-kills the server.

text: A single long-lived DuckDB connection (or Polars pool) per process MUST be reused across reads, Parquet file footer/metadata MUST be cached so the same partition is not re-parsed each read, and the columnar engine MUST be warmed once at boot so the FIRST columnar read inside a timed run pays no engine-init or footer-scan cost; tests MUST assert the connection is reused across N reads, footers are parsed once per file per session, and first-read latency excludes engine-init.
rationale: Opening a DuckDB connection and re-reading Parquet footers per read is a hidden fixed cost analogous to JIT cold-compile; #33 warms numba but no requirement warms/reuses the columnar engine, so warm targets could be missed on the first columnar-served bar.

text: The optimizer pipeline (optimize_config: baseline run + N-combo sweep + rank + propose serialization) MUST have its OWN committed end-to-end latency budget and per-phase regression gate on the canonical search space, distinct from the raw K-combo sweep budget (#215), since it adds a full baseline backtest, cross-combo ranking, and proposal construction; a test MUST gate optimize_config wall-time and assert it does not regress phase-over-phase.
rationale: #215/#29 gate raw sweeps but the agent-facing optimize_config does extra serial work (baseline + rank + propose) that could dominate and is never budgeted, so the user-visible optimizer latency is ungated.

text: The cache-efficiency regression gate (#263/#446) MUST additionally track and gate, phase-over-phase on the canonical fixture: (a) the in-process hot/LRU cache hit-ratio within a sweep (must stay >= baseline) and (b) the raw->SoA conversion invocation count per (symbol,interval,range) (must stay == 1 across a K-combo sweep, never K). A test MUST fail the gate if a refactor reintroduces re-load or re-conversion even when Bybit-call and Postgres-SELECT counts are unchanged.
rationale: #217/#218 assert single-conversion/zero-reload asymptotically, but without a standing phase-over-phase COUNTER gate a regression that re-converts SoA per combo (a classic refactor slip) passes every other gate because wire/DB call counts look identical.

text: After the Phase-1 cache fix the warmup-band stage (_WARMUP_BAND=10%) wall-time MUST be a bounded small constant (no longer proportional to pre-window history length), so the 10% progress band corresponds to near-zero real work; a test MUST assert warmup-band stage time stays under a small absolute bound on the canonical fixture and does not grow when pre-window history is 4x'd (pairing the progress-monotonicity rule #245 with a time bound).
rationale: #245 guarantees progress stays monotonic but not that the warmup band is actually cheap; if warmup still does history-proportional work it remains a latency sink hidden inside the 10% band, defeating the per-stage budget (#222).

text: The Phase-6 vectorized first-touch fast-path MUST bound/chunk its peak extra memory: materializing barrier-touch arrays of size O(open_positions * bars-in-life) MUST be streamed in bounded chunks (or capped) so a deep concurrent book + long horizon cannot spike RSS above the run's ceiling (#30/#374); a test on a deep-book eligible config MUST assert the fast-path's peak incremental RSS stays under a documented bound while still delivering the >=10x speedup (#40).
rationale: The fast-path trades sequential evaluation for vectorized arrays; #40 gates its speedup and parity but nothing bounds the materialized-array memory, which on the wide/deep fixture can dwarf the SoA klines and OOM the process.

text: The @njit kernels MUST be compiled to a SINGLE explicit type signature (no per-call retypification across runs) and object-mode fallback MUST be FORBIDDEN: the boot warm-up MUST assert each kernel is truly nopython (e.g. kernel.nopython_signatures present and non-empty, no fallback warning) and, if numba would only object-mode-compile it, the system MUST treat that as JIT-unavailable and route to the pure-Python path with a degraded signal rather than silently running ~100x slower in object mode; a test MUST assert object-mode compilation is detected and rejected.
rationale: #33/#141 cover compile-time-out-of-budget and version-fallback, but a numba object-mode fallback compiles SUCCESSFULLY yet runs ~100x slower; without an explicit nopython assertion the JIT path can silently miss its >=5M/sec floor (#15) while appearing healthy.

text: The numba THREADING LAYER (NUMBA_THREADING_LAYER) MUST be explicitly selected and pinned to a layer coherent with the process model (e.g. a fork/spawn-safe layer when a ProcessPool is used) so prange/nogil threading does not deadlock or oversubscribe under asyncio.gather x ProcessPool x BLAS; a test MUST assert the configured threading layer is applied at import and that a sweep combining process-level and kernel-level parallelism neither deadlocks nor spawns > the cgroup/CPU budget (complementing the thread-COUNT pins of #212/#577).
rationale: #212/#577 pin thread COUNTS but not the threading LAYER; an OMP/TBB layer that is not fork-safe can deadlock or fight the ProcessPool, a distinct and notorious numba-under-multiprocessing failure.

text: The 'slow' performance+scaling lane MUST declare a documented total CI wall-time budget, be shardable/parallelizable across runners, and use the MINIMAL fixture sizes empirically proven to surface each asymptote (linear-scaling, P-doubling, symbol-doubling), so the perf gate stays cheap enough to actually run every phase and cannot be quietly marked optional as fixtures accumulate; a meta-test MUST assert the lane's declared budget exists and that scaling fixtures use the documented minimal sizes.
rationale: #476 bounds the GOLDEN suite's per-PR time but the perf/scaling battery (#1/#2/#6/#7/#12/#13/#231/#389/#507) can balloon; without its own budget+sharding it gets skipped, silently dropping the scaling-law gates that prevent super-linear regressions.

text: At each scan boundary the portfolio-valuation marks ('last close <= scan_time') for ALL P carried positions MUST be resolved with vectorized/batched searchsorted across the per-symbol arrays (one batched lookup, advancing the never-resetting merge-walk pointers), NOT P independent Python-level bisects; a P-doubling test MUST assert mark-seeding time grows <= ~2x (linear in P) and that the result is bit-identical to the legacy per-position linear scan.
rationale: #8/#63 establish O(log N)/O(1) per-lookup parity, but the per-scan re-seeding of the whole open book can still be P separate Python calls; batching it removes a per-scan O(P) Python-overhead term that the trailing/equity O(P) work (#10) would otherwise compound.

text: The confidence_filter MUST preserve its exact ORDINAL 'at-least' inclusion semantics, not mere exact-match: confidence_filter='moderate' MUST admit BOTH high- and moderate-confidence signals while rejecting low (and 'any' admits all), with the high>moderate>low ordering frozen — verified by a fixture feeding all three confidence levels at each filter setting and asserting the admitted set is byte-identical to the legacy ordinal comparison (a refactor to string-equality would pass a single-threshold admit/reject test like #367 yet fail this).
rationale: #367 pins confidence_filter with one admit/reject oracle but does not pin the ordinal '>=' inclusion across all levels; a refactor could silently switch to exact-match and still pass #367.

text: The classification of a BREAKEVEN trade (recorded pnl exactly == 0 within the money quantization) MUST be reproduced bit-identically across every count-based metric: win_rate (is 0-pnl a win, loss, or neither), profit_factor (does it touch gross_profit/gross_loss), avg_win/avg_loss denominators, and max_consecutive_wins/losses streak continuation — frozen by a fixture containing an exactly-zero-pnl close and asserting each metric matches the legacy sentinel.
rationale: No requirement pins how a pnl==0 trade is bucketed; win_rate/profit_factor/streak metrics are sensitive to it and float64 could land a near-zero pnl on either side of zero.

text: A structural close-count reconciliation MUST hold on every fixture: metrics.total_trades == len(trade list) == (wins + losses + breakevens), and the trade list MUST include EVERY closed position across ALL close_reasons (take_profit, stop_loss, liquidation, equity_*, trailing_profit, breakeven_timeout, max_duration, mr_time_stop, backtest_end) with none omitted or double-counted — a count identity distinct from the Σpnl money reconciliation (#66).
rationale: #66 reconciles money sums but nothing asserts the trade-COUNT identity; a refactor could drop a liquidation/backtest_end close from the list while Σpnl still happens to reconcile.

text: The EQUITY_DROP_PCT_SMART CONDITIONAL re-anchor CONDITION (#44 names it 'conditional' but never pins the predicate) MUST be frozen exactly: the precise quantity compared (new basket/wallet equity vs the existing cycle_start_equity anchor) and the direction of the inequality under which SMART re-anchors versus leaves the anchor unchanged — verified by paired fixtures that drive equity just above and just below the re-anchor threshold and assert the anchor moves in one case and not the other.
rationale: #44 only asserts SMART fires-once and re-arms; the conditional re-anchor's exact predicate is unpinned and is path-dependent for all subsequent sizing.

text: On a scan SKIPPED by skip_if_positions_open the engine MUST NOT re-arm smart_drawdown_fired and MUST preserve (not zero/re-anchor) cycle_start_equity — i.e. the skip branch is excluded from BOTH the non-smart re-anchor (#45) and the smart re-arm (#44) — verified by a fixture chaining a trading scan → a skip_if_positions_open-skipped scan → a trading scan and asserting smart did NOT re-arm across the skip and the anchor was carried.
rationale: #71 pins that skip still evaluates closes and preserves the anchor, but the interaction with smart_drawdown re-arm (skip must NOT re-arm) is not explicitly pinned; the optimized engine could collapse skip into the non-skip re-arm path.

text: The measurement BASIS of the close_on_profit_pct and EQUITY_RISE_PCT rules MUST be pinned exactly (which equity each is measured against — cycle_start_equity vs running wallet, realized-only vs realized+open-uPnL) AND EQUITY_RISE MUST value intrabar on the FAVORABLE extreme symmetrically to the DROP rule's adverse-extreme intrabar valuation (#54) — frozen by fixtures whose threshold is crossed only intrabar, asserting RISE/close_on_profit fire on the max(close_equity,intrabar_equity) basis just as DROP fires on min(...).
rationale: #43 pins the cascade ORDER and #54 pins only the DROP/drawdown intrabar adverse-extreme; the close_on_profit/EQUITY_RISE measurement basis and their intrabar favorable-extreme symmetry are unpinned (analogous to #559 which only covers the profit_pct GOAL, not the close rule).

text: The entry_fee charged at open MUST deduct from the running wallet and therefore reduce sizing_capital for the NEXT signal admitted in the SAME scan, exactly as legacy — extending the intra-scan sequential depletion (#560, which lists locked_margin and carried uPnL but omits entry_fee) — verified by a multi-signal single-scan fixture where the k-th signal's qty reflects the (k−1) prior positions' entry fees, bit-identical to the oracle.
rationale: #560 pins intra-scan depletion via locked_margin + carried uPnL but does not name the entry_fee deduction, which also lowers same-scan sizing_capital and would shift later qty.

text: Sizing ACCEPT/REJECT boundary fixtures MUST pin exact-equality under float64: a signal whose computed qty == min_qty MUST be ADMITTED (reject only qty < min_qty per #60) and a signal whose required margin == available_balance MUST be ADMITTED (reject only margin > available per #60), asserting the float64/numba path does not flip these `<`/`>` boundary comparisons versus the Decimal oracle at exact equality.
rationale: #60 states the operators but no fixture pins the exact-equality case under float64, where a last-bit difference (cf. #484 for barriers) could flip qty==min_qty or margin==available and add/drop a trade.

text: Sentinel 'disable' config values MUST disable their close rule identically in the SEQUENTIAL engine, not only gate the fast-path: max_drawdown_pct == 100 means EQUITY_DROP never fires, stop_loss_pct/take_profit_pct at their documented off-sentinels never fire, trailing_profit_pct unset never trails, etc. — verified by fixtures at each sentinel asserting zero firings of the disabled rule and byte-identical output to a config omitting it.
rationale: #75 uses max_drawdown_pct>=100 only as a FAST-PATH eligibility gate; nothing pins that the SEQUENTIAL engine treats 100 (and other sentinels) as a true disable, so the oracle and refactor must agree the rule is inert at the sentinel.

text: The Python Decimal context (precision and rounding mode) used by the legacy engine and the oracle-regeneration script MUST be explicitly set and pinned (not left to the interpreter default getcontext()), so a host/interpreter with a different default precision cannot shift any Decimal quantization site (#343) and silently re-baseline an oracle; a meta-test asserts the context is set explicitly and hash-pins its prec/rounding.
rationale: #343 enumerates quantization sites and #525 pins tolerance constants, but the global Decimal context precision/rounding that governs every Decimal op (and the regen script) is itself unpinned — a default-precision overflow or differing rounding would corrupt oracles.

text: Liquidation and backtest_end force-close trades MUST be included in the adaptive-blacklist incremental win/total counters exactly as legacy (a liquidation pnl<0 counts as a loss; a backtest_end close counts per its pnl sign), since the blacklist is computed from the backtest's OWN closed-trade history (#80) — verified by a fixture whose only losses are liquidations and asserting the point-in-time blacklist win-rate equals the legacy full-history recompute.
rationale: #80/#326 pin the blacklist's point-in-time/windowed semantics but never pin WHICH close_reasons feed its win/total; omitting or including liquidation/backtest_end closes would change the blacklist set.

text: The disposition of a signal that passes all 17 filters but has NO entry kline (signals_no_kline, #62) MUST be pinned relative to the per-scan and lifetime counters: specify whether it consumes a max_trades/scan_entered slot and whether it increments signals_entered/target_goal — verified by a fixture where a no-kline signal sits among admissible signals at the max_trades boundary and asserting the same downstream signals are admitted/early-stopped as legacy.
rationale: #563 enumerates counter increments for dedup/margin/qty rejections but omits the signals_no_kline disposition relative to max_trades and target_goal counters, which can shift which later signals are admitted or trigger early-stop.

text: Position sizing MUST use the leverage from config RAW and MUST NEVER clamp it to any per-symbol max-leverage field that instrument_info might carry — preserving the legacy behavior — so that shipping a richer instrument_info snapshot under the no-op guarantee (#72/#336/#394) cannot silently reduce leverage and change qty/liquidation price; verified by a test supplying instrument_info with a max-leverage field below config leverage and asserting qty and liq price are computed from the raw config leverage, bit-identical to the no-instrument-info path.
rationale: #336/#394 pin qty_step/min_qty defaults and snapshot pinning but do not address a max-leverage field; a parity landmine where the optimized path 'helpfully' clamps leverage and diverges.

text: The sizing/uPnL MARK-PRICE FALLBACK when an open position's symbol has NO candle with open_time <= current_time (a just-listed symbol still open from a prior scan, or a hole at the mark time) MUST be pinned bit-identically (e.g. fall back to entry_base_price → uPnL contribution 0, or skip from the Σ uPnL book) — verified by a misaligned-listing fixture where an open position has no prior mark at a scan time and asserting its sizing_capital/Σ-uPnL contribution equals the legacy linear-scan 'no prior mark' result.
rationale: #349 covers misaligned timelines generally and #241 covers the searchsorted side='right'-1 == -1 'no prior mark' index, but the ENGINE-level fallback value for an OPEN position's mark (its uPnL contribution) is not pinned — only the index mapping is.

text: The TIMESTAMP recorded for each equity_curve point on a close MUST be pinned to the exact legacy source (exit-fill time vs close-bar open_time vs close-bar close_time) and the terminal backtest_end point's timestamp pinned (each symbol's last-close time vs the global max), because Sharpe/Sortino periodization derives from inter-point time deltas (#492) — verified by a fixture asserting each point's ts equals the legacy source, not just that the ORDER (#240) matches.
rationale: #240 pins curve ORDER and the seed/terminal dedup, and #492 pins the periodization math, but the per-point timestamp SOURCE that feeds those deltas is unpinned; a refactor using close_time instead of fill_time would change Sharpe while preserving order.

text: The exact wallet quantity the backtest substitutes for the live path's totalAvailableBalance when re-anchoring cycle_start_equity (#45) MUST be pinned (running wallet_balance only, vs wallet+carried-uPnL, vs wallet−Σlocked_margin), since the live engine reads an account field absent in backtest — verified by a fixture chaining an equity-rule cycle termination into the next scan's first entry and asserting the re-anchor value (and the resulting first-trade qty) is bit-identical to the oracle.
rationale: #45/#341 pin that re-anchor occurs and that the first post-termination trade sizes off it, but the precise backtest-equivalent quantity substituted for the live totalAvailableBalance is unpinned and directly sets all subsequent sizing.

text: A signal whose OWN signal_time falls AFTER the next scan's first-signal time (so its entry-fill bar at first open>=signal_time lies beyond next_scan_time) MUST resolve its fill bar and its participation in subsequent evaluation windows bit-identically to legacy — verified by a fixture with an intra-scan late-signal_time signal asserting the same fill bar, the same first scan whose window evaluates it, and no double/skipped evaluation under the merge-walk inter-scan windowing (#244).
rationale: #237 keys scan order on FIRST-signal time and #244 pins window membership, but a non-first signal in a scan can have a signal_time beyond next_scan_time; the fill-bar resolution and window participation for that case is an unpinned edge the SoA/global-timeline walk could shift.

text: The SUMMARY max_drawdown_pct and every '_pct' summary metric (net_profit_pct, etc.) MUST pin their exact denominator/peak basis — max drawdown as % of the running peak equity where the peak includes the starting_capital seed anchor (#240), net_profit_pct over starting_capital — distinct from the per-point EquityPoint.drawdown_pct sequence (#334) — verified by a fixture whose peak-vs-starting basis would change the reported max_drawdown_pct and asserting the legacy basis.
rationale: #334 pins the per-EquityPoint drawdown_pct sequence but the SUMMARY max_drawdown_pct and _pct metrics' denominator/peak basis are not separately pinned and could diverge (e.g. peak-relative vs starting-relative).

text: ANY unhandled exception raised inside the engine/persist pipeline on the PURE-Python/Postgres path (a genuine bug or bad data — not a 120s timeout #299/#363, cooperative cancel #208, SIGTERM #451, or accelerated-path fault that restarts on fallback #301) MUST leave backtest_runs in status='failed' with an actionable message, ZERO partial backtest_trades/results/equity_curve rows committed, and the _MAX_CONCURRENT slot released exactly once — verified by a fault-injection test raising a deterministic exception mid-engine and asserting clean terminal state.
rationale: Failure-path requirements cover timeout, cancel, SIGTERM, and accelerated→fallback restart, but a plain unhandled exception on the safety-net pure path leaving clean state (no orphan rows, slot freed) is not explicitly required.

text: A side×outcome boundary fixture MUST include a pnl-exactly-zero (breakeven) trade in EACH of the four quadrants plus a zero-funding and a zero-excess-return case, asserting the absolute-floor comparator (#565) and the breakeven classification (this set) hold together — so near-zero money fields and breakeven bucketing are validated jointly rather than in isolation.
rationale: #526 pins side×outcome signs and #565 pins the near-zero absolute-floor comparator, but no fixture forces an exactly-breakeven trade through the four-quadrant sign matrix, leaving the pnl==0 interaction with the absolute-floor gate untested.

text: The strategy_kind/cohort label stamped on each trade (trend vs mean_reversion) and the routing decision that produced it MUST be reproduced bit-identically per trade, including a MIXED-cohort run where some scans route to MR and others to trend within one backtest — verified by a mixed-route fixture asserting each trade's strategy_kind, its cohort-specific sizing/stops (#329), and the trade ordering are byte-identical to the oracle.
rationale: #82/#178/#329/#330 pin MR sizing/gating and cohort defaults in isolation, but a single run that interleaves MR and trend routes (per-trade strategy_kind labeling and mixed sizing within one wallet/blacklist history) is not pinned as a combined fixture.

text: The fetch-ingest path MUST normalize Bybit v5 newest-first (descending open_time) kline pages into strictly-ascending order before store_klines and before content_sha256 computation, and the pagination cursor loop MUST correctly walk descending pages to full gap-run coverage; a test MUST feed a mocked client returning descending pages and assert the stored/sealed rows are ascending, de-duplicated, complete, and produce the canonical-representation sha.
rationale: Bybit's REST kline endpoint returns candles newest-first; if the ingest path assumes ascending it will mis-advance the pagination cursor (leaving interior holes) and compute a non-canonical content_sha256, which then triggers spurious hash-mismatch refetches. No existing requirement addresses exchange response ordering.

text: content_sha256 MUST be stamped with a hash-ALGORITHM-VERSION tag (canonicalization scheme + column order + numeric serialization rules), and a future change to the canonical representation MUST bump that version so a sha computed under an old scheme is treated as 'no comparable hash' (no integrity check / no refetch) rather than a mismatch; a test MUST assert a version-tagged old sha never triggers refetch while a same-version content change does.
rationale: Reqs 118/254/296 pin sha-mismatch refetch but assume the canonicalization is eternally fixed. Any future tweak to row/column serialization would make every historic sha mismatch and re-download the entire sealed history — the exact bug the project is eliminating. Versioning the hash scheme is the only safe escape.

text: The completion frontier (per (symbol,interval)) MUST be computed ONCE at run start and held STABLE for every seal/forming/clip/lazy-seal decision within that single run, so a UTC-midnight rollover mid-run cannot flip a day between forming and sealed partway through and produce an internally-inconsistent result; cross-RUN re-evaluation still recomputes fresh (req 84/402). A test spanning a simulated midnight within one run asserts the boundary day keeps one classification for the whole run.
rationale: Req 249/402/578 cover skew-lag and cross-access/cross-session re-evaluation, but none pin frontier STABILITY within a single run. A long cold run that crosses midnight could classify the same day as forming early and sealed late, corrupting both the seal decision and the engine's last-bar set.

text: Runtime sealing of newly-closed days during a cold run MUST batch manifest seal upserts into O(1) (or O(symbols)) round-trips via a single executemany / INSERT…ON CONFLICT over all (symbol,interval,day) tuples, and a day MUST be included in the batch only after its rows are durably stored in ALL required tiers; a test cold-running an N-day×M-symbol fixture against a counting DB wrapper asserts seal-write statement count is independent of day count and that a tier-store failure leaves that day unsealed for retry.
rationale: A cold 90d×50sym run seals ~4,500 days. Without batching that is 4,500 manifest round-trips dwarfing the engine. No existing req bounds the runtime seal-WRITE statement count (590 is the closest in spirit but was not in the supplied list); this also pins the seal-only-after-durable ordering for the batched path.

text: The batched symbol=ANY($1) kline load MUST include an open_time BETWEEN $start AND $end predicate so Postgres partition pruning restricts the scan to the monthly partitions overlapping the requested range (not all partitions), verified by an EXPLAIN/plan-assertion test showing only the overlapping monthly partitions (and kline_cache_default only when a >6mo range is requested) are scanned.
rationale: Req 16 mandates ANY($1) for one query, and req 108/456 cover monthly partitioning, but nothing asserts the load actually PRUNES partitions. A symbol=ANY query without an open_time bound scans every monthly partition, re-introducing history-bound cost the whole effort is trying to kill.

text: Derived coarse-interval (15m/1h/4h) manifest entries MUST be marked as DERIVED (no independent fetched_at, no content_sha256, no Bybit fetch decision) and sealed purely as a function of their constituent sealed fine bars (req 257), and the read/seal path MUST distinguish derived-coarse from legacy-exchange-fetched-coarse rows (req 455) so a derived row is never refetched and a legacy-fetched row is never silently recomputed; a test asserts the two coarse provenances are distinguishable and each follows its correct fetch/derive path.
rationale: Reqs 116/257/455 establish derive-from-fine and legacy coarse sealing separately but never pin how the manifest TAGS a coarse row's provenance. Without a derived-vs-fetched marker the seal/refetch predicate cannot tell which coarse rows to recompute vs leave immutable, risking either refetch storms or stale derived bars.

text: When reading a Parquet partition where `symbol` is the hive partition column (not stored in the file body), the read path MUST reattach the symbol from the decoded partition path and round-trip it to the EXACT original symbol string (reversing the req-438 reserved-char encoding), so the returned per-symbol arrays carry the correct symbol identity; a test asserts a symbol with reserved/encoded characters reads back byte-identical to the original and is never mislabeled or dropped.
rationale: Hive partitioning stores the partition key in the directory name, not the row data. Req 438 covers encoding the path but not the decode-on-read round-trip. A botched decode silently mislabels every candle in that partition, corrupting which symbol's klines feed the engine.

text: A fully negative-cached / known-empty month (entire month pre-listing or post-delist) MUST be served from the manifest as an empty candle set with ZERO Bybit calls, ZERO kline SELECTs, and WITHOUT opening any Parquet/Feather file or Postgres partition (no durable columnar artifact is created for an all-empty month); a test asserts a backtest spanning an all-negative-cached month performs zero file opens and zero DB reads for that month and the run completes with those symbol-days as signals_no_kline.
rationale: Reqs 90/448 cover negative-cache zero-fetch but say nothing about the columnar tier short-circuiting. Opening (or worse, materializing) an empty Parquet partition per negative-cached month re-introduces I/O and small-file sprawl the design forbids; the manifest must answer 'empty' before any tier is touched.

text: A cross-engine columnar read parity test MUST assert DuckDB read_parquet, Polars scan_parquet, and pyarrow return BYTE-IDENTICAL arrays for a sealed day — int64 open_time stays int64 epoch-ms (no auto-cast to Arrow/DB timestamp logical type) and OHLCV stays float64 (no Decimal/float32 drift) — so the serving engine choice can never change the SoA representation; the test runs all available backends over a sample range and diffs the raw buffers.
rationale: Req 404 pins fine→coarse RESAMPLE agreement across backends, but not raw-read representation. DuckDB and Polars both eagerly cast int64-that-looks-like-a-timestamp to a temporal type and may surface decimals; a silent cast breaks the zero-copy int64 SoA contract (req 437) and the searchsorted epoch math.

text: The cold batched ANY($1) kline load MUST execute under a bounded Postgres statement_timeout and, on timeout or a too-large result, fall back to symbol-batched/chunked loads (bounded symbol groups × date sub-ranges) rather than hanging or holding a pooled connection indefinitely; a test simulating a slow/oversized load asserts the statement_timeout fires, the chunked fallback completes correctly, and the connection is returned to the pool.
rationale: Reqs 373/376/557/596 cover pool-concurrency and off-loop work, but none bound a SINGLE giant load's DB statement time. A wide cold range (200sym×365d) as one ANY query can exceed statement limits and pin a pool connection, starving the shared live-trading path — needs an explicit timeout + chunked fallback.

text: The exact per-symbol instrument_info snapshot actually used for sizing/qty-flooring in a run (qty_step, min_qty, tick_size per symbol) MUST be persisted with the run as part of its data-provenance fingerprint (reqs 394/395), so qty flooring is reproducible and a later exchange instrument_info change is detectable; a test asserts the snapshot is stored, that re-running at the stored snapshot reproduces bit-identical qty, and that a differing live snapshot is flagged rather than silently substituted.
rationale: Req 394 says the SAME snapshot must be shipped for parity and 395 records a data fingerprint, but neither persists the actual qty_step/min_qty/tick_size values used. Without storing them, a reproduction run (req 371) cannot reconstruct identical qty flooring after the exchange updates instrument specs.

text: When a sweep varies date_range_start/date_range_end or the symbol set across combos (so combos do NOT share an identical window), the orchestrator's shared-preload optimization (req 505) MUST either superset-preload the union span/symbols once and serve each combo a zero-copy SLICE, or fall back to correct per-combo loads — it MUST NEVER serve a combo klines for the wrong window/symbols; a test with window-varying and symbol-varying combos asserts each combo's loaded klines exactly match its own config's bounds and results equal per-combo standalone oracles.
rationale: Req 505 assumes all combos share one (symbols,interval,window) and preloads once. Sweeps legitimately vary the window or whitelist; a naive single-preload would silently feed every combo the first combo's data, producing wrong-but-fast results. The shared-cache key must be window/symbol-aware.

text: Atomic columnar writes (temp-write + rename, req 109) MUST be durably committed: on POSIX the parent DIRECTORY entry MUST be fsync'd after rename (and the temp file fsync'd before rename), and on Windows the equivalent flush/replace MUST be used, so a power-loss/crash immediately after rename cannot leave a directory entry pointing at unflushed/zero-length data; a test (or documented fault-injection) asserts the rename+fsync ordering and that a crash-after-rename yields either the complete new file or the prior state, never a truncated file.
rationale: Req 109/251/440 cover atomic rename and crash-consistency at the manifest level, but POSIX rename durability also requires fsync of the containing directory — otherwise the rename can be lost on power-loss even though the data file was written. This is a concrete crash-durability gap distinct from the sealed-last ordering.

text: The in-process hot/LRU cache containment/overlap lookup (range-containment hit, req 503; partial overlap, req 598) MUST be backed by a sub-linear index (e.g. per-(symbol,interval) interval tree or sorted-range map), NOT a linear scan over all cached entries, so cache-hit resolution stays O(log E) as the number of cached ranges E grows over a long sweep/session; a microbenchmark asserts lookup time grows ≤ log(E) as E doubles.
rationale: Reqs 503/598 mandate containment/overlap hits but not their cost. A long sweep accumulates thousands of cached (symbol,interval,range) entries; an O(E) linear scan per lookup turns the warm cache into a quadratic bottleneck — re-introducing the super-linear cost the engine work eliminated.

text: A symbol whose candles STOP mid-window (mid-backtest delist, or a contract that ceases trading before date_range_end) MUST have its post-last-candle days negative-cached/known-empty so reruns issue 0 fetches for them, and signals for that symbol after its last candle MUST resolve to signals_no_kline (never a fabricated fill from the stale final close); a test seeds a symbol that delists mid-window and asserts post-delist days are zero-fetch on rerun and late signals count as signals_no_kline identically to the legacy engine.
rationale: Reqs 89/90/392 cover symbols delisted BEFORE the window; req 259/411 cover unknown/re-listed lifecycle. None pins the in-window mid-delist case where a symbol simply runs out of candles partway, which both the negative-cache (to avoid perpetual refetch of the tail) and the engine parity (signals_no_kline) must handle.

text: The v58 backfill computing first_open_ts/last_open_ts/candle_count/gap_count from existing kline_cache (req 275) MUST do so with a SINGLE set-based aggregate (GROUP BY symbol,interval,day_utc with min/max/count over open_time), NOT per-day queries, so the backfill's read cost is one bounded scan independent of day count; a test on a representative multi-symbol coverage volume asserts the fact-population phase issues O(1) aggregate statements and completes within the migration's statement budget (req 133).
rationale: Req 275 requires populating fact columns from kline_cache and req 133 bounds the seal UPDATE, but the fact-DERIVATION read (min/max/count per day across the whole kline_cache) is itself a potential full-table per-day storm. A set-based GROUP BY aggregate is the only way to keep the backfill from locking/scanning kline_cache row-by-row.

text: If kline_cache (Postgres SOR) has or gains any retention/pruning that deletes old rows, sealed ranges whose rows are pruned MUST have their durable columnar (Parquet/Feather) partition treated as the system-of-record for those ranges (and the Postgres-fallback of req 251/444 MUST detect the pruned-rows case and NOT report short data), OR sealed ranges MUST be explicitly exempt from pruning; a test prunes Postgres rows behind a sealed+materialized day and asserts reads still return full data from the columnar tier (not a truncated Postgres read mistaken for SOR).
rationale: Req 113/251 declare Postgres the write-of-record and columnar a regenerable derived cache — but that invariant silently breaks the moment Postgres prunes a sealed range that exists only in Parquet. Without resolving the SOR-handoff, the req-251 'fall back to Postgres on missing partition' rule would serve truncated data as if complete.

text: The committed deterministic generator for the canonical 90d×50sym benchmark fixture (req 294/528) MUST emit VALID, engine-exercising data: every OHLC bar satisfies low ≤ min(open,close) ≤ max(open,close) ≤ high with non-degenerate intrabar range, and the synthetic scheduled-scan signals are engineered to actually hit the path-dependent edges at scale (multi-signal scans, equal first-signal timestamps, tied abs(score) exceeding max_trades, same-symbol duplicates within a scan, and a capital-exhaustion scan); a test asserts the generated fixture contains ≥1 instance of each such edge and that all bars are OHLC-valid.
rationale: Reqs 294/528 make the fixture reproducible/offline but never constrain its CONTENT. A generator emitting degenerate bars (high==low) or signals that never collide would let the large-scale parity oracle (req 467/500) pass vacuously — the very accumulation/ordering bugs micro-fixtures miss would go unexercised at depth.

text: All auxiliary offline reference series required by the hermetic suite — Buy&Hold BTC/USDT klines (req 238/335), btc_vol regime BTC data (req 355), and the MR mean-reference series at mr_mean_interval (req 355) — MUST be materialized into the committed deterministic fixture so the parity+cache suite runs with network fully blocked (req 293); a test asserts these auxiliary series load from the committed fixture with zero DB/exchange dependency and that the B&H/regime/MR fixtures resolve offline.
