rationale: Req 293 blocks network and 294/528 commit the symbol klines + signals, but the auxiliary BTC/MR/regime series fetched on SEPARATE paths (reqs 238/355) are not named in the committed-fixture requirements. If they aren't committed, any drill/regime/MR/B&H golden fixture would attempt a live fetch and either hang or fail the no-egress guard.

text: When multiple gap/TTL days (unsealed negative-cache or transient-gap entries, reqs 92/93/248) reach the completion frontier in the same run and require one-shot re-verification before permanent sealing, those re-verify fetches MUST be batched and funneled through the SHARED Bybit rate-limit semaphore (req 229) so a 're-verify storm' cannot burst past the rate cap or blow the cold budget; a test seeding many simultaneously-healing gap days asserts re-verify fetch concurrency stays ≤ the configured cap and total re-verify calls are bounded.
rationale: Req 248 mandates one re-verify per healing gap day and 229 caps fetch concurrency for normal gap-fill/drill, but the SPECIFIC burst of many gap days crossing the frontier together isn't tied to the shared semaphore. Unbounded, a long-dormant cache healing en masse on one run could trip Bybit rate limits and stall the run.

text: An ENGINE-level prod shadow/dark-compare mode (distinct from the read-path-only shadow of #291 and the CI overlap test of #464) MUST be available for the rollout window: a configurable sample of REAL production backtest runs executes through BOTH the legacy and the optimized engine, the legacy result stays authoritative/persisted, and any trade/metric/equity-curve divergence beyond tolerance is emitted as a structured metric — verified by a test asserting shadow mode runs both engines, returns the legacy result, and reports divergence without affecting the persisted run.
rationale: 291 only dark-reads the Parquet/DuckDB layer; nothing dark-runs the SoA/numba ENGINE against legacy in production before trusting it, so a numeric engine drift that escapes the CI golden suite would reach users with no prod safety net.

text: sweep_results rows MUST carry the same optimization fingerprint (engine git SHA + resolved optimization-flag set + data-provenance/manifest hash) as backtest_runs/backtest_results (reqs 278/318/430/395), added as additive nullable columns via the SAME idempotent v58 DDL and NULL-tolerant for pre-existing sweep rows — verified by a test asserting a persisted sweep combo records the fingerprint and that two sweeps run under different flag sets are distinguishable while old NULL-fingerprint sweep rows still load.
rationale: 430/318/394 stamp the fingerprint only on backtest_runs/results; sweep combos persist to sweep_results and would be un-attributable post-hoc, so a parity discrepancy found in a ranked sweep could not be traced to the code/flag version that produced it.

text: A sweep that is interrupted and RESUMED across a binary/flag change (deploy mid-sweep) MUST detect that already-completed (sweep_id, config_hash) combos were produced by a DIFFERENT engine git SHA / flag set than the resuming process and either re-run those combos for engine-generation consistency or refuse to merge them into one ranked set — so a single sweep's ranking can never silently mix two engine versions — verified by a test that writes combos under SHA-A, resumes under SHA-B, and asserts the ranked set is single-generation (re-run) or fails loudly, never a silent mix.
rationale: 306 proves crash-resume identity only under the SAME engine; a rolling deploy can swap the binary between combos, and reqs 28/76/317 rank across all combos, so a mixed-SHA ranking would compare incomparable numbers without detection.

text: The deferred seal-backfill completion marker (req 412/574) MUST be a persisted object CREATED by the same idempotent v58 DDL (CREATE TABLE/ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS), hold explicit enumerated states (not_started / in_progress / complete) plus progress counters, survive process restart, and be the SOLE gate for any 'cache-complete / fully-optimized' status — schema_version=58 MUST NOT imply the backfill is done — verified by a test booting at schema=58 with marker=in_progress and asserting status reports 'optimizing/partial' (not complete) and the marker is created idempotently on first and second migration runs.
rationale: 412/574 require a marker 'outside schema_version' but never pin that the marker's STORAGE is itself created by the idempotent v58 DDL, has defined restart-survivable states, and is the single authority for completeness — leaving room for schema_version=58 to be mistaken for backfill-complete.

text: The in-place seal-state RESET admin path (req 452) MUST also reset/clear the backfill-completion marker for the affected scope (or explicitly enqueue lazy reseal per #592) so that reset days are PROVABLY re-sealed on a subsequent run — they MUST never be left simultaneously unsealed AND covered by a marker that reports 'complete' — verified by a test that resets a sealed range, asserts the marker scope flips off 'complete', and that the next run re-seals the reset days (zero stuck-unsealed days).
rationale: 452 resets seal flags but says nothing about the new completion marker (412/574); if the marker still reads 'complete', operators/status would believe the cache is fully sealed while the just-reset days are unsealed, defeating remediation.

text: Migration v58 MUST take a SESSION-scoped Postgres advisory lock (pg_advisory_lock at session level, auto-released by Postgres on connection loss) — NOT a lock that survives a dead backend — so a migrating pod that crashes/OOMs mid-DDL or mid-backfill never leaves the fleet-wide migration lock held and never deadlocks surviving pods' boot — verified by a test that acquires the lock, kills the holding connection, and asserts a second migration runner acquires it and proceeds rather than blocking until lock_timeout/crash-loop.
rationale: 265/305/413 require an advisory lock and a lock_timeout but never specify session-scoped auto-release on crash; a transaction- or app-held lock orphaned by an OOM-killed migrator pod would block every other pod's startup until manual intervention.

text: Optimization-flag resolution PRECEDENCE MUST be deterministic, documented, and tested — master kill-switch (req 277) overrides per-path flags, which override the staged default, which overrides an unset/empty value resolving to the safe path (req 425) — and the fully-resolved snapshot MUST be the one persisted per run (req 278); a test MUST feed conflicting sources (e.g. master-off but per-path-numba-on) and assert the documented precedence wins and is the snapshot recorded.
rationale: 277/423/424/425/462 each constrain individual flag behaviors but none pins the ORDERING when multiple sources conflict, so a master kill-switch could be silently overridden by a per-path env var and an operator-intended safe revert would not actually take effect.

text: A re-apply-after-rollback cycle — v58 applied → application binary rolled back to legacy (schema retained per #432) → v58 application redeployed — MUST be a verified no-op on the second v58 boot: zero DDL re-execution (all IF NOT EXISTS), zero re-backfill (completion marker already 'complete'), schema byte-identical, and golden-master parity preserved — verified by a forward/back/forward migration-cycle test asserting no duplicate DDL, no second backfill pass, and oracle-identical results.
rationale: 432 covers a one-way rollback drill (flip kill-switch, retain schema) but not the FORWARD RE-DEPLOY after a binary rollback, where the second v58 apply must recognize completed state and not re-run DDL or re-backfill against an already-sealed manifest.

text: The deploy pipeline MUST run the v58 dry-run/pre-flight (req 289) as a GATING step that BLOCKS the apply when it reports sealed ranges that would land in kline_cache_default without a dedicated monthly partition (req 122/399), or symbol_lifecycle coverage gaps beyond a configured threshold — verified by a test that a dry-run reporting an un-partitioned >6mo sealed range or excessive lifecycle gaps causes the promotion hook to fail the deploy rather than proceed to apply.
rationale: 289 only requires the dry-run to EXIST and write nothing; nothing wires it into the deploy as a gate, so a risky migration whose dry-run flagged a partition/lifecycle hazard could still be applied blindly.

text: Deferred seal-backfill PROGRESS (days sealed, days remaining, marker state, elapsed) MUST be exposed on the queryable runtime status route (req 428), and an alertable structured signal MUST fire if the backfill has not reached 'complete' within a configured wall-clock budget (stuck/stalled-backfill detection) — distinct from the v58-DDL-failure alert (#429) and in-run degradation signals — verified by a test asserting progress fields are queryable and that a backfill exceeding the budget emits the stall alert while a nominal backfill stays silent.
rationale: 163 logs backfill counts and 574 makes the marker gate status, but no requirement surfaces live progress for a deferred async backfill nor alerts on a backfill that silently stalls (constant live-write contention via SKIP LOCKED, a wedged worker), which would leave the cache permanently sub-optimal undetected.

text: A CI migration PERFORMANCE gate MUST run v58 (DDL + chunked seal-backfill) against a committed representative-size kline_cache_coverage snapshot and assert the schema_version-advancing DDL is sub-second (constant fast-default, no table rewrite per #127) and the backfill completes within a committed wall-time/statement budget, failing the build on phase-over-phase regression — separate from the readiness-probe budget (#266) and the statement-count budget (#133).
rationale: 133 bounds statement count and 266 bounds readiness time, but neither is a committed REGRESSION gate on realistic data volume, so a later edit that turns the set-based backfill super-linear or adds a rewriting DDL could pass unit tests yet stall production boot.

text: The manifest content_sha256 (req 118/254) MUST be a pure function of the canonical candle bytes and INDEPENDENT of the engine git SHA, optimization-flag generation, or which code version sealed the day — so two different code versions concurrently sealing the same (symbol,interval,day) during a rolling deploy converge to the IDENTICAL hash and never trigger a spurious cross-version hash-mismatch refetch (req 296) — verified by a test computing the sha under two simulated code generations and asserting byte-equality.
rationale: 254 only proves fetch-vs-Postgres-rebuild sha equality within one version and 444 proves Phase-1-vs-Phase-5 equality; neither guarantees sha stability across the heterogeneous engine SHAs that coexist during a rolling deploy, where a version-dependent hash would make every overlap-window seal look corrupted and refetch.

text: The FIRST production deploy that carries the v58 schema MUST ship with every behavior-changing optimization flag defaulting OFF/dark (schema-first, behavior-later): the additive columns/tables and the seal-backfill land and bake in prod while the engine/cache/read-path behavior stays legacy-equivalent — verified by a test asserting the shipped first-deploy flag defaults reproduce the golden master byte-for-byte WITH the v58 schema present, decoupling the irreversible-ish migration from the reversible behavior change.
rationale: 152 documents/asserts a staged-rollout matrix but does not MANDATE that the schema-carrying deploy be behavior-dark; coupling the migration and the engine switch in one deploy means a rollback of the behavior also fights an un-baked schema, and a migration bug and an engine bug surface simultaneously and indistinguishably.

text: The master kill-switch and per-path optimization flags MUST be re-read at the START of each run (not cached once at process boot), so an operator flipping a flag (or the master safe-mode of #277) takes effect on the very NEXT run without a process restart or redeploy — verified by a test that mutates the flag env between two runs on the same long-lived process and asserts the second run honors the new value, while #424's within-run freeze still holds for any single run.
rationale: 424 freezes flags WITHIN a run and 277 defines a master switch, but neither states the resolver reads per-run rather than boot-caching; if flags were cached at boot, an emergency kill-switch flip would require a full restart/redeploy of a long-lived server, defeating its purpose as a fast revert.

text: Each persisted backtest/sweep result MUST record whether the seal-backfill completion marker was 'complete' at run time (and a manifest-coverage/seal state indicator), so a result computed DURING the backfill window — on a partially-sealed manifest that legitimately issued extra cold fetches and ran slower — is post-hoc distinguishable from a fully-warm-cache result, as additive nullable fields NULL-tolerant for old rows — verified by a test asserting the field reflects marker state and that a result produced mid-backfill is flagged.
rationale: 430 records schema_version-in-effect and 278 the flag set, but neither captures the BACKFILL-marker state, so a slow/extra-fetch result produced before the backfill finished would be indistinguishable from a true warm-cache run and could be mis-read as a cache regression.

text: A documented, tested admin path MUST REBUILD the sealed manifest (seal flags + fact columns first_open_ts/last_open_ts/candle_count/gap_count, NULL/sentinel sha) directly from the Postgres SOR (kline_cache + symbol_lifecycle) WITHOUT any Bybit refetch and WITHOUT un-sealing or dropping v58 columns/schema — distinct from the un-seal-then-refetch reset of #452 — so a corrupted or partially-written manifest is regenerable in place; the rebuilt manifest MUST be byte-identical (modulo NULL sha) to the v58 backfill output — verified by a test that corrupts manifest rows, runs the rebuild, and asserts manifest equality to the backfill with zero exchange calls.
rationale: 452 remediates by un-sealing and forcing refetch (network cost, re-download of data already in Postgres) and 592 only lazily seals on access; there is no in-place, network-free FULL manifest rebuild from existing Postgres rows for the case where the manifest itself is skewed/corrupt but the klines are intact.

text: The oracle-diff comparator MUST assert COLLECTION-LENGTH equality FIRST (trade-count, equity_curve point count, warnings count) and turn the gate RED on any extra or missing element, rather than zipping to min(len) and silently passing; a meta-test MUST perturb each collection's length (drop/add one trade, drop one curve point) and assert the comparator fails — complementing per-field perturbation (#346) and epsilon calibration (#522), which only catch value drift in equal-length collections, not an extra/dropped trade.
rationale: A SoA/parallel refactor is most likely to ADD a spurious same-bar exit or DROP a signal, changing list length; a comparator that compares element-wise up to the shorter length would pass while the engine silently opened one trade too many/few. No existing item proves the comparator detects length mismatch.

text: A perf-gate TEETH meta-test MUST inject a synthetic slowdown into the engine (an artificial O(N²) pass or a sleep proportional to candle count) and assert the benchmark/scaling regression gate turns RED — the performance analog of the parity mutation battery (#469) and the fast-but-wrong reconciliation check (#468) — proving the wall-time/engine-CPU regression gate is not vacuously green when the canonical fixture happens to stay under the absolute floor.
rationale: #468 makes a fast-but-WRONG engine fail on reconciliation (a correctness assertion), and #469 proves the PARITY gate has teeth, but nothing proves the PERFORMANCE regression gate actually fires on a real slowdown; a mis-wired or too-loose perf threshold could pass every phase undetected.

text: A degenerate signal_price from scan_source (≤0, NaN, or NULL) MUST be handled byte-identically to the legacy engine in BOTH the max_price_drift division (drift=(entry_fill−signal_price)/signal_price) and any sizing/mark math — guarded/skipped without raising and without fabricating a trade — frozen by a fixture carrying a zero/NaN signal_price and asserting identical disposition (e.g. signals_no_kline or a drift-reject) versus the oracle.
rationale: The optimized float64/searchsorted drift path could divide by zero or propagate NaN where the legacy Decimal path guarded it; no existing item pins parity for a degenerate signal_price input, only degenerate KLINE/empty-window inputs (#308) and the drift basis (#332/#568).

text: An EMPTY scan (a scan_source row containing zero signals) MUST be ordered (its first-signal-time sort key #237 is undefined), iterated, and anchored exactly as legacy — pinning whether an empty scan re-arms smart_drawdown_fired / re-anchors cycle_start_equity / advances the close-rule evaluation window, or is skipped entirely — frozen by a fixture chaining an empty scan between two trading scans, DISTINCT from the all-signals-filtered scan case (#499) where signals exist but are rejected.
rationale: #499 covers a scan whose signals are all filtered (signals present), but an empty scan has no first-signal timestamp to sort on and may take a different anchoring/iteration branch; the stable-sort and re-arm logic (#237/#499) is undefined for zero-signal scans and must be pinned so SoA/global-timeline ordering can't drop or mis-anchor it.

text: A MemoryError fault-injected mid-engine (or mid-SoA/timeline build) MUST fail the run cleanly: status='failed' with an actionable OOM message, the _MAX_CONCURRENT slot released exactly once, all run-scoped allocations freed, and the shared _load_klines LRU / Arrow hot cache / sealed manifest left UNCORRUPTED (a subsequent run reads byte-identical klines) — a fault-injection test analogous to mid-load timeout (#242) and accel-path-throws (#301), since OOM on the wide capacity point (#374) is a realistic large-run failure mode.
rationale: Failure injection covers timeout (#242), accel-throws (#301/#155), and fetch errors (#300), but never an out-of-memory error mid-build — the most likely failure on the wide-capacity path — leaving slot-leak and cache-corruption-on-OOM untested.

text: Migration (v58) tests and delete-before-insert persistence tests MUST run against an isolated/ephemeral schema or within a per-test rolled-back transaction, and a meta-test MUST assert that executing the full suite under randomized order (#518) and parallel workers leaves NO cross-test schema or row residue (the v58 migration test cannot leave columns/seals visible to another test; a persistence test cannot leave orphan backtest_* rows) — complementing the no-prod-DB guard (#474) and the cache-reset autouse fixture (#518).
rationale: #474 forbids pointing at prod and #518 resets in-process caches, but neither guarantees DB-state isolation between tests; a v58 migration test that ALTERs shared schema or a delete-before-insert test that leaves rows can make another test pass/fail based on execution order, especially under the mandated randomized-order/parallel lanes.

text: Stored golden-oracle artifacts MUST be persisted as human-reviewable, diffable canonical text (e.g. sorted-key JSON, one record per line where practical) — never an opaque binary blob or pickle — and a meta-test MUST assert each oracle parses, is valid UTF-8 text, and round-trips through the comparator; this makes an oracle change reviewable in a PR diff, complementing hash-pinning (#347) which DETECTS an oracle change but does not make it human-auditable.
rationale: #347 hash-pins oracles and #183 forbids hand-editing, but if oracles are stored as binary/pickle a reviewer cannot see WHAT a regen changed; a phase could regen an oracle to its own (wrong) output and the diff would be an unreviewable hash flip — reviewability is a distinct, testable property.

text: A READ-SIDE persisted-run consistency guard test MUST assert no completed backtest_runs row can exist with a backtest_results row but missing/mismatched backtest_trades or equity_curve (a torn/partial persist), that the three persistence writes commit in ONE transaction, and that the GET/render path surfaces such an inconsistency as an error rather than rendering a wrong result — the read-side invariant complementing the write-side 'no partial rows on timeout' (#363) and failed-run-retry cleanup (#531).
rationale: #363/#531 assert the WRITE side leaves no partial rows on failure/retry, but nothing asserts the READ side detects a results-without-trades inconsistency (from a crash between non-atomic writes) — a half-saved run could otherwise render a results header with zero/garbage trades and a frontend that trusts metrics.total_trades.

text: A CI-wiring meta-test MUST assert the per-phase PERFORMANCE/benchmark regression gate (not only the bit-identical parity gate of #198) is actually attached to every phase PR's workflow AND that the 'slow'-marked benchmark/scaling tests (#201/#312) are EXECUTED in the designated perf lane (not skipped in all lanes) — so the perf gate cannot silently stop running as more fixtures are marked slow, mirroring the silent-skip guard of #465.
rationale: #198 wires the bit-identical PARITY guard to every PR, but no item asserts the PERF gate is wired per-phase or that 'slow'-marked benchmarks actually run somewhere; a perf test marked slow could be collected-but-skipped everywhere and the regression gate would be green by never running.

text: The committed benchmark artifact (#35) MUST have a SCHEMA-SNAPSHOT contract test asserting it contains every required field for each phase (cold wall-time, warm wall-time, engine-only CPU time, peak RSS, Bybit call count, Postgres query count, candle-evals/sec, columnar bytes-read/row-groups #446, and the per-stage timing breakdown #481) with the same names/types — so a phase cannot quietly DROP a tracked metric from the artifact and thereby evade a gate that reads it.
rationale: #534 snapshots the per-RUN stats record and #361 tests harness-meter accuracy, but the BENCHMARK ARTIFACT's own field set (which the regression gates consume) has no schema-contract test; dropping 'peak RSS' or 'Bybit count' from the artifact would silently disable the RSS/cache gates.

text: Engine OUTPUT (ordered trades + ordered equity_curve + all metrics) MUST be byte-identical across two DIFFERENT PYTHONHASHSEED values run in separate interpreters on the canonical and a multi-position fixture — a direct executable test of the no-dict/set-iteration-order-leakage PROPERTY (#206) at the engine-output level, distinct from the config_hash cross-interpreter determinism of #348.
rationale: #206 states the property and #348 tests config_hash determinism across PYTHONHASHSEED, but nothing runs the ENGINE itself under two hash seeds to prove dict/set ordering (e.g. position book, funding dedupe, blacklist counters) never leaks into trade/curve order — the exact failure SoA/dict re-keying could introduce.

text: A fixture MUST pin the ENTRY-BAR drill case where the entry-fill bar's own [low,high] spans a TP/SL/liquidation barrier (the 'entry + its stop both inside one bar' trigger of #46): the engine MUST drill the entry bar, replay 1m sub-bars in chronological order resolving entry-fill THEN the first touched level with pessimistic liq→SL→TP precedence, and the result MUST be non-optimistic versus the always-LTF oracle — DISTINCT from #482 (same-bar exit eligibility resolved at 5m) and #175 (both-levels mid-life, not the entry bar).
rationale: #46 names 'entry+its-stop-both-inside' as a drill trigger but no FIXTURE freezes it; #482 pins same-bar exit eligibility on the un-drilled 5m path and #175 pins a mid-life both-levels bar — the drilled entry-bar (where fill and exit must be ordered within 1m sub-bars) is an unfrozen, optimism-prone edge.

text: A concurrent DELETE /backtest/{id} issued WHILE that run is still executing MUST resolve deterministically — either blocked until the run reaches a terminal state, or it cooperatively cancels (threading.Event) then deletes — leaving zero orphaned backtest_trades/results/equity_curve rows, releasing the _MAX_CONCURRENT slot exactly once, and never corrupting the shared caches/manifest; verified by a delete-during-run test, an operational edge beyond #407's post-completion delete.
rationale: #407 covers DELETE of a COMPLETED run; the race of deleting a run mid-execution (does it leak the slot, orphan partially-persisted rows, or race the engine's own persist?) is untested and is a realistic operator action during a stuck/slow run.

text: A meta-test MUST prove the close_reason enum-coverage UNION assertion (#345) has teeth: deliberately removing/disabling the single fixture that exercises one close_reason (e.g. mr_time_stop, equity_rise, breakeven_timeout, or PAUSE_TRADING #475) MUST turn the union-coverage gate RED — so a silently-untested exit branch fails CI rather than the union passing because another fixture coincidentally covered it.
rationale: #345 asserts the union of observed close_reasons covers the enum, but nothing proves that gate actually fails when a branch becomes uncovered; without a teeth meta-test the union assertion could be mis-computed (e.g. comparing against an under-specified enum) and pass vacuously.

text: The benchmark harness MUST exclude deterministic FIXTURE MATERIALIZATION (kline+signal generation from the committed seed #528, and one-time SoA/columnar build where it is explicitly out-of-scope per #219/#233) from the timed engine/region, and a meta-test MUST assert the timed window begins only AFTER fixture materialization completes — so a slow or regressed fixture generator can neither inflate a measured regression nor mask a real engine speedup.
rationale: #219 separates cold-first-combo from steady-state and #233 amortizes the columnar build, but nothing guards that the perf number's timed region starts after the (deterministic, repo-seeded) fixture is built; a generator slowdown could pollute the engine measurement and a generator that caches across iterations could fake a speedup.

text: When a run DEGRADES mid-flight (accel→pure-Python/Postgres per #301/#155), a test MUST assert the persisted per-stage timing breakdown (#549) and recorded numeric kernel (#496) reflect the ACTUAL executed fallback path, and that the WASTED accel-attempt wall-time is attributed to a distinct stage/field (not silently folded into the engine stage) — so a post-hoc latency triage of a degraded run is not misattributed and the per-stage budget gates (#222/#481) account for the failed attempt.
rationale: #496 records which kernel produced the numbers and #549 persists stage timings, but nothing requires the time SPENT on the failed accelerated attempt to be attributed separately; a degraded run could report an inflated 'engine' stage that breaches a stage budget or hides the cost of the failed attempt, making incident triage wrong.

text: A parity test MUST shuffle the PHYSICAL DB row order of the scan_source signals (and of kline rows) before the batched ANY($1)/range load and assert byte-identical engine output (trades + ordered equity_curve + metrics + signals_no_kline), proving the loader's deterministic sort (#447/#564/#237) never leaks the database's physical/return row order into per-symbol kline arrays, scan grouping, intra-scan signal admission order, or sizing depletion order.
rationale: #447 and #564 assert the batched loader RETURNS sorted/grouped data, but the strongest test — deliberately returning rows in a DIFFERENT physical order and asserting identical results — is not specified; ANY($1) gives no order guarantee, so a missing/incorrect ORDER BY would silently leak DB row order into path-dependent sizing and admission.

text: The STATIC symbol_whitelist / symbol_blacklist config fields (distinct from the adaptive blacklist of #80/#326) MUST filter signals at the exact legacy position in the 17-step chain with the exact legacy match semantics (whitelist = admit-only-if-listed, blacklist = reject-if-listed, case/format normalization preserved), and a whitelist/blacklist-rejected signal MUST increment the same per-scan/lifetime disposition counters (#563/#571) as legacy; verified by a paired admit/reject golden fixture asserting the admitted set and disposition map are byte-identical under the SoA/dict refactor.
rationale: symbol_whitelist and symbol_blacklist exist in the AutoTradeConfig surface (maxItems 200) and gate signal admission, but no requirement pins their parity — only the adaptive (own-trade-history) blacklist is covered. A SoA/dict re-keying could silently change membership matching.

text: The existing-symbol dedup MUST be against the currently-OPEN position book ONLY (not lifetime): a symbol whose position has CLOSED and is later re-signaled MUST be admitted and re-opened exactly as legacy, so the SoA/dict refactor cannot accidentally make dedup lifetime-scoped and drop legitimate re-entries; verified by a fixture chaining open→take_profit→re-signal→re-open and asserting the second trade exists and is byte-identical to the oracle.
rationale: #69 names 'existing-symbol dedup' but never pins that it is open-book-scoped, not lifetime. A natural-key set keyed by symbol could wrongly suppress all re-entries after first close, deleting trades while still passing single-trade fixtures.

text: skip_if_positions_open MUST skip a scan IF AND ONLY IF the open book is non-empty at that scan; with an EMPTY open book the scan MUST admit signals normally AND re-arm smart_drawdown_fired / re-anchor cycle_start_equity as a normal trading scan (NOT take the anchor-preserving skip branch of #71/#623); verified by a fixture interleaving an empty-book scan that admits trades and asserting it did not take the skip path.
rationale: #71/#623 pin the skip branch (anchor preserved, no re-arm) when positions ARE open, but the boundary — empty book ⇒ not skipped, normal re-arm/re-anchor — is unpinned. A refactor could over-apply the skip/preserve branch and suppress re-arm.

text: Engine behavior when sizing_capital (running wallet + carried uPnL − Σ locked_margin) goes ≤ 0 and when the running wallet goes NEGATIVE (cumulative liquidations/losses exceed starting_capital) MUST be pinned bit-identically to legacy — whether new signals are rejected, sized off a floored-to-0 capital, or the run halts — and the three-way reconciliation Σtrade.pnl == final_equity − starting_capital MUST still hold (Decimal-exact) with a negative final_equity; verified by a bankruptcy fixture that drives the wallet below zero.
rationale: No requirement pins the deeply-underwater / bankruptcy regime. sizing_capital ≤ 0 or wallet < 0 is a real path on a leveraged backtest, and the SoA/float64 refactor could change rejection behavior or break reconciliation sign handling — a silent divergence the bulk fixtures never reach.

text: The OUTER run-window boundary handling MUST be pinned: signals with signal_time exactly at date_range_start/date_range_end, candles with open_time exactly at each boundary, whether date_range_end is inclusive for candle evaluation, and the exact bar at which still-open positions are backtest_end force-closed (the final in-window bar's close) MUST be byte-identical to legacy; verified by boundary fixtures whose signals/candles sit exactly on date_range_start and date_range_end.
rationale: #244 pins INTER-scan window membership but the outer [date_range_start, date_range_end] inclusivity and the terminal force-close bar are unpinned. An off-by-one at the run boundary changes which signals enter and at what price open positions force-close.

text: When a LIFETIME target_goal (trade_count or profit_pct) halts the run mid-stream (#338/#559), the disposition of STILL-OPEN positions MUST be pinned bit-identically — whether they are immediately backtest_end force-closed at the halt point (and at which bar/price) or left open — and the terminal equity_curve point and reconciliation MUST reflect that choice; verified by a fixture whose goal is hit while positions remain open.
rationale: #338/#559 pin the granularity of WHEN the goal stops admitting, but not what happens to positions already open at the halt. Force-close-at-halt vs leave-open changes final_equity, the terminal curve point, and reconciliation.

text: The Sharpe/Sortino ANNUALIZATION factor MUST be simulation_interval-aware and frozen per interval (bars-per-year scaled for 5m/15m/1h/4h): the optimized engine MUST reproduce the legacy annualization constant for each interval bit-identically (within epsilon), distinct from the inter-point epoch-delta periodization of #492; verified by paired coarse-interval fixtures (#358) asserting Sharpe/Sortino match the oracle at each interval.
rationale: #492 pins the epoch-delta return basis but not that the annualization constant scales with the run interval. A coarse-interval run using a 5m-fixed annualization factor would silently mis-scale Sharpe/Sortino while passing the 5m gate.

text: The EXACT FORMULA of every summary metric in the ~45-key set MUST be frozen against the oracle — expectancy, avg_win, avg_loss, largest_win, largest_loss, gross_profit/gross_loss, profit_factor numerator/denominator, recovery/Calmar (if present), avg_trade_duration, and percent-time-in-market — including each denominator, sign convention, and whether breakeven trades are included (#620), beyond the degenerate-sentinel checks of #337; verified by a multi-outcome fixture asserting every metric value (not just its non-NaN-ness) equals the legacy computation.
rationale: #337/#356 pin only degenerate sentinels (zero-trade/all-wins/all-losses); the actual continuous formulas of the non-degenerate metrics are unpinned. A SoA/numpy metrics rewrite could change a denominator or breakeven-inclusion and drift a metric while still passing sentinel checks.

text: close_on_profit_pct cycle-termination semantics MUST be pinned: whether a close_on_profit basket-flatten zeroes cycle_start_equity (terminating the cycle) and re-anchors/re-arms on the next non-skipped scan like the non-smart equity rules (#45), or merely flattens positions without resetting the cycle; verified by a fixture chaining a close_on_profit flatten into the next scan's first entry and asserting the next-trade sizing reflects the legacy anchor state.
rationale: #43 pins the cascade ORDER (DROP→close_on_profit→RISE) and #45 pins non-smart cycle termination, but it is never pinned whether close_on_profit itself is a cycle-terminating rule. The post-flatten next-trade sizing depends on this and is path-dependent.

text: The BREAKEVEN price basis for BREAKEVEN_TIMEOUT (#57/#169) MUST be pinned: whether the mutated TP/SL 'breakeven' level is the gross entry_base_price or the entry adjusted for entry+exit fees+accrued funding (true net-zero pnl), so the post-timeout exit fill price and the recorded trade pnl are bit-identical to legacy; verified by a fixture that triggers the timeout and asserts the breakeven exit price and the resulting (near-zero) pnl match the oracle.
rationale: #57/#169 pin that TP mutates to breakeven and is skipped while trailing is active, but never the breakeven PRICE definition (gross vs net-of-fees). These differ by the fee/funding amount and change the recorded exit price and pnl.

text: The intra-scan sequential sizing/depletion ORDER (#560) MUST be pinned to the abs(score) admission-ranking order (with the index tie-break of #328), NOT the raw signal-list order: each k-th admitted signal's qty reflects the (k−1) already-opened positions processed in ranked order, so a SoA/dict refactor that sizes in a different order cannot change any qty; verified by a single-scan multi-signal fixture where ranked-vs-list sizing order changes a later signal's qty.
rationale: #560 pins that same-scan depletion occurs but not the ORDER signals are processed for sizing. Ranked-order vs original-list-order changes which capital each signal sees, altering qty — an order-sensitive divergence.

text: The per-scan current_time used for the sizing/uPnL mark ('last close ≤ current_time', #61/#606) and the max_signal_age basis (#333) MUST be pinned to its exact legacy value (the scan's first-signal signal_time per the stable sort #237), and ALL signals admitted in that scan MUST share that single mark while each still fills at its OWN signal_time (#62); verified by a fixture with intra-scan signals of differing signal_time asserting one shared mark but per-signal fill bars, byte-identical to the oracle.
rationale: #606 asserts current_time is 'fixed for that scan' but never pins its VALUE. If the refactor defines scan current_time differently (max signal_time, or per-signal), the sizing mark and age basis shift for every signal in the scan.

text: A position opened on the LAST in-window bar (entry-fill bar == the final candle) that is immediately backtest_end force-closed in that SAME bar MUST be reproduced bit-identically: entry==exit bar, fee/slippage treatment per #325, any funding boundary on that bar (#339), MFE/MAE per #535, and the three-way reconciliation; verified by a frozen fixture whose entry fills on the terminal bar.
rationale: The open-on-terminal-bar same-bar force-close is a real edge combining #325/#535/#68 that no single fixture exercises; the SoA refactor could mis-handle a zero-duration trade at the run boundary.

text: An IMMEDIATE same-bar liquidation on the entry-fill bar (high leverage where the entry bar's adverse extreme breaches the liquidation band) MUST close the position in its own fill bar with close_reason='liquidation', pnl = −locked_margin (no exit fee/slippage, #58), bit-identically to legacy — distinct from the SL-clamp case (#321, where a configured SL pulls inside the band) and the entry-bar drill (#687); verified by an SL-omitted high-leverage fixture whose entry bar liquidates.
rationale: #482/#687 pin same-bar EXIT eligibility and entry-bar drill, and #321 pins the SL-clamp, but none pins liquidation firing on the entry bar itself. This is reachable at high leverage and exercises the liq-on-entry-bar path under SoA searchsorted.

text: A NEGATIVE configured funding_rate_fixed_pct (the schema allows −0.5…0.5) MUST invert the funding direction bit-identically (long RECEIVES / short PAYS), with the magnitude and sign frozen and the three-way reconciliation holding with the inverted term, complementing the positive-rate short-receives case (#310) and the basis-pin (#558); verified by a negative-rate fixture across a funding boundary.
rationale: #310 freezes short-RECEIVES under a positive rate but the negative-rate flip (long receives) is unpinned. A sign error in the float64/numba funding helper would only surface on the negative-rate path.

text: At EXACT-EQUALITY boundaries the float64/numba path MUST reproduce the legacy admit/reject decision for the threshold filters — score == min_score, signal-age == max_signal_age_minutes, drift == max_price_drift_pct — honoring the legacy `>=` vs `>` inclusivity identically (extending the sizing exact-equality boundaries of #626 to the filter chain); verified by boundary fixtures sitting exactly on each threshold.
rationale: #367/#333/#332 pin these filters generally and #626 pins sizing exact-equality, but the filter-chain `>=`/`>` boundary at exact equality is unpinned. A last-bit float64 difference or a flipped comparator changes admission for an on-threshold signal.

text: The sweep/optimize_config OBJECTIVE-metric computation and the single-winner selection MUST be deterministic and oracle-matched: equal-objective ties resolve by a frozen key (config_hash or combo index) so optimize_config PROPOSES the identical winning config regardless of combo completion order, beyond the rank-stability of #317; verified by a tie-inducing sweep asserting the proposed winner is invariant across reordered completions.
rationale: #317 pins rank-list stability but optimize_config additionally collapses the ranking to ONE proposed config a human applies; the winner tie-break must be deterministic or the proposal flips between runs of an identical search.

text: The recorded trade entry_time and exit_time SOURCE MUST be pinned bit-identically — entry_time = the entry-fill bar's open_time (not signal_time, not scan current_time) and exit_time = the source time of the bar where the close_reason fired (intrabar exits use that bar's reference) — since trade-duration metrics and the equity-point ts (#633) derive from them; verified by a fixture asserting each trade's entry/exit_time equals the legacy source, not merely that #346's deep-equality passes.
rationale: #346 asserts deep equality on the time fields and #633 pins equity-point ts, but the SEMANTIC source of the trade entry/exit_time fields is unpinned. A refactor stamping signal_time vs fill-bar open_time would pass #346 against its own oracle yet diverge from legacy if the oracle were regenerated wrong.

text: The equity_curve for a ZERO-trade run MUST be frozen exactly: which points exist (the starting_capital seed anchor at the first scan's first signal_time per #240, and whether a terminal final_equity point is appended when no close ever occurs), so the no-trade curve and its path-dependent metrics (max_drawdown=0, Sharpe/Sortino sentinels) are byte-identical, beyond the metrics-present check of #308; verified by a zero-trade fixture hashing the ordered curve.
rationale: #308 asserts total_trades present-and-0 and #240 pins curve construction for trading runs, but the EXACT point set of a no-close run (seed only? seed+terminal-dedup?) is unpinned, and path-dependent metrics are computed over it.

text: A degenerate/unknown scan_source signal FIELD — malformed/missing side, an out-of-enum confidence, or a NULL/non-numeric score — MUST be dispositioned byte-identically to legacy (guarded/skipped without raising and without fabricating a trade, mapping to the legacy filter step or signals_no_kline), complementing the degenerate signal_price case (#678); verified by fixtures carrying each malformed field and asserting identical disposition and counters versus the oracle.
rationale: #678 covers only degenerate signal_price. Real stored scan rows can carry a missing side, an unexpected confidence string, or a NULL score; the optimized loader/engine must handle these exactly as legacy rather than raising or admitting a spurious trade.

text: A config that is BOTH direction='reverse' AND routes to mean_reversion MUST compose the transforms in the legacy order — the side-flip (#235) applied, then MR sizing with mr_capital_pct/mr_leverage and mr_tight_stop_pct override (#329), with signal_sides/drift still on the RAW pre-reverse direction and max_same_direction on the POST-reverse side — frozen so neither transform is dropped or applied in the wrong order; verified by a reverse-MR fixture against a stored oracle.
rationale: #235 (reverse) and #329 (MR sizing) are pinned independently, but their COMPOSITION is not. The SoA/numba refactor could collapse or reorder the pre/post-reverse direction usages specifically on the MR route, a path neither fixture exercises.

text: The oracle comparator MUST normalize signed zero (treat float64 -0.0 as equal to Decimal/oracle 0.0) for every money/excursion field, and a fixture producing an exactly-zero pnl/funding/mfe/mae on the float64/numba path MUST neither false-fail the bit-identical/epsilon gate nor persist a -0.0 in backtest_trades/equity_curve JSONB that byte-differs from the oracle's 0.0 or perturbs the full-JSONB manifest hash.
rationale: float64 arithmetic readily yields -0.0 where Decimal yields 0; a naive byte/hash comparison or JSONB round-trip would diverge on legitimately-zero fields. None of #199/#483/#494/#565 pin signed-zero normalization, so a benign -0.0 could false-fail the golden gate or destabilize the manifest hash.

text: Inf/NaN-valued metrics (e.g. profit_factor on an all-wins run, expectancy/Sharpe edge cases) MUST be coerced at the JSONB persistence boundary to a JSON-VALID documented sentinel (null or a fixed string), survive the Postgres JSONB write + GET read + frontend render without raising a JSON-parse/serialization error, keep metrics.total_trades present, and freeze that sentinel in the oracle — verified by a test that persists and re-GETs an Inf-profit_factor result.
rationale: Postgres JSONB rejects literal NaN/Inf, so an un-coerced infinite profit_factor would abort _persist_results or corrupt the GET payload. #204/#356 cover in-engine finite-guarding and #494 lossless round-trip, but none pin the JSON-illegal Inf/NaN value's serialized sentinel surviving the JSONB→GET→UI path.

text: The engine MUST NOT mutate its passed-in inputs (klines, signals, scan_contexts, instrument_info, config): a test runs the engine twice on the SAME in-memory objects and asserts byte-identical output AND that every input object is deep-equal to a pre-run copy — so an in-place sort/dedup/normalization cannot make a second run diverge or leak state across sweep combos that share the same immutable input references.
rationale: Sweep combos and warm reruns share input snapshots (#27/#217/#505); an in-place mutation (e.g. sorting signals, normalizing open_time) would silently corrupt the next combo. #351 makes SoA arrays read-only and #518 resets caches, but neither asserts the engine leaves its raw Python inputs unmutated.

text: A meta-test MUST assert that no golden/parity/cache fixture or stored-oracle artifact reads the wall clock (datetime.now/utcnow/time.time/date.today) — all temporal inputs come from frozen/injected clocks and committed data — so the suite's pass/fail can never depend on the calendar date it runs on, complementing the engine-clock-free rule (#493) and hermetic-data rule (#294) at the TEST-harness level.
rationale: A fixture that derives 'today' or a relative window from the real clock would pass in CI now and silently break on a future date (or shift the frontier/seal classification under test). The engine and data are guarded, but the test scaffolding itself is not.

text: An assertion-completeness meta-test MUST enumerate every registered golden fixture and fail CI if any fixture does not invoke ALL of: the full oracle-diff (19 trade fields + strategy_kind + ordered equity_curve + all ~45 metrics), the three-way reconciliation (Σtrade.pnl == net_profit == final_equity − starting_capital), and the metrics.total_trades-present check — so a fixture can never silently weaken to a pnl-only or metrics-only assertion as the battery grows.
rationale: #477 guarantees config-surface coverage and #689 proves enum-union teeth, but nothing guarantees each fixture actually applies the FULL comparator+reconciliation; a fixture asserting only pnl would pass while hiding mfe/mae/close_reason/funding drift.

text: optimize_config's PROPOSED AutoTradeConfig MUST be faithful to its measured result: backtesting the proposed config standalone (and re-reading the sweep's stored combo for that config_hash) MUST reproduce the per-combo metrics the optimizer reported — discrete fields bit-identical, continuous within the <1% epsilon — so the config a human applies to live trading reflects the numbers the optimizer ranked it on.
rationale: The optimizer proposes a config a human applies to the live scheduled scan; if the proposed config does not reproduce the ranked metrics (param drift, hash mismatch, snapshot skew), the operator acts on phantom numbers. #611 budgets optimize_config latency but no requirement pins proposal↔backtest result parity.

text: Service-vs-engine integration parity: the full POST /backtest pipeline (service-layer scan_source load + signal filtering + persist) MUST yield trades, ordered equity_curve, and all metrics byte-identical (discrete) / within epsilon (continuous) to a DIRECT in-process engine call on the same resolved inputs, and a single sweep combo's persisted result MUST equal a standalone single backtest_run of that exact config — proving the service/sweep wrappers add no divergence beyond the bare engine.
rationale: The golden battery exercises the engine seam (#476), and #207 covers parallel-vs-serial sweep, but nothing asserts the SERVICE wrapper (filter chain + persist) reproduces the engine output, nor that a sweep-combo path equals a single-run path — the two real entry points the UI and MCP tools use.

text: A K-combo sweep's persisted ranks MUST form a contiguous 1..K permutation with every combo present exactly once (no gap, no duplicate, none dropped, none extra), asserted independently of the tie-break key (#317) and the set-based rank UPDATE (#28) — so a VALUES-based bulk rank write can never silently drop, double-rank, or orphan a combo even under out-of-order completion.
rationale: #317 pins tie-break determinism and #28 pins write-count, but neither asserts the rank set is a complete contiguous permutation; a join/UPDATE…FROM(VALUES) bug could leave a combo unranked or two combos sharing a rank without violating either existing requirement.

text: A CI guard meta-test MUST assert the oracle-regeneration script (#183) is NEVER imported, invoked, or wired into any test, fixture, conftest, or CI gate lane — it is a manual-only entrypoint — so an optimization phase cannot auto-regenerate the stored oracles to its own (possibly wrong) output and turn the golden gate vacuously green, complementing the oracle hash-pin (#347).
rationale: #347 detects an oracle CONTENT change but cannot prevent the gate lane from regenerating oracles as a side effect; an accidental conftest import of the regen path (or a 'regenerate on mismatch' convenience) would re-baseline drift to green. The wiring itself must be guarded.

text: The Decimal-exact golden oracle for the canonical 5m no-drill path MUST be byte-identical across the Windows and Linux CI lanes (Decimal arithmetic is platform-independent): a test asserts the stored Decimal-oracle content hash matches on both OS runners, while float64/numba paths fall back to the per-arch epsilon policy (#302) — so the bit-identical gate is provably OS-portable given Windows dev + Linux prod.
rationale: The project is Windows-dev/likely-Linux-prod; #302 handles cross-ARCH float tolerance but never asserts the DECIMAL golden path is OS-identical. If the Decimal oracle were silently regenerated per-OS, a real cross-OS engine divergence on the exact path would be masked.

text: A run with exactly ONE closed trade (a single equity return, so return-stdev is undefined) MUST yield documented, oracle-frozen sentinels for stdev-dependent metrics (Sharpe, Sortino, and any volatility/expectancy denominator) — not NaN, Inf, or a raise — complementing the zero-trade (#356/#308), all-wins, and all-losses (#337) degenerate fixtures with the n=1 boundary that those do not cover.
rationale: Zero-trade and all-win/all-loss baskets are pinned, but the single-close case (n=1 returns → division-by-zero/undefined stdev in Sharpe/Sortino) is a distinct degenerate boundary that compute_all_metrics must sentinel deterministically; an unguarded n=1 would NaN-poison the metrics and route to the 'no trades' fallback.

text: A handle-leak soak test of N≥50 heterogeneous runs (mixing drilldown, columnar reads, and sweeps) MUST assert open file descriptors, Parquet/Feather mmap handles, DuckDB/Polars connections, and asyncpg pool connections all return to their pre-soak baseline with no monotonic growth — distinct from the RSS-stability soak (#226) and the pool-exhaustion-under-concurrency test (#373).
rationale: mmap'd Feather (#112/#440), long-lived DuckDB connections (#610), and atomic temp-files (#286) are all handle sources; a leak there exhausts FDs and pool slots on a long-lived server WITHOUT necessarily growing RSS, so the existing memory soak and single-shot pool test cannot catch it.

text: The GET-time equity_curve LTTB downsample MUST be deterministic: identical stored curve input yields byte-identical downsampled output across repeated GET calls and across separate processes/interpreters, so the rendered curve, its view-layer caching, and any client-side hashing are stable — complementing trough-preservation (#368) and the O(n) latency bound (#515).
rationale: LTTB bucket-boundary or tie handling can be order/seed-sensitive; if the same stored curve downsamples differently between calls or workers, the UI flickers and any client cache key churns. Determinism of the downsampler output is asserted nowhere (#368 only pins which extrema survive).

text: A sweep in which EVERY combo raises mid-execution MUST resolve to a single clean terminal state (failed or completed-with-empty-ranked-set per the documented contract), release all _MAX_CONCURRENT slots and pooled/ProcessPool workers, persist NO partial ranked set, and surface an actionable aggregate error — never hang, never leave the sweep 'running', and never leak a worker — complementing the one-combo-fails isolation test (#357).
rationale: #357 covers a single failing combo among successes; the all-fail case is a distinct terminal-state/cleanup path (does the orchestrator deadlock waiting for a ranked set that never materializes? does it leak the pool?) that operationally must fail loud and clean.

text: A zero-friction fixture (fee_rate_pct=0 AND slippage_bps=0 AND funding_rate_model='none') MUST yield each trade.pnl exactly equal to the raw entry→exit mark-to-mark price PnL (entry_fee==exit_fee==funding_paid==0) and satisfy the three-way reconciliation, freezing in the oracle that the fee/slippage/funding terms collapse to precisely zero with no residual rounding artifact — complementing the non-zero fee/slippage mechanics fixtures (#323/#324/#366).
rationale: The cost terms are always-on in the existing fixtures; the all-zero boundary verifies the float64/numba kernel produces clean zeros (not 1e-17 dust) for fee/slippage/funding so a deep run's reconciliation can't accumulate spurious sub-quantization drift, and that the basis math degenerates exactly to gross PnL.

text: optimize_config failure handling MUST be pinned: when its baseline backtest or required scan_source/snapshot load fails, optimize_config surfaces an actionable error and persists no partial proposal; when SOME sweep combos fail it still returns a ranked proposal over the SUCCESSFUL combos with the failures reported (not all-or-nothing) — verified by fault-injection on the baseline and on a subset of combos.
rationale: optimize_config layers a baseline run + sweep + rank + proposal; the failure modes of its EXTRA stages (baseline-run failure, partial-combo failure during ranking) are unaddressed by the sweep-only fault tests (#357), yet a half-built or silently-empty proposal handed to a human operating live trading is a real hazard.

text: A contract snapshot test MUST pin the COMPLETE GET /backtest/{id} response ENVELOPE shape (status, progress_pct, summary, warnings[], the metrics object, the equity_curve point schema, and the trades-pagination cursor schema) as additive-only, so the refactor cannot drop, rename, or retype any envelope-level field the frontend renders — broader than the metrics-keys superset (#203) and the total_trades presence guard (#202).
rationale: #203/#202 guard the metrics dict and total_trades, and #534 guards the obs-stats record, but the top-level GET envelope (status/summary/warnings/equity_curve-point/trade-cursor shapes the UI binds to) has no full-shape snapshot; a refactor renaming e.g. an equity_curve point key or the cursor field would break rendering while passing every existing contract test.

text: The builder-default hash-pin (#466) MUST be extended to the instrument_info and scan_context (regime) fixture builders: a meta-test asserts their default keyword values are byte-identical to a committed snapshot, since sizing/qty-flooring oracles (#336/#394/#631) and regime-active oracles (#236/#567) derive from these builders and an unnoticed default shift would silently re-baseline every dependent oracle.
rationale: #466 pins only _config/_signal/_klines builder defaults, but the instrument_info (qty_step/min_qty/tick_size) and per-scan ScanContext builders feed a whole class of sizing and regime oracles; a drift in their defaults would re-baseline those oracles exactly the way #466 prevents for the three it covers.

text: Always-on production instrumentation (the cache hit/miss + sealed/negative-cache counters #264, candle-eval throughput #510, per-stage timers #39/#549, Bybit/Postgres call counters, provenance #280) MUST add ≤ a documented bounded overhead (e.g. <2%) to engine wall-time and ≤ bounded allocations, verified by a counters-on vs counters-off benchmark on the canonical fixture — so observing the optimization can never defeat it (extends #209 which bounds only the progress callback).
rationale: The optimization adds many always-on production counters/timers beyond the progress callback. If each is sampled per-candle they can silently re-introduce per-candle Python work and erase the speedup; only the progress overhead (#209) is currently bounded, leaving the broader instrumentation ungated.

text: The never-resetting merge-walk price-location POINTER state (per-symbol advancing cursor indices into the shared read-only SoA arrays) MUST be per-run/per-combo mutable state initialized to 0 at each run's start and NEVER shared global mutable state across concurrent runs/sweep combos; a concurrency test MUST assert two combos sharing the same read-only arrays hold independent pointer arrays, neither corrupts the other's cursor position, and each combo's result equals its standalone oracle.
rationale: #307 enumerates per-run mutable engine state (blacklist, cycle_start_equity, smart_drawdown_fired, funding dedupe) but OMITS the merge-walk pointers — the very state RC-1/#63 rely on. If pointers are accidentally shared with the read-only SoA arrays across combos, concurrent sweeps corrupt each other's cursor (both a parity break and a perf regression forcing O(N) rescans).

text: On the pure-Python/Postgres fallback path (no zero-copy Arrow view per #437), the raw-kline→SoA conversion MUST release the raw row representation incrementally so transient peak RSS DURING the build stays ≤ ~1.5× the final SoA array size (no momentary 2× spike holding both raw dicts/lists and the int64/float64 arrays), verified by a tracemalloc/RSS-peak test over the conversion of a large symbol-range on the fallback path.
rationale: #31 forbids per-row dicts on the columnar path and #234 bounds per-candle loop allocation, but neither bounds the TRANSIENT build-time spike on the Postgres-read fallback, which must materialize rows before converting. A 2× spike at the wide capacity point (#374) could OOM the long-lived server even though steady-state SoA size is within the ceiling.

text: Per-scan ScanContext/regime construction (the fresh-per-scan_time context of #82/#567 driving regime/session/btc_vol gates) MUST be O(1)-amortized per scan with invariant inputs (e.g. the BTC/regime reference series) loaded/derived ONCE and reused across scans; a regime-ACTIVE scaling test MUST assert that doubling scan count S at fixed kline data grows total ScanContext-build time ≤ ~2× (linear in S, no super-linear term) and that it stays a bounded share of engine wall-time on the ~2160-scan canonical fixture.
rationale: #567 pins the regime classifier's PARITY but no requirement bounds the COMPUTE cost of building a fresh ScanContext per scan_time. On a regime-active 2160-scan run this is an O(scans) hotspot that can dominate after the seeding/setup fixes land, and a naive per-scan re-derivation of the BTC/regime reference series would be super-linear.

text: A rerun that ADDS or REMOVES symbols from a previously-run (symbols, interval, window) MUST load only the DELTA symbols from Bybit/Postgres and serve every unchanged symbol entirely from the warm in-process LRU/hot cache (0 Bybit calls and 0 kline SELECTs for the unchanged symbols); a test caches a 50-symbol set, reruns with 1 added + 1 removed symbol, and asserts only the 1 added symbol is fetched/loaded while the other 49 issue zero data I/O.
rationale: Warm-rerun guarantees cover same-symbols param change (#504) and sub-range containment (#503) but not symbol-SET deltas, which are the common sweep/iteration pattern (operator adds a symbol and reruns). Without this, adding one symbol re-pays the full 50-symbol load and breaks the warm-rerun budget.

text: The sweep's final cross-combo RANKING (sort + rank-stamp over K combos' metrics) MUST be O(K log K) compute over already-computed per-combo metrics with NO engine/backtest re-execution, and MUST stay a bounded share (e.g. <2%) of total sweep wall-time even at K=500; a test doubling K at fixed per-combo cost asserts ranking time grows ≤ ~2.2× and never dominates, distinct from the write-statement bound (#28) and rank determinism (#317).
rationale: #28 bounds rank WRITE statements and #317 pins rank determinism, but neither gates the rank COMPUTE cost. A re-rank that accidentally re-runs combos or sorts via an O(K²) comparison would silently inflate large-K sweep latency below the write/determinism radar.

text: Each completed sweep combo's RESULT objects (trade list + full equity_curve) MUST be persisted then released before the next combo's results accumulate, so peak process RSS does not grow with K full result sets held simultaneously; a ≥100-combo sweep memory test MUST assert in-memory retained result-set bytes stay bounded by ~concurrency×per-combo-result (not K×per-combo-result), distinct from the kline working-set flatness of #216.
rationale: #216/#314 bound the kline WORKING-SET RSS across combos and #547 covers progressive surfacing, but nothing bounds accumulation of the RESULT objects (trades/curves) themselves. A 500-combo sweep that buffers all result sets until the end would blow RSS independent of the kline cache.

text: Parquet row-group size MUST be tuned/bounded so that a narrow date-range predicate (a Phase-1 bounded per-symbol span, #19/#442) prunes at SUB-FILE granularity via row-group min/max stats — a single giant row-group per monthly file that defeats pushdown is forbidden; a test reading a small sub-range of a month partition MUST assert only the overlapping row groups are scanned (row-groups-read < total row-groups) and bytes-read scales with the requested span, complementing the within-file sort (#107) and the bytes-read gate (#446).
rationale: #107 sorts by open_time for stats pruning and #446 tracks bytes-read, but neither sets a row-group SIZE budget. With month-granularity partitions (#108), one row-group per file would force a full-month read for a 1-day span, silently defeating predicate pushdown while the partition-level pushdown still 'looks' correct.

text: content_sha256 for a sealed day MUST be computed in a single O(candles) pass folded into the ascending-normalize/store step (not a separate re-read/re-sort pass), and MUST stay a bounded share (e.g. <5%) of cold-ingest wall-time; a test on a dense day asserts hashing adds bounded cost and is not a second full scan of the candle set.
rationale: #254/#640 pin sha CORRECTNESS (canonical representation, ascending order) but no requirement bounds the CPU cost of hashing every sealed day on the cold path. A naive implementation that re-reads and re-sorts rows just to hash doubles the cold-ingest scan and inflates the network-bound cold budget.

text: Warm reads of a sealed day MUST NOT recompute content_sha256 by default — the stored manifest sha is trusted and hashing runs ONLY on the explicit integrity-check/escape-hatch (#296) or manual force-reverify (#397) path — so a warm rerun pays ZERO hashing cost; a test asserts a warm sealed-day read performs no sha computation (hash function call_count==0) while the escape-hatch path still recomputes.
rationale: If the loader recomputed sha on every read to 'verify' sealed days, each warm load would pay O(candles) hashing, silently defeating the <2s same-process and <5s cross-process warm targets (#3/#386). The integrity check must be opt-in, not on the default warm path.

text: Every production run MUST emit a mark-seeding / price-lookup OPERATION counter (number of searchsorted/merge-walk advances and per-scan window-setup ops), and a cache-efficiency-style gate (like #263/#446) MUST assert this counter scales as O(total_candles + P×S) and FAIL phase-over-phase on any growth toward O(P×S×T) or O(scans×N_total) — so a real-world regression of the O(P×T²) seeding or O(scans×N) setup fix is detectable in prod without re-running the scaling benchmark.
rationale: The user's headline targets are the O(scans×N) setup and O(P×T²) seeding eliminations, but these are only guarded by offline scaling tests (#6/#7/#12/#13). A production operation-count counter makes a re-introduced super-linear seeding/setup term alertable in real runs, mirroring how #279 makes the re-download bug detectable in prod.

text: optimize_config's baseline backtest and its subsequent N-combo sweep MUST share a SINGLE kline-load, raw→SoA conversion, and global-timeline build (the baseline does not load/convert data the sweep then reloads); a counter test on optimize_config asserts kline-load==1, SoA-build==1, and timeline-build==1 across baseline+sweep combined, extending the sweep-only sharing of #505 and the optimize_config latency gate of #611.
rationale: optimize_config (#611) runs a baseline THEN a sweep over the same data. #505 shares materialization across SWEEP combos but says nothing about the baseline run, so a naive pipeline pays the load/convert/timeline build twice (once for baseline, once for the sweep), inflating optimizer latency.

text: All multiprocessing.shared_memory segments backing the ProcessPool sweep's shared SoA/timeline (#609) MUST be explicitly closed and UNLINKED on sweep completion, 120s timeout-kill, AND cooperative cancel, so no /dev/shm (or Windows named-segment) leak accumulates across many sweeps; a soak test running N successive sweeps (including cancelled/timed-out ones) MUST assert the count of live shared-memory segments returns to baseline after each sweep.
rationale: #609 bounds shared-memory RSS during a sweep but not cleanup; #226 covers run-scoped allocation release but not OS-level shared-memory segments. A leaked /dev/shm segment per sweep would exhaust shared memory on a long-lived server over many sweeps — a failure mode invisible to in-process RSS soak tests.

text: The single-run pre-flight compute estimator (#224/#408/#517) MUST model drilldown-ON cost — incorporating an expected drilled-bar fraction (or a drill-on multiplier over the candles×scans base) plus the per-drilled-bar 1m replay cost — so a drill-enabled run's projected time is not under-estimated and timeout-killed; a calibration test on a drill-heavy fixture asserts the drill-aware estimate brackets actual within the documented factor while the drill-off estimate is unchanged.
rationale: The estimators (#224/#408/#517) model candles×scans and cold-fetch pages but ignore drill cost. A drill-ON run with a high ambiguous-bar fraction (#506) does materially more work per bar, so a drill-blind estimate can pass pre-flight then hit the 120s timeout-kill — exactly the outcome pre-flight exists to prevent.

text: Garbage collection MUST be managed across a full sweep workload (not only around a single-run kernel per #234): GC MUST be disabled/frozen or generation-tuned around each combo's kernel and the cumulative GC pause time across a K-combo sweep MUST stay below a documented bound, so per-combo latency variance from GC churn does not erode steady-state throughput; a ≥50-combo sweep test asserts total GC pause stays under the bound and per-combo wall-time variance from GC is bounded.
rationale: #234 freezes/bounds GC around ONE run's kernel, but a long sweep allocates and frees per-combo position books and result objects, accumulating cross-combo GC pressure. Unmanaged, GC pauses inject latency spikes that defeat the steady-state per-combo target (#219) even though each combo individually looks fine.

text: When a sweep combo fails with MemoryError mid-flight, the orchestrator MUST reactively REDUCE effective concurrency for the remaining combos (rather than re-dispatching at the same concurrency and repeat-OOMing), complete the remaining combos at the lowered concurrency, and record the throttle event; a fault-injection test forcing an OOM in one combo asserts the remaining combos still complete (none dropped), effective concurrency drops, and peak RSS stays under the ceiling — extending the pre-fan-out throttle (#551) and single-run OOM handling (#680) to the in-flight reactive case.
rationale: #551 throttles concurrency BEFORE fan-out and #357/#680 isolate a single failure, but none react to an in-flight OOM by lowering concurrency. Without reactive throttling, a sweep sized just over the RAM ceiling OOMs combo after combo at full concurrency, cascading failures instead of degrading gracefully.

text: For a wide run that exceeds the in-RAM SoA ceiling and is executed via the bounded symbol-batched/streamed loading path (the non-reject branch of #374), that streamed path MUST meet its OWN documented (relaxed) latency budget, process symbols in bounded batches so peak RSS never materializes the full SoA at once, and release each batch before loading the next; a wide-fixture test asserts the streamed run completes under its assigned cap (#544), stays under the RSS ceiling throughout, and never holds more than one batch's SoA resident.
rationale: #374 mandates reject-or-batch and bounds RSS, and #544 makes the cap configurable, but no requirement gates the LATENCY of the streamed path or asserts it never transiently materializes the full SoA. A streamed path that loads all symbols then 'frees' them defeats the memory goal, and one with no latency budget can silently run for minutes.

text: The benchmark harness MUST report BOTH the median-of-N (after discarding warmup, the flake-resistant signal of #213) AND the p95 over N iterations, and the gates MUST consume them per their defined contract: the relative phase-over-phase regression gate (#36) and the ≥100× claim (#38) use the MEDIAN, while the production-SLO/concurrency gate (#387) uses the p95-under-concurrency figure; a meta-test asserts the harness emits both statistics and that each gate reads the correct one, removing the #213-vs-#387 statistic ambiguity.
rationale: #213 mandates median-of-N for flake resistance while #387 mandates p95-under-concurrency as the SLO — two different statistics that are currently unreconciled, so an implementer could use one figure for both gates and either mask tail-latency regressions (using median for the SLO) or false-fail on noise (using p95 for phase-delta). Pinning which statistic feeds which gate makes the perf gates unambiguous and testable.

text: Cold gap-fill store_klines MUST persist fetched candles via a batched bulk insert (COPY or executemany over each contiguous gap-run page), so a cold N-candle fetch issues O(pages/gap-runs) INSERT round-trips, not O(candles); a test cold-fetching a multi-thousand-candle span against a counting DB wrapper MUST assert the kline-row INSERT statement count is ~ceil(candles/page) and independent of candle count.
rationale: Reqs 508 (trades), 590/643 (seal upserts) batch their writes, but the kline-ROW write on a cold run is never required to be batched. A 90d×50sym cold run inserts ~1.3M candles; per-row INSERTs would dominate the cold budget and hold the asyncpg connection far longer, starving the live pool.

text: A warm identical rerun MUST issue ZERO Postgres WRITES — 0 store_klines inserts, 0 manifest seal upserts, and 0 coverage (GREATEST) upserts — asserted by a write-statement counter, distinct from the existing 0-Bybit-call/0-kline-SELECT read guarantees; a test reruns a fully-sealed config and asserts the write counter stays at 0.
rationale: Reqs 3/17/77/190 only gate reads and exchange calls. A regression where a warm rerun re-issues idempotent ON-CONFLICT-DO-NOTHING/GREATEST upserts (no data change, but real write-lock traffic on the shared coverage table) would pass every existing gate while degrading the live trading path.

text: A candle whose open_time is NOT congruent to 0 mod the interval T (off-grid/misaligned timestamp from the exchange) MUST be handled by a single documented, oracle-frozen rule (snap-to-grid, reject-and-log, or pass-through) applied identically in day-bucketing, candle_count, gap detection, content_sha256, and the SoA epoch arrays; a test feeding a misaligned candle asserts byte-identical day-key, candle_count, sha, and engine output versus the legacy path.
rationale: The entire sealed-manifest/day-bucket/candle_count/sha model assumes candles land on the interval grid. Exchange data occasionally carries off-grid or duplicated-shifted timestamps; an unspecified rule would silently corrupt candle_count, gap classification, and the content hash (spurious mismatch refetch), and could shift searchsorted entry-bar selection — no requirement currently pins this.

text: The PRECISE boundaries of every known-empty sub-range within a sealed gap day (pre-listing fringe, post-delist fringe, confirmed interior structural hole) MUST be persisted in a queryable structure (a gap-ranges column/table), NOT merely as gap_count, so the count-free predicate can skip exactly those sub-ranges and still fetch/serve any genuinely-missing in-life sub-range; a test on a day with two disjoint holes asserts the exact holes are zero-fetch while the present candles serve and no non-hole sub-range is suppressed.
rationale: Req 599 requires serving present candles and 0-fetch for 'recorded known-empty sub-ranges' and 260 records gap_count>0, but neither specifies WHERE the sub-range boundaries live. A bare gap_count cannot tell the loader WHICH sub-ranges to skip vs fetch, making 599 unimplementable and risking either perpetual refetch or suppression of real candles.

text: content_sha256 MUST define a canonical sentinel hash for a zero-row (fully known-empty) sealed day (e.g. the hash of the empty canonical byte sequence), so pre-listing/post-delist/empty-outage days hash deterministically and the integrity escape-hatch (#118/#296) treats an empty-day hash as a normal comparable hash; a test seals the same empty day twice, asserts identical sha, and asserts zero refetch on the integrity path.
rationale: The sha/integrity machinery (118/254/296) is specified only for days with rows. A negative-cached or outage day has zero rows; without a defined empty-day canonical hash the integrity hatch could treat it as 'no hash → no check' inconsistently, or a NULL-vs-sentinel ambiguity could trigger a spurious refetch of a provably-empty day.

text: Postgres (the SOR) MUST carry a monotonic data-generation/epoch token, every derived artifact (Parquet/Feather/Arrow file, in-process LRU/hot/1m entries) MUST embed the generation it was built from, and a read whose artifact generation does not match the current SOR generation (e.g. after a Postgres PITR/restore that rewinds or corrects klines) MUST invalidate+rebuild rather than serve data now AHEAD of or inconsistent with the SOR; a test restores Postgres to an earlier generation and asserts the columnar/in-process caches self-invalidate and re-derive.
rationale: Format-version (256/287) and per-day sha (255/602) catch schema/corruption, but not the operational case where Postgres is restored/PITR'd while the separate derived columnar volume and long-lived in-process caches retain NEWER (now-orphaned) data. Backups/restores are a routine ops event for a write-of-record DB and would otherwise produce silent SOR-vs-cache divergence.

text: Bybit throttle/outage backoff state MUST be SHARED across concurrent runs/sweep combos as a process-level circuit breaker (open on sustained 429/5xx, half-open probe, close on success) so that when the exchange is degraded the aggregate retry/fetch load is bounded fleet-wide rather than each cold run independently hammering; a test that makes the mocked client fail asserts concurrent cold loads trip a single shared breaker and total attempts stay within the configured bound.
rationale: Req 229 bounds per-fetch concurrency and 247 bounds a single run's backoff, but nothing coordinates BACKOFF state across the _MAX_CONCURRENT runs + sweep workers. During a real Bybit outage, N independent runs each retrying would multiply load, blow the cold budget, and risk an IP ban — a shared breaker is the standard mitigation.

text: Forked ProcessPool sweep workers MUST NOT read from or mutate the parent process's in-process Python dict caches (_load_klines LRU, 1m-drill cache, raw→SoA memo, derived-coarse memo) inherited copy-on-write at fork; workers MUST access shared kline/SoA data only via the explicit shared-memory arrays (#609) and treat their inherited dict caches as empty/cleared; a test asserts a forked worker never serves a value from an entry that existed only in the parent's dict cache and that worker writes do not COW-balloon RSS.
rationale: Req 609 covers shared-memory ARRAYS, but on Linux fork the children also inherit the parent's mutable Python DICT caches copy-on-write. If a worker reads a stale inherited entry (or mutates one, triggering COW page duplication and RSS blow-up), results/perf diverge from the spawn (win32) path — a subtle fork-vs-spawn parity and memory hazard unaddressed.

text: An extended multi-day IN-LIFE empty stretch (a confirmed exchange outage or a genuinely no-trade ultra-low-volume contract producing zero candles across whole days within [listing,delist]) MUST be sealable per-day as known-empty (gap_count==expected, zero rows) and MUST never become a perpetual refetch candidate, distinct from pre-listing/post-delist negative caching; a test seeds a 3-day in-life zero-candle stretch and asserts each day seals known-empty and reruns issue 0 Bybit calls for them.
rationale: Reqs 90 (pre-listing/post-delist) and 260 (interior hole with gap_count>0 on a partially-present day) do not cover a WHOLE in-life day (or run of days) that is legitimately empty. Without an explicit rule such a day looks identical to 'never fetched' and would be re-downloaded on every run forever.

text: The committed deterministic fixture generator MUST also materialize reproducible MANIFEST-STATE seeds — a mix of sealed-full, sealed-short(listing), sealed-with-gap, negative-cached(pre-listing/post-delist), and forming/live-edge days — so the seal/frontier/negative-cache/lazy-seal cache-gate tests run fully offline and deterministically; a test asserts the manifest fixtures load with zero DB/exchange dependency and exercise each day-class.
rationale: Reqs 294/528/657/658 make the KLINE+SIGNAL data offline-reproducible but say nothing about reproducible MANIFEST state. Cache-gate tests (sealed-once, count-free predicate, frontier boundary, lazy-seal) need deterministic seeded manifest rows; otherwise they depend on incidental DB state and become flaky/non-hermetic.

text: The cache-efficiency regression gate MUST additionally track and gate, phase-over-phase on the canonical fixture, the COUNT of Parquet/Feather file opens per run (must stay ≤ months_touched × symbols_touched, i.e. month-granular, never per-day) so a partition-granularity or open-per-day regression is caught even when bytes-read and row-groups-scanned (#446) appear unchanged; a test asserts the file-open count is month-granular and does not grow with day count.
rationale: Req 446 gates bytes-read and row-groups-scanned but not file-open COUNT.
