A regression that re-opens the same month file once per requested day (or falls back to day-granular partitioning, violating #108) inflates syscall/footer-parse overhead while bytes-read stays flat — invisible to the existing gate.

text: The batched symbol=ANY($1) loader MUST return a (possibly empty) array entry for EVERY requested symbol — including symbols with zero rows in the window (negative-cached, not-yet-listed-in-window, or holed) — so the symbol→contiguous-int-code mapping (#603) stays dense and stable and no symbol is silently omitted/reindexed; a test loads a symbol set where some have zero rows and asserts the returned mapping covers all requested symbols in stable order with empties preserved.
rationale: Reqs 447 (per-symbol bucketing) and 603 (int-code mapping) assume every requested symbol yields an array, but neither requires the loader to EMIT empty symbols rather than drop them. Dropping a zero-row symbol would shift int-code assignment, mis-key the position book/hot loop, and change signals_no_kline accounting — a silent parity break on misaligned/listing-edge fixtures.

text: The durable columnar hive layout MUST include `interval` in the partition key (e.g. symbol=<SYM>/interval=<I>/year=<Y>/month=<M>/part.parquet) or otherwise guarantee single-interval files, so the stored fine base, any legacy-fetched native-coarse rows (#455), and auxiliary-series intervals (B&H/btc_vol/MR-mean at their own intervals, #355) can never collide at the same symbol/month path; a test materializes two intervals for one symbol/month and asserts they land in distinct, non-overlapping partition files.
rationale: Req 107 specifies symbol/year/month with NO interval in the path. While 116 mandates a single stored BASE granularity, reqs 455 (legacy native-coarse rows must be sealed) and 355 (auxiliary series at non-run intervals) mean multiple intervals can be materialized; without interval in the path they would overwrite/merge into one file and silently corrupt reads.

text: A backtest whose requested window lies ENTIRELY within the current forming/live-edge UTC day (an intraday backtest of 'today') MUST run correctly served exclusively from Postgres with 0 sealed days, 0 columnar artifacts written, 0 negative-cache entries, and 0 days sealed, while still producing a real result with metrics.total_trades present; a dedicated fixture/test asserts this all-forming-day path issues no seal/columnar writes and is byte-identical to the legacy engine.
rationale: Reqs 594 (future clamp) and 439 (straddle boundary) cover windows that PASS THROUGH the forming edge, but not a window wholly inside today's forming day — a common user case (intraday backtest). This path must never seal/materialize a forming day (#435/#443) yet must still serve and simulate; no requirement pins the all-live-edge case explicitly.

text: Every production run MUST emit additive structured DATA-QUALITY counters NOT already in #264/#534 — off-grid/rejected candle count, candles clipped as forming pre-store (#435), runtime lazy-seals performed (#592), columnar partitions rebuilt/self-healed (#255/#602), and Postgres-fallback-for-sealed-day events (#251) — and these fields MUST be pinned by the observability schema-snapshot contract test (#534) so a data-quality regression is attributable without re-running the benchmark; a test asserts each counter reflects a seeded condition.
rationale: Req 264/534 enumerate cache hit/miss, sealed-this-run, negative-cache skips, Bybit calls, and bytes-read, but omit data-quality/self-heal signals (off-grid rejects, forming-clip, lazy-seals, rebuilds, sealed-day Postgres fallbacks). These are exactly the events that reveal a silent cache-correctness or data-integrity drift in production.

text: An mmap'd Feather V2 / Arrow IPC artifact MUST be validated for byte-order (and Arrow schema) compatibility with the reading host before zero-copy use; on a byte-order or arch mismatch (cross-arch CI/deploy per #302) the reader MUST invalidate+rebuild from Postgres rather than mis-read, and the int64-epoch/float64-OHLC zero-copy view MUST be asserted correct on each CI arch; a test writes an artifact tagged with a mismatched byte order and asserts the reader rebuilds instead of returning garbled columns.
rationale: Req 256/287 cover format/schema versioning and 302 runs a multi-arch CI matrix, but nothing requires the mmap zero-copy path to validate endianness/byte-order. A Feather file built on one arch and mmap-read on another (mixed CI runners, ARM deploy) could be silently misinterpreted at the byte level — a correctness hazard unique to the zero-deserialization mmap path.

text: A STRICT-OFFLINE / cache-only run mode MUST exist (config/env flag) that serves only from the manifest/Postgres/columnar tiers and FAILS LOUD with an actionable miss message on any cache miss instead of issuing a Bybit fetch, enabling deterministic incident-replay and a hermetic CI lane that proves a warm run needs no exchange; a test runs a fully-warm config in offline mode (asserting success, 0 Bybit calls) and a partially-cold config (asserting a clear miss error, not a silent fetch).
rationale: The suite mocks Bybit (#293), but production reproducibility/incident-replay needs a real run-mode that guarantees zero exchange dependence and turns an unexpected miss into a loud, debuggable error rather than a silent (and slow/non-reproducible) network fetch. No requirement provides an operator-facing no-fetch replay/verify mode.

text: A backtest's read of the still-forming (Postgres-only) day MUST be a consistent point-in-time snapshot (single query or repeatable-read transaction) so the live scanner/auto-trade path concurrently upserting the forming candle cannot expose a torn/half-updated forming bar to the engine; a test issues concurrent live-style forming-candle upserts during a backtest's forming-day load and asserts the loaded bar is internally consistent (never a mix of pre- and post-upsert OHLC) and byte-identical to one committed snapshot.
rationale: The forming day is shared read/write between the live scanner (continuous upserts) and a backtest read (kline_cache_service is shared, per #281/#419). Without a snapshot/isolation guarantee, a backtest could load a forming bar mid-upsert and get inconsistent OHLC, producing a non-reproducible result — a concurrency edge no requirement currently pins for the read side.

text: The deploy pipeline MUST include a guard that BLOCKS promoting an application binary whose max-supported schema_version is LOWER than the live DB's schema_version (which crash-loops at boot because the migration runner RAISES RuntimeError on a newer stored version per async_persistence.py:1617-1621), failing the deploy with an actionable message that directs the operator to the master kill-switch (keep-v58-code, optimizations-off) rollback path instead of a structural downgrade; verified by a test that a pre-v58 binary targeted at a v58 DB is rejected by the promotion hook (req 414 only documents the constraint; this enforces it in CI/CD).
rationale: Req 414 explicitly flags that 153/276/290 assume a graceful no-op the runner does not perform and concludes binary-downgrade-below-v58 is forbidden, but no requirement makes the deploy pipeline actually PREVENT such a deploy — leaving a crash-loop reachable by an ordinary rollback.

text: The master kill-switch and per-path optimization flags MUST be operable WITHOUT a process restart/redeploy: they MUST be backed by a re-readable source (a DB config row or a watched config file re-read at each run start per req 673), NOT solely a process-start environment variable (which cannot change without restarting the container), so an operator can disable a misbehaving optimization within seconds during an incident; verified by a test that flipping the persisted config value takes effect on the very next run on the same long-lived process with no restart, while req 424's within-run freeze still holds.
rationale: Reqs 277/425/462/673 frame kill-switches as 'env var / config flag' and require per-run re-read, but a process-start env var is immutable without a restart — making the promised 'flip a switch in an incident' impossible in practice unless the source is DB/file-backed.

text: Migration v58 MUST detect when a target manifest column (sealed/first_open_ts/last_open_ts/gap_count/listing_snapped/delisted/content_sha256/fetched_at) ALREADY EXISTS with an INCOMPATIBLE type/width (e.g. a pre-existing manually-added sealed INT, or first_open_ts INT4) — because ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS silently SKIPS the column and leaves the wrong type — and MUST fail-loud with an actionable message (or be documented-and-tested as safely reconciling it), verified by a test that pre-creates a manifest column with a wrong type and asserts v58 does not silently proceed on the incompatible column.
rationale: Reqs 126-128/441 verify idempotency and correct types on a CLEAN DB, but ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS is a silent no-op against a pre-existing same-named column of a different type — a real hazard given §9's history of parallel branches touching schema.

text: The columnar (Parquet/Feather/Arrow) format-version guard MUST reject NEWER/UNKNOWN format stamps as well as older ones, invalidating+rebuilding from Postgres rather than mis-reading — so during a rolling deploy where a newer-format pod has already written a file, an OLDER-format pod that reads it does not garble columns; verified by a test that an older reader encountering a higher/unknown format stamp falls back to a Postgres SOR-identical rebuild instead of returning wrong data (req 256/287 only cover a newer reader handling an older/incompatible file, not the reverse overlap direction).
rationale: Reqs 256/287 cover new-code reading old-format files, but the rolling-deploy overlap also has OLD-code reading NEW-format files; unless the FIRST shipped format already rejects unknown/newer stamps, an older pod silently misreads a newer file written by a peer.

text: The sealed-manifest read/seal path MUST tolerate Postgres read-replica lag: a day sealed on the primary but NOT YET visible on a lagging read replica MUST be classified UNSEALED/refetchable (count-free, correct-but-slower) and MUST NEVER raise or be treated as an integrity error, i.e. the manifest logic must not assume read-your-writes across primary/replica; verified by a test simulating a replica that lacks a just-sealed row and asserting the loader degrades to a safe refetch/read rather than erroring or serving inconsistent state.
rationale: No requirement addresses read-replica lag; if any manifest read is served by a replica during/after deploy, a not-yet-replicated seal row could be misread as an error or inconsistency unless explicitly treated as unsealed.

text: The v58 advisory-lock strategy MUST be verified compatible with the production connection-pooling MODE: session-scoped advisory locks (req 665) behave incorrectly under transaction-level pooling (e.g. pgBouncer transaction mode), so if transaction pooling is in use the migration+backfill locks MUST be acquired on a dedicated DIRECT (non-pooled) connection; verified by a test (or documented+asserted deploy contract) that the migration runner uses a direct connection for advisory locking and that two concurrent runners still serialize correctly under the prod pooling mode.
rationale: Reqs 265/305/413/665 specify session-scoped advisory locks but none verify those locks behave correctly under the production connection-pooling mode; pgBouncer transaction pooling silently breaks session-scoped advisory locks.

text: The sealed-manifest Postgres table (rows keyed (symbol,interval,day_utc)) MUST have a documented growth bound and a retention/archival policy for PERMANENTLY-delisted/dead symbols, so the manifest table itself does not grow without limit over years of universe churn; archived/pruned dead-symbol manifest rows MUST be re-derivable from the Postgres SOR (req 675) if ever needed, and a test MUST assert manifest row count is bounded by (live-universe × in-life history) plus a bounded retention tail, distinct from the Parquet/Feather disk GC of reqs 261/285/403.
rationale: Reqs 261/285/403 bound the durable columnar (file) cache, but the Postgres manifest TABLE (one row per symbol×interval×day forever) has no growth/retention requirement and accumulates dead-symbol rows indefinitely.

text: The deferred v58 seal-backfill MUST persist a resumable CHECKPOINT/CURSOR (e.g. last-processed (symbol,interval,day_utc) chunk boundary) so that after an interruption/restart it RESUMES from the checkpoint and SKIPS already-sealed chunks rather than re-scanning the entire kline_cache_coverage from the start, bounding total backfill work to O(unsealed days) across any number of restarts; verified by a test that interrupts the backfill mid-way, restarts it, and asserts it does not re-process already-sealed chunks (efficiency), beyond mere idempotency (req 132) and progress counters (req 663).
rationale: Reqs 132/574/663 guarantee the backfill is idempotent, auto-resuming, and reports progress, but none require it to resume EFFICIENTLY from a checkpoint — a naive idempotent re-scan from row 0 on every restart could repeatedly re-touch a huge coverage table and never finish on a crash-looping pod.

text: A boot-time self-check MUST assert the ACTUALLY-IMPORTED versions of numba/llvmlite/pyarrow/duckdb match the committed hash-pinned lockfile versions (req 269), catching a stale container image or a drifted/partially-rebuilt layer where the running wheels differ from what CI resolved; on a benign mismatch it MUST emit a structured warning, and on an ABI-breaking mismatch (e.g. numba/llvmlite/numpy incompatibility) it MUST DISABLE the JIT/columnar path and fall back rather than risk a runtime crash; verified by a test simulating an imported version ≠ pinned version and asserting the warning/disable behavior.
rationale: Reqs 146/269/270 verify wheel availability and lockfile sync at BUILD time, but nothing verifies at RUNTIME that the deployed process actually imported the pinned versions — a stale image or drifted layer can run mismatched wheels that CI never saw.

text: The copy-prod-scans operational workflow (copying production scan/kline data into a dev/test/staging environment) MUST rebuild the sealed manifest in the TARGET environment from the copied Postgres rows via the in-place rebuild path (req 675) WITHOUT any Bybit refetch, so the target env does not re-download already-copied sealed days on its first backtest; verified by a test that a fresh manifest-less environment seeded with copied klines seals from the SOR with zero Bybit calls and a subsequent backtest issues zero sealed-day refetches.
rationale: The project has a copy-prod-scans skill/workflow, and req 675 provides a manifest rebuild mechanism, but no requirement WIRES the rebuild into the copy workflow — copying klines without the manifest would make the target env re-fetch every sealed day, silently violating the warm-cache guarantees in non-prod envs.

text: There MUST be a documented, tested operational path to IDENTIFY and optionally BULK-RE-RUN/invalidate backtest_results+sweep_results produced by a specific engine git SHA / optimization-flag generation later found defective (using the persisted optimization fingerprint of reqs 278/318/496/661), so subtly-wrong-but-within-tolerance results from a bad phase can be remediated rather than silently retained as authoritative; verified by a test that flags a generation, enumerates exactly the results carrying that fingerprint, and re-runs them to corrected values without touching results from other generations.
rationale: Reqs 278/318/496/661 RECORD the fingerprint so a discrepancy is traceable, but no requirement provides the downstream REMEDIATION path to find and re-run the poisoned result population once a phase is found defective.

text: The background symbol_lifecycle population/refresh task (req 583) MUST NOT clobber operator-corrected listing_time/delist_time values: refresh MUST upsert exchange-discovered values only where no manual override exists (or carry a manual-override flag/precedence), so a hand-fixed listing date for a mis-reported symbol survives the next refresh; verified by a test that manually corrects a lifecycle row, runs the refresh task, and asserts the override is preserved (not overwritten by the exchange value).
rationale: Reqs 259/272/583 populate and refresh symbol_lifecycle from exchange info, but none protect a manually-corrected row from being silently overwritten by the recurring refresh — a real operational hazard when the exchange's reported listing/delist is wrong and an operator fixes it.

text: Every NEW NUMERIC operational config (sweep concurrency cap, per-tier cache byte/entry budgets, 1m-drill cache ceiling, negative-cache/gap TTLs, frontier skew_margin, Postgres statement_timeout, thread caps, pre-flight thresholds) MUST have a documented default and be RANGE-VALIDATED at boot — an out-of-range/negative/zero/non-numeric value MUST be clamped-to-safe or rejected with a structured warning, never silently applied as a pathological value (e.g. concurrency 0 hanging the sweep, a 0-byte cache thrashing, a negative skew sealing the live edge); verified by a test feeding out-of-range numeric configs and asserting safe clamping/rejection plus a warning.
rationale: Reqs 425/462 cover boolean flag PARSING (on/off, typo names) and 587 covers the cache DIR, but no requirement validates the NUMERIC operational configs' ranges — a misconfigured budget/concurrency/TTL/skew could silently degrade correctness or hang runs.

text: When the master safe-mode kill-switch (req 277) is engaged during an incident, the system MUST (in addition to routing the NEXT run to the legacy path) be able to COOPERATIVELY ABORT in-flight accelerated runs/sweeps within a bounded wall-clock time (via the threading.Event cancel of req 208), so an actively-misbehaving optimization can be stopped without waiting for natural per-combo completion (up to 120s each) or a process restart; verified by a test that engages safe-mode mid-run and asserts the in-flight accelerated run is cancelled to a clean terminal state within the bound, with its slot released exactly once.
rationale: Reqs 277/673 make the kill-switch affect the NEXT run and 424 freezes flags within a run, but nothing lets an operator STOP an already-running misbehaving accelerated run/sweep — during an incident, waiting out 120s×combos before the switch bites is operationally unacceptable.

text: The deferred v58 seal-backfill MUST be PAUSABLE/ABORTABLE via an admin control without leaving the manifest inconsistent (already-sealed days stay sealed, the completion marker stays in_progress, fact columns remain valid), so an operator can halt it if it is loading the production DB and resume later idempotently from its checkpoint; verified by a test that pauses the backfill mid-run, asserts manifest+marker consistency and that backtests still run correctly on the partially-sealed manifest, then resumes to completion.
rationale: Reqs 663/669/574 cover the marker, stall-alerting, and auto-resume, but none give the operator an explicit PAUSE/ABORT control — if the backfill is degrading prod, the only options today are let-it-run or crash the pod, neither of which is a clean operational lever.

text: A subsequent migration (v59+) MUST NOT assume the v58 DEFERRED seal-backfill has completed (they are decoupled per req 412): v59 DDL/backfill MUST apply correctly while the v58 backfill-completion marker is still 'in_progress', and the migration runner MUST advance schema_version through v59 without blocking on or corrupting the v58 backfill; verified by a test that runs a representative v59 migration against a DB at schema=58 with marker=in_progress and asserts both the v59 change applies and the v58 backfill subsequently resumes and completes.
rationale: Req 412 deliberately decouples the fast DDL (schema_version bump) from the slow async backfill, but no requirement ensures the NEXT migration tolerates an in-progress v58 backfill — a v59 shipped weeks later (the rollout is multi-week per §9) could collide with or assume completion of the still-running backfill.

text: On graceful shutdown/SIGTERM (deploy/restart), the process-lifetime singleton ProcessPool/ThreadPoolExecutor (req 608) and any multiprocessing.shared_memory/mmap segments backing the shared SoA arrays (req 609) MUST be cleanly torn down — no leaked shared-memory segment files, no zombie/orphaned worker processes, no dangling temp columnar files — so repeated redeploys on the same host do not accumulate leaked OS resources; verified by a shutdown test asserting zero residual shared-memory segments and zero orphaned child processes after termination.
rationale: Reqs 608/609 introduce long-lived pools and shared-memory segments and 451 covers clean RUN terminal state on SIGTERM, but nothing requires clean teardown of the POOL/shared-memory OS resources themselves — leaked /dev/shm segments and zombie workers accumulate across the frequent restarts a rolling deploy implies.

text: The v58 migration (monthly partition creation, ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS, session/advisory-lock acquisition, set-based chunked backfill, any CONCURRENTLY-created index) MUST be tested in CI against the EXACT PostgreSQL MAJOR version running in production, so version-specific DDL/locking/partition-pruning behavior differences are caught before deploy rather than surfacing as a production-only migration failure; verified by a CI lane that applies the full _MIGRATIONS list on the prod PG major version and asserts success + the post-migration schema introspection.
rationale: All migration tests (120-134, 412-416, 431, 670, etc.) verify behavior but none pin the PostgreSQL MAJOR version under test; partition syntax, ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS, advisory-lock, and CONCURRENTLY semantics vary across PG versions and a CI-vs-prod version skew could pass CI yet fail in prod.

text: Because the optimization ships over a multi-week, multi-PR phased rollout (§9), the dependency wheel-availability + resolution checks (reqs 146/269/270/421) MUST be RE-RUN at EACH phase merge that touches pyproject/lockfile (not once), so an upstream yank, a new incompatible numba/llvmlite/numpy release, or a lost prebuilt wheel for the deploy target is caught at the merge that would ship it; verified by a CI guard, analogous to the per-merge migration-version re-verification of req 584, that fails any phase whose dependency set no longer resolves to installable prebuilt wheels on every deploy target.
rationale: Req 584 enforces per-merge re-verification for migration version ints, but the equally-volatile dependency surface (wheels can be yanked / new majors released mid-rollout) is only checked as a point-in-time gate (146/269/270/421), not re-verified at each phase that mutates deps.

text: A bidirectional deploy-order test MUST assert the backend and frontend can ship in EITHER order during the rollout window without breakage: a NEW frontend rendering an OLD backend's responses (absent the new optional metric/manifest/provenance keys) and an OLD frontend rendering a NEW backend's responses (carrying the extra additive-optional keys) both render without error and present total_trades and all shared ~45 metric keys; verified by paired contract tests exercising both old→new and new→old API shape directions across the GET result, /backtest-cache/status, and /backtest/compare endpoints.
rationale: Reqs 162/203/460/575 assert additive-only shapes and old-row rendering, but none explicitly test the DEPLOY-ORDER property that frontend and backend may roll out in either sequence during the overlap window — both directions (new-FE/old-BE and old-FE/new-BE) must be proven safe to avoid a deploy-ordering outage.

text: Both the live scanner/auto-trade fetch path and the backtest cold-fetch path MUST write closed-day seals into the ONE shared sealed manifest using identical frontier/seal logic, so a closed day fetched by EITHER caller is immutably sealed and served to the OTHER with zero refetch; verified by a test that (a) a live-path fetch of a closed day produces a sealed manifest entry a subsequent backtest reads with 0 Bybit calls, and (b) a backtest cold-fetch of deep history is later read warm by the live scanner's indicator load with 0 Bybit calls.
rationale: kline_cache_service is shared (CLAUDE.md), but the list only guarantees the live path PRESERVES current-day freshness (#281/#419/#427); it never states the live scanner's routine historical fetches SEAL closed days so backtests benefit (and vice versa). Without this, a symbol only touched by live trading forces every backtest to cold-fetch, and the seal logic could silently diverge between the two callers.

text: A multi-day contiguous gap-run fetch that FAILS partway MUST seal (day-granular, count-free, post-frontier-confirmed) every fully-covered closed day it already fetched and stored, leaving ONLY the unfetched remainder unsealed, so a retry fetches just the remaining span and never re-downloads the already-stored closed days; verified by a test that fails page N of an M-page gap-run and asserts days covered by pages 1..N-1 are sealed while the retry's request span begins at the first uncovered day.
rationale: #247 says a failed/partial fetch 'leaves the day unsealed' and #501 requires full coverage in one invocation, but neither pins DAY-granular progress sealing within a multi-day run on partial failure. A 10-day gap-run that dies on day 8 must not re-fetch days 1-7 on retry — otherwise a flaky exchange repeatedly re-downloads sealed-capable history, the exact re-download class this project is eliminating.

text: The columnar read path MUST bound the number of simultaneously open/mmap'd Parquet/Feather file handles (lazy-open plus LRU-close of file/mmap handles under a configured cap) so a wide run touching thousands of monthly partitions cannot exhaust the process file-descriptor limit (EMFILE); verified by a test under a low simulated fd/handle cap asserting a wide multi-symbol multi-month read keeps concurrent open handles <= the cap and still completes correctly.
rationale: #725 only asserts handles return to BASELINE after a soak (no leak); nothing bounds PEAK concurrent open/mmap handles DURING a single wide run. 200 symbols x interval-in-key x ~12 months = thousands of partition files; mmap'ing them concurrently can hit the default ulimit (often 1024) and crash a run that is otherwise within RSS/time budgets.

text: Incrementally sealing days into a not-yet-complete month partition MUST bound cumulative write amplification (append atomic part-files or equivalent rather than rewriting the entire month file on each daily seal) so cumulative materialization bytes for a month stay ~O(month size), NOT O(days squared); verified by a test that seals N days one-at-a-time into one month and asserts total bytes written ~= month size within a bounded factor, not N x the growing month size.
rationale: #253 permits read-modify-write of the month file (or 'multiple atomic part files') but never bounds the resulting write amplification. A naive full-month rewrite on every daily seal is O(days^2) cumulative bytes and disk-write churn that degrades steady-state warm latency and disk life on a long-lived process sealing 50 symbols/day.

text: An in-flight run MUST consume a point-in-time snapshot of its loaded days fixed at SoA-build: a concurrent content-changing force-reverify/correction (#296/#397) of those same days MUST NOT mutate the running run's already-materialized read-only arrays (the correction is observed only by the NEXT run), and the run MUST record the data-generation (#755) it actually consumed; verified by a test that injects a content-changing reverify of a run's days after SoA-build but before completion and asserts the result matches the pre-correction data and records the pre-correction generation, while the next run sees corrected data.
rationale: #755 makes caches self-invalidate for the NEXT read and #436 invalidates tiers on reverify, but nothing pins that an ALREADY-RUNNING run is isolated from a mid-run correction. Without this an integrity refetch or admin reverify firing mid-run could splice corrected and pre-correction klines into one persisted result, and the recorded data-generation would not match the data actually used.

text: The pre-flight cold-fetch network estimator (#408) MUST treat closed days present in the Postgres SOR but not yet manifest-sealed (the lazy-sealable state during the deferred-backfill window per #574/#592) as WARM (zero fetch needed), so a feasible warm run is never spuriously rejected or over-estimated as network-infeasible merely because the deferred seal-backfill has not yet reached those days; verified by a test with klines present in Postgres but sealed IS NULL asserting the pre-flight reports ~0 cold pages and admits the run.
rationale: #408 counts 'unsealed/missing candles' via the manifest to estimate cold cost, but during the decoupled v58 backfill window (#412/#574) most historic days are present-in-Postgres yet still sealed IS NULL. Counting those as needing fetch would make the estimator over-report network cost and wrongly reject runs that are actually fully warm, breaking the system precisely during the rollout window.

text: The per-run data-provenance fingerprint (#395) MUST additionally include a deterministic hash over the loaded scan_source signals AND per-scan ScanContexts (each loaded signal's symbol/side/score/confidence/signal_time/signal_price plus the regime context), not only over the kline manifest, so a silent change to stored scan results between runs is detectable and a result's FULL input set is reproducible; verified by a test that two runs over differing signal data get distinguishable fingerprints while identical signal data yields an identical signal-fingerprint.
rationale: The provenance fingerprint (#395/#650) covers klines and instrument_info but NOT the scan_source signals, which are an equally primary, mutable engine input (scans can be re-run/reconciled in prod, and copy-prod-scans moves them). Without a signal-data fingerprint a backtest can silently change numbers on rerun with no way to attribute it, defeating the reproducibility guarantee.

text: A documented, tested operational path MUST enumerate persisted backtest_results/sweep_results by DATA-generation (the data-provenance/manifest-sha fingerprint of #395/#755) so that when a sealed day is later corrected (hash-mismatch refetch #296 or manual reverify #397) exactly the stored results whose provenance referenced that day's OLD content can be identified and re-run to corrected values, distinct from enumerating by ENGINE git-SHA/flag generation (#778); verified by a test that corrects a day and enumerates precisely the results that consumed its prior content without touching unaffected results.
rationale: #778 remediates results by ENGINE generation (bad code/flags). A correctly-coded run over LATER-CORRECTED data is a different failure axis: seal-forever plus an exchange restatement means stored results silently used now-stale klines. Operators need to find exactly the results that consumed a corrected day, which requires indexing results by data-generation, not engine-generation.

text: The strict-offline / cache-only run mode (#766) MUST also cover the in-memory-only 1m drill cache (#104/#588) and all auxiliary series (Buy&Hold BTC, btc_vol BTC, MR-mean): a drill-enabled or auxiliary-dependent config run in a FRESH process (empty 1m/aux in-memory cache) under offline mode MUST FAIL LOUD with an actionable miss message rather than silently issue a Bybit 1m/aux fetch or silently skip the drill/auxiliary computation; verified by a fresh-process offline-mode test asserting a drill-config miss raises a clear error with 0 Bybit calls, consistent with #588's documented cross-process drill contract.
rationale: #766 defines offline mode for the kline tiers, but #104/#588 keep 1m drill data in-memory-only (never durably persisted). In a fresh process, offline mode plus a drill config has an inherent cache miss that #766 never addresses; it must fail-loud (not silently fetch, which violates offline, nor silently skip drilling, which corrupts results), reconciling #766 with #588.

text: A day/range MUST be recorded as known-empty (negative-cached/sealed-empty) ONLY upon a SUCCESSFUL HTTP-200 response genuinely returning zero candles for an IN-LIFE range; a non-200/timeout/429, or an empty result for a range outside confirmed [listing,delist] bounds, MUST NOT create a known-empty seal (leave it unsealed for retry), so a transient exchange failure or a lifecycle-bound mismatch can never permanently freeze a real day as empty; verified by a test that a mocked 429/5xx on an in-life range produces zero negative-cache entries and the day stays refetchable, while a true 200-empty in-life day seals known-empty.
rationale: #247 pins the POSITIVE seal (seal only after 200). The NEGATIVE-cache/known-empty seal is the more dangerous direction: incorrectly recording a transiently-failing real day as permanently empty silently drops real trades forever. The 'empty outside lifecycle bounds is fine, empty inside bounds on error is not' distinction is not separately pinned.

text: The standing per-phase cache-efficiency regression gate (#263/#446/#760) MUST additionally track and fail phase-over-phase on the canonical fixture for: manifest seal-WRITE statement count (must stay O(symbols), never O(days)) and total Postgres WRITE statements (a warm rerun must stay 0 per #751; a cold run bounded), so a regression that re-seals per-day or re-writes coverage on a warm rerun is caught even when Bybit-call, kline-SELECT, bytes-read, and file-open counters are unchanged.
rationale: #590/#643/#751 TEST the O(1)/O(symbols) seal-write and zero-warm-write properties once each, but the standing phase-over-phase GATE family (#263/#446/#760) only watches read-side counters (Bybit calls, SELECTs, bytes, file-opens). A write-amplification regression (per-day seal upserts, coverage re-writes on warm reruns) would pass every existing standing gate.

text: If v58 builds any index CONCURRENTLY (req 431) and the build is interrupted (crash/OOM/lock_timeout), Postgres leaves an INVALID index (pg_index.indisvalid=false) that silently never serves queries and degrades the seal-check to a full scan; v58 (or a boot self-heal) MUST detect an invalid/incomplete manifest/lifecycle index and DROP+rebuild it; verified by a test that simulates an interrupted CONCURRENTLY build and asserts the next boot detects and rebuilds the invalid index.
rationale: Req 431 mandates CONCURRENTLY index creation outside the txn but never handles the canonical failure mode — an interrupted CONCURRENTLY build leaves a non-functional INVALID index that silently makes the seal-check O(total rows) instead of index-backed (req 512). DURING-failure of the index build is unaddressed.

text: The deploy-time fleet-consistency check (req 463) MUST additionally assert every running instance reports the SAME applied schema_version AND the SAME seal-backfill completion-marker state via the status route (reqs 428/663), so a rolling deploy where one pod is stuck pre-v58 or stuck mid-backfill is detected as a fleet inconsistency before promotion; verified by a test that divergent schema_version/marker states across instance status payloads are flagged.
rationale: Req 463 only checks flag-set + engine git SHA agreement across instances. A pod that failed to migrate (schema 57) or whose backfill stalled would pass 463's flag/SHA check yet produce slower, extra-fetching results — an undetected partial rollout.

text: On startup the process MUST reap orphaned OS resources left by a PRIOR hard-killed (SIGKILL/OOM, non-graceful) instance — stale named multiprocessing.shared_memory segments / /dev/shm or Windows named segments backing the shared SoA/timeline (reqs 609/744/784) and leftover columnar temp files (req 286) — so creating a same-named segment does not fail with EEXIST and resident segments do not accumulate across crash-restarts; verified by a test that plants a stale named segment + temp file, boots, and asserts they are reaped/reattached and a sweep proceeds.
rationale: Req 784 covers CLEAN teardown on graceful SIGTERM, but a hard kill (OOM/SIGKILL, the realistic large-run failure) bypasses teardown and leaks named segments; the next boot must recover from that residue, which no requirement addresses.

text: The durable columnar cache directory's volume-persistence contract MUST be documented and enforced — whether it is a PERSISTENT volume (survives deploy, enabling cross-process mmap warm per req 386) or EPHEMERAL (wiped per deploy) — and the first run after a deploy that WIPED the volume MUST degrade gracefully to the documented cold-build budget (req 233), never to a timeout-kill; verified by a test that clears the columnar volume and asserts the first post-deploy run completes within the documented cold budget issuing 0 Bybit calls (served from Postgres).
rationale: Req 386's cross-process warm guarantee silently assumes the Parquet/Feather volume survives deploys. In a containerized deploy the volume is often ephemeral, so EVERY deploy's first run is cold — the persistence contract and its post-deploy latency consequence are unstated.

text: When the backend boots against a READ-ONLY Postgres (hot standby/replica, or a primary temporarily read-only), the migration runner MUST detect read-only and SKIP/DEFER v58 DDL+backfill (wait or serve read-only) rather than raising on the first write and crash-looping; verified by a test that runs the migration runner against a read-only connection and asserts no crash, no partial apply, and the app reaches a degraded-but-serving readiness state.
rationale: All migration reqs assume a writable primary. A pod scheduled against a read replica, or a primary in read-only during failover, would hit the v58 DDL write and crash-loop — a real deploy hazard with no covering requirement.

text: The v58 dry-run/pre-flight (req 289) MUST emit a PREDICTED sealed-day count, and after the actual backfill completes a reconciliation check MUST assert actual-sealed ≈ predicted within a documented tolerance, surfacing an alertable signal on a material shortfall; verified by a test that injects a backfill shortfall and asserts the reconciliation flags it.
rationale: Req 289 produces dry-run counts and 668 gates on them, but nothing reconciles the PREDICTION against the ACTUAL backfill result. A backfill that sealed far fewer days than predicted (an unforeseen filter/bug) would be silently masked by the count-free fallback (correct but permanently slower).

text: The per-run data-provenance fingerprint (reqs 395/674) MUST stay well-defined and DISTINGUISHING for runs executed during the backfill window when sealed days legitimately carry NULL content_sha256 (req 274): it MUST fall back to a deterministic coverage-generation token (or per-day last_open_ts+candle_count digest) so two runs over different underlying klines still get distinct fingerprints; verified by a test that two mid-backfill runs over differing klines receive distinguishable fingerprints despite NULL shas.
rationale: Req 395's provenance fingerprint is built from the sealed-manifest sha set, but req 274 mandates NULL sha for backfilled days — so during the (potentially long) backfill window the fingerprint could collapse to an indistinct value, defeating the 'two differing-data runs are distinguishable' guarantee exactly when extra cold fetches make provenance most important.

text: The pre-flight cost/feasibility estimator's calibration constants (reqs 224/408/517) MUST be HOST-aware — measured by a fast boot-time micro-benchmark on the actual deploy host or supplied per deploy-target config — so a prod host slower than the CI host where calibration was frozen does not systematically under-estimate and let runs hit the 120s timeout-kill; verified by a test that under a simulated 2× slower host the estimator's projection scales and still brackets actual within the documented factor.
rationale: Req 517 calibrates the estimator against measured runs but never specifies that calibration must reflect the PROD host's speed. A constant frozen on a fast CI runner will under-estimate on a slower prod instance, causing feasible-looking runs to be timeout-killed — the cross-host calibration gap.

text: The rolling-deploy overlap window MUST be explicitly bounded and tested for the re-download vector: legacy (pre-v58, count-based) instances CANNOT honor seals and WILL re-fetch sealed-short days while they run; this MUST be documented as bounded to the overlap window, MUST NOT corrupt the manifest (store_klines ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING + req 418 column preservation), and new-code instances MUST re-seal idempotently; verified by an overlap test asserting a legacy instance's count-based refetch of a sealed-short day leaves the manifest intact and the new instance re-seals it with zero net change.
rationale: Req 464 tests that legacy upserts don't clobber manifest columns, but never confronts that legacy COUNT-BASED detection inherently re-downloads sealed-short days during the overlap — a transient re-emergence of the exact bug being fixed. It must be acknowledged, bounded, and proven harmless rather than assumed away.

text: A backtest reading a (symbol,interval,day) CONCURRENTLY with the deferred backfill (or a runtime lazy-seal, req 592) sealing that same day MUST observe a CONSISTENT classification for the whole run — fully-unsealed-refetchable or fully-sealed-cached, never a torn mid-seal state that loads part from cache and part from refetch; verified by a test that seals a day mid-backtest-read and asserts the reading run's loaded klines and seal classification are internally consistent and byte-identical to one coherent snapshot.
rationale: Req 642 fixes the frontier stable WITHIN a single run, but a CONCURRENT backfill/lazy-seal UPDATE flipping a day's sealed flag between a reading run's manifest lookup and its kline load is a distinct cross-actor race that could split one run's data across cache and refetch tiers.

text: The post-migration deploy canary (reqs 288/427) MUST additionally execute a MINIMAL SWEEP (≥2 combos, asserting parallel orchestration + rank persistence) and a MINIMAL DRILLDOWN-ON run (asserting the 1m drill/Phase-B path) against the migrated DB, since the sweep orchestrator and the drill path are distinct code paths a broken v58/flag deploy can break independently of the single drill-off backtest; verified by the canary including sweep and drill-on assertions that fail on a regression in either path.
rationale: Req 288's canary runs only a tiny drill-off backtest; reqs 379/505/609 and the drill path are entirely separate machinery a bad deploy could break while the canary stays green. The deploy gate must exercise the high-risk paths it intends to protect.

text: A containerized PROMOTION SMOKE on the EXACT production image (not just the CI host or a generic manylinux container, reqs 270/421) MUST assert end-to-end health on that image: boot-time numba JIT warm-up completes within its watchdog (req 422), a canonical backtest is oracle-identical, and it completes under budget — so an image whose wheels/ABI/threading differ from CI is caught before traffic; verified by a CI job running the smoke inside the prod image.
rationale: Reqs 270/421 verify wheels INSTALL on the base image, but not that the assembled prod image actually WARMS the JIT under the watchdog and produces oracle-identical numbers end-to-end. ABI/threading-layer surprises surface only when the kernel runs on the real image.

text: Deploy promotion MUST use TWO staged gates reflecting the schema-first/behavior-later split (req 672): Gate-1 (v58 DDL applied + schema_version=58 confirmed) authorizes serving on the legacy-equivalent path; Gate-2 (seal-backfill completion marker=='complete', req 663) authorizes enabling the behavior-changing optimization flags fleet-wide — so flags are never enabled before the cache is fully sealed; verified by a test that the promotion automation refuses to flip behavior flags while the marker is 'in_progress' and permits it only after 'complete'.
rationale: Req 672 states the schema-first/behavior-later principle but defines no enforced promotion gating. Without an explicit Gate-2 on backfill completion, an operator could enable optimization flags during the backfill window, producing the slower, extra-fetching, partially-sealed behavior reqs 574/674 only flag after the fact.

text: The migration-integrity gates — idempotent re-run + 0→58 vs 57→58 schema equivalence (req 415), the v58-definition-frozen checksum guard (req 457), the zero-destructive-DDL expand-only guard (req 581), and master-kill-switch golden-master parity (reqs 277/432) — MUST be WIRED to run on EVERY phase PR (not once), so a later phase editing async_persistence.py or the manifest schema cannot silently break migration idempotency, the expand-only invariant, or the rollback path; verified by a CI-wiring meta-test (analogous to reqs 465/684) asserting these gates are attached to each phase workflow.
rationale: The migration ships across a multi-week, multi-PR phased rollout (§9). Reqs 415/457/581/432 define the gates but, like the parity/perf gates needing reqs 198/684 to be WIRED per-phase, nothing guarantees the migration gates actually run on each subsequent phase that could regress them.

text: v58's ALTER TABLE/CREATE on the live-shared kline_cache_coverage MUST be safe against any active LOGICAL REPLICATION/CDC publication on that table — the added columns must be replica-applicable and ADD COLUMN must not break the replication slot/subscription — OR the absence of logical replication on the deploy target MUST be documented and asserted as a precondition; verified by a test (or documented+asserted deploy contract) that v58 DDL applied with a publication present does not invalidate the slot and the replica applies the added columns.
rationale: kline_cache_coverage is a hot shared table; if prod replicates it via logical replication/CDC for analytics or HA, a v58 DDL that breaks the slot is a production incident. No requirement addresses replication/publication safety of the schema change.

text: A boot-time REGISTRY-COMPLETENESS self-check MUST assert that every optimization flag and numeric operational config the code actually READS is present in the central registry with a documented default and (for numerics) a range validator (reqs 147/152/780) — the inverse of the operator-typo guard (req 462), which only checks env-var NAMES the operator sets — so a newly-added-but-unregistered flag/config can never be read with an undocumented/unvalidated default; verified by a meta-test that a code-read flag missing from the registry fails the check.
rationale: Req 462 guards operator-side misspellings and req 780 range-validates a NAMED list, but neither guarantees the code-side is complete: a phase could add a new flag read directly from os.environ without registering a default/validator, escaping both the typo guard and the range check — a config-surface drift across the phased rollout.

text: The committed deterministic fixture generator (klines + scheduled-scan signals, #294/#528) MUST emit BYTE-IDENTICAL output on Windows and Linux runners (no float-formatting, hashing, dict/set-ordering, or newline/locale drift in the generator itself); a meta-test MUST regenerate the fixture on each CI OS lane and assert identical bytes, distinct from #723 which only pins the Decimal ORACLE across OS, not the INPUT generator that feeds every perf/parity oracle.
rationale: Windows dev + Linux prod (CLAUDE.md) means a generator that drifts across OS would silently produce two different 'canonical' fixtures, invalidating cross-lane parity/perf comparison. #723 covers the oracle output, not the generator input; #528 asserts regenerability but not cross-OS byte-equality.

text: A randomized PROPERTY/FUZZ harness over the in-process cache range-algebra MUST issue a long random sequence of _load_klines requests with varying containing / sub-range / partial-overlap / disjoint / delta-symbol bounds (and interleaved forming-day touches) and assert EVERY returned per-symbol series is strictly-ascending, de-duplicated, and byte-identical to the Postgres SOR, plus the documented call-count invariants (0 refetch on warm, single-flight on cold) hold for every step — validating #503/#598/#653/#736 across the input space rather than at the handful of point fixtures.
rationale: Containment/overlap/delta-symbol cache logic is the highest-risk correctness surface (a wrong-range or stale slice silently corrupts a backtest). Point fixtures (#503/#598/#736) cannot cover the combinatorial range algebra; a fuzz harness catches seam/index bugs the examples miss.

text: A UNIT-level DIFFERENTIAL harness for compute_all_metrics MUST diff the legacy vs refactored metrics implementations over a randomized grid of equity curves and trade lists (varied length, sign mix, zero-variance, single-point, all-wins, all-losses, irregular spacing) asserting Sharpe/Sortino/max_drawdown/profit_factor/expectancy/streak metrics are bit-identical (within the defined epsilon) — independent of the full engine, analogous to the trading_rules kernel differential (#319), since #520 only sets a coverage floor and #700 freezes point values on one fixture.
rationale: backtest_metrics.py is path-dependent and easy to subtly break (O(n²) max-dd vs running-peak, periodization). #319 protects the trading_rules kernels with a randomized differential but no equivalent exists for the metrics library; coverage (#520) and single-fixture formula freezes (#700) do not exercise the input space.

text: A meta-test MUST prove the THREE-WAY reconciliation cross-check (#66/#184/#532) has teeth by independently perturbing each leg — a single recorded trade.pnl, metrics.net_profit, and final_equity — and asserting the Σtrade.pnl == net_profit == final_equity − starting_capital assertion turns RED for each perturbation, so the reconciliation gate can never vacuously pass, complementing the engine-defect mutation battery (#344/#469) which mutates engine logic but never the recorded reconciliation operands.
rationale: The reconciliation identity is the single most-cited safety invariant (asserted on every fixture). If the helper itself were miswired (e.g. comparing a value to itself, or a tolerance too loose), every fixture would pass vacuously. #344/#468 prove engine/perf gates have teeth but not the reconciliation comparator specifically.

text: A coverage RATCHET gate MUST assert line AND branch coverage on each gated module (backtest_engine/service, kline_cache_service, backtest_metrics, trading_rules, and the new SoA/numba/manifest/columnar/sweep modules per #200/#303/#520) does not DECREASE phase-over-phase versus a committed per-module baseline — a monotonic ratchet ABOVE the 90% floor — so a phase cannot add untested accelerated code while diluting overall coverage yet still clearing the static 90% bar.
rationale: A flat 90% floor lets a large phase add substantial untested code as long as aggregate stays ≥90%. A per-module ratchet catches local regressions the global floor masks — critical for the import-guarded fallback branches and new kernels that are easy to under-test.

text: A golden fixture MUST freeze the FUNDING-ZERO-CROSSING case: a position that opens AND closes strictly BETWEEN two 0/8/16h boundaries while funding_rate_model is ACTIVE (no boundary crossed during its life) MUST record funding_paid == 0 exactly, with the three-way reconciliation holding — distinct from #365 (funding_rate_model='none') and #339 (open/close exactly ON a boundary), so the (date,hour) dedupe's 'no boundary touched ⇒ no charge' path is pinned.
rationale: Reqs pin WHEN/SIGN/COUNT/dedupe/basis of funding on a crossed boundary and the model='none' zero case, but none freeze the 'model active, position lifetime contains no boundary ⇒ exactly zero charge' path — a likely off-by-one site if the SoA bar-walk mis-detects boundaries.

text: A coarse-bar (5m, no drill, no SL) fixture MUST pin LIQUIDATION-over-TAKE-PROFIT same-bar precedence: when both the liquidation price and the TP price fall inside one bar's [low,high], the engine MUST resolve close_reason='liquidation' with pnl = −locked_margin (no exit fee/slippage), freezing the liq>TP outcome that the liq→SL→TP ordering of #41 leaves implicit (#41 explicitly pins only SL-vs-TP and SL-vs-liq, never liq-vs-TP-both-touch).
rationale: #41 defines the precedence order but the explicit guards/fixtures only cover SL interactions. A refactor that checks favorable TP before the adverse liquidation in the same bar would be optimistic and unpinned — a real non-optimistic-resolution gap for the no-SL high-leverage case (#181/#706 reach liquidation but don't pin a same-bar TP touch).

text: A fixture MUST freeze the SLIPPAGE-OUT-OF-BAR-RANGE case: when slippage_bps applied to the next-bar-open fill pushes a Buy entry fill ABOVE that bar's high (or a Sell entry fill BELOW its low), the legacy clamp-or-no-clamp behavior MUST be reproduced bit-identically (the slipped fill price and resulting pnl), extending #323 which freezes slipped fills in-range but never pins the boundary where slippage exceeds the bar's own range.
rationale: Slippage is applied to the open price and can mathematically exceed the bar's high/low; whether legacy clamps to the bar range or allows the out-of-range fill materially changes pnl and is currently unspecified. The SoA/numba reimplementation could silently choose the other behavior.

text: A fixture MUST pin DEGENERATE-PRESENT instrument_info fields: a symbol whose instrument_info row is PRESENT but carries qty_step==0, min_qty==0, or tick_size==0 MUST be handled byte-identically to legacy (guarded — no divide/floor-by-zero raise, documented fallback to the default qty_step/min_qty or pass-through), distinct from the instrument_info-ABSENT default (#336) and the max-leverage-field case (#631).
rationale: #336 covers a wholly-absent snapshot and #631 a max-leverage field, but a present-but-zero qty_step/tick_size is a realistic exchange/data edge that would crash a naive flooring/rounding kernel; the legacy guard must be reproduced, not 'fixed'.

text: A benchmark REPRESENTATIVENESS assertion MUST gate the canonical fixtures so the perf numbers reflect real work: the drill-OFF fixture MUST open ≥ a documented minimum trade count and reach a documented peak concurrent open-book depth, the drill-ON fixture MUST reach ≥ a documented drilled-bar fraction, and total candle-evaluations MUST fall in an expected band — these workload invariants frozen and checked WITHIN the timed run alongside the result fingerprint (#467/#500), so a perf gate can never be satisfied by a trivially-filtered or zero-trade run.
rationale: A fixture where everything is filtered out runs in microseconds and passes every latency gate while testing nothing. #657 ensures edge cases EXIST (≥1 each) but not that the AGGREGATE workload is non-trivial/representative; #468 asserts reconciliation in the timed run but not that meaningful work occurred.

text: A meta-test MUST assert the stored-oracle battery is SINGLE-GENERATION-CONSISTENT: every committed oracle artifact embeds the SAME engine git SHA (no piecemeal-regenerated mixed-SHA battery) AND that SHA equals the current engine SHA on the canonical 5m no-drill path, so a partial/stale regeneration is detected — complementing the per-file hash-pin (#347) and the regen-script SHA-bump rule (#529) which catch single-file edits but not a battery split across two engine versions.
rationale: If oracles are regenerated one fixture at a time across phases, the battery could silently mix outputs from two engine generations, masking a real divergence. #662 enforces single-generation for SWEEP combos but no equivalent exists for the golden oracle battery.

text: A harness-hermeticity meta-test MUST run the parity+cache suite (and the JSON-oracle write/read round-trip) under a NON-UTF-8 / non-C default locale and a differing LC_NUMERIC (decimal-comma) and assert byte-identical results and oracle hashes, so number formatting, JSON serialization, and file encoding in the test/oracle path can never depend on the host locale — complementing the TZ-independence (#479) and wall-clock-free (#717) harness rules.
rationale: Decimal-comma locales and non-UTF-8 defaults are a classic source of JSON/number-formatting drift that would corrupt oracle comparisons or persisted JSONB; nothing currently pins locale/encoding independence of the harness, only TZ and clock.

text: A schema-snapshot contract test MUST pin the agent-facing MCP tool RESPONSE/output shapes (backtest_get, sweep_results, backtest_compare, scans_get) as ADDITIVE-ONLY — no field dropped/renamed/retyped, total_trades and the ~45 metric keys always present — complementing the request/parameter snapshot (#460) and the GET envelope snapshot (#730), since MCP agents consume these outputs to drive optimize/propose workflows.
rationale: #460 freezes MCP tool INPUT schemas and #730 the REST GET envelope, but the MCP tool OUTPUT shapes that agents parse are unpinned; a refactor dropping/retyping an output field would silently break agent consumers without failing any existing gate.

text: Every perf/scaling/concurrency test MUST run under a HARD per-test wall-clock timeout (pytest-timeout or equivalent) sized above its budget, so a severe accidental O(N²) regression or a deadlock in the parallel-sweep/concurrency lane FAILS AS A TIMEOUT rather than hanging the CI runner indefinitely; a meta-test MUST assert each test in the slow/flakiness lanes declares a timeout.
rationale: #677 proves the perf gate turns RED on an injected slowdown, but a sufficiently severe regression or a concurrency deadlock could hang the runner for hours before the gate ever evaluates. A hard timeout is the operational safety net that makes the gate itself robust.

text: The engine-shadow/dark-compare divergence metric (#660) and the read-path shadow divergence metric (#291) MUST be wired to a documented ALERT THRESHOLD, and a test MUST assert the alert FIRES on a seeded beyond-tolerance divergence and stays SILENT on a clean run — closing the loop that prod parity divergence is not merely emitted but actionable/gating during the rollout window.
rationale: #660/#291 emit divergence signals but stop at emission; an un-alerted metric is invisible during an incident. The threshold + fire/silent test makes the rollout-window parity safety net actually catch a regression in production.

text: A plain SWEEP RE-RUN DETERMINISM test (no crash, no injected reordering) MUST run the identical sweep twice on the same warm long-lived process and assert byte-identical per-combo metrics AND an identical ranked 1..K set, distinct from crash-resume identity (#306) and out-of-order rank stability (#317) — proving steady-state repeatability with no cross-run cache-state or scheduling leakage into results.
rationale: #306 (crash-resume) and #317 (reorder) cover adversarial cases, but the basic 'run it twice, get the same answer' guarantee for a full sweep on a shared-cache long-lived process is not a standing test; subtle cross-run mutable-cache leakage (#733) would surface here.

text: The RECORDED trade.leverage and trade.qty (and strategy_kind) fields MUST reflect the COHORT-EFFECTIVE values on the persisted/oracle output: an MR-routed trade records mr_leverage (not config leverage) and its mr_capital_pct/mr_tight_stop_pct-derived qty/stop, while a trend trade records config leverage — frozen by a mixed-cohort fixture asserting the recorded FIELDS (not only the internal sizing math of #329) match the oracle per trade.
rationale: #329 pins the cohort sizing/stop COMPUTATION but not that the recorded, persisted, frontend-rendered leverage/qty FIELDS carry the cohort-effective values; a refactor could compute correctly yet stamp the generic config leverage on the trade row, misrendering MR trades.

text: The batched scan_source loader (#377/#564) MUST handle DUPLICATE/REPEATED scan rows (the same scan persisted twice, or overlapping scan windows) byte-identically to legacy — pinning whether duplicates are deduped by scan id or each is processed — since double-processing a scan would double-anchor cycle_start_equity, double-re-arm smart_drawdown, and re-admit signals; verified by a fixture seeding a duplicated scan row and asserting identical trades/anchoring versus the oracle.
rationale: The O(1) batched range load (#377) changes how scans are fetched/grouped; if prod scan_source ever contains duplicate or window-overlapping rows, the batched path could process a scan twice where the legacy per-scan load did not (or vice-versa), silently corrupting path-dependent state. No req pins duplicate-scan handling.

text: The Bybit-client and Postgres test doubles backing the '0 Bybit calls / 0 kline SELECTs / 0 writes' assertions (#3/#17/#190/#751) MUST be STRICT mocks that RAISE immediately (with symbol+range in the message) on any UNEXPECTED call, not permissive spies whose count is only checked post-hoc — so an errant fetch/read fails fast with a stack trace at the call site; a meta-test MUST assert an injected unexpected call raises rather than silently passing until a later count assertion.
rationale: Permissive spies let an unexpected call succeed and only surface as a count mismatch far from the cause (or not at all if the count assertion is missing on some path). Strict raising doubles make the cache-efficiency guarantees self-enforcing and pinpoint the offending call, complementing the no-egress (#293) and offline-mode (#766) guards.

text: A test MUST assert CROSS-FIELD CONSISTENCY between the per-run structured stats log (#264/#534) and the persisted result fingerprint (#278/#318/#496): the active optimization-flag set, engine git SHA, and recorded numeric kernel reported in the structured log MUST equal those stamped on the backtest_runs/results row for the same run, so a triage reading the log and a triage reading the row can never disagree about which code path produced the numbers.
rationale: #534 pins the log schema and #318 the row fingerprint independently, but nothing asserts they agree for a given run. A drift between the two (e.g. log says numba-active, row says fallback) would make incident triage actively misleading — especially after a mid-run degradation (#496).

text: The EQUITY_DROP_PCT / max_drawdown_pct close-RULE trigger BASIS MUST be pinned bit-identically: specify whether the drawdown that fires the rule is measured from a FIXED cycle_start_equity anchor or from a RUNNING PEAK equity ratcheted within the cycle, and if peak-based, whether the peak includes open uPnL and is updated intrabar on the favorable extreme — this is the DROP-side analog of req 624 (which pins only the RISE/close_on_profit measurement basis) and is DISTINCT from the summary max_drawdown_pct METRIC denominator of req 636 — verified by a fixture where equity rises then falls so a peak-based vs start-based threshold fire on different bars, asserting the legacy firing bar.
rationale: req 624 pins the RISE/close_on_profit basis and req 54 pins intrabar adverse-extreme valuation, but no requirement pins whether the EQUITY_DROP close rule itself fires on drawdown-from-running-peak vs drawdown-from-cycle-start — a decision-relevant invariant that changes WHICH bar the cycle terminates on.

text: execution_mode='batch' MUST be pinned to size EVERY admitted signal in a scan against the SAME scan-start sizing_capital with NO intra-scan locked_margin / carried-uPnL / entry-fee depletion (the explicit converse of the immediate-mode sequential depletion pinned in reqs 560/625/703), so a multi-signal batch scan produces bit-identical qty for each signal independent of admission order — verified by a paired immediate-vs-batch single-scan multi-signal fixture proving the two modes size differently and each matches its own frozen oracle.
rationale: reqs 81/179 require distinct batch-vs-immediate oracles but never pin the MECHANISM; req 560 pins immediate sequential depletion, leaving the batch-mode no-depletion sizing rule (the thing that actually distinguishes the two modes) unspecified.

text: The within-bar evaluation PHASE STRUCTURE across P open positions MUST be pinned: whether the engine completes ALL positions' funding+liq/TP/SL per-position checks first and THEN evaluates the portfolio-equity cascade once (all-positions-then-portfolio) versus interleaving portfolio-equity after each position, plus the position ITERATION order within each phase — because each per-position close mutates the wallet/Σ-uPnL feeding the portfolio cascade and trailing — verified by a multi-position same-bar fixture where the two structures would fire a different equity rule or produce a different close set, distinct from req 490 (which pins only the recording order of already-decided independent same-bar exits) and req 42 (which pins rule-TYPE order, not the multi-position loop structure).
rationale: req 42 gives the rule-type sequence for a candle but is ambiguous about the P-position loop structure; req 490 covers recording order of independent exits but not whether portfolio-equity is evaluated before/after the full per-position sweep — a path-dependent invariant under SoA/timeline iteration.

text: The TRAILING_PROFIT ACTIVATION level (the per-unit/ROI threshold derived from trailing_profit_pct at which trailing arms and peak-tracking begins) MUST be reproduced bit-identically and MUST use the same ROI-on-margin-at-leverage basis as the TP/SL barrier derivation (req 320), so trailing arms on the exact legacy bar — req 56 pins the clear-peak / preserve-peak / peak-from-bar-extreme / trigger-at-`peak×0.5` state machine but NOT the activation-level computation itself — verified by a fixture sweeping trailing_profit_pct and leverage asserting the arming bar and the ratcheted peak are byte-identical to the oracle.
rationale: req 56 pins the trailing state machine and trigger but explicitly omits how the activation threshold is computed from trailing_profit_pct; a refactor could shift the arming bar (and thus the exit) while still passing the state-machine checks.

text: The mean-reversion reference MEAN computation MUST be pinned exactly: the aggregation kind (SMA vs EMA), the price field (close vs typical/HLC3), the window (mr_mean_period bars at mr_mean_interval), and inclusive/exclusive treatment of the forming/current bar — since mr_min_edge_pct, mr_target_capture_pct and the MR target/stop prices (req 330) are all derived from this mean — verified by a fixture freezing the computed mean, edge, and target/stop prices so the SoA/coarse-derivation refactor cannot shift which MR signals qualify or their resulting barrier levels.
rationale: req 330 references 'the mean' and the params that consume it but never pins the mean FORMULA; SMA-vs-EMA / price-field / current-bar-inclusion are decision-relevant and would silently change MR admission and barriers.

text: The mean-reversion TARGET-CAPTURE exit MUST be pinned: whether reaching the mr_target_capture_pct target price closes the MR position, the exact close_reason recorded for it (a dedicated mr_target reason vs reusing take_profit), and that the chosen reason is ADDED to the close_reason enum-coverage union assertion (reqs 345/475 omit it) — with the exit price and the three-way reconciliation (req 66) frozen in the oracle — verified by an MR fixture that exits via target capture asserting close_reason, exit_price, and Σpnl reconciliation are byte-identical to the oracle.
rationale: req 330 computes the MR target but no requirement pins the target-reached EXIT mechanism or its close_reason; the enum-coverage union (req 345) would falsely pass while an MR-target exit branch ships untested.

text: The ORDER of tick_size rounding relative to slippage MUST be pinned for fills, and the per-barrier rounding DIRECTION for TP/SL/liquidation prices MUST be frozen: specify whether slippage is applied to the mark and the result tick-rounded (and in which direction), or the barrier/fill is tick-rounded then slipped — because the two orders yield different last-tick fills — extending req 343 (which pins only the rounding MODE/precision) with the rounding ORDER and per-barrier direction — verified by a fixture where a fill/barrier sits within one tick of a boundary so the order/direction flips the recorded price, asserted bit-identical to the Decimal oracle.
rationale: req 343 enumerates quantization sites and modes but not the ORDER-of-operations between slippage and tick rounding nor per-barrier rounding direction; these compose to a different recorded fill price on boundary cases.

text: A cycle that completes NATURALLY (every open position closes via TP/SL/liquidation/time/trailing with NO equity-rule cycle-termination) MUST be pinned: whether the next non-skipped scan re-anchors cycle_start_equity to the running wallet and re-arms smart_drawdown_fired identically to an equity-terminated cycle (req 45) or carries the prior anchor — distinct from req 45 (equity-terminated), req 71/623 (skip_if_positions_open skip), and req 695 (empty-book trading scan) — verified by a fixture chaining a fully-TP-closed book into a next-scan entry and asserting the re-anchor/re-arm state and the next trade's sizing match the oracle.
rationale: The re-anchor/re-arm rules are pinned for equity-terminated and skipped scans, but not for a cycle that empties naturally without an equity-rule firing — an unspecified path that determines the next trade's sizing basis.

text: fill_to_max_trades MUST pin its exact admission semantic: whether it keeps consuming abs(score)-ranked signals until max_trades positions are successfully FILLED (continuing past rejected / signals_no_kline / qty-undersized / margin-rejected signals) versus stopping after max_trades are ATTEMPTED — interacting with the no-kline disposition (req 630) and the per-scan/lifetime counters (req 563) — verified by a fixture with interleaved rejected and admissible signals at the max_trades boundary asserting the filled set and the scan_entered/signals_entered counters are byte-identical to the oracle under fill_to_max_trades both on and off.
rationale: req 81 only requires 'identical results' for fill_to_max_trades and req 563 pins counters generically; the core fill-to-max semantic (skip-rejected-and-keep-going vs stop-at-attempts) is never explicitly pinned and changes which trades open at the boundary.

text: The smart-drawdown SELECTIVE close predicate MUST pin the exact valuation point that classifies a position as an 'intrabar-losing' position to close (req 44): per-unit uPnL valued at the bar's adverse extreme vs at the bar close vs off equity_ref_entry, and the per-side sign basis — so the SoA/numba path closes EXACTLY the legacy subset of positions on a SMART fire — verified by a fixture with a mixed book (some positions winning, some losing intrabar) asserting precisely which positions SMART closes, in the legacy close/equity-point order, matches the oracle.
rationale: req 44 says SMART 'closes ONLY intrabar-losing positions' but never defines the valuation point for 'losing'; under float64/SoA the chosen valuation must select the identical position subset or trades and equity points diverge.

text: The interaction between mean_reversion routing and regime classification MUST be pinned: whether MR entries are gated on the per-scan regime label equalling mr_regime='ranging', and what becomes of an MR-routed signal when the live regime is trend/volatile (dropped, routed to trend, or admitted regardless) — so the per-scan regime classifier (req 567) feeding the MR gate cannot silently change which signals route to MR or how they size (req 329) — verified by paired fixtures driving the regime to ranging vs non-ranging and asserting MR admission/routing/sizing is byte-identical to the oracle.
rationale: reqs 236/567 cover regime gates/classifier and reqs 243/329/330 cover MR caps/sizing/edge math, but the specific mr_regime↔regime-label gating that decides whether MR even runs in a given scan is unpinned.

text: The equity-rule cascade FALL-THROUGH after a NON-terminating smart-drawdown fire MUST be pinned: when EQUITY_DROP_PCT_SMART selectively closes some positions WITHOUT zeroing cycle_start_equity (so the cycle does not terminate), specify whether evaluation short-circuits for that tick (SMART is one-shot and consumes the cascade) or falls through to close_on_profit and EQUITY_RISE on the surviving book in the SAME tick, and whether the conditional re-anchor (req 622) is computed before or after any such fall-through closes — verified by a fixture arming SMART + close_on_profit + RISE simultaneously where a non-terminating SMART fire leaves survivors that would trip RISE.
rationale: req 43 pins the cascade with 'early return on cycle termination', but SMART is selective and may NOT terminate; whether the remaining cascade still evaluates the survivors in that tick is genuinely ambiguous and path-dependent.

text: The recorded exit_price of a LIQUIDATION trade MUST be pinned bit-identically (the computed liquidation price vs the bar's adverse extreme vs the clamped SL), independent of the fact that pnl = −locked_margin via the separate branch (reqs 58/340) and that no exit fee/slippage applies (req 325) — verified by the SL-omitted liquidation fixture (req 181) asserting the trade's exit_price field equals the legacy liquidation-price value (not the bar extreme), so a refactor cannot drift the recorded fill while leaving pnl correct.
rationale: reqs 58/325/340 pin liquidation pnl/fee/slippage but never pin what exit_price the liquidation trade RECORDS; req 346 deep-equality only catches drift if a requirement fixes the intended value, which is currently absent.

text: max_signal_age_minutes behavior for non-positive ages MUST be pinned: because all signals in a scan share current_time = the scan's first-signal signal_time (reqs 237/704), a later in-scan signal has age = current_time − signal_time ≤ 0 and MUST be admitted (never rejected as 'too old'), and the engine MUST pin whether age is computed against the shared scan current_time or each signal's own signal_time — verified by a fixture with intra-scan signals whose signal_time exceeds the scan current_time, asserting the age-filter admit/reject decision and disposition counters are byte-identical to the oracle, distinct from req 333 (single signal exactly on the threshold) and req 635 (late-signal fill-bar resolution).
rationale: req 333 pins the threshold-boundary case and req 704 pins the shared mark, but the negative/zero-age outcome for later in-scan signals (a direct consequence of the shared current_time) is never pinned and could flip admission under a per-signal-reference refactor.

text: Coarse-interval and auxiliary reference series (a coarse simulation_interval rerun, Buy&Hold BTC, btc_vol BTC, MR-mean at mr_mean_interval) MUST be DERIVED from the fine (5m) sealed base via resample (#116/#405) rather than triggering a SEPARATE coarse-interval fetch/load: running e.g. 1h after a warm 5m on the same symbols+window MUST issue 0 Bybit calls and 0 kline-table SELECTs (it derives from the cached/loaded fine base), and even COLD a coarse run MUST fetch only the fine base once (no extra coarse pages). Verified by a test that warms 5m then derives 1h asserting 0 Bybit + 0 kline SELECT, and a cold coarse run issuing only fine-base fetches.
rationale: The cross-INTERVAL warm-data axis is unpinned: #405 only memoizes a resample within one session/run, #504 covers same-interval different-strategy, and #355 asserts auxiliary paths route through the cache but still implies a separate coarse fetch. Sweeping simulation_interval or comparing timeframes is a common workflow that would silently re-download a coarse series the system can derive for free.

text: A COLD-SWEEP worst-case latency budget MUST be defined and gated — first sweep after a fresh deploy with empty in-process LRU/hot cache, boot-warmed JIT, and cold columnar files — distinct from the warm-sweep budget (#215), the single-run cold worst-case (#214), and cold-compute (#509): wall-time MUST ≈ one-time shared cold setup (load+SoA+timeline, paid once per #505) + warm-sweep compute, with a bounded time-to-first-combo that includes the one-time cold setup. A cold-sweep test asserts the budget and that the shared cold setup is amortized across combos (not repaid per combo).
rationale: Every other cold/warm corner has an explicit budget (214/215/386/509/540) except the first-sweep-after-deploy combined case, which is exactly what an operator hits when kicking off optimize_config on a fresh process. Without a gate, a regression that re-pays cold setup per combo would be invisible.

text: A holistic per-run Postgres ROUND-TRIP regression gate MUST sum ALL query sources on the canonical fixture — kline loads, scan_source signals, per-scan ScanContexts, instrument_info, symbol_lifecycle, manifest seal-check, and the persist writes — and assert the aggregate is O(1) w.r.t. scan count and independent of candle count (constant as scans/candles double), failing phase-over-phase on any increase. This catches a NEW per-scan/per-candle query source added by a later phase that individually slips under each per-source gate (#16/#263/#377/#434/#751).
rationale: Each query source is individually bounded, but no gate sums them. A later phase adding (e.g.) a per-scan provenance write or a per-symbol lifecycle re-check would pass every existing per-source gate while regressing total DB round-trips and tail latency under concurrency.

text: Total setup cost (kline load + SoA build + global-timeline build + scan-anchor binding) MUST scale with Σ(actual per-symbol bounded spans), NOT N_symbols × full_window, so a HETEROGENEOUS/sparse universe is cheap: a fixture of 50 symbols where 25 trade the full 90-day window and 25 trade only ~1 day MUST show total setup time ≈ proportional to actual candle volume (~half a dense-50 run), not 50×W. Distinct from the equal-span symbol-doubling test (#507) and the bounded-load memory rule (#19, which bounds the LOAD but not the build/timeline cost).
rationale: Crypto universes are intrinsically heterogeneous (many alts list mid-window or have short histories). #507 doubles equal-span symbols; nothing asserts the SoA/timeline BUILD (not just the load) tracks Σ actual spans, so a build that materializes full-window arrays for short-lived symbols would scale with N×W and silently dominate setup on wide sparse runs.

text: GET /backtest/{id}/trades pagination MUST be O(page_size) via an indexed (run_id, ordinal/cursor) access path with latency independent of the run's total trade count: a test on a run with thousands of trades asserts per-page fetch latency stays flat as total trade count grows and that paging never re-scans the full trade set. The downstream-feature read-latency analog of the equity_curve GET bound (#515), complementing trade-order/no-skip-dup correctness (#473).
rationale: Optimizing the engine to produce thousands of trades fast is pointless if the UI's paginated trades endpoint then does an O(all-trades) scan per page. #473 pins ordering/no-skip-dup and #508 pins batched insert, but no requirement bounds the GET read latency for trade-heavy runs.
