text: The global sorted-unique timeline AND the vectorized scan-anchor binding (#604) MUST be memoized per (symbol-set, interval, window, scan-set) and reused across SEPARATE rerun invocations (the interactive 'tweak a strategy param and rerun' workflow), so a 2nd rerun on identical data but different strategy config records timeline-build==0 and anchor-bind==0. Extends the SoA-conversion across-reruns memo (#218) to the timeline/anchor artifacts; distinct from once-per-sweep sharing (#505).
rationale: #218 memoizes the dict→array SoA build across reruns, but the O(total_candles) timeline build (#516) and the scan-anchor binding are not stated to survive across separate backtest_run calls — only within one sweep (#505). The dominant single-run iteration UX (adjust TP/SL, rerun) would needlessly rebuild the timeline each time.

text: Progressive per-combo result persistence MUST apply bounded-queue BACKPRESSURE: when combos complete faster than results persist (slow/contended DB), the orchestrator bounds the in-memory pending-persist result-set queue (blocking dispatch or buffering ≤ a configured cap) so finished-but-unpersisted trade lists + equity_curves cannot accumulate unbounded and breach the RSS ceiling. A test with a deliberately slow persist asserts retained pending-result bytes stay ≤ the cap, no combo result is lost, and the sweep still completes — operationalizing the bound asserted in #738.
rationale: #738 asserts retained result bytes ≤ ~concurrency×per-combo, but that bound only holds if persist keeps pace with compute. Under DB contention (which the pool-saturation tests #373/#376 deliberately create) finished results can pile up behind a slow writer; without an explicit backpressure mechanism the assertion is unenforced and RSS can spike.

text: The FIRST on_progress callback and first persisted progress_pct for a single long run MUST fire within a bounded wall-time after run start (e.g. < 1 s) even on the canonical/wide fixture, so the UI shows liveness promptly and a stalled-looking bar is impossible after the cache fix collapses warm-up to near-zero. Complements progress monotonicity (#245) and sweep time-to-first-result (#547) with a single-run time-to-first-progress bound.
rationale: #245 guarantees progress is monotonic and ends at 100, and #547 bounds a sweep's time-to-first-result, but nothing bounds how soon a single (possibly multi-second) run emits its first progress signal — a real UX regression risk if setup work runs before the first callback.

text: The numba/SoA reimplementation of the shared trading_rules.py kernels (RC-11) MUST be CONFINED to the backtest engine path: the live scanner/auto-trade ORDER-EXECUTION path MUST continue calling the original pure-Python trading_rules helpers with NO numba import, NO JIT warm-up, and NO SoA/columnar dependency, so live order sizing/TP-SL placement never pays compile latency, never blocks on JIT warm, and is unaffected if the accel stack is absent/broken. Verified by a test asserting the live execution path imports neither numba nor the SoA kernel and yields unchanged sizing/barrier values.
rationale: trading_rules.py is shared between backtest and live trading (#319/#520). Reimplementing it under numba creates a real risk that live order execution inherits a JIT dependency — paying compile latency or crashing if a wheel is missing. The live-path non-interference reqs (#281/#375/#419/#427) cover CPU/loop/DB contention but not dependency/kernel isolation of the execution helpers.

text: Engine wall-time MUST scale ≤ linearly with TURNOVER (close-event count) at fixed candles×scans: a high-turnover fixture (tight TP/SL, low concurrent P but many sequential closes) holding candles×scans constant while 10×-ing the number of closes MUST show engine-only time grow ≤ ~linearly with close count and stay bounded (each close's equity-point emit, O(1) blacklist counter update #11, wallet update, and preallocated-slot reuse #607 are constant-cost). A turnover axis distinct from the deep-concurrent-book P axis (#389) and the persist/metrics-vs-trades scaling (#220/#378/#508).
rationale: Turnover (sequential closes over time) is orthogonal to concurrent open-book depth P: a scalping config can hold only 1-2 positions yet generate thousands of closes. #389 measures the P axis and #220/#378/#508 measure persist/metrics, but no scaling test isolates ENGINE time vs close count, where an O(closes²) accumulation (e.g. re-scanning trade history for the blacklist) could hide.

text: Each run MUST record peak concurrent open-position count (max P) and total close/turnover count as additive nullable structured metrics (pinned by the observability schema-snapshot #534), so the O(P)/turnover costs are attributable, the pre-flight estimator (#224/#517) can be calibrated against realized P and turnover, and a slow run is diagnosable (deep book vs high turnover) without re-running. A test asserts both fields are present and reflect a seeded fixture's true peak-P and close count.
rationale: Latency on this engine is driven by candles, scans, P (per-candle Σ-uPnL/trailing), and turnover (per-close work). #510 records candle-eval throughput but not P or turnover, leaving the two dominant non-candle cost drivers unmeasured in prod and the pre-flight estimator (#517) uncalibratable against them.

text: After a sweep is cancelled or 120 s-timeout-killed mid-flight, the NEXT sweep on the same long-lived process MUST reach full steady-state throughput and nominal time-to-first-combo: the singleton ProcessPool/ThreadPool (#608) returns its workers to idle (not destroyed/re-spawned) and shared-memory segments are cleaned (#744), so an aborted sweep leaves no residual worker-pool or shared-memory degradation. A test runs an aborted sweep immediately followed by a clean sweep and asserts the clean sweep's first-combo and per-combo steady-state times are within nominal budget — the sweep analog of single-run #550.
rationale: #550 asserts the next single RUN is nominal after a kill, but a sweep adds worker-pool and shared-memory state that an abort can corrupt or leave half-torn. Without this, an aborted sweep could force the next sweep to re-spawn workers/re-warm JIT or leak /dev/shm, degrading the recovery path that operators hit most during incidents.

text: The DuckDB/Polars columnar-engine warm-up (#610) MUST follow the same boot discipline as the numba JIT (#271/#422): deferred/non-blocking so it never delays the readiness/liveness probe, watchdog-bounded so a hung warm-up cannot stall boot, and on warm-up failure it logs and DISABLES the columnar tier (falls back to Postgres) rather than crashing boot; a backtest arriving before columnar warm-up completes (the #458 analog) awaits-bounded or serves from Postgres. Verified by a test simulating a hung/failed columnar warm-up asserting boot completes ready, the tier reports degraded (#164), and runs serve Postgres-identical klines.
rationale: #610 mandates warming the columnar engine at boot 'so the first read pays no init', but unlike the JIT (which has explicit deferral #271, watchdog #422, and pre-warm-window routing #458) the columnar warm-up has no stated non-blocking/watchdog/fallback discipline — so a slow or broken DuckDB/Polars init could block readiness or crash-loop boot, the exact failure modes the JIT reqs were written to prevent.

text: The oracle-diff comparator MUST correctly handle non-finite and sentinel equality at zero tolerance: NaN-vs-NaN MUST compare EQUAL (naive `==` returns False and would false-fail every degenerate-metric fixture), +Inf/-Inf MUST match the same infinity, the documented Inf/NaN sentinel (#715) MUST match its frozen value, and the comparator MUST assert SYMMETRIC EXACT KEY-SET equality for the trade dict and the ~45-metric dict (an EXTRA or MISSING key fails, not only a value drift) AND TYPE equality (int total_trades vs float fails); a meta-test MUST perturb each of these (inject NaN, swap a sentinel, add/drop a key, retype a field) and assert the comparator turns RED.
rationale: Existing comparator reqs (#346 per-field, #676 collection-length, #522 epsilon calibration, #714 signed-zero) never pin NaN==NaN, Inf==Inf, symmetric metric-KEY-set equality, or type equality — so a degenerate fixture false-fails on NaN or a dropped/retyped metric key silently passes when the comparator iterates only oracle keys.

text: The Bybit client test double backing every parity/cache fixture MUST emit BYTE-FAITHFUL Bybit v5 wire payloads — kline rows as the exact list `[startMs, open, high, low, close, volume, turnover]` with STRING-typed numerics, millisecond integer epochs, and newest-first DESCENDING page order (#640) — so the engine's parse/float-cast/normalize path is actually exercised; a meta-test MUST assert that swapping the double for a permissive float/ascending mock would change behavior, and that a MALFORMED page (wrong arity, non-numeric/null OHLC, empty string) drives the documented legacy disposition without sealing or corrupting the manifest.
rationale: No requirement pins the test double's wire FAITHFULNESS; if mocks return pre-parsed floats/ascending while prod Bybit returns descending string arrays, the entire #31 columnar/#640 normalize/parse path and string-cast bugs ship untested behind green parity gates.

text: The per-stage timing model MUST explicitly define EXCLUSIVE-vs-OVERLAPPING semantics and reconcile concurrency: because B&H fetch (#542), 1m drill fetch (#22), and async loads run CONCURRENTLY with other stages, the exhaustive-sum invariant (#481) MUST be specified as either critical-path attribution (Σ exclusive stage time == wall-time) OR overlap-aware (Σ stage time MAY exceed wall-time with a tracked concurrency-overlap term), and a test on a run with deliberately-overlapping stages MUST assert the chosen model holds and no latency hides in an unattributed gap.
rationale: #481 asserts Σ(stage durations)==wall-time within epsilon, which is mathematically impossible once #542/#22 make stages overlap concurrently — the requirements contradict, and neither pins how overlapping concurrent stages reconcile with the exhaustiveness/per-stage-budget gates.

text: Every COLD wall-time/cold-compute measurement in the benchmark harness MUST provably begin from cleared caches: the harness MUST reset and ASSERT-EMPTY (hit-count==0, entry-count==0) the _load_klines LRU, the 1m drill cache, the Arrow/SoA hot frame, the raw→SoA conversion memo, the derived-coarse memo, AND drop/invalidate any process-local columnar handle immediately before the cold-timed region, so a 'cold' number is never silently warmed by a prior iteration or a prior test; a meta-test MUST assert that leaving a cache warm makes the cold measurement detectably smaller and is caught.
rationale: #690 only guarantees fixture materialization precedes the timed window and #518 resets caches between TESTS, but within a single perf test that measures BOTH cold and warm, nothing asserts the cold region starts from a verified-empty cache — a leaked warm entry would understate the cold budget and mask a cold-path regression.

text: The pure simulation engine AND the entire timed pipeline MUST be RNG-free: a guard test (analogous to the no-wall-clock guards #493/#717) MUST assert that no `random`, `numpy.random`, `secrets`, or hash-seeded sampling call occurs during a backtest/sweep run, so results are provably independent of any RNG seed and identical across interpreters; the test MUST fail if a future phase introduces stochastic sampling (e.g. reservoir sampling for downsample, randomized tie-breaks, approximate quantiles).
rationale: Determinism reqs (#206/#686 PYTHONHASHSEED, #348 config_hash) cover dict/hash order but NEVER assert the engine invokes no RNG; a later optimization (randomized LTTB, sampled metrics, randomized work-stealing tie-break) could leak seed-dependence into trades/metrics and silently break reproducibility while passing single-seed tests.

text: A CACHE-layer mutation/canary BATTERY (the cache analog of the engine-defect battery #344/#469) MUST inject distinct cache faults — serve a sealed day with one altered candle, drop a sealed-day's Postgres rows behind a valid manifest, return a stale forming-day OHLC, mis-bucket one symbol's rows, hand back a partial-overlap slice with a torn seam — and assert the relevant gate turns RED for EACH: cold-vs-warm byte-identity (#353), manifest-vs-store integrity (#295), columnar count-shortfall fallback (#602), per-symbol bucketing parity (#447), and range-algebra correctness (#503/#598).
rationale: The mutation battery (#469) proves the ENGINE parity gates have teeth but there is no equivalent proving the CACHE parity/integrity gates (#353/#295/#602/#447) actually detect a wrong-data cache — a subtly-corrupt cache that still 'looks warm' could pass every count-based assertion.

text: A requirement-to-test TRACEABILITY gate MUST maintain a committed matrix mapping every spec requirement ID to ≥1 EXECUTING test, and CI MUST FAIL when a requirement has no covering test OR a test references a deleted/renamed requirement ID (orphan), so the 861-item surface cannot silently drift out of coverage as phases land; the gate MUST distinguish 'covered by an executing assertion' from 'mentioned in a comment'.
rationale: Targeted completeness gates exist for narrow slices (config-surface #477, close_reason union #345, test-count floor #465) but there is no general requirement↔test traceability gate, so a requirement can ship with zero tests and no CI signal — the workflow's Step-16 traceability is a process step, not an enforced gate.

text: An oracle↔fixture PAIRING guard MUST assert that each stored-oracle artifact's stamped config_hash (and stamped symbols/interval/window, #183) equals its live fixture's actually-computed config_hash and inputs, so an oracle can never be silently paired with the WRONG fixture (a copy-paste or rename that maps fixture A's config to oracle B); a meta-test MUST mismatch a fixture's config against its oracle and assert the guard fails before the value comparison runs.
rationale: #347 hash-pins oracle CONTENT and #519 asserts existence, but nothing verifies the oracle belongs to the fixture it is compared against — a mispaired fixture/oracle could pass (both internally consistent) while testing the wrong scenario, and config-builder edits (#466) would not surface it.

text: Exceptions raised inside the user-supplied on_progress callback OR the cooperative cancel-check callback MUST be ISOLATED — caught, logged as a structured warning, and the backtest/sweep MUST continue to a normal terminal state — never aborting the run or leaving a half-persisted result; verified by a test that injects a raising progress callback and a raising cancel-check and asserts the run completes with oracle-identical results and the slot released exactly once.
rationale: Progress/cancel callbacks (#208/#209/#245) are external/UI-supplied and can raise (serialization bug, closed socket), but no requirement isolates a raising callback — an uncaught callback exception would crash an otherwise-correct run and is a realistic DURING-run failure mode distinct from timeout (#299) and engine bug (#637).

text: A sweep COOPERATIVELY CANCELLED mid-flight (threading.Event, distinct from crash #306 and all-combos-fail #727) MUST resolve deterministically: every already-COMPLETED combo stays persisted with a coherent ranked set computed over ONLY the completed subset (a valid contiguous 1..k permutation, #721) OR the sweep is marked partial/incomplete per a documented contract, in-flight combos leave no partial rows, and all workers/slots/shared-memory are released; verified by a cancel-mid-sweep test asserting the persisted ranks are valid over completed combos and no combo is left half-written or double-ranked.
rationale: Cancellation mid-sweep is covered for single runs (#208) and for crash-resume (#306) and all-fail (#727), but the partial-completion ranking semantics of a deliberately-cancelled K-combo sweep (rank over the completed subset vs mark-incomplete) are unspecified and untested — a real operator action during a long sweep.

text: The oracle-regeneration script (#183/#529) MUST produce oracles from the DECIMAL-LEGACY kernel specifically — never a float64/SoA/numba path — and a meta-test MUST assert the regen path runs under the pinned Decimal context (#628) with the legacy kernel selected, so the bit-identical reference the entire gate compares against is always the canonical-precision engine and a phase cannot regenerate the oracle from its own float64 output.
rationale: The whole golden gate (#73/#199) compares against a Decimal-exact oracle, but no requirement pins that the REGEN script invokes the Decimal-legacy kernel — if regeneration ran the float64/numba path the oracle would silently encode last-bit float drift as 'canonical', defeating the bit-identical guarantee.

text: A FRESH-CLONE hermetic bootstrap CI smoke MUST: clone the repo, install the CORE package WITHOUT the accel extra, and run the FULL golden+cache parity suite green entirely offline — network blocked (#293), no prod/real DB (#474), all kline+signal+manifest+auxiliary inputs materialized from the committed deterministic generator (#294/#528/#658/#759) — proving the suite depends on nothing but the repository and the pure-Python path; the smoke MUST fail if any test reaches for a live exchange, a non-test DSN, or an un-generated fixture.
rationale: Individual hermeticity rules exist (#293 no-HTTP, #474 no-prod-DB, #294 offline data, #267 core-install) but no single end-to-end smoke proves a from-scratch clone runs the entire parity/cache battery offline on the pure-Python core — the actual contributor/CI bootstrap path that must never silently require network, prod data, or the accel stack.

text: A sweep MUST be concurrency-DEGREE invariant: running the identical sweep at concurrency C=1, C=2, and C=max MUST yield byte-identical per-combo metrics AND an identical final ranked 1..K set, proving the parallelism DEGREE (not just serial-vs-parallel #207, nor out-of-order completion #317) never leaks into results via scheduling, shared-memory contention, or pointer/cache state; a test MUST run ≥3 concurrency settings and diff all combo results and ranks.
rationale: #207 compares serial to 'parallel' and #317 covers out-of-order completion, but neither varies the concurrency DEGREE across multiple values — a bug that only manifests at C≥3 (shared-pointer reset #733, hot-cache eviction-under-pressure #589, work-stealing #383) would pass the binary serial-vs-parallel check yet diverge at higher parallelism.

text: Engine output (ordered trades + ordered equity_curve + all metrics + signals_no_kline) MUST be INVARIANT to the ORDER symbols are listed in the config/whitelist: a test permuting the requested-symbol order MUST assert byte-identical output, proving the symbol→contiguous-int-code mapping (#603/#761) and the global-timeline merge are order-stable and that requested-symbol order never leaks into sizing/mark/portfolio-reduction order — distinct from #692 (physical DB row order) and #447 (per-symbol array bucketing).
rationale: #692 shuffles DB row order and #447 pins per-symbol bucketing, but the CONFIG symbol-list order is a separate input axis that feeds the order-dependent int-code assignment (#603/#761) and could leak into the Σ-uPnL reduction or close/equity-point ordering — never tested for invariance.

text: The always-on production candle-eval THROUGHPUT meter (#510) MUST be internal-consistency tested like the harness meters (#361): a mocked run with a KNOWN candle count and known per-candle work MUST assert the emitted candle-eval COUNT equals the true count and that count÷engine-CPU-time equals the emitted throughput, so the prod metric the regression-detection (#742) and pre-flight calibration (#859) rely on cannot be a fabricated/miscounted number.
rationale: #361 unit-tests the BENCHMARK harness's meters but the ALWAYS-ON production throughput/candle-eval metric (#510) that prod regression alerting and estimator calibration depend on has no equivalent correctness test — a miscounting prod meter would silently mask or fabricate regressions.

text: A DRILL-specific cold-vs-warm byte-identity test MUST assert that a drill-enabled config's FIRST run (populating the in-memory-only 1m drill cache from fetched/derived 1m sub-bars) and its SECOND in-process run (served entirely from the warm 1m drill cache, 0 Bybit 1m calls per #23) produce byte-identical trades, ordered equity_curve, and metrics — proving the 1m cache changes WHERE 1m data comes from but never WHAT the drill resolves, the drill analog of the 5m cold-vs-warm guarantee (#353).
rationale: #353 proves cold-vs-warm identity for the 5m/durable tiers but the 1m drill cache is in-memory-ONLY (#104/#105/#588) and never durably persisted, so its cold-populate vs warm-serve identity is a distinct, untested path that a drill resolution bug could diverge on between first and second run.

text: A TIME-TRANSLATION metamorphic test MUST shift ALL input epochs (every kline open_time, every signal_time, and date_range_start/end) by a constant offset and assert the resulting trades and equity_curve are identical to the unshifted oracle up to the same constant time offset (entry/exit times shifted, prices/pnl/qty/close_reasons/ordering unchanged), proving no absolute-epoch constant (a hardcoded year, a fixed funding-epoch origin, an annualization anchor) leaked into any sizing/mark/entry/exit/equity/funding/age decision — distinct from no-wall-clock (#493) and no-look-ahead (#533).
rationale: #493 forbids wall-clock reads and #533 forbids future-index reads, but neither catches a hardcoded ABSOLUTE epoch constant (e.g. a fixed funding-boundary origin or annualization anchor tied to a specific calendar date) that would survive both guards yet break when the backtest window moves in absolute time.

text: The deploy-time fleet-consistency check (#463/#800) MUST additionally verify that all running instances report the SAME resolved NUMERIC operational config — sweep concurrency cap, per-tier cache byte/entry budgets, 1m-drill ceiling, frontier skew_margin, thread caps (#212/#577), statement_timeout, pre-flight thresholds — not only the flag set + engine SHA + schema_version, so a partial rollout where one pod runs divergent budgets (causing divergent latency/RSS/seal behavior for the same logical config) is detected before promotion; verified by a test that divergent numeric-config payloads across instance status routes are flagged.
rationale: #463/#800 compare flags, SHA, schema_version, and backfill-marker across instances but NOT the resolved numeric budgets/caps — yet a skew_margin or concurrency-cap divergence between pods directly changes seal decisions and latency/RSS behavior, a silent fleet inconsistency invisible to the existing check.

text: The Bybit HTTP client MUST reuse a bounded keep-alive connection pool (persistent TCP/TLS sessions) across all cold gap-fill pages, per-symbol fetches, and 1m-drill window fetches, so a cold run issuing hundreds of requests pays the TLS/handshake cost ~once per pooled connection rather than per request; a test issuing N sequential and concurrent fetches against a mock asserts the underlying TCP connection count stays <= the configured pool cap (not ~N), and a cold-latency test asserts handshake time is amortized out of the per-page cost.
rationale: Cold wall-time is network-bound (#5/#502); the existing reqs bound request COUNT and concurrency but never pin connection reuse. Per-request TLS handshakes can dominate a many-page cold fetch and silently blow the cold budget even at the correct request count.

text: The Python<->nopython boundary-crossing count for the @njit kernel MUST be O(chunks) (one nopython call per bounded candle-chunk per #210), NOT O(candles) or O(candles x positions): the kernel MUST process an entire candle-span (the whole chunk, looping internally in nopython) per invocation rather than being called once per candle/position from Python; a counter/profile test asserts the dispatcher entry count scales with chunk count and is independent of candle and position count.
rationale: #15 sets a per-candle throughput floor and #210 chunks for cancel, but neither forbids a per-candle Python->numba call. Boundary-crossing overhead (argument unboxing/dispatch) can dominate at 5M evals/sec and silently defeat the JIT if the loop body stays in Python and only the math is jitted.

text: The pure-Python SoA fallback hot loop MUST iterate native Python sequences (materialized once via tolist()/memoryview at chunk entry), NOT per-element numpy indexing (arr[i]), which boxes a fresh Python scalar per access and is ~5-10x slower; a test asserts the bar loop performs no element-wise numpy __getitem__ in the hot path and that the fallback still meets the >=100k candle-evals/sec floor (#14).
rationale: #234 bounds per-candle allocation and #14 sets the fallback throughput floor, but neither pins HOW the SoA arrays are traversed. Element-wise numpy indexing in pure Python is the single most common way to silently miss the 100k/sec floor while appearing to use SoA arrays.

text: The Parquet/Feather compression codec MUST be selected for DECOMPRESSION throughput on the hot read path (e.g. uncompressed/LZ4/zstd-low, never a max-ratio/high-level codec that throttles reads), with a documented read-MB/s target; a gate asserts decompress time stays a bounded share (<= a documented %) of total columnar read time on the canonical fixture, distinct from the disk-footprint budget (#233).
rationale: #233 bounds footprint and #739 tunes row-group pruning, but codec choice trades disk size against read CPU. A high-ratio codec minimizes #233's footprint while inflating warm-read latency, defeating the columnar tier's purpose; the read-speed axis is unpinned.

text: The columnar->SoA handoff MUST be genuinely zero-copy: Arrow int64 epoch and float64 OHLCV columns MUST be exposed to numpy via to_numpy(zero_copy_only=True) / np.frombuffer with NO cast, NO copy, and NO logical-type materialization; a test asserts to_numpy(zero_copy_only=True) does not raise for each column and that the resulting numpy buffer shares memory with the Arrow buffer (no hidden densify/copy), so #437's representation claim is enforced at the API boundary.
rationale: #437 declares int64/float64 Arrow storage 'so the SoA view is zero-copy' but nothing tests that the actual fetch path doesn't silently copy/cast (a single Decimal/timestamp logical column or a chunked array forces a copy). zero_copy_only=True is the concrete, testable assertion that the claim holds.

text: All searchsorted query keys (scan_times, signal_times, mark times) MUST be normalized to the SAME int64 epoch-ms dtype as the timeline/per-symbol arrays ONCE before the scan loop, so numpy performs zero per-call dtype upcast or temporary-array copy; a microbenchmark asserts the search-key array dtype == array dtype and that a deliberately mismatched-dtype key path is normalized (not silently upcast per call, which forces an O(N) copy and can also alter boundary equality).
rationale: #604 vectorizes anchor binding and #64 accepts datetime-OR-epoch input, but a dtype mismatch between the float/object search key and an int64 array makes numpy copy/upcast on every searchsorted call - a silent perf cliff AND a potential boundary-equality shift on the side='left'/'right' decisions (#62/#63).

text: The asyncpg connection pool MUST be pre-warmed at boot (min_size >= _MAX_CONCURRENT, connections established before readiness) so the first backtest after boot does not pay connection-establishment latency inside its timed/120s window; a test asserts the pool reports >= _MAX_CONCURRENT live connections at readiness and that the first run's DB stage excludes connect/auth cost.
rationale: Cold-start worst-case (#214) and pool-exhaustion (#373) are covered, but lazy pool growth means the very first post-deploy run pays TCP+auth+TLS per connection inside the timed path - a measurable cold-start tax not pinned by any existing req.

text: After an accelerated-path mid-run fault triggers a clean fallback rerun (#301/#385), the fallback MUST reuse the warm in-process LRU / immutable SoA kline data already populated by the failed attempt - issuing 0 Bybit refetches and 0 Postgres kline SELECTs on the restart, re-executing only the engine - rather than reloading data from scratch; a test injects an accel fault AFTER the load/SoA-build stage and asserts the fallback's data-I/O counters stay at zero while the engine re-runs.
rationale: #385 mandates freeing the failed attempt's allocations and completing within 120s, but the immutable kline DATA must SURVIVE in the LRU so the fallback is fast. Without pinning data reuse, a fault-and-restart could re-pay the entire cold load and push the combined attempt past the cap on a wide run.

text: A committed profile-denylist regression gate MUST run after Phases 3 and 4: the cProfile/py-spy artifact's top-N cumulative-time symbols MUST NOT contain any name on a committed denylist of eliminated hotspots (Decimal.quantize/__add__/__mul__, the per-scan window-setup function, _evaluate_candles_until linear seeding, per-row dict construction); the test FAILS if any denylisted symbol reappears in the hot profile, converting #37's narrative 'top-2 check' into an enforced gate.
rationale: #37/#223 require a profile artifact and a human check that setup/seeding left the top-2, but provide no automated FAIL. A later phase can silently reintroduce a Decimal call or a per-scan rebuild that a denylist-vs-top-N assertion would catch deterministically.

text: A standing per-phase COMPUTE-cache efficiency gate (the compute-side analog of the data-side #263/#446) MUST assert, on the canonical fixture, that SoA-build, global-timeline-build, scan-anchor-bind, and coarse-resample INVOCATION counts stay at their warm minimums (0 on an identical same-process rerun; exactly 1 per (symbol,interval,range) artifact cold) and never INCREASE phase-over-phase, failing the build on any reintroduced rebuild/re-conversion even when Bybit/Postgres data counters are unchanged.
rationale: #218/#612/#854 establish the individual memos but #612 gates only WITHIN a sweep. There is no standing phase-over-phase gate on the rerun path that catches a refactor reintroducing a per-run SoA/timeline/anchor/resample rebuild - the compute-side equivalent of the data-side cache regression gate.

text: When ALL F1/F2/F3 features are disabled (regime_filter, gate_session, gate_btc_vol, mean_reversion, cohort routing all off), per-scan ScanContext construction MUST be skipped entirely - zero ScanContext objects allocated - not built-and-ignored; a test asserts 0 ScanContext allocations across a ~2160-scan plain-config run and that this path is measurably faster than the regime-active path, complementing the output no-op guarantee (#72) with a COST guarantee.
rationale: #72 guarantees identical OUTPUT with features off and #735 bounds context build when regime is ACTIVE, but nothing forbids paying the per-scan context-build cost when every feature is disabled. On the ~2160-scan canonical fixture a built-and-discarded context per scan is pure wasted setup.

text: The production @njit kernel MUST compile with boundscheck=False (after parity is established) for the timed path, while a dedicated CI test compiles the IDENTICAL kernel source with boundscheck=True and runs it over the full fuzz/differential grid (#319) to PROVE no out-of-bounds access occurs - so the fast production build cannot segfault on an index edge the slow build would have caught; the test asserts the boundscheck=True build passes the grid and that prod config sets boundscheck=False.
rationale: boundscheck=False is a real numba throughput lever but turns an indexing bug into a silent segfault/UB. No existing req addresses bounds-checking; pairing a fast prod build with a CI-only checked build over the fuzz grid makes the optimization safe and testable.

text: A whole-run allocation ceiling MUST hold: total Python allocations (tracemalloc peak) for a run MUST scale with (trades + symbols + curve_length), NOT candle count; a candle-doubling test at fixed trade count asserts total allocated bytes stay within +-10%, complementing the per-candle (#234) and per-open/close (#607) zero-alloc rules with a run-level invariant that catches an allocation that is O(1) per candle but still scales with candle count.
rationale: #234 bounds per-candle and #607 per-position allocation, but a single small allocation that escapes the per-candle check (e.g. a transient per-bar tuple created outside the counted loop) still produces O(candles) total churn and GC pressure. A run-level candle-doubling allocation-flatness gate is the missing backstop.

text: Sweep cross-combo RANKING MUST operate on a compact per-combo METRICS projection (the ranking objective fields only), and MUST NOT reload each combo's full trade list or equity_curve from the DB or hold them in memory to rank; a test asserts the ranking stage issues zero backtest_trades/equity_curve reads and that ranking memory is O(K x small-metrics), not O(K x full-result), complementing the O(K log K) compute bound (#737).
rationale: #737 bounds ranking COMPUTE and #28 bounds rank WRITES, but neither forbids the ranker from reloading or retaining full result objects (trade lists + curves) for K combos to sort them - an O(K x full-result) memory/IO blowup at K=500 that the compact-projection rule prevents.

text: Each sweep MUST emit REALIZED parallelism (mean active workers and/or CPU-utilization over the sweep) as a structured metric, and a standing per-phase gate MUST assert measured sweep speedup vs the committed serial baseline does not REGRESS phase-over-phase - operationalizing the one-time #29 target as a recurring gate so a refactor that silently serializes the sweep (lost nogil, accidental lock, ProcessPool fallback to threads) is caught.
rationale: #29 asserts a speedup target once; #232/#384 note GIL/process caveats. But nothing emits realized parallelism in prod or gates speedup phase-over-phase. A standing speedup-no-regress gate plus a utilization metric makes under-parallelization detectable both in CI and in production.

text: The global sorted-unique timeline build MUST exploit the per-symbol arrays' existing ascending order (k-way merge of pre-sorted arrays, or numpy concatenate+unique kept within #516's linear-bounded budget) and MUST NOT perform a Python-level sort nor re-sort per scan; a test asserts no per-scan sort occurs, the build is invoked once per run (#379/#505), and its transient peak memory stays <= ~1.5x the final timeline-array size (no momentary full duplicate).
rationale: #516 bounds timeline build time/memory and #379 says build-once, but np.unique on a naive concatenation is O(N log N) with a transient full copy and a Python-level sort across scans would be catastrophic. Pinning pre-sortedness exploitation and the transient-memory bound closes the gap between 'O(total_candles)' (#516) and how it's actually achieved.

text: The engine-level shadow/dark-compare mode (#660) that runs BOTH legacy and optimized engines MUST be sampling-rate-bounded with a documented added-CPU/latency budget, so dual execution cannot saturate the live host during the rollout window; a test asserts shadow overhead stays within the configured sample fraction x single-run cost and that disabling shadow removes ALL dual-execution cost (no residual legacy-engine warmup).
rationale: #660 introduces dual-engine shadow comparison but doubles compute on sampled real runs. Without an explicit sampling+budget bound, enabling shadow parity verification in prod could starve the live scanner/auto-trade path - the very thing #375 protects against - so the cost of the safety mechanism must itself be bounded and testable.

text: In-memory equity_curve points MUST be held in primitive columnar/tuple form (parallel float64/int64 arrays or plain numeric tuples, NO Decimal or per-point object), so full-JSONB serialization is a single O(n) pass over primitives within the persist budget; a test asserts no Decimal/object is allocated per curve point during serialize, curve length == close_count (+ seed/terminal per #240), and the serialized bytes round-trip byte-identically (#494).
rationale: #221 bounds curve length and #552 sets a persist budget, but nothing pins the point REPRESENTATION. Holding Decimal/objects per point makes serialization allocate and slow on a trade-heavy run; primitive columnar storage is the concrete lever that keeps full-JSONB serialize O(n) and the manifest hash (#78) stable.

text: The @njit kernel MUST receive the kline SoA, position book, and per-symbol pointer state as typed numpy arrays/scalars only - NO numba reflected list, NO typed.Dict, and NO Python object crossing the nopython boundary inside the per-candle/per-chunk hot path; a test asserts the kernel's nopython signature contains only array/scalar types and that reflected-list/dict arguments are absent (these force boxing and ~10-100x slowdowns or object-mode).
rationale: #607 mandates preallocated typed arrays for zero-alloc and #615 forbids object mode, but neither bans numba reflected lists / typed.Dict passing through the hot loop, which are a notorious numba performance footgun that compiles in nopython yet runs orders of magnitude slow - a distinct failure from object-mode fallback.

text: The cold gap-fill pagination loop MUST check the cooperative cancel (threading.Event) and the 120s run deadline BETWEEN pages, aborting cleanly when tripped - storing already-fetched candles idempotently, sealing the fully-covered closed days it completed (#789), and leaving the remainder unsealed for retry - rather than running the full multi-page fetch to completion past the cap; a test forces a deadline/cancel mid-pagination and asserts the loop stops promptly, persists partial progress, and leaves a clean retryable state.
rationale: #363 enforces the 120s cap over the whole pipeline and #530 covers cancel during the 1m DRILL fetch, but the 5m cold gap-fill pagination loop (which on a wide cold run can be the longest single phase) has no pinned between-page cancel/deadline check - so a slow exchange could blow the cap inside an uninterruptible fetch loop.

text: Columnar materialization (the first-time Parquet/Feather WRITE of sealed days, including the atomic temp-write+rename and any month read-modify-write per #253) MUST run in the executor / off the asyncio event loop, so a cold columnar build cannot block the shared FastAPI loop or the in-process live scanner/auto-trade coroutines; a test asserts a probe coroutine's latency stays within bound while a large columnar materialization is in progress, mirroring the off-loop SoA-build rule (#557).
rationale: #557 moves the SoA build off the loop and #233 budgets columnar build time, but the Parquet WRITE path (serialize + compress + fsync, potentially hundreds of MB) is CPU/IO-heavy and, if run on the event loop, stalls live trading exactly like a blocking kline load would - an uncovered event-loop-starvation vector.

text: While a full-concurrency sweep saturates multiple cores, the live scanner/auto-trade coroutines and API endpoints MUST retain a reserved share of CPU and asyncpg pool such that their p95 latency degrades <= a documented factor; a test runs a wide sweep at max concurrency and asserts a live-path/API probe's p95 stays within bound - the SWEEP analog of the single-backtest event-loop-starvation rule (#375), since a sweep is far harsher on shared CPU/DB than one run.
rationale: #375 bounds live-path degradation under ONE CPU-heavy backtest and #376 reserves a DB-pool fraction, but a max-concurrency sweep contends for ALL cores and many pool connections simultaneously. The measured live-path SLO UNDER A SWEEP (not a single run) is the missing end-to-end assertion that the thread/process/DB budgets actually protect production trading.

text: The min_score filter MUST compare against the EXACT legacy score basis — pin whether it uses the raw SIGNED score or abs(score) — so a negative-score (sell) or exactly-zero-score signal at the min_score boundary is admitted/rejected bit-identically; a fixture with a negative-score and a zero-score signal sitting exactly on min_score asserts the admit/reject decision and disposition counters match the oracle.
rationale: Req 69 pins abs(score) RANKING and req 367/708 pin min_score admit/reject at a threshold, but none pin whether the min_score FILTER itself compares raw-signed vs abs score. With sells likely carrying negative scores and min_score default 0, this sign basis decides whether entire sides are dropped — a high-impact unpinned parity edge the SoA/numba refactor could silently flip.

text: Every numeric strategy-config INPUT field (take_profit_pct, stop_loss_pct, capital_pct, leverage, fee_rate_pct, slippage_bps, trailing_profit_pct, funding_rate_fixed_pct, all mr_* params) MUST be coerced from its API/JSON representation into the engine numeric domain identically across the Decimal-legacy and float64/numba paths, so a binary-inexact value (e.g. 0.1, 0.055) acquires no path-dependent value; a fixture using such values asserts bit-identical barrier prices, fees, and qty regardless of kernel.
rationale: Req 343 pins engine-INTERNAL quantization sites and req 319 diffs the trading_rules KERNELS over a random grid, but neither pins the INPUT coercion of the raw config values feeding those kernels. A float64 path that constructs the same barrier from a differently-coerced fee_rate/leverage could drift before any kernel runs — the entry point of every money calculation.

text: The existing-open-symbol dedup MUST be pinned for the CROSS-COHORT case: specify whether a symbol already open as a TREND position blocks a new mean_reversion signal for that same symbol (and vice versa), since trend and MR are distinct strategies that may legitimately co-hold or must dedup; frozen by a fixture where a symbol is open under one cohort and a same-symbol signal arrives under the other, asserting the admit/reject and any second-position outcome match the oracle.
rationale: Req 694 pins re-entry AFTER close within the same cohort and req 243 pins the mr_max_trades cap, but the trend-vs-MR same-symbol concurrency rule is unpinned. A mixed-cohort run (req 639) could diverge if the refactor makes dedup cohort-agnostic or cohort-scoped differently than legacy.

text: A position that LIQUIDATES on a bar that also crosses a 0/8/16h funding boundary MUST charge funding first (per the within-bar funding→liq order of #42), record that funding in trade.funding_paid, and still satisfy the three-way reconciliation even though the liquidation pnl = −locked_margin via the separate branch (no exit fee/slippage); frozen by a fixture liquidating a position exactly on a funding boundary, asserting funding_paid is non-zero and Σtrade.pnl reconciles with the funding term.
rationale: Reqs 340/472 pin liquidation pnl and the kitchen-sink reconciliation, and 339/558 pin funding boundary/basis, but none pin the CROSS-TERM where funding is charged on the same bar a position liquidates. Whether liquidation suppresses or retains the bar's funding charge is an unpinned sign/accumulation edge.

text: A 1m drill FETCH FAILURE (network/exchange error) on an individual coarse bar mid-run MUST fall back to 5m resolution for THAT bar only — never abort the run, never poison other bars' drill decisions, never persist partial 1m data — and the fallback bar's fill MUST stay non-optimistic versus the always-LTF oracle; a fault-injection test failing the 1m fetch on a subset of drilled bars asserts the rest of the run still drills where data is present and the result is byte-identical to a drill-on oracle with those bars resolved at 5m.
rationale: Req 53 covers full-book 1m COVERAGE fallback, 311 covers partial SUB-BAR coverage, and 530 covers cancel/timeout DURING the drill fetch, but none cover a genuine per-bar fetch FAILURE mid-run and its required per-bar isolation + non-optimistic 5m fallback.

text: When position sizing floors qty to qty_step and the result is EXACTLY 0 (qty_step exceeds the computed qty), the engine MUST disposition the signal byte-identically to legacy — pin whether it is rejected as qty<min_qty, a distinct zero-qty rejection, or never reaches sizing — and increment the same per-scan/lifetime disposition counters (#563/#571); a fixture with a tiny capital_pct/price combination yielding floored-qty==0 asserts the disposition and counters match the oracle.
rationale: Req 60 rejects qty<min_qty and 626 admits qty==min_qty, but the floored-to-exactly-zero case (distinct from min_qty) is unpinned. The float64 floor could land at a different epsilon and change whether the signal counts toward max_trades/target_goal.

text: The risk-free-rate constant used in Sharpe and the minimum-acceptable-return / downside-deviation target used in Sortino MUST be pinned to the exact legacy values (typically 0) and hash-pinned as named constants, so the refactor introduces no different rf/MAR assumption; a fixture asserts Sharpe/Sortino over a known equity curve equal the legacy values and a meta-test pins the rf/MAR constants.
rationale: Reqs 492/699 pin periodization and the annualization factor and 700 pins formulas generally, but the specific risk-free-rate / Sortino-MAR constants are never explicitly pinned. A nonzero or differently-defaulted rf would shift both ratio metrics within the band yet diverge from the oracle.

text: The Buy&Hold BTC baseline MUST be computed on a fully isolated path with no shared mutable state with the symbol engine, and its buy_hold_final_value/excess_return MUST be EXCLUDED from the Σtrade.pnl three-way reconciliation, EVEN WHEN BTC/USDT is itself a traded symbol in the run; a fixture trading BTC alongside others asserts the B&H baseline does not double-count BTC trades, does not perturb the wallet, and the reconciliation holds over trades only.
rationale: Reqs 238/335 pin B&H cache routing and computation, but the case where BTC is BOTH the benchmark and a traded symbol (shared loaders, potential state cross-talk, accidental inclusion in Σpnl) is unpinned — a realistic config that could break reconciliation if the refactor folds the benchmark into the wallet.

text: When a symbol is subject to BOTH the static symbol_whitelist/blacklist (#693) and the adaptive blacklist (#80/#326), the engine MUST apply them at the exact legacy chain positions and precedence — pin the outcome when a symbol is whitelisted but adaptively-blacklisted (and blacklisted-static vs adaptively-clean) — so the two blacklist mechanisms compose identically; a fixture exercising each overlap asserts the admitted set and disposition step match the oracle.
rationale: Req 693 pins static whitelist/blacklist position and 80/326 pin the adaptive blacklist independently, but the INTERACTION/precedence between the two filters when they disagree on the same symbol is unpinned — a chain-ordering edge a refactor could reorder.

text: Each persisted BacktestTrade MUST carry a deterministic ordinal/sequence index assigned by the optimized engine identically to legacy (reflecting the legacy close-order or open-order, pinned), since GET /backtest/{id}/trades cursor pagination (#473/#853) and any UI ordering key on it; a test asserts the ordinal field values are a contiguous gap-free sequence matching the in-memory/oracle trade order, and that the SoA/parallel refactor never renumbers or reorders them.
rationale: Req 473 asserts the persisted ORDER matches the oracle but does not pin the ordinal/sequence-number FIELD that the cursor and UI rely on. If the engine stamps an explicit index, the refactor must assign it identically; an off-by-one or reorder in the ordinal would silently skip/duplicate a page even with correct row order.

text: The mr_short_enabled / mr_long_enabled side-gates MUST drop the disallowed MR side at the exact legacy chain position with identical disposition counters: with mr_long_enabled=false an MR long signal is filtered (not opened) and with mr_short_enabled=false an MR short is filtered; paired admit/reject fixtures per side assert the admitted set, strategy_kind stamping, and counters are byte-identical to the oracle.
rationale: Req 178 includes mr_short/mr_long_enabled in a broad cohort fixture and 845 covers regime gating, but the explicit side-gating disposition (which signals are dropped and how they count) is not pinned as its own admit/reject parity check distinct from the mr_long_ack bypass.

text: Two EXACTLY-duplicate signals within ONE scan (identical symbol, signal_time, score, side) MUST be dispositioned byte-identically to legacy — pin whether they dedup to one admission or both are independently attempted — distinct from differing-score same-symbol duplicates (#495) and duplicated SCAN rows (#832); a fixture seeding an exact in-scan duplicate asserts the resulting trade set, sizing depletion, and counters match the oracle.
rationale: Req 495 covers same-symbol signals of DIFFERING score and 832 covers duplicated SCAN rows, but the exact-duplicate-signal-within-one-scan case is unpinned. SoA/dict re-keying could collapse or duplicate it, changing sizing depletion or admission count.

text: Add an ALL-PERMISSIVE max-admission golden fixture (every 17-step filter set permissive, confidence_filter='any', min_score at floor, high max_trades, no blacklist) with a frozen oracle so a LARGE fraction of signals admit — stressing intra-scan sizing depletion, existing-symbol dedup, the adaptive-blacklist counter, and a deep concurrent open book at high admission volume — asserting bit-identical trades, ordered equity_curve, all metrics, and three-way reconciliation; complements the zero-trade degenerate fixtures (#308) at the opposite extreme.
rationale: The battery has extensive zero-trade/degenerate coverage (308/356/711) but no high-admission stress oracle. Path-dependent sizing depletion, dedup, and blacklist accumulation bugs surface only under heavy admission, which the canonical and degenerate fixtures may not reach at parity-fixture scale.

text: The equity_curve SEED-anchor timestamp source MUST be pinned when the FIRST scan is empty or all-filtered (so it has no admitted signal to source a signal_time from): specify the exact legacy fallback (first non-empty scan's first signal_time, date_range_start, or first kline open_time) so the seed point's ts is byte-identical; a fixture leading with an empty/all-filtered scan asserts the seed anchor ts and the ordered-curve hash match the oracle.
rationale: Req 240 sources the seed anchor from 'the first scan's first signal_time' and 679/499 cover empty/all-filtered scan anchoring, but none pin what seeds the curve anchor when the FIRST scan yields no signal_time. Sharpe/Sortino periodization (#492) derives from inter-point deltas, so a divergent seed ts shifts path-dependent metrics.

text: When the engine-shadow/dark-compare mode (#660) or read-path shadow (#291) detects a beyond-tolerance divergence, the emitted structured signal MUST LOCALIZE it — the diverging trade ordinal/symbol, the specific field (pnl/exit_price/close_reason/equity-point), and the magnitude vs the legacy value — not merely a boolean/aggregate divergence metric, so a prod parity incident is triageable without re-running both engines; a test seeds a single-field divergence and asserts the localized fields appear in the signal.
rationale: Reqs 660/829 emit and threshold-alert a divergence METRIC, but neither requires localizing WHICH trade/field/magnitude diverged. Without localization a rollout-window divergence is detectable but not diagnosable, undermining the shadow-mode safety net.

text: If a per-trade ROI/return/pnl_pct field is recorded on BacktestTrade, its denominator (margin/locked_margin vs notional) and sign convention MUST be frozen against the oracle and reproduced bit-identically (within epsilon for continuous) by the float64/numba path; a side×outcome fixture asserts each quadrant's per-trade return field equals the legacy value, so the refactor cannot drift the recorded ratio while pnl stays correct.
rationale: The oracle-diff (#346) enumerates 19 trade fields + strategy_kind and 700 pins summary-metric formulas, but a per-trade ROI/return field's specific denominator and sign basis are not individually pinned. A margin-vs-notional denominator swap would keep pnl correct yet silently change the recorded per-trade return the UI shows.

text: v58 MUST add/relax the DB CHECK-constraint (or enum domain) on backtest_runs.status so the new terminal/transitional values introduced by the optimization — 'cancelled' (#451), 'failed'-with-timeout-message (#299/#363), 'interrupted by restart' (#450), and any queued state (#536) — are insertable; a test MUST insert each status value against the v58-migrated schema and assert no constraint violation, since a stale CHECK constraint would make the clean-terminal-state reqs (#450/#451/#536/#688) un-persistable in production.
rationale: Reqs 450/451/536/688 mandate new run-status values but none ensures the persisted backtest_runs.status CHECK/enum constraint actually accepts them; CLAUDE.md shows close_reason/status CHECK constraints have historically rejected new values.

text: The legacy/old-code result-persistence upsert (ON CONFLICT(run_id) for backtest_results/backtest_runs, and the sweep_results write) MUST NOT null or clobber the additive fingerprint/stage-timing/provenance/kernel/marker-state columns on a row previously written by new code — the backtest_results analog of req 418 (which only protects kline_cache_coverage); verified by a test running a legacy-shaped result upsert against a v58 row carrying populated additive columns and asserting those columns survive unchanged through a rolling deploy/rollback.
rationale: Req 418 protects only kline_cache_coverage from legacy clobber; the new additive columns on backtest_results/backtest_runs/sweep_results (#395/#430/#496/#549/#661/#674) have the same exposure during the deploy overlap window and are uncovered.

text: The deploy pipeline MUST take and verify a Postgres restore-point/backup as a GATING step immediately before v58 applies, with a documented and tested data-restore procedure, because the master kill-switch (#277) reverts BEHAVIOR but cannot undo DATA damage from a buggy seal-backfill (e.g. wrongful seals or corrupted manifest fact columns); verified by a rollback-drill test that restores the pre-migration snapshot and asserts kline_cache/coverage are byte-identical to the pre-v58 state.
rationale: All rollback reqs (414/432/452/675/768) cover behavior reversion and in-place manifest remediation, but none provides a data-level restore checkpoint before the irreversible-ish v58 DDL+backfill mutates the shared coverage table.

text: The chunked v58 seal-backfill MUST monitor streaming-replica lag (or WAL-generation rate) and automatically pause/throttle when it exceeds a configured threshold, so its large UPDATE volume cannot lag or starve the live-trading read replica that the scanner/auto-trade path reads from; verified by a test that, under simulated rising replica lag, asserts the backfill throttles/pauses and lag stays within the configured budget — distinct from the deadlock/lock bound (#416) and the readiness budget (#266).
rationale: The chunked backfill's WAL/replica-lag impact on the shared live-trading replica is a distinct operational hazard from lock contention (416) and probe timeout (266) and is uncovered.

text: The completion-frontier now_utc MUST derive from a SINGLE coordinated/authoritative clock source shared by every app instance and the backfill (e.g. Postgres NOW() or an NTP-disciplined reference), so two instances with skewed host clocks cannot disagree on a day's sealed-vs-forming classification and issue divergent seal/refresh decisions; verified by a test running two instances on deliberately skewed simulated clocks against one DB and asserting identical seal/forming classification for the boundary day — the cross-INSTANCE agreement gap beyond skew-margin (#249), backward-clock ratchet (#390), and within-run stability (#642).
rationale: 249/390/578/642 address per-clock margin, monotonic ratchet, backfill==runtime helper, and within-run stability, but none guarantees that multiple app instances with skewed clocks agree on the frontier — a real distributed seal-correctness hazard.

text: The DEFERRED seal-backfill MUST recompute the per-(symbol,interval) completion frontier per-chunk as it progresses (not freeze it once at backfill start), OR it MUST be proven+documented that a day which crosses the frontier DURING a long-running backfill is left unsealed by the backfill and correctly sealed by the next runtime lazy-seal pass (#84/#402/#592) with no double-seal or permanent mis-classification; verified by a test that advances a simulated clock across a day boundary mid-backfill and asserts the crossing day ends up sealed exactly once and never mis-classified.
rationale: 578 pins backfill==runtime frontier helper and 642 pins within-RUN stability, but a long deferred backfill is not a 'run'; the frontier-advances-mid-backfill case (a day finishing forming during the backfill) is an uncovered subtle parity/seal edge.

text: The deploy automation MUST AUTOMATICALLY revert — engage the master safe-mode kill-switch (#277) and/or halt-and-roll-back — when the post-deploy canary/smoke (#288/#427/#809/#810) or the sealed-day-refetch promotion check (#582) FAILS, not merely emit an alert; verified by a test that a failing canary/non-zero sealed-day-refetch causes the promotion hook to trigger the revert action, closing the loop between the failure-alert signal (#429) and the kill-switch (#277).
rationale: 429/582/668/810 emit gating/alert signals and 277 provides the kill-switch, but nothing wires a failing canary to an AUTOMATIC revert; an alert that requires manual action leaves a window of bad production behavior.

text: Every change to the master kill-switch or any per-path optimization flag (especially the DB-config-row / watched-file backed flags of #769) MUST be written to a persisted audit trail recording timestamp, actor/source, and old→new value, so an incident timeline can correlate a parity/latency change to the exact flag flip that caused it; verified by a test asserting a flag change appends an audit record with the correct before/after values — distinct from the per-RUN resolved-flag snapshot (#278).
rationale: 278 records the flag set per run but nothing records WHO/WHEN changed a flag; without a flag-change audit trail an incident cannot be correlated to an operator action during the rollout.

text: v58 MUST pre-check available Postgres disk/headroom for the DDL, any monthly-partition creation, and the seal-backfill's projected row/WAL footprint, and fail-fast with an actionable message BEFORE starting rather than aborting mid-backfill and leaving partial seal state; verified by a test that under a simulated insufficient-disk condition the migration refuses to start, leaves schema_version unchanged, and surfaces the shortfall — distinct from the columnar-cache-dir free-space check (#587).
rationale: 587 checks the columnar cache directory volume, but no requirement guards Postgres disk for the migration's own DDL/partition/backfill writes, whose mid-run exhaustion would leave partial state.

text: A single migration-completeness meta-test MUST enumerate EVERY additive persisted column introduced by ANY phase's persistence writes — optimization fingerprint (#430), per-stage timings (#549), data-provenance/signal-provenance (#395/#650/#794), recorded numeric kernel (#496), peak-P/turnover (#859), runtime backfill-marker state (#674), data-quality counters (#764), and sweep fingerprint (#661) — and assert each is created by an idempotent ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS with a constant default in v58 (or a clearly-later versioned migration), so no persistence write can ever reference an unmigrated column at runtime.
rationale: Individual reqs mandate many additive result/run/sweep columns but none asserts the UNION of them is actually shipped by the idempotent v58 DDL; a phase's persistence write referencing a never-migrated column is an untested production-break risk.

text: At boot the running code's EXPECTED schema_version MUST be compared against the DB's actual schema_version and any mismatch — migration silently skipped/never-run (DB < expected) or DB ahead of code without a rollback path (DB > expected) — MUST emit a gating/alertable structured signal (and route to the documented legacy/kill-switch path where applicable) rather than silently serving degraded; verified by a test booting code whose expected version differs from the DB and asserting the alert fires — distinct from the v58-DDL-failure alert (#429) and fleet-consistency check (#800).
rationale: 429 covers an explicit v58 DDL failure and 800 covers cross-instance consistency, but a silently-skipped migration (DB at 57, code expecting 58) that then serves on the legacy path with no alert is an undetected mis-deploy.

text: Results produced in dark mode (all behavior-changing optimization flags OFF during the schema-first/behavior-later rollout per #672) MUST STILL populate the new fingerprint columns (flags=all-off snapshot, engine git SHA, schema_version=58) while remaining byte-identical to the pre-v58 oracle, so even legacy-equivalent rollout-window results are post-hoc attributable and distinguishable from later optimized results; verified by a test asserting a dark-mode run is oracle-identical AND carries a fully-populated all-off fingerprint.
rationale: 672 asserts dark-mode byte-identity with v58 schema but does not assert the fingerprint columns are populated for dark-mode runs, leaving rollout-window results unattributable in a later incident.

text: The migration runner MUST surface an actionable, specific error naming the missing privilege and the failing statement when run by a DB role lacking CREATE TABLE / ALTER TABLE / CREATE INDEX (CONCURRENTLY) rights, and MUST leave schema_version and all partial DDL unapplied (clean no-op), rather than a generic failure or partial apply; verified by a test running v58 as a least-privilege role and asserting the precise privilege-error message and an unchanged schema_version — extending the generic migration-error surfacing of #165.
rationale: 165 covers surfacing a failing DDL generically, but the specific least-privilege deploy-target case (a restricted DB role) needs an actionable, attributable error and a guaranteed clean no-op, which is uncovered.

text: The background symbol_lifecycle refresh (#583) MUST correctly handle lifecycle TRANSITIONS: on discovering a symbol delisted since the last refresh it sets delisted=true and delist_time and triggers sealing of the now-complete in-life tail (enabling the zero-refresh dead-symbol guarantee of #392), and on a re-listing (#411) it extends/clears delist_time so the post-delist negative cache is reopened — verified by paired tests driving a delist transition and a re-list transition and asserting the negative-cache/live-edge-refresh decisions update correctly without un-sealing valid in-life days.
rationale: 392 covers a delisted symbol's steady state and 411 covers re-list candle detection, but the lifecycle-row UPDATE semantics at the moment of the delist/re-list TRANSITION (which drive the seal and negative-cache decisions) are uncovered.

text: The deferred seal-backfill MUST be resilient to a per-chunk/per-symbol data error (corrupt coverage row, unexpected NULL, or a seal-compute exception): it MUST skip+log+surface the offending (symbol,interval,day) chunk and continue sealing the rest of the universe — never aborting the whole backfill on one bad chunk — and record the skipped chunks for later remediation; verified by a test that injects a poison chunk and asserts the remaining days still seal, the marker still reaches a defined terminal/partial state, and the bad chunk is reported, distinct from idempotency (#132) and checkpoint-resumability (#775).
rationale: 132/775/782 cover idempotency, checkpoint-resume, and pause/abort, but none ensures one corrupt symbol-day cannot abort the entire universe-wide backfill — a realistic operational failure on large histories.

text: A committed golden DB-SCHEMA snapshot (full column set with types/defaults, indexes, constraints, and partition bounds) MUST be diffed against the live introspected schema after v58 in a CI/deploy gate, failing on ANY unexpected drift, as a holistic backstop above the per-aspect checks (#128 column presence, #154 no-drop/rename/retype, #770 incompatible-pre-existing-type, #456 partition alignment) and the schema_version int; verified by a test that a deliberately drifted column type/default/index trips the golden-schema gate.
rationale: Individual checks cover specific columns/constraints, but no single golden-schema diff catches an unexpected aggregate drift (wrong default, missing index, misaligned partition) introduced by any phase editing async_persistence.py.

text: A heavy backtest/sweep populating the shared process-level kline LRU/Arrow hot cache MUST NOT evict the LIVE scanner/auto-trade path's hot working set (the current/forming-day klines and recent indicator-lookback ranges it depends on): the cache MUST partition or priority-weight live-path entries (pinned/reserved budget or a separate live tier) so a backtest cannot cause the live path to re-read from Postgres/Bybit; verified by a test that runs a cache-thrashing backtest and asserts the live path's subsequent fetches stay warm (0 added Bybit calls / 0 added kline SELECTs) while only backtest entries were evicted.
rationale: kline_cache_service is explicitly shared between live and backtest (reqs 281/419/857), and reqs 102/388 bound the LRU and define LRU eviction, but NOTHING prevents a backtest sweep from evicting the live trading path's hot entries — a real production latency/cost regression on the money-making path that no existing requirement guards.

text: The per-day sealed manifest and the legacy GREATEST-monotonic coverage high-water-mark (_update_coverage / last_covered) MUST stay mutually consistent: no (symbol,interval,day) may be sealed=true while the legacy high-water-mark still reports that day as not-yet-covered, and a range MUST be classified identically (complete vs gapped) by both the manifest gate and the legacy coverage path when both are consulted; verified by a consistency test asserting the two coverage mechanisms never disagree on any day across a representative coverage volume, since reqs 114/150/401 keep BOTH mechanisms live but never assert they agree.
rationale: Reqs 77/114 keep _update_coverage GREATEST-monotonic and req 150 keeps the legacy count-based path toggleable, while the manifest is layered on top (req 401 reuses the same PK) — but a day sealed in the manifest yet behind the legacy high-water-mark (or vice-versa) is an undetected skew that breaks the toggle-off fallback (req 150) and the during-deploy overlap (req 807).

text: If req 588's option (b) is taken (persisting sealed 1m drilled sub-bars for cross-process warm drill reruns), that durable 1m-drill store MUST have its OWN completion-frontier/seal/immutability semantics mirroring the 5m manifest, MUST stay physically separate from kline_cache/kline_cache_coverage (never poisoning 5m coverage), MUST clip the still-forming 1m bar before persisting, and MUST be served to a FRESH-process drill rerun with 0 Bybit 1m calls; verified by a cross-process drill-rerun test asserting 0 1m calls, the 5m coverage table byte-unchanged, and no forming 1m bar persisted.
rationale: Req 588 names option (b) (persist sealed 1m sub-bars) as a way to honor the cross-process 0-call guarantee, but no requirement specifies the SEAL/frontier/clip/separation contract for that durable 1m store — without it, persisting 1m data either re-poisons 5m coverage (violating reqs 105/191) or freezes a forming 1m bar.

text: The in-memory 1m drill cache (keyed (symbol, bar_open_epoch)) MUST EXCLUDE the still-forming 1m sub-bar(s) of a drilled live-edge 5m bar (any 1m bar with open_time >= the 1m completion frontier), so drilling the forming 5m bar always re-fetches its forming 1m sub-bars fresh while sealed 1m sub-bars of past 5m bars stay memoized; verified by a test that drills a forming 5m bar and asserts the forming 1m sub-bar is re-fetched on rerun while its sealed sibling sub-bars issue 0 fetches.
rationale: Reqs 104/105/228/380 define the in-memory 1m drill cache and req 158 covers forming-DAY invalidation of the 5m LRU, but none address the finer case of a forming 1m sub-bar INSIDE a drilled live-edge 5m bar being memoized as if complete — caching a still-forming 1m candle would serve a stale partial fill on the next rerun.

text: When the backtest window includes the still-forming/live-edge day, the auxiliary reference series — Buy&Hold BTC final close, btc_vol regime BTC data, and the MR mean-reference series — MUST be valued off the SAME point-in-time forming-candle snapshot the engine consumed for the symbol klines (not a separately-fetched fresher forming candle), so a run is internally consistent and reproducible; verified by a fixture whose window ends on the forming day asserting the engine's terminal mark and the B&H/btc_vol/MR-mean forming values all derive from one coherent snapshot.
rationale: Reqs 238/335/355 route aux series through the cache and req 767 pins a consistent forming-day snapshot for the SYMBOL load, but nothing ties the AUXILIARY series (fetched on separate paths) to that same forming snapshot — a fresher B&H/btc_vol forming candle than the engine's would make excess_return / regime gating non-reproducible run-to-run.

text: A genuine Postgres failure DURING a run (connection lost or query error mid kline-load OR mid _persist_results, distinct from the 120s timeout #299/#363, cooperative cancel #208, and accelerated-path fault #301) MUST resolve backtest_runs to status='failed' with an actionable DB-error message, commit ZERO partial backtest_trades/results/equity_curve rows, release the _MAX_CONCURRENT slot exactly once, and leave the shared LRU/hot cache/manifest uncorrupted; verified by a fault-injection test dropping the connection mid-load and mid-persist and asserting clean terminal state plus byte-identical klines on the next run.
rationale: Req 637 covers an unhandled ENGINE exception and reqs 299/363 cover TIMEOUT during engine/persist, but a transient Postgres connection loss / query error mid-run is a distinct, realistic failure mode (esp. under the connection-pool pressure of #373/#376) with no requirement asserting clean failed-state + slot release + cache integrity for it.

text: The derive-coarse-from-fine resampling (reqs 116/405/849) MUST be independently toggleable via its own flag in the central registry (req 147), and with the flag OFF a coarse-interval (15m/1h/4h) backtest MUST revert to the legacy native per-interval fetch/load path and produce byte-identical klines and trades to the pre-optimization engine; verified by a flag-off test asserting a 1h backtest fetches native 1h and is oracle-identical, so a derive-coarse correctness bug is operationally revertible without disabling the whole columnar/read layer.
rationale: Req 147 enumerates the toggleable optimizations but OMITS derive-coarse-from-fine, and req 149's columnar-read kill-switch reverts the READ tier (Parquet→Postgres) NOT the derive-vs-fetch decision — leaving no way to roll back ONLY the coarse-derivation if reqs 117/258/404/449 prove subtly wrong while keeping every other optimization on.

text: Symbol-lifecycle DISCOVERY (listing_time/delist_time + the resulting pre-listing/post-delist negative-cache day set) MUST be deterministic across processes: two independent cold runs over the same symbol+window MUST converge to identical lifecycle bounds and identical negative-cached day ranges regardless of which process discovered first or fetch ordering; verified by a test that runs discovery in two fresh processes against the same mocked exchange and diffs the resulting symbol_lifecycle rows and negative-cache day sets for byte-equality.
rationale: Reqs 259/272/411/583 define lifecycle population and absent-row semantics, but none assert that DISCOVERY is deterministic/convergent across processes — a non-deterministic discovered listing boundary would make the negative-cache set (and thus warm-rerun 0-call guarantees and the data-provenance fingerprint #395) depend on which process ran first.

text: The hot, live-shared kline_cache_coverage and the sealed-manifest table MUST tolerate sustained high-frequency seal/coverage upsert churn (forming-day GREATEST upserts + per-day seal upserts across many runs) without unbounded dead-tuple/index bloat degrading seal-check or coverage-upsert latency: the write pattern MUST be autovacuum/HOT-update-friendly (or carry a documented vacuum/fillfactor policy), verified by a churn test that performs many seal+coverage cycles and asserts seal-check and coverage-upsert latency stay within bound (no monotonic degradation) after the churn.
rationale: Reqs 512/514 require the seal-check be index-backed and O(range) and req 774 bounds manifest ROW COUNT, but none address Postgres MVCC dead-tuple/index bloat from the high-churn UPSERT pattern on the shared coverage table — a classic cause of gradually-degrading query latency on hot upsert tables that the index-backed-O(range) gate would not catch.

text: The fetch-ingest path MUST validate each candle's OHLC sanity BEFORE store_klines and BEFORE sealing — every field finite (no NaN/Inf), non-negative, low <= min(open,close) <= max(open,close) <= high, and on the interval grid — and apply ONE documented policy for a malformed candle (reject-and-record-the-sub-range-as-a-confirmed-gap, never silently store-and-seal it), so a corrupt exchange candle can never be frozen into an immutable sealed day nor poison its content_sha256; verified by a test feeding a malformed (high<low / negative / NaN) candle and asserting the day is NOT sealed as containing it and the manifest sha reflects only valid candles.
rationale: Req 657 validates the synthetic FIXTURE generator's OHLC validity and reqs 491/752 handle NaN/off-grid at ENGINE evaluation time, but nothing validates REAL exchange data at INGEST before the immutable seal — a single bad Bybit candle stored into a sealed day is permanently frozen (seal-once-ever) and silently corrupts every backtest reading that day.

text: A sweep that VARIES simulation_interval across combos (e.g. some 5m, some 1h, some 4h) MUST load the fine 5m base exactly ONCE, derive+SoA-build each DISTINCT coarse interval exactly once (memoized, reused across all combos of that interval), and build each per-interval global timeline exactly once — never per combo; verified by a counter test on an interval-varying sweep asserting fine-base-load==1, per-coarse-interval derive==1 and SoA-build==1, and per-interval timeline-build==1 across the whole sweep.
rationale: Req 505 assumes a single shared timeline per sweep (one interval), reqs 651 covers window/symbol-varying combos, and reqs 218/405/854 memoize SoA/resample/timeline — but none explicitly gate the interval-VARYING sweep where each distinct coarse interval needs its own derive+SoA+timeline built once; without an explicit gate a later phase could re-derive/re-build per combo and silently lose the sharing.

text: Intra-combo progress/heartbeat DB writes across a K-combo sweep MUST be throttled and aggregated at the SWEEP level so total progress_pct/heartbeat write statements stay O(100) regardless of K (not K x each combo's O(100)); a test on a >=50-combo sweep MUST assert aggregate progress-write statement count stays bounded and constant as K doubles, and that progress-emission overhead stays <2% of sweep wall-time.
rationale: #209 bounds a SINGLE run's progress writes and #547 covers per-combo RESULT persistence, but neither bounds the AGGREGATE intra-combo progress-write load across many concurrent combos, which can reach K x O(100) and saturate the asyncpg pool / DB during a wide sweep.

text: Repeated delete-before-insert re-runs of the same run_id (the interactive tweak-and-rerun loop and failed-run retries) MUST NOT cause unbounded dead-tuple/index bloat on backtest_trades / equity_curve that degrades persist or GET latency: the write pattern MUST be autovacuum/HOT-update-friendly or carry a documented fillfactor/vacuum policy; a churn test re-running one run_id N times MUST assert persist and GET latency stay within bound (no monotonic degradation) and table+index size stays bounded after the churn.
rationale: #942 covers kline_cache_coverage/manifest churn and #531 covers re-run CORRECTNESS (no orphans), but neither addresses dead-tuple/index bloat on the backtest_trades/equity_curve tables from repeated delete-before-insert cycles, which silently slows the persist and read paths over a long-lived process.

text: The GET-path downsampled equity_curve (and the metrics object) for a COMPLETED (immutable) run MUST be served from a precomputed/materialized downsample or a read-through cache so repeated and concurrent GETs do NOT re-parse and re-LTTB the full stored JSONB each time; a test MUST assert the second and concurrent GETs of the same run skip the full-curve parse/downsample (served from cache/materialized view) and that GET latency stays flat under repeated/concurrent dashboard polling independent of stored-curve length.
rationale: #515 bounds a single downsample to O(n), #726 pins determinism, and #368 pins trough-preservation, but completed results are immutable and dashboard polling / multi-panel / multi-user GETs each re-read and re-downsample the full JSONB; no requirement caches or materializes the GET-path view.

text: The stored full equity_curve JSONB MUST carry a documented size budget and a write-time guard for pathological high-turnover runs (#858 makes close-count, hence curve length, very large): the persist path MUST keep the manifest hash over the full ordered curve (#78) yet bound stored row size / serialize cost, and a high-turnover fixture MUST assert the backtest_results row size and serialize time stay within budget while GET still returns a correct bounded downsample.
rationale: #221 bounds curve LENGTH to close-count and serialize TIME/RSS, but a high-turnover run can still produce a multi-MB curve JSONB that bloats backtest_results and every read of it; no stored-BYTE-size budget or write-time handling exists for the pathological-curve case.

text: Indicator/feature series derived from klines for the F1 regime/session/btc_vol gates and the F2 MR mean (trend-EMA, ATR, btc_vol series, MR reference-mean inputs) MUST be computed ONCE per (symbol/BTC, interval, range) and reused across all sweep combos and across reruns - only the per-combo THRESHOLD comparison (regime_volatile_atr, regime_trend_ema_dist_pct, mr_min_edge_pct, mr_mean_period/interval) varies per combo; a counter test on a regime/MR-threshold-varying sweep MUST assert the underlying feature-series derivation is invoked once per (symbol,interval,range), not once per combo, and a same-process rerun records 0 re-derivations.
rationale: #735 bounds per-scan ScanContext cost within ONE run and #218/#405 memoize SoA/coarse resample, but the expensive klines->ATR/EMA/btc_vol/mean derivation feeding the regime/MR gates is not stated as memoized across the many combos of a threshold-sweeping optimize_config run, so it can be recomputed K times.
