text: When the mmap'd Feather/Arrow working set exceeds available page cache (the wide capacity point #374, or a multi-worker host #445 where N workers each hold their own mapping), the read path MUST bound major-page-fault thrashing - via a working-set-aware read budget, bounded symbol-batched/streamed reads, or an explicit fallback to chunked Postgres - so cross-process reads degrade gracefully rather than collapsing into fault storms; a test under a constrained page-cache/RAM limit MUST assert a wide mmap read completes within its documented relaxed budget and the major-fault rate stays bounded.
rationale: #386 budgets cross-process mmap latency ASSUMING page-in succeeds and #445 sets a shared-memory aggregate ceiling, but neither handles the regime where the mmap working set exceeds page cache and triggers major-fault thrashing, which on the wide fixture would blow the latency budget unpredictably.

text: Because the sealed-manifest fetch gate is inserted into the SHARED kline_cache_service, a per-phase regression gate MUST assert the LIVE scanner/auto-trade kline-fetch latency (current/forming-day path) does not regress beyond a documented bounded factor versus the pre-optimization baseline, with the added per-fetch manifest seal-check being index-backed and O(requested days); a test MUST measure live-path fetch latency with the manifest gate on vs off and fail on regression beyond the bound.
rationale: The manifest gate lands on the service the LIVE trading path depends on; #281/#934 guard live CORRECTNESS/eviction and #512 guards BACKTEST seal-check latency, but no gate guards the live trading path's hot fetch latency against the newly-inserted per-fetch manifest lookup - a downstream perf regression that would silently slow real trading.

text: Any per-run invariant checks executed on PRODUCTION runs (the three-way Sigma trade.pnl reconciliation and the data/signal provenance-fingerprint hashing #395/#794) MUST be O(trades + signals + manifest-days) and stay within a documented bounded share of wall-time, NOT O(candles); a test doubling candle count at fixed trade/signal count MUST assert these checks' time stays constant within +-10% and their combined overhead stays under the bound on a trade-heavy run.
rationale: The provenance/signal fingerprint hashing (#395/#794) runs per production run over potentially thousands of signals + many manifest days, and the reconciliation over thousands of trades has real cost; no requirement bounds these or proves they scale with trades/signals rather than candle count, so they could silently dominate a fast run.

text: For a window straddling the seal/forming boundary, the sealed-columnar tier read and the forming-day Postgres read (#439/#597) MUST be issued CONCURRENTLY (not serially) and then stitched, so the per-load latency ~ max(columnar, Postgres) rather than their sum; a test with latency-bearing mocks for both tiers MUST assert the straddling-window load wall-time approximates the slower single tier, not the additive sum, across many symbols.
rationale: #439/#597 pin stitch CORRECTNESS but never require the two tier reads to overlap; serially awaiting columnar-then-Postgres needlessly adds both latencies on every boundary-straddling load (the common 'window ending today' case), multiplied across all symbols.

text: A sweep MUST emit an observable progress/heartbeat signal (combos dispatched/total, shared-setup-complete marker) within a bounded wall-time after launch - BEFORE the first combo completes - so the UI shows liveness during the one-time shared cold setup (load+SoA+timeline per #505/#850); a test MUST assert a sweep's first progress event fires within the bound and advances monotonically, distinct from time-to-first-RESULT (#547) and single-run time-to-first-progress (#856).
rationale: #547 bounds time-to-first-RESULT (first combo done) and #856 bounds a single run's first progress, but a cold sweep paying one-time shared setup before ANY combo finishes has no liveness-before-first-combo requirement, so its progress bar can appear stalled for the entire shared-setup window.

text: While the deferred v58 seal-backfill is actively running, concurrent BACKTEST kline-load and manifest seal-check latency MUST stay within a documented bounded factor of nominal (the backfill's chunked writes + lock/throttle policy must not starve backtest reads), so runs launched during the bake window are not pushed toward the 120s cap; a test running backtests concurrently with an active backfill MUST assert backtest read latency stays within bound and no run is timeout-killed due to backfill contention.
rationale: #416 bounds LIVE-write latency during the backfill and #266 bounds readiness, but neither bounds concurrent BACKTEST read latency during the active backfill - which, combined with the slower partially-sealed manifest (#574/#793) that legitimately issues extra cold fetches, could push a mid-bake-window run into the timeout-kill.

text: Sealing a closed day MUST depend ONLY on durable Postgres SOR rows + the manifest seal commit, NEVER on successful Parquet/Feather columnar materialization — reconciling the seal-after-Parquet ordering of #251 with the Postgres-only Phase-1 seal of #444: a columnar-write failure MUST leave the day SEALED (served from Postgres, retryable for lazy materialization) and MUST NOT leave it unsealed/refetchable; verified by a test that injects a columnar-write failure during a cold run and asserts the day still seals, a rerun issues 0 Bybit refetches, and reads serve byte-identical klines from Postgres.
rationale: #251 (seal after Parquet written) and #444 (seal Postgres-only before Parquet exists) are in direct tension; without an explicit rule that materialization failure never blocks sealing, a transient Parquet-write fault leaves a closed day perpetually unsealed and refetched — re-introducing the very re-download bug the project exists to kill.

text: When kline_cache (Postgres SOR) has or gains retention pruning (#656), the columnar GC/eviction (#261/#403) MUST NOT evict a Parquet/Feather partition that is the SOLE remaining durable copy (its Postgres rows pruned), AND a columnar read-integrity failure (corruption #255 / count-shortfall #602 / format-mismatch #256) on such a partition MUST fail-loud or trigger a controlled bounded Bybit re-fetch+re-seal of exactly that range rather than silently returning the now-short/zero-row Postgres fallback (#251); verified by a test that prunes Postgres behind a sealed+materialized day and asserts (a) GC refuses to evict that partition and (b) a corrupted such partition fails-loud or re-fetches instead of returning truncated data.
rationale: #656 raises the pruning scenario (columnar becomes SOR) but pins neither downstream consequence; once Postgres is pruned the normal #251 Postgres fallback is invalid, so GC-evicting the last copy is silent data loss and integrity-fallback to Postgres returns truncated klines.

text: The in-place manifest rebuild-from-SOR (#675/#777) MUST preserve every known-empty/negative-cache record that is NOT re-derivable from kline_cache rows + symbol_lifecycle alone — confirmed interior structural gaps (#260/#753) and genuine in-life 200-empty days (#797) — because a zero-row day is indistinguishable from a never-fetched hole post-rebuild; either the gap-range/known-empty records persist durably and survive rebuild, or the rebuilt manifest re-verifies each such ambiguous in-life empty day exactly ONCE before re-sealing, NEVER perpetually refetching it; verified by a rebuild test over a day carrying a confirmed interior structural gap and an in-life 200-empty day asserting zero perpetual refetch after rebuild.
rationale: #675/#777 reconstruct seal+facts from Postgres+lifecycle, but lifecycle cannot explain interior structural gaps or in-life outages, so a naive rebuild silently re-opens confirmed-empty ranges to perpetual refetch — defeating negative caching after any manifest rebuild / copy-prod-scans seeding.

text: content_sha256's EXACT included field set (open_time + OHLC, and explicitly whether volume and turnover participate) MUST be documented, pinned by a meta-test, carry the algorithm-version tag (#641), and be byte-identical between the ingest-time hash and the Postgres-rebuild/Parquet-rebuild hash (#254/#444); the restatement-detection consequence of the chosen set (e.g. a volume-only exchange restatement provably DOES or provably does NOT trigger the #296 hash-mismatch refetch) MUST be explicit and tested; verified by hashing a day, restating only volume, and asserting the documented include/exclude refetch behavior.
rationale: #254 fixes canonical ordering/serialization but never pins WHICH columns are hashed, leaving it ambiguous whether non-OHLC (volume/turnover) exchange restatements trigger spurious integrity refetches or silently miss a real correction.

text: Before sealing a closed day, the seal path MUST ensure no STALE pre-frontier/forming candle persisted un-clipped (e.g. by a legacy rolling-deploy instance per #807, which lacks the #435 forming-clip) survives via store_klines ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING: the day's boundary/terminal candles MUST be confirmed fetched at/after the frontier and any pre-existing same-(symbol,interval,open_time) row overwritten (delete+reinsert or seal-time upsert) before the seal commits, so DO-NOTHING staleness can never be frozen into an immutable sealed day; verified by a test that pre-stores a stale forming candle, runs the closed-day fetch+seal, and asserts the sealed row holds the FINAL value and content_sha256 reflects it.
rationale: #435 clips forming candles only for new code; during the #807 overlap window a legacy instance can store an un-clipped forming candle that DO-NOTHING then prevents correcting once it closes, freezing stale OHLC into a sealed immutable day with a wrong sha.

text: scan_source signals AND per-scan ScanContexts MUST be memoizable in an in-process cache keyed on (scan_source identity, interval, window) EXCLUDING strategy config, so a 'tweak a strategy param and rerun' over the same window (#504) issues 0 signal-table queries and 0 ScanContext-build/Postgres queries on the rerun while only the engine re-executes; the memo MUST be bounded/evictable and return byte-identical signals/contexts to a fresh load (#564); verified by a test asserting the second rerun's signal+scan-context query count is 0, mirroring the kline LRU (#504) and timeline/anchor memo (#854).
rationale: #504/#854/#218 warm klines/timeline/SoA across reruns, but the signal + ScanContext LOAD is re-queried every rerun, so the interactive param-sweep workflow keeps paying repeated O(1)-but-nonzero Postgres round-trips that the rest of the warm path eliminated.

text: A batched symbol=ANY($1) kline load whose result set is large (e.g. ~2.6M+ rows for the canonical 50sym×90d×5m, more for wide/long runs) MUST stream rows into the columnar SoA arrays via a server-side cursor / bounded fetch batches rather than materializing the entire result as Python Records at once, so transient peak RSS during the FETCH stays bounded (≤ a documented multiple of final SoA size) even for in-ceiling runs that do not trigger the symbol-batched wide path (#374/#748); verified by a large-result load test asserting peak RSS during fetch stays under the bound and no full-result-set Python materialization occurs, complementing the conversion-phase bound of #734.
rationale: #734 bounds only the dict→array CONVERSION and #374/#748 stream only for runs EXCEEDING the SoA ceiling; a normal in-ceiling canonical run still fully materializes millions of asyncpg Records up front, producing an unbudgeted transient memory spike (Records + arrays) the RSS ceiling never accounts for.

text: A schema-introspection test MUST assert the exact uniqueness targets idempotent writes rely on: store_klines' ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING targets a unique index/PK on exactly (symbol, interval, open_time), and the manifest seal upsert's ON CONFLICT targets exactly (symbol, interval, day_utc) reusing the existing coverage PK (#401); verified post-v58 by introspecting the live constraints and asserting the conflict columns match, so a future migration cannot silently break ingest idempotency (producing duplicate candle rows) or duplicate/divergent manifest seals.
rationale: #114/#401 assert upsert SEMANTICS but no test pins that the underlying unique constraints actually match the ON CONFLICT column lists; a silent PK/index drift would turn DO-NOTHING into duplicate-row insertion or break seal convergence with no failing test.

text: The manifest MUST track an explicit MATERIALIZED state per (symbol,interval,day) DISTINCT from SEALED, so the read path deterministically routes a sealed-but-Postgres-only day to Postgres (not a missing-Parquet open), the GC/eviction knows which partitions actually exist (refcount/skip-if-open per #403), and observability can report columnar-coverage LAG (count of sealed-but-unmaterialized days); verified by a test asserting a Phase-1-style Postgres-only sealed day reports materialized=false and reads from Postgres, then flips to materialized=true after lazy columnar build with NO re-seal and no content change.
rationale: Reconciling #251/#444 (and the seal-independent-of-materialization rule above) requires a first-class materialized state; without it the read path cannot distinguish 'sealed, not yet in columnar' from 'sealed and materialized but partition missing/corrupt', and columnar build-lag is unobservable for ops.

text: The Bybit fetch-window construction (start_ms/end_ms params) and page-cursor stitching MUST be boundary-exact: the candle whose open_time equals a requested boundary or a page-seam cursor MUST be fetched exactly once — never dropped, never duplicated across page seams or across adjacent contiguous gap-run spans (#95/#501) — correctly accounting for Bybit's inclusive/exclusive endpoint semantics; verified by a mocked-pagination test over a span long enough to force multiple seams asserting the stored/sealed set has exactly one candle per interval grid slot with zero boundary gaps and zero duplicates, and that content_sha256 is invariant to seam placement.
rationale: #501 asserts full-coverage pagination and #640 normalizes page order, but neither pins the inclusive/exclusive boundary math, so an off-by-one at a window edge or page seam could silently drop or duplicate the boundary candle — corrupting the canonical content hash and the sealed day.

text: The per-run data-provenance fingerprint over loaded klines (#395/#805) MUST be a pure function of the manifest content/coverage-generation, IDENTICAL regardless of which storage tier (Postgres / Parquet / Arrow-hot / mmap-Feather) actually served each day, so a tier-warmth change between runs is never misread as an input-data change; verified by a test computing the fingerprint for the same sealed range warmed to each tier in turn and asserting byte-equality, complementing the tier-provenance COUNTERS (#280) which record which tier served but MUST NOT perturb the fingerprint.
rationale: #280 records the serving tier and #395 defines the fingerprint, but nothing asserts tier-invariance; if the fingerprint accidentally hashed served bytes per-tier, merely warming a cache would spuriously change provenance and defeat the silent-data-change detection #395 exists to provide.

text: The v58 seal-backfill MUST mutate ONLY kline_cache_coverage/sealed-manifest and symbol_lifecycle rows and MUST NEVER INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE any kline_cache candle row (it derives facts from existing candles, never rewrites them); verified by a test that snapshots a content hash of every kline_cache row before and after running the full backfill and asserts the candle data is byte-identical, proving the backfill cannot silently alter the klines underlying already-persisted historic results.
rationale: Reqs 154/920 cover no-drop/rename/retype and a restore-point, but nothing asserts the backfill leaves the actual candle ROWS untouched. A buggy backfill that rewrote/clipped klines would corrupt data under existing results with no detection; the master kill-switch reverts behavior but not data.

text: The persistent store backing the optimization flags and new numeric operational configs (the DB config row / watched-file source of reqs 769/780) MUST itself be created and seeded with every flag/config at its documented SAFE default by an idempotent migration (CREATE TABLE / INSERT … ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING), so a freshly-migrated environment has every value defined before the first run reads it; verified by a test that on a clean migrated DB every flag/config the code reads resolves from the seeded store to its documented default with no missing-row/NULL/parse error.
rationale: Reqs 769/814/425 assume the flag/config store exists and is readable, but no requirement migrates or seeds it. A fresh deploy could read an absent config row and fall into undefined behavior.

text: No code path may issue SELECT * against backtest_runs / backtest_results / backtest_trades / kline_cache_coverage / sealed-manifest tables — every read MUST enumerate columns explicitly — so v58's additive columns cannot change result-set shape, break positional/ordinal decoding, or pull unmigrated columns during the rolling-deploy window; verified by a static guard test asserting no SELECT * on these tables plus a runtime test that a v58 row decodes identically through new and legacy column-enumerated projections.
rationale: Req 575 says legacy code projects known columns, but nothing forbids SELECT *. A single SELECT * anywhere would silently pick up v58 columns (or break old code) across the expand-only rollout.

text: EVERY non-backtest consumer of the shared kline_cache_service — specifically the AI Manager / position-management path (ai_manager_task) and the position_reconciler, in addition to the live scanner/auto-trade path — MUST be unaffected by the sealed-manifest fetch gate: its current-day/indicator kline reads stay byte-identical to pre-deploy behavior; verified by a test exercising the AI Manager and reconciler kline-read paths against the v58 schema with the gate enabled and asserting identical returned data and current-day refresh behavior.
rationale: Reqs 281/419/427 only name the live scanner/auto-trade path. CLAUDE.md lists ai_manager_task and position_reconciler as separate consumers of the same shared service; the gate could regress their reads undetected.

text: v58's ADD COLUMN/CREATE DDL on the live-shared kline_cache_coverage MUST NOT block IN-FLIGHT backtest/scanner READ queries against that table beyond the bounded lock_timeout budget (the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock conflicts with readers, not only the live writers covered by reqs 416/579), OR migrations MUST run in a documented no-in-flight-read window; verified by a test holding an in-flight coverage read open during v58 DDL and asserting the read is not blocked past the bounded budget.
rationale: Reqs 416/579 address contention with live WRITES; an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE DDL lock also blocks concurrent READS (backtest kline loads, scanner indicator loads), a distinct deadlock/latency vector.

text: An alertable structured signal MUST fire when the rolling-deploy version-skew window — during which pre-v58 (count-based) and v58 (sealed-manifest) instances run concurrently and legacy instances can re-download sealed-short days per req 807 — exceeds a documented maximum duration, so a stalled/stuck rollout that prolongs the re-download vector and manifest churn is detected and force-completed or rolled back; verified by a test that a simulated prolonged mixed-version fleet raises the skew-duration alert while a brief overlap stays silent.
rationale: Req 800 detects version divergence at a point in time and req 807 documents the overlap is 'bounded', but nothing alerts when the bound is actually exceeded in production — a stuck rollout silently extends the re-download bug window.

text: Successful migration application (DDL + each backfill chunk batch) MUST emit a duration + outcome METRIC to the runtime monitoring system (not only the boot log of req 163), carrying schema_version, days-sealed, and elapsed seconds, and deploy automation MUST be able to alert when migration duration exceeds an anomaly multiple of its committed baseline; verified by a test asserting the success-path metric is emitted with the correct fields on a migration run.
rationale: Req 163 logs, req 670 is a CI gate, reqs 429/669 alert on FAILURE/stall. There is no SUCCESS-path duration metric to monitoring, so a migration that succeeds-but-slowly in prod is invisible to dashboards.

text: The content_sha256 canonicalization-SCHEME version (req 641) MUST be persisted as its OWN queryable manifest column (not only folded into the hash bytes), so a future canonicalization change can SELECT and selectively re-verify/re-hash ONLY old-scheme sealed days in a bounded migration without re-reading every sealed day; verified by a test asserting the scheme-version column is present, set on every seal, and can target a partial re-hash of exactly the old-scheme rows.
rationale: Req 641 version-tags the hash so an old scheme reads as 'no comparable hash', but with the version buried inside the digest you cannot efficiently enumerate which rows need re-hashing — a future scheme bump would force a full re-read of all sealed history.

text: The migration framework MUST record per-migration provenance — applied-at UTC timestamp and the git SHA/build identifier of the code that applied each migration version — in a durable metadata row, so the schema's history is auditable and a deploy/incident can verify exactly which build applied v58 (and when); verified by a test asserting the metadata captures applied-at and code identity for v58 and that a re-run does not duplicate or overwrite the original provenance.
rationale: schema_version records only the integer reached, not who/when applied it. With a multi-week multi-PR phased rollout, provenance of each applied version is needed to triage 'which build wrote this schema state'.

text: A documented, tested operational lever MUST allow DISABLING the deferred seal-backfill entirely for a deploy target (skip optimization, run pure count-free-but-unsealed), leaving v58 columns present-but-unused (sealed all NULL/false) while the system serves backtests correctly treating every historic day as unsealed/refetchable; verified by a test booting the v58 schema with the backfill disabled and asserting backtests run correctly (count-free predicate, all days refetchable) with the completion marker never claiming 'complete'.
rationale: Req 574 covers correctness DURING an in-progress backfill, but there is no lever to NOT run the (potentially slow/risky) backfill at all on a given target while still booting on v58 — an operator may need to ship the schema but defer the heavy backfill indefinitely.

text: The deploy automation MUST measure the ACTUAL wall-clock duration that v58 held the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE / advisory lock on the live-shared kline_cache_coverage in PRODUCTION and gate/alert if it exceeded the documented budget, because the CI estimates (reqs 431/670) run against a representative-but-not-identical table; verified by a test asserting the held-lock duration is captured during apply and a simulated over-budget hold triggers the gating signal.
rationale: Reqs 431/670 only bound lock time in CI against a synthetic table. Real prod table size/bloat/concurrent load can blow the lock budget; the actual held-lock duration must be measured and gated post-apply.

text: On a code ROLLBACK to an older format generation, the older code encountering NEWER-format-stamped columnar (Parquet/Feather/Arrow) files (per req 771) MUST QUARANTINE them (move aside / mark, not delete) rather than destroy them, so a subsequent roll-forward to the newer code transparently REUSES the still-valid newer-format artifacts instead of paying a full cold rebuild; verified by a rollback→roll-forward cycle test asserting newer-format files survive the rollback window and are reused (zero rebuild) after roll-forward.
rationale: Reqs 453/771 cover disable/re-enable reuse and newer-reader-vs-older-file, but not the rollback case where OLD code must avoid DELETING newer-format files it cannot read — destroying them would force an expensive cold rebuild on every roll-forward.

text: The queryable runtime status route (req 428) MUST report the RESOLVED, actually-imported versions of numba/llvmlite/pyarrow/duckdb (or 'absent' per import-guard), alongside engine SHA / schema_version / flags, so a deploy can verify the running image shipped the intended accel-dependency set (or intentionally omitted it) and a fleet-consistency check (req 463) can flag a pod whose accel-dep versions diverge; verified by a contract test asserting the status payload includes the accel-dep version fields and that divergent payloads are flagged.
rationale: Req 776 boot-self-checks imported vs pinned versions internally, but those versions are not exposed on the status route, so the deploy pipeline/fleet check cannot externally verify every pod shipped the same accel wheels.

text: The migration runner MUST be safe and produce identical effect whether invoked from app-boot auto-migrate OR from a standalone CLI/management 'migrate' command, sharing the SAME advisory-lock election (reqs 265/665) so that an operator running the CLI migration concurrently with app-instance boots cannot double-apply, deadlock, or partially apply v58; verified by a test running the CLI entrypoint and an app-boot runner concurrently and asserting v58 DDL+backfill execute exactly once.
rationale: Reqs 265/305/580/665 cover concurrent APP-INSTANCE boots, but a separate ops-run CLI/management migration entrypoint racing app boot is a distinct real deployment pattern not asserted.

text: The master safe-mode kill-switch and per-path flag state MUST be DURABLE (DB-backed / persisted, not process-environment-only), so a pod restart, replacement, or autoscale event DURING an incident cannot silently re-enable a misbehaving optimization by losing an env-only override; verified by a test that engages safe-mode, simulates a process restart, and asserts the next run on the fresh process still resolves safe-mode ON without operator re-intervention.
rationale: Req 769 says flags MAY be DB-backed but does not assert the kill-switch survives a restart; if safe-mode were env-only, an orchestrator-driven pod replacement mid-incident would re-enable the broken path.

text: Re-running the SAME config across the dark→optimized flag flip MUST stay within the <1% continuous-field tolerance (and discrete-identical) versus the EARLIER dark-mode (legacy-path) result for that config — a direct old-result-vs-new-result cross-check, not only each-vs-oracle — so enabling the optimizations fleet-wide does not present a visibly different number for a config a user already saw; verified by a test that runs a config in dark mode, flips flags, re-runs, and asserts the two results reconcile within tolerance with identical discrete outcomes.
rationale: Reqs 929/74 assert dark==oracle and optimized within 1% of oracle separately; a direct dark-result-vs-optimized-result consistency check across the rollout flip is the user-visible guarantee neither states explicitly.

text: At boot the process MUST detect when an EXPECTED-persistent columnar cache volume (per the documented persistence contract of req 802) comes up EMPTY — indicating a misconfigured/non-persistent mount that will silently force a cold rebuild every deploy — and emit an alertable signal distinguishing this from an intentionally-ephemeral volume; verified by a test that an expected-persistent-but-empty cache dir raises the misconfiguration alert while an intentionally-ephemeral empty dir stays silent.
rationale: Reqs 587/802 cover writability/free-space and graceful degradation, but a persistent volume that silently mounts empty (lost PVC, wrong mount) degrades every deploy to cold-build with no alert — it just 'works slowly' forever.

text: A meta-test MUST assert a bijection between committed stored-oracle artifacts and registered golden fixtures: every oracle file is referenced by exactly ONE fixture and every fixture references exactly one oracle — failing CI on an ORPHAN/dead oracle (a committed artifact no fixture reads, which silently rots) and on two fixtures sharing one oracle path (a copy-paste collision); this is the inverse of #519 (fixture with a missing oracle) and distinct from #869 (oracle↔fixture config_hash match).
rationale: #519 only catches a fixture whose oracle is missing/empty; nothing detects a committed oracle that has gone dead (no fixture), nor two fixtures accidentally pointing at the same artifact — both let a parity gate silently stop testing what its filename implies.

text: A monotonic FIXTURE-COUNT ratchet meta-test MUST assert the number of registered golden/parity fixtures (and the covered set of close_reason enum values and §3 config-surface fields) never DECREASES versus a committed baseline across phases, so an optimization phase cannot delete, disable, or xfail a parity fixture to make its own gate pass — distinct from the code-coverage ratchet (#819), the enum-coverage teeth (#689), and the silent-skip floor count (#465), none of which pin the fixture COUNT against a baseline.
rationale: Coverage % and enum-union can stay green while a specific high-value fixture is quietly removed; a committed fixture-count/coverage-set baseline that can only grow prevents stealth deletion of parity tests as fixtures accumulate over a multi-phase rollout.

text: The COMPLETE oracle-diff (all 19 BacktestTrade fields + strategy_kind, the ordered equity_curve, and all ~45 metrics) MUST be asserted on the PERSISTED-THEN-RELOADED result read back from the DB, not only on the in-memory result, on at least the canonical and one drill fixture — catching a persistence-layer field drop, retype, JSONB key reorder, or value truncation that the numeric round-trip (#494), the envelope schema (#730), and the stored-reconciliation (#352) each miss because none re-runs the FULL value-level comparator on the reloaded object.
rationale: #494 only checks numeric round-trip + manifest-hash stability and #730 only checks envelope SHAPE; a regression that persists pnl correctly (reconciles) but drops/retypes mfe/close_reason/strategy_kind on write would pass both yet corrupt the rendered trade — only a full reloaded oracle-diff catches it.

text: A strategy-config-MODE interaction matrix test MUST exercise the cross-product of orthogonal business modes — execution_mode {immediate,batch} × direction {straight,reverse} × drilldown {on,off} × cohort {trend,mean_reversion} × funding_rate_model {none,fixed_8h} — on ONE shared signal/kline set, each combination frozen against its own oracle, so an INTERACTION bug between two modes that each pass their isolated fixture (e.g. reverse+MR composition #713, or batch-sizing under drill) is caught; distinct from the engine-FLAG matrix (#313) and the kernel×read-path matrix (#498), which vary infrastructure, not strategy config.
rationale: All existing matrix tests vary engine flags/kernels/read-paths; the strategy-config modes are tested singly (immediate-vs-batch #179/#81, reverse #235/#309, cohort #178/#329) but their pairwise/triple interactions are unpinned, where path-dependent sizing/anchoring bugs hide.

text: A MUTATION-TESTING gate (mutmut/cosmic-ray or equivalent) MUST run over backtest_engine.py, trading_rules.py, and backtest_metrics.py and FAIL the build if the surviving-mutant rate exceeds a documented ceiling (mutation kill-score floor), providing automated breadth beyond the hand-curated canary battery (#344/#469) which only injects an ENUMERATED defect set; the gate MUST be marked slow and run in a dedicated lane with a committed time budget.
rationale: #469's canary battery proves the suite catches a fixed list of defects, but cannot prove it catches the unenumerated long tail; a mutation-kill-score gate measures the parity suite's true sensitivity to ARBITRARY single-statement defects in the SSOT kernels the optimization reimplements.

text: The frozen Phase-0 baseline benchmark artifact MUST embed the deterministic fixture-GENERATOR content hash (and the input fixture's content hash), and the ≥100× headline (#38) and phase-over-phase regression gates MUST FAIL if the current generator/fixture hash differs from the baseline's — forcing a baseline RECAPTURE so the speedup ratio is never compared across two different input fixtures; distinct from the same-hardware stamp (#230) and the in-run re-measure (#312), neither of which ties baseline validity to the input-generator version.
rationale: If the committed fixture generator (#528) is tweaked between phases, the frozen Phase-0 wall-time becomes apples-to-oranges and the 100× claim is meaningless or falsely green/red; binding the baseline to the generator hash makes a silent fixture change invalidate the stale baseline.

text: The GET-time equity_curve LTTB downsample MUST have its OWN frozen golden oracle: a committed input curve mapped to a committed expected downsampled output, asserted byte-identical, so a change to the LTTB algorithm, bucket count, or endpoint handling is caught — beyond the trough-preservation property (#368), the determinism property (#726), and the O(n) bound (#515), none of which pins the actual downsampled VALUES.
rationale: #368/#726 only assert structural properties (trough kept, repeatable) and #515 only latency; an LTTB implementation swap that still preserves the trough and is deterministic could silently change every other rendered point — only a value-level oracle on the downsample output detects it.

text: A meta-test MUST assert NO automatic test-retry/rerun mechanism (pytest-rerunfailures, flaky markers, CI retry-on-fail) is active on the golden/parity/cache/perf gate lanes, and the async flakiness lane (#524) MUST assert byte-identical pass on EVERY iteration with zero retries — so a genuinely intermittent ordering/concurrency bug can never be masked by a green-on-retry, complementing #524 (which runs N times but does not forbid retry-to-green).
rationale: #524 runs the async tests N≥10 times but a CI-level rerun-on-failure plugin could still retry a flaking parity test to green; an explicit no-retry guard ensures the FIRST observed failure is fatal for correctness gates.

text: Running the oracle-regeneration script TWICE MUST yield BYTE-IDENTICAL artifacts: oracle files MUST stamp only engine git SHA + config hash (+ inputs) and MUST NOT embed any wall-clock timestamp, hostname, absolute path, PID, or dict/set-ordering-dependent serialization, so any oracle diff appearing in a PR is provably a real engine-output change; a meta-test asserts regen idempotency, complementing the harness no-wall-clock rule (#717) and the human-readable-text rule (#682).
rationale: #717 forbids wall-clock in the test harness and #682 mandates diffable text, but neither forbids the regen script from stamping a generation timestamp/host into each artifact — which would make every regeneration produce spurious diffs and defeat the SHA-pin review discipline (#347/#529).

text: Two CONCURRENT executions of the SAME run_id (operator double-submit / duplicate enqueue) MUST resolve to exactly one clean terminal result with no torn/duplicated backtest_trades, results, or equity_curve rows (results upsert ON CONFLICT(run_id) + delete-before-insert serialized or guarded), the _MAX_CONCURRENT slot accounting MUST stay correct, and one submission MUST be rejected or coalesced deterministically — distinct from the timeout-vs-completion race (#364), delete-during-run (#688), and sequential failed-then-retry (#531).
rationale: All existing race tests assume one execution per run_id; a duplicate concurrent submit of the same id could double-run, interleave delete-before-insert with the peer's insert, and leak/double-release a slot — an unhandled real-world operational race.

text: A run that ends in status='failed', timeout-killed, or cancelled MUST still emit (and persist where additive-nullable, per #549) the per-stage timing breakdown and cache/Bybit/Postgres/candle-eval counters and the flag+SHA fingerprint accumulated UP TO the failure point, so a non-completed run is diagnosable; a fault-injection test asserts the partial stats record exists with the terminal reason and the stage where work stopped — distinct from #691 which covers a DEGRADED-but-COMPLETED run only.
rationale: #691 covers an accel→fallback run that still completes; nothing requires observability output for a run that FAILS/TIMES OUT/CANCELS, which is exactly the run an operator most needs to triage — without partial stage timings a prod timeout is a black box.

text: The canonical large-fixture RESULT FINGERPRINT (#467/#500: hash over ordered trades + ordered equity_curve + all metrics) MUST be asserted byte-identical on the deps-FORCE-ABSENT (numba/pyarrow/duckdb simulated-ImportError) fallback lane as well as the main lane, so a divergence that only manifests at benchmark depth on the PURE-Python/Postgres path is caught — the fallback lane's perf gate (#478) measures TIME and the golden suite (#304) is the micro-fixtures, so the large-fixture parity fingerprint on the fallback path is currently ungated.
rationale: #304 reruns the (micro) golden suite deps-absent and #478 gates fallback TIME, but the at-scale parity fingerprint (#467/#500) is only asserted on the main/accelerated lane; an accumulation/seeding bug unique to the pure-Python SoA path at 90d×50sym scale would slip through.

text: A test MUST assert the engine + persist pipeline emits a BOUNDED, O(1)-per-run number of log records (independent of candle, scan, position, and trade count) — no per-candle/per-scan/per-trade logging — so a stray hot-loop log statement that both floods production logs and silently degrades throughput is caught; complements the per-candle zero-allocation rule (#234) and the progress-callback overhead bound (#209), neither of which bounds log VOLUME.
rationale: A debug log left inside the bar loop or per-position loop would not allocate engine objects (#234 stays green) nor touch the progress callback (#209 stays green) yet would emit O(candles×P) log lines — flooding log pipelines and adding I/O latency that no existing gate detects.

text: A symbol present in BOTH the static symbol_whitelist AND symbol_blacklist (a contradictory operator config) MUST resolve to the EXACT legacy precedence (frozen in an oracle — e.g. blacklist-wins-reject), so the SoA/dict refactor cannot flip which side wins; distinct from #693 (separate whitelist vs blacklist semantics) and #910 (static vs ADAPTIVE blacklist overlap), neither of which pins the static-whitelist∩static-blacklist contradiction.
rationale: #693 pins whitelist and blacklist behavior independently and #910 pins static-vs-adaptive composition, but the degenerate case where the same symbol is in both static lists at once has an undefined-by-the-spec precedence that a refactor could silently invert.

text: A per-field TOLERANCE-TAG completeness meta-test MUST assert that EVERY one of the 19 trade fields + strategy_kind and all ~45 metric keys is explicitly tagged in a committed registry as exactly one of {discrete-bit-identical, continuous-money-epsilon, near-zero-absolute-floor, ratio-two-sided-epsilon}, with no field untagged (which would silently default to a loose/strict comparison) and no field multiply-tagged — operationalizing #483/#565/#569 as an enforced field-by-field contract rather than per-fixture ad-hoc tolerance.
rationale: #483 (discrete vs continuous), #565 (near-zero floor), and #569 (ratio two-sided) define tolerance CLASSES but nothing guarantees every recorded field is assigned to a class; a newly added metric with no tag would be compared under whatever the comparator's default is, silently mis-gating it.

text: Each phase's parity gate run MUST include built-in LIVENESS controls proving the gate is actually executing and discriminating: a positive control (an identity comparison that MUST pass) AND a negative control (a deliberately-seeded one-field divergence that MUST fail) collected and reported in the SAME lane, so a misconfigured gate that collects zero tests, errors during collection, or no-ops is detected as RED — distinct from the silent-skip floor count (#465) and the epsilon-sensitivity calibration (#522).
rationale: #465 counts collected tests and #522 calibrates the epsilon boundary, but neither proves the gate machinery itself runs and discriminates each phase; an in-lane pass-must-pass + fail-must-fail control pair makes a vacuously-green (mis-wired) gate impossible to ship.

text: The within-bar equity-rule cascade and trailing evaluation MUST read the wallet/uPnL basis AFTER that bar's funding has already been applied (funding is first in the within-bar order of #42): the basket equity the EQUITY_DROP(/SMART)/close_on_profit/EQUITY_RISE rules and the trailing peak evaluate against MUST reflect the just-charged/credited funding, not the pre-funding wallet — frozen by a fixture where a funding charge on a 0/8/16h boundary bar pushes basket equity across an EQUITY_DROP threshold so the rule fires ONLY because funding was applied first, asserting the firing bar and close set match the oracle.
rationale: #42 pins the funding→liq/TP/SL→equity→trailing→time ORDER and #55/#339/#558 pin funding amount/timing/dedupe, but NOTHING pins that the equity-cascade valuation consumes the post-funding wallet within the same bar — a refactor that values equity from the pre-funding snapshot would fire equity rules on a different bar while leaving funding_paid correct.

text: The entry-price basis of the open-position uPnL used by the portfolio equity-rule cascade (uPnL = qty × (mark − equity_ref_entry) × side_sign, #327) MUST be pinned as either the SLIPPED actual fill (consistent with trade PnL, #323) or the UN-slipped entry mark (the sizing reference, #60/#61) — whichever the legacy engine uses — so the SoA/numba refactor cannot silently switch the equity uPnL entry basis; frozen by a fixture with non-zero slippage_bps where the slipped-vs-un-slipped equity basis would fire an equity rule on different bars, asserting the firing bar matches the oracle.
rationale: #50 calls equity_ref_entry 'the un-drilled 5m fill' (drill stability) and #323 pins slippage on the PnL fill with sizing un-slipped, but the slipped-vs-un-slipped choice for the EQUITY uPnL basis specifically is never pinned, leaving two plausible entry references (slipped fill vs un-slipped mark) that fire equity rules differently.

text: A position opened on a scan's entry-fill bar MUST participate in (or be excluded from) that SAME bar's portfolio-equity cascade, trailing-peak initialization, and smart-drawdown selective-close evaluation EXACTLY as the legacy engine — distinct from same-bar liq/TP/SL exit eligibility (#482) — frozen by a fixture where the newly-opened position's entry-bar uPnL would change which equity rule fires, whether SMART selects it, or the initial trailing peak, asserting the entry-bar equity/trailing/SMART behavior is byte-identical to the oracle.
rationale: #482 pins only whether a fresh position's OWN liq/TP/SL can exit it on the entry bar; whether that position is in the basket for the entry-bar EQUITY cascade, contributes to SMART's selective-close set, or seeds the trailing peak on its open bar is a separate, unpinned ordering question that changes path-dependent equity-rule firing.

text: The within-scan-boundary ORDER of (a) evaluating carried-book close rules over the scan's window versus (b) admitting the scan's new signals MUST be pinned bit-identically, AND whether a position opened by scan S is subject to close-rule evaluation within S's OWN window or only from scan S+1 — because this determines same-scan open-then-close behavior and whether the scan-time equity/sizing mark includes the just-opened positions — frozen by a fixture where the two orders produce a different trade set or equity_curve, asserting the legacy order.
rationale: #237 (scan order), #244 (window candle membership), and #71 (skip path) never pin the admit-vs-evaluate sequencing at the boundary nor whether same-scan-opened positions are evaluated in their own window — a fundamental cycle-structure invariant the SoA/global-timeline refactor could silently invert.

text: sizing_capital's carried-uPnL summation across the open book (running wallet + Σ carried uPnL − Σ locked_margin, #61) MUST iterate open positions in the SAME legacy insertion/open order as the equity-rule Σ-uPnL reduction (#327), so the path-dependent sizing mark is order-stable under SoA/dict re-keying — frozen by a multi-position fixture where reordering only the sizing-uPnL summation would shift a later signal's floored qty across a min_qty or available-margin boundary, asserting the admitted set and each qty match the oracle.
rationale: #327 pins the iteration order for the EQUITY-rule Σ uPnL but never asserts that sizing_capital's separate carried-uPnL accumulation uses the identical order; a refactor could order one correctly and the other by symbol-index, drifting qty via float summation reorder.

text: The basket equity E that the equity-rule cascade compares against its thresholds MUST be pinned as wallet + Σ open-uPnL WITHOUT subtracting Σ locked_margin (since locked margin is never deducted at open per #58), explicitly DISTINCT from sizing_capital which DOES subtract Σ locked_margin (#61) — so a refactor cannot conflate the two capital quantities — frozen by a fixture with open positions whose Σ locked_margin is large enough that subtracting-vs-not-subtracting margin would fire an equity rule on a different bar, asserting the legacy E composition.
rationale: The engine uses two different capital quantities — equity-rule basket equity and sizing_capital — that differ precisely by the Σ locked_margin term; #624 pins realized-vs-realized+uPnL for the equity rules but never pins the locked_margin term, and a SoA refactor reusing sizing_capital's (margin-subtracted) formula for equity rules would shift every equity-threshold decision.

text: The DISTINCT mark prices each consumer uses MUST be pinned and never conflated: sizing_capital marks open positions at 'last close ≤ scan_time' (#61), while the per-candle equity-rule cascade values open positions at THAT candle's close (and the adverse/favorable intrabar extreme per #54/#624) — frozen by a fixture where using the sizing mark for the equity cascade (or vice versa) would change which equity rule fires or a trade's qty, asserting each consumer uses its legacy mark.
rationale: The engine queries the SoA price arrays for at least two semantically different marks (scan-time last-close for sizing vs current-candle close/extreme for equity rules); the refactor that centralizes searchsorted lookups could accidentally feed one mark to both consumers — a parity hazard no current requirement isolates.

text: The never-resetting merge-walk price-location pointer MUST correctly serve MULTIPLE queries for the SAME (symbol, mark_time) without advancing past or corrupting the cursor for a subsequent equal-time query — e.g. two same-symbol open positions (cross-cohort co-hold per #904) marked at one instant, or a sizing mark immediately followed by an equity mark at the identical current_time — returning bit-identical prices each call and a separate uPnL term per position in insertion order; verified by a fixture issuing repeated same-(symbol,time) lookups and a two-same-symbol-position fixture asserting both marks equal the legacy per-position linear scan.
rationale: #381 handles a query that PRECEDES the prior query (non-monotonic→searchsorted fallback) and #733 pins per-run pointer state, but neither covers repeated EQUAL-time queries for the same symbol; the per-symbol advancing pointer must be idempotent on equal keys, a real correctness edge when two positions share a symbol or two consumers mark at the same instant.

text: The liquidation maintenance-margin basis MUST remain the legacy FIXED assumption baked into trading_rules and MUST NOT be silently sourced from a maintenance-margin / risk-limit field that a richer instrument_info snapshot (shipped under the no-op guarantee #72/#336/#394) may carry — the maintenance-margin analog of leverage-not-clamped (#631) — frozen by a fixture supplying instrument_info with an MM/risk-limit field and asserting the computed liquidation price (and the 'SL wins only if closer than liq' comparison of #41) equals the legacy fixed-MM computation bit-for-bit.
rationale: #322 pins the liquidation formula's MM assumption and #631 pins leverage not being clamped to an instrument max, but the parallel hazard — a fuller instrument_info MM field silently overriding the fixed MM and shifting the liquidation price — is unaddressed and would change both liquidation triggers and SL-vs-liq precedence.

text: The sizing denominator entry_base_price in qty = (sizing_capital·capital_pct/100·leverage)/entry_base_price (#60) MUST be pinned as the EXACT legacy representation — the raw candle open versus a tick_size-rounded value — so the float64/numba path computes a bit-identical floored qty; frozen by a fixture where a raw-open-vs-tick-rounded denominator flips the floored qty by exactly one qty_step, asserting qty, notional, entry/exit fees, and liquidation price all match the Decimal oracle.
rationale: #343 enumerates quantization sites generically and #60 gives the qty formula, but the specific question of whether the sizing DENOMINATOR is the raw open or a tick-rounded price is never called out — a one-tick difference there changes floored qty, which cascades into notional, both fee legs, and the liquidation price.

text: The adaptive-blacklist point-in-time membership at scan time T MUST pin whether a trade that CLOSES exactly on T's bar is visible to that same scan's win/total computation (closed strictly-before-T vs closed-at-or-before-T), consistent with the 48h sliding-window edge (#326), so the count-free incremental counter and the legacy full-history recompute agree at the boundary — frozen by a fixture with a trade closing on the exact scan-boundary bar asserting its inclusion/exclusion in that scan's blacklist decision matches the legacy recompute.
rationale: #80 says the blacklist sees 'trades closed before T' and #326 pins the 48h lookback boundary, but the inclusivity of a close landing exactly ON the scan bar (≤T vs <T) is unpinned; an off-by-one there flips a symbol's blacklist status for that scan and changes the admitted set point-in-time.

text: A component cross-check MUST hold on every fixture between the per-trade fields and the summary metrics: Σ over trades of (entry_fee+exit_fee) MUST equal the metrics total-fees key and Σ trade.funding_paid MUST equal the metrics total-funding key (each within Decimal tolerance), so an offsetting error (e.g. over-charged fee compensated by under-charged funding) that still nets to a correct three-way reconciliation (#66) is caught; verified on a multi-trade fixture mixing fees, positive and negative funding, and a liquidation (zero-fee) trade.
rationale: #66/#818 reconcile and stress only the NET three-way identity, which a pair of offsetting component errors passes; no requirement cross-checks the fee and funding SUMS against their dedicated summary-metric keys, leaving a class of compensating-error bugs invisible.

text: A meta-test MUST prove the WITHIN-TRADE decomposition identity has teeth: independently perturbing a single recorded trade's entry_fee, exit_fee, or funding_paid field MUST turn the per-trade check trade.pnl == gross_price_pnl − entry_fee − exit_fee − funding_paid (the #66 per-trade identity) RED for each perturbation, distinct from the run-level leg perturbation of #818 — so a refactor that mis-splits a trade's pnl into its fee/funding components while keeping trade.pnl correct is caught.
rationale: #818 only proves the three RUN-level legs (Σpnl, net_profit, final−start) have teeth; the per-trade decomposition (#66's 'trade.pnl = pnl − fees − funding') is asserted but never proven to fail on a single mis-attributed component, so a component-split drift inside an otherwise-correct trade.pnl would pass silently.

text: A golden fixture MUST pin the GAP-OPEN-THROUGH-BARRIER case on the entry-fill bar: when the next-bar OPEN (the no-look-ahead fill per #62) is itself already strictly beyond the configured TP, SL, or liquidation price (a price gap), the engine MUST reproduce legacy behavior bit-identically — pin whether the recorded entry fill is the gapped open and the same-bar exit price is the OPEN (worst realistic fill) or the nominal barrier level, and whether close_reason is the breached barrier — distinct from #482 (entry bar's [low,high] merely spanning the barrier), #821 (liq-vs-TP both touch within range), and #822 (slippage pushing fill out of range).
rationale: A gap that opens beyond the barrier is a distinct, common crypto-futures intrabar case from a bar whose range merely contains the barrier; the recorded fill/exit price and close_reason are ambiguous and must be frozen so the searchsorted/SoA refactor cannot silently pick open-vs-barrier.

text: A fixture MUST pin engine behavior for a DEGENERATE/CONTRADICTORY strategy-barrier config that upstream validation does not reject — a take_profit_pct/stop_loss_pct combination that computes a TP at or below entry (or SL above entry) for a long (and the mirror for a short), or TP price == SL price — asserting the legacy disposition (immediate same-bar close on the first-touched, an SL-clamp interaction per #321, or no-fire) is reproduced bit-identically and the run never raises or diverges between the Decimal and float64/numba paths; distinct from sentinel-disable values (#627) and degenerate instrument_info (#823).
rationale: Crossed/equal barriers are a realistic operator misconfiguration; the engine must be deterministic and the SoA/numba kernel must not flip which side fires when TP and SL collapse onto the same or inverted price.

text: The always-LTF reference oracle that anchors every drill non-optimistic bound (#74/#470/#573) MUST itself be self-validated: a meta-test MUST assert the always-LTF replay reduces to the EXACT coarse 5m result on bars with no intrabar ambiguity (single-level or no touch), so the upper-bound generator is proven correct before it is trusted — otherwise the non-optimistic ≤ guarantee is vacuous against a wrong reference.
rationale: The entire drill correctness story compares the fast path against an always-LTF oracle; if that oracle generator is itself buggy the sandwich/non-optimistic gates pass against garbage. Nothing currently validates the LTF reference.

text: The 1m drill sub-bar candle data for EVERY drilldown golden/property fixture MUST be materialized in the committed deterministic fixture (offline), and a test MUST assert all drill fixtures (mandatory-drill #175, entry-bar drill #687, partial-1m-coverage #311, different-sub-bar #369) resolve with ZERO exchange dependency under blocked network (#293) — distinct from #658 which only materializes the Buy&Hold/btc_vol/MR-mean auxiliary 5m series, not the 1m drill inputs.
rationale: Drill fixtures need committed 1m sub-bars to run hermetically; without them the drill battery silently depends on a live 1m fetch and cannot be offline-reproduced in CI.

text: Stored-oracle artifacts MUST serialize every float money/price/excursion field at FULL round-trip precision (Python repr / %.17g, never a truncated/fixed-decimal format), and a meta-test MUST assert that parsing each oracle float and re-serializing yields the byte-identical string AND that the parsed value equals the engine's in-memory float64 bit-for-bit — so the reference the comparator diffs against can never be a lossily-rounded number that masks a sub-epsilon drift or false-fails a benign one; distinct from #682 (human-readable text) and #494 (DB round-trip).
rationale: If the oracle file itself truncates float precision, the Decimal-exact/epsilon comparator is comparing against a corrupted reference, silently widening or narrowing every tolerance.

text: The benchmark/cache-efficiency regression gates MUST HARD-FAIL (not skip, not pass-by-default) when a committed phase baseline artifact (Phase-0 frozen numbers, per-phase baselines, the cache-counter baselines of #263/#446/#760/#798) is MISSING, empty, or unparseable, and a pre-gate guard test MUST assert every baseline file referenced by a gate exists and is non-empty before any comparison runs — the regression-gate analog of the oracle-presence guard (#519).
rationale: A deleted or unwritten baseline currently turns a comparison gate into a silent no-op (nothing to compare against → green); the gate must fail loud, mirroring how a missing oracle must turn a golden test red.

text: A WIRING meta-test MUST prove the oracle comparator actually reads the central tolerance registry (the named epsilon/Decimal-reconciliation constants of #525 and the per-field tolerance TAGS of #998) rather than duplicated literals: monkeypatching a registry epsilon MUST change the comparator's pass/fail boundary, and re-tagging a field discrete→continuous (or vice-versa) MUST change whether a sub-epsilon perturbation on that field is tolerated — so the constants/tags cannot drift out of sync with the code that enforces them.
rationale: #525 pins the constants and #998 pins the tags exist/are complete, but neither proves the comparator consumes them; a hardcoded duplicate literal would let a phase loosen a threshold while the registry still reads correct.

text: A registry-completeness meta-test MUST enumerate every process-global cache tier (the _load_klines LRU, 1m drill cache, Arrow/SoA hot cache, raw→SoA conversion memo, derived-coarse resample memo, signal/ScanContext memo #961, timeline/anchor memo #854) and assert the autouse reset fixture (#518) clears EACH to empty — failing CI when a newly-added cache tier is not wired into the reset — the cache-tier analog of the flag/config registry-completeness check (#814).
rationale: #518 mandates a reset fixture and randomized-order run, but a new cache tier silently omitted from the reset can make a '0 Bybit calls'/cold-measurement test pass or fail by execution order; only a registry-driven completeness assertion catches the omission deterministically.

text: A READ-SIDE count-identity test MUST assert, for at least the canonical and one drill fixture, the three-way trade-count identity at the PERSISTED layer: metrics.total_trades (in the stored backtest_results JSONB) == SELECT count(*) FROM backtest_trades WHERE run_id == the total rows returned across ALL pages of GET /backtest/{id}/trades — so a persistence drop/duplicate or a pagination boundary miss cannot leave the rendered trade count inconsistent with the headline metric; distinct from the in-memory count identity (#621) and the no-skip/dup ordering check (#473).
rationale: The frontend renders total_trades and a paginated trade list from two different reads; only an explicit persisted+paged count identity guarantees they agree, catching a torn persist or a cursor off-by-one that #621/#473 do not jointly assert.

text: A concurrency test MUST assert that two DIFFERENT run_ids executing and persisting simultaneously are fully isolated at the persistence layer: each run's delete-before-insert of backtest_trades/equity_curve and results upsert is strictly run_id-scoped (a run never deletes, overwrites, or interleaves another run's rows), so the two persisted result sets each equal their standalone oracle — distinct from same-run_id double-submit (#993), explicit DELETE scoping (#407), and shared-cache engine-state isolation (#307).
rationale: Under _MAX_CONCURRENT the persist paths of distinct runs run concurrently; an unscoped delete or a shared staging buffer would cross-contaminate rows, a failure mode none of the existing isolation tests cover.

text: The Decimal-legacy golden oracles for the non-5m coarse simulation_interval paths (15m/1h/4h, #358) MUST be byte-identical across the Windows and Linux CI lanes (Decimal arithmetic is platform-independent and the optimized engine derives coarse-from-fine per #116/#849), asserted by a stored-oracle content-hash equality test on both OS runners — extending the OS-portability guarantee #723 (which pins only the 5m path) so a derive-coarse or resampler difference cannot pass on one OS and fail on the other.
rationale: Windows dev + Linux prod means coarse-interval oracles must also be OS-portable; #723 only covers 5m, leaving the derive-coarse coarse-interval Decimal oracle unguarded across OS.

text: On a run that ends FAILED, timeout-killed, or cancelled, the partial per-stage timing breakdown emitted/persisted up to the failure point (#994) MUST still reconcile under the chosen exclusive/overlap timing model (#864): the sum of recorded stage durations MUST equal elapsed-until-failure within epsilon with the terminal/aborted stage explicitly marked, so no latency hides in an unattributed gap on the abort path — a teeth check that partial observability is internally consistent, not merely present (#994) and complementing the success-path exhaustive-sum invariant (#481).
rationale: #994 asserts partial stats exist and #481 asserts the sum reconciles on success, but a failed run's partial stage timings could be garbage/unattributed; triage of a timeout/crash needs the partial breakdown to provably account for all elapsed time up to the kill.

text: The committed benchmark artifact (#35/#685) MUST stamp the engine git SHA, the fully-resolved optimization-flag set, AND the deterministic fixture-generator content hash on every recorded measurement, and a test MUST assert two artifacts produced under different flag sets / generator versions are distinguishable — so a stored benchmark number is reproducible and attributable to the exact code+flags+input that produced it, complementing the same-hardware stamp (#230) and the generator-hash regression gate (#989) which do not record the flag set or engine SHA in the artifact itself.
rationale: Without the flag set + engine SHA stamped IN the benchmark artifact, a perf number cannot be reproduced or tied to the code path that produced it, and a phase-over-phase comparison could silently mix flag configurations.

text: A meta-test MUST assert the parity/cache/perf suites use the SINGLE shared oracle-comparator helper everywhere (no per-fixture bespoke assert logic): every registered fixture's comparison resolves through the one comparator entrypoint, and a guard fails CI if a fixture asserts trade/metric equality via a local/ad-hoc comparison that bypasses the registry-driven tolerance tags (#998) and NaN/Inf/sentinel handling (#862) — so comparator hardening can never be silently undermined by a fixture rolling its own diff.
rationale: #718 ensures each fixture invokes the full diff but not that they all route through ONE comparator; a fixture with bespoke equality logic could miss the centralized NaN/tag/length handling, making its gate quietly weaker than the battery's standard.

text: Volume AND turnover (quote-volume) MUST be persisted in Postgres, the Parquet/Feather columnar tier, and the Arrow hot frame and MUST be the source for derived-coarse volume/turnover aggregation (#404), while the per-candle ENGINE kernel/SoA operates ONLY on int64-epoch+OHLC (volume/turnover never enter any trade/equity/filter/sizing decision); verified by (a) a coarse-derived volume==native-coarse volume test and (b) a guard test that zeroing or omitting volume/turnover from the engine input changes zero trades/metrics, so dropping them from the hot kernel is provably parity-safe yet they survive for resampling.
rationale: #437 lists OHLCV-as-float64 in Arrow and #404 aggregates coarse volume, but no requirement pins that volume/turnover are RETAINED through every storage tier for resampling while being EXCLUDED from the engine hot path — leaving the volume column's lifecycle and its no-parity-impact unverified.

text: Backtest/sweep cold gap-fill Bybit fetches MUST be rate-PRIORITIZED strictly below the live scanner/auto-trade path on the shared Bybit rate-limit semaphore/circuit-breaker (#229/#756): the live path MUST hold a reserved rate share so a large cold backtest cold-fill can never throttle, queue behind, or trigger backoff on live order-related kline fetches; verified by a test that saturates the cold-fetch path and asserts a concurrent live-path fetch's latency and rate-share stay within a documented bound.
rationale: #229/#376/#756/#934 share/limit/circuit-break and protect live CACHE entries, but none PRIORITIZE the live trading path over backtest on the finite Bybit rate budget — a wide cold backtest could starve live order-decision data fetches.

text: When the loader fetch-and-discovers a symbol with UNKNOWN/NULL lifecycle (#259), it MUST PERSIST the discovered listing_time (earliest returned candle) and, on a confirmed terminal/no-more-data edge, delist_time back into symbol_lifecycle, so subsequent runs negative-cache the pre-listing/post-delist day ranges instead of re-issuing an empty pre-history fetch every run; verified by a test that a first cold run over a pre-listing-spanning window discovers+writes listing_time and a second run issues 0 Bybit calls for the now-known pre-listing days.
rationale: #259 mandates fetch-and-discover for unknown lifecycle and #931 covers the background refresh task, but nothing makes the BACKTEST cold-fetch WRITE the discovered boundary back — so a symbol with unknown lifecycle perpetually re-fetches its empty pre-listing range, violating the cold-bound and warm-rerun goals.

text: Concurrent COLD loads of OVERLAPPING (not merely identical) (symbol,interval,range) spans MUST coordinate so the shared sub-range is fetched from Bybit exactly once (range-aware single-flight/in-flight-merge), not once per overlapping requester; verified by a test launching ≥3 concurrent cold loads of partially-overlapping ranges of the same symbol against a mocked client and asserting each interval-grid slot in the overlap is fetched exactly once.
rationale: #246 coalesces only byte-IDENTICAL ranges; two sweep combos/runs with different Phase-1 bounded spans (#19/#354) overlap but are not identical, so the shared sub-range is double-fetched cold, inflating the cold Bybit-call budget and risking rate-limit trips.

text: A manifest seal-upsert / coverage-write FAILURE that occurs AFTER the klines for a day are already durably stored MUST be non-fatal to the run (and to a warmup) — the run completes normally and the day simply stays unsealed for the next run's lazy-seal (#592) — and MUST never leave a torn/partial manifest row; verified by a fault-injection test that makes the seal upsert raise after store, asserting the run returns oracle-identical results, the manifest has no partial row, and a subsequent run lazy-seals the day with zero Bybit refetch.
rationale: #247 covers a failed FETCH (leave unsealed) and #643 covers a data-tier store failure, but neither pins that a failure of the manifest SEAL WRITE itself (data already persisted) is best-effort/non-fatal — a strict path could abort an otherwise-correct run on an incidental seal-write error.

text: The manifest MUST encode an UNAMBIGUOUS day-CLASS taxonomy — {sealed-full, sealed-short-listing (#94), sealed-with-interior-structural-gap (#260/#753), sealed-fully-empty-negative (pre-listing/post-delist/in-life-200-empty #758)} — such that no two classes collapse to the same column state and the read path routes each correctly (serve-present-candles vs serve-empty vs Postgres-fallback); verified by a schema/behavior test that seeds one day of each class and asserts each is distinctly classified and produces the correct zero-fetch read behavior and engine disposition.
rationale: #85/#94/#260/#753/#754/#758 add the individual columns/flags but no requirement pins that the four distinct sealed-day CLASSES are mutually distinguishable and correctly routed — risking a real interior hole being mis-served as fully-empty (or vice-versa), a silent data-correctness bug.

text: An in-place restatement of an EXISTING candle in a sealed day (same (symbol,interval,open_time), different OHLCV) arriving via the normal store path MUST be a no-op under ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING and MUST leave the day's stored rows and content_sha256 byte-unchanged — the restatement is applied ONLY via the explicit sha-mismatch reverify (#296) or manual force-reverify (#397); verified by a test that re-stores an existing sealed candle with altered OHLC and asserts the stored row and sha are unchanged (immutability wins on the normal path), complementing #396's refusal of a NOVEL candle.
rationale: #396 pins refusal of a novel candle into a sealed day, but the same-open_time-DIFFERENT-OHLC restatement case is only implicitly handled by ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING and is never asserted as an immutability guarantee, leaving the silent-ignore behavior untested.

text: A mid-session change to symbol_lifecycle (operator override #779, background refresh #931/#583, or relist detection #411) MUST invalidate the in-process negative-cache / known-empty day memo for that symbol so the loader re-evaluates which days are pre-listing/post-delist/in-life on the next access within the same long-lived process; verified by a test that negative-caches a symbol's pre-listing range, mutates its listing_time earlier mid-session, and asserts the newly-in-life days are no longer served as known-empty (re-fetched/served) without a restart.
rationale: #436 invalidates caches on sha-reverify and #402 re-evaluates the forming-day exclusion, but no requirement invalidates the in-process NEGATIVE-cache memo when lifecycle bounds change — a long-lived process would keep serving stale 'pre-listing empty' for a symbol whose listing was just corrected.

text: A committed per-TIER read-latency micro-benchmark MUST measure and regression-gate each storage tier independently for a fixed sealed-range read — Arrow/SoA hot (zero-copy, fastest) ≤ mmap'd Feather ≤ Parquet-on-disk ≤ Postgres SOR — asserting each tier stays within its own documented budget and the ordering holds, so a tier-specific regression (e.g. lost Parquet predicate/partition pushdown, a hidden copy in the Arrow handoff, an unindexed Postgres scan) is caught even when end-to-end wall-time and bytes-read (#446) stay green.
rationale: #3/#386/#509 gate END-TO-END latency and #446 gates bytes-read, but no gate isolates per-TIER read LATENCY, so a regression confined to one serving tier can hide behind a warm hot-cache hit on the canonical fixture.

text: Because sealed days are immutable, sealed-day kline READS MUST be safely offloadable to a Postgres read-replica (documented + tested: replica staleness is harmless for immutable sealed data), while FORMING/live-edge-day reads MUST route to the primary OR tolerate documented bounded replica lag with a consistency guard (no stale live edge served as current); verified by a test that serves sealed-day reads from a lagging replica byte-identically and asserts forming-day reads either hit primary or are flagged/clamped, never returning a stale forming candle as final.
rationale: #772 only tolerates replica lag for manifest seal-CLASSIFICATION; nothing specifies that immutable sealed-day kline READS can be replica-offloaded to relieve the primary the live path uses, nor pins the forming-day read-routing rule — a real read-scaling/operational need with a parity hazard.

text: The SET of symbols loaded for a run MUST be derived EXACTLY from the distinct symbols present in the run's scan_source signals plus the required auxiliary symbols (BTC/USDT for Buy&Hold #238 and btc_vol #355, MR-mean reference per #355) — no signaled symbol omitted (which would silently turn its signals into signals_no_kline) and no extraneous symbol loaded (wasted fetch/RAM); verified by a test asserting the loaded-symbol set equals distinct(signal_symbols) ∪ {aux} and that adding/removing a signal symbol changes the load set accordingly with byte-identical engine output.
rationale: #19/#377 bound per-symbol spans and batch the signal load, but no requirement pins that the symbol-load UNIVERSE is exactly the signaled-symbols(+aux) set — a scoping bug could skip a signaled symbol (dropping trades) or over-fetch, with no standing guard.

text: The per-(symbol,interval,day) MATERIALIZED flag (#964) MUST be committed ONLY after the Parquet/Feather partition is durably fsync'd+renamed (#652), and a crash leaving file-present-but-flag-false (or flag-true-but-file-missing) MUST self-heal deterministically — adopt-the-existing-file after content_sha256 verification, or atomically re-materialize — never duplicating rows, never serving a partial file, and never permanently diverging the flag from on-disk reality; verified by a crash-injection test in each direction asserting the next read is SOR-identical and the flag/file converge.
rationale: #251/#652/#964/#286 cover seal-after-rows, fsync ordering, the materialized-state concept, and temp-file reaping, but none pin the materialized-FLAG-vs-FILE crash reconciliation (file-present/flag-false and the inverse), a real crash-consistency hole in the columnar tier.

text: The Bybit fetch path MUST pin the instrument CATEGORY (linear USDT-perpetual for crypto-futures) and the interval-parameter MAPPING (engine '5m/15m/1h/4h/1m' → Bybit v5 '5/15/60/240/1') as a single documented mapping, so a futures-symbol fetch can never silently retrieve spot or wrong-category/wrong-interval klines; verified by a test asserting the fetch issues category=linear and the correct interval param for each interval, and that a category/interval-mapping mismatch is rejected rather than storing/sealing foreign-market data.
rationale: #640/#863 pin v5 wire-payload NORMALIZATION (ascending, string-numerics, ms epochs) but nothing pins the CATEGORY and interval-string→Bybit-param mapping — a category/interval mapping error would fetch and immutably seal wrong-market data that parity fixtures (which mock the client) cannot catch.

text: An in-life day that returns a genuine HTTP-200 EMPTY result (zero candles, e.g. a brief exchange data gap that the exchange may later backfill) MUST be recorded as a TTL-bearing transient gap and re-verified exactly once at/after the completion frontier (#248) BEFORE being permanently sealed known-empty (#758) — it MUST NOT be immediately seal-forever on first 200-empty (#797); verified by a test that a first-run 200-empty in-life day is left TTL-refetchable, the exchange then backfills candles, and a later run picks up the real candles instead of having frozen the day empty.
rationale: #797 seals an in-life 200-empty day known-empty while #248 re-verifies TTL gaps once before permanent seal — these conflict for the exchange-backfill case; no requirement reconciles them, risking a real day being permanently frozen empty by a transient exchange gap.

text: A pre-deploy GATING step MUST rehearse the FULL v58 migration (transactional DDL + the actual seal-backfill, with writes) against a recent RESTORED CLONE of production data — not a synthetic CI schema (#785) and not the no-write dry-run (#289) — asserting it completes within the committed budget and that post-migration sealed-day counts match the dry-run prediction within tolerance (#804); verified by a CI/deploy job that runs against a prod-snapshot fixture and fails if the real-data backfill errors, exceeds budget, or diverges from the predicted seal count.
rationale: Synthetic CI data (#785) and the no-write dry-run (#289) cannot surface data-shape-specific backfill failures (skewed coverage, odd lifecycle rows, off-grid candles). A clone rehearsal is the only gate that exercises the real distribution before prod apply.

text: The chunked seal-backfill MUST keep each chunk's transaction short enough to survive a server-global idle_in_transaction_session_timeout / statement_timeout (per-chunk COMMIT, no long-open transaction), OR explicitly set a safe local timeout for its session, so an org-wide idle/statement timeout cannot kill a backfill chunk mid-flight and strand the marker; verified by a test running the backfill under a deliberately short idle_in_transaction_session_timeout and asserting it completes without a killed-transaction error.
rationale: Existing reqs pin lock_timeout (#579) and read statement_timeout (#649) but never idle_in_transaction_session_timeout, which production DBs commonly set globally and which silently aborts a long multi-statement transaction — a realistic prod-only backfill failure.

text: If the ELECTED deferred-backfill runner instance terminates mid-backfill (autoscale, rolling-deploy, crash/OOM), another live instance MUST detect the released election (advisory lock dropped on connection death per #665) and RESUME the backfill from its persisted checkpoint (#775) within a bounded detection window — no manual restart, no waiting for a full fleet redeploy; verified by a test that kills the elected runner mid-backfill and asserts a second instance takes over and drives the marker to 'complete'.
rationale: #580 elects exactly one runner and #775 makes it resumable, but nothing guarantees re-election/failover when the chosen runner dies — leaving the backfill orphaned in_progress until a coincidental restart, blocking the optimization-enable gate (#811) indefinitely.

text: A Postgres primary→replica PROMOTION (failover) occurring during the v58 rollout MUST leave a consistent, resumable state: schema_version, the sealed manifest, and the backfill-completion marker/checkpoint (all in-DB) survive the promotion, and the new primary's runner RESUMES the deferred backfill from the persisted checkpoint with no double-seal and no re-scan of completed chunks; verified by a test simulating a failover mid-backfill and asserting resume-to-complete with byte-identical final manifest.
rationale: Backfill-runner failover (#580/#775) covers process death within one DB, but DB failover is a distinct event: the new primary must pick up an in-flight backfill from durable state, and nothing currently asserts cross-failover resume correctness.

text: An OLD frontend (and old API consumers) encountering a NEW backtest_runs.status enum value it has no rendering case for — 'cancelled' (#451), 'queued' (#536), 'interrupted by restart' (#450) — MUST degrade to a safe generic terminal/transitional display (never crash, blank the run list, or drop the row); verified by a contract test feeding each new status through the legacy renderer/serializer and asserting graceful fallback, complementing the metric-key/envelope additive guards (#730/#787) which cover fields but not new ENUM values.
rationale: The rollout introduces new status values; #918 makes them insertable and #787 covers additive FIELD compat, but an old client with a fixed switch on status will mishandle an unrecognized enum value during the deploy overlap window.

text: store_klines auto-creating a monthly partition for a >6mo-old or future range (#137/#284/#399) MUST coexist with an EXISTING partition retention/DETACH policy: re-creating a partition that a retention job previously DETACHED must not error, must not double-attach, and must not enter a create/detach thrash loop, and the system MUST detect when a write targets a detached-then-needed historic month; verified by a test that detaches a monthly partition, has store_klines persist a candle into that month, and asserts a clean single re-create/attach with the row landing in its dedicated partition (not kline_cache_default).
