rationale: #456 aligns auto-created partition BOUNDS to the migration-38 grid but nothing addresses interaction with an active DETACH/retention tool that may have already removed a historic month — a real prod operational collision causing errors or thrash.

text: The full-coverage seal-backfill UPDATE MUST bound its peak WAL-GENERATION RATE and total WAL footprint (via chunked commits with a documented per-window cap), distinct from the replica-LAG throttle (#921), so it cannot blow the PITR/backup-storage budget or saturate replication bandwidth by emitting WAL proportional to the entire coverage table in a burst; verified by a test asserting per-chunk WAL/byte footprint stays under the configured cap and total WAL is spread across chunks rather than one large transaction.
rationale: #921 throttles on observed replica lag and #133 bounds statement count, but neither bounds the absolute WAL-generation rate/footprint, which independently threatens backup storage, PITR retention windows, and any downstream WAL consumer (logical replication #813).

text: Backtest_results/sweep_results rows COPIED from production into a dev/test/staging environment (the copy-prod-scans workflow) carry an optimization fingerprint + data-provenance (#278/#318/#395) referencing an engine SHA / data generation absent in the target; the target MUST render such rows as read-only/foreign-provenance and the reproduce-from-fingerprint loop (#371) MUST report 'not reproducible in this environment' rather than re-running and surfacing a spurious mismatch; verified by a test loading a foreign-provenance result row into a fresh environment and asserting the non-reproducible classification.
rationale: copy-prod-scans (#777) handles rebuilding the kline MANIFEST in the target but not copied RESULT rows whose fingerprints point at code/data generations the target lacks — the #371 reproduce loop would otherwise false-fail or silently re-baseline against a different engine.

text: New application code that can WRITE the new backtest_runs.status values ('cancelled'/'queued'/'interrupted by restart') MUST be gated on confirmation that the v58 status CHECK-constraint/enum relaxation (#918) is applied (schema_version=58), so a deploy that enables the new code before the constraint is relaxed cannot fail an INSERT of a new status in production; verified by a test asserting a new-status write is refused/deferred to the legacy terminal value when the DB still reports a pre-v58 constraint.
rationale: #918 adds the constraint and #290/#811 gate column-READS on v58, but the WRITE side of the new status values has its own deploy-ordering hazard: writing 'cancelled' against an un-relaxed CHECK constraint raises and corrupts the terminal-state handling (#450/#451/#536).

text: Each built deploy image MUST declare its intended PROFILE (accel: numba+pyarrow+duckdb present; or pure-Python: accel absent), and a containerized gate MUST assert the image's ACTUALLY-importable accel set matches the declared profile — failing if an accel image is missing a wheel/ABI-broken OR a pure image unexpectedly bundles the accel stack; verified by a CI job comparing declared-vs-actual importable accel deps on the exact image, distinct from the status-route version REPORT (#979) and fleet flag consistency (#463).
rationale: #979 reports versions at runtime and #810 smokes the prod image, but nothing pins a per-image PROFILE contract caught at BUILD time — so a misbuilt image (accel profile, missing wheel) ships and only silently degrades to pure-Python in prod instead of failing the build.

text: The equity_curve MANIFEST hash (over the full curve JSONB, #78) and the sweep config_hash (#282/#348) MUST be PROVEN invariant to the presence and values of the new additive result-row columns (optimization fingerprint, stage timings, data/signal provenance, recorded kernel, marker-state per #430/#549/#395/#496/#674); verified by a test that populates every new sibling column with differing values and asserts both the manifest hash and config_hash are byte-identical to a row without them.
rationale: #282 covers config_hash vs INFRA FLAGS and #78 defines the manifest-hash source, but no test explicitly proves the newly-ADDED sibling COLUMNS (which a careless hash implementation might fold in) leave neither hash perturbed — a regression that would break ON CONFLICT(sweep_id, config_hash) matching and curve-cache stability.

text: After the backfill marker flips to 'complete', a VERIFICATION pass MUST sample-or-fully re-validate that each sealed day actually satisfies the seal predicate (day.end < frontier, within [listing,delist], rows present matching candle_count−gap_count) and emit an alertable signal on any day that fails re-validation, catching a buggy backfill that sealed days it should not have; verified by a test seeding a wrongly-sealable day and asserting the post-backfill verification flags it, distinct from the count-reconciliation-vs-prediction check (#804).
rationale: #804 reconciles the COUNT of sealed days against the prediction but never re-checks that each sealed day is actually CORRECTLY sealable — a backfill bug that seals the wrong days (or the forming day) would pass the count check yet silently freeze bad data immutably.

text: The master safe-mode kill-switch (#277) MUST, in addition to routing engine/cache/read/sweep to the legacy path, HALT new manifest SEAL WRITES (stop sealing days during live/backtest fetch) so a discovered seal-correctness bug stops propagating immediately while reads continue via the count-free predicate (unsealed ⇒ refetchable); verified by a test that engages safe-mode and asserts zero new seal upserts occur on a subsequent cold fetch while reads of already-sealed days still succeed.
rationale: #277 routes execution paths to legacy but does not explicitly stop the manifest WRITE/seal side; if the sealing logic itself is the bug, an operator needs to stop minting new (potentially wrong) immutable seals without halting service — a distinct, testable kill-switch behavior.

text: The seal-backfill and the background symbol_lifecycle population MUST be ORDER-INDEPENDENT and CONVERGENT: running backfill-then-lifecycle and lifecycle-then-backfill MUST yield a byte-identical final manifest (same sealed set, same listing_snapped/delisted/gap_count classifications), so a deploy where lifecycle population races the backfill (#583) reaches a deterministic end state; verified by a test that applies the two orders against identical seed data and diffs the resulting manifest rows.
rationale: #400/#454/#931 cover lifecycle being empty/late/transitioning individually, but none asserts that the two asynchronous populators CONVERGE regardless of interleaving — a real race in a multi-pod deploy that could otherwise leave different pods' first-run behavior dependent on who finished first.

text: Aggregate/analytics queries that may now read the new additive result columns (analytics_summary, portfolio_overview, signal_analytics, backtest_compare over mixed populations) MUST COALESCE/ignore NULL new fields and MUST NOT error or skew when aggregating over a MIX of pre-v58 rows (NULL fingerprint/stage-timing/provenance) and post-v58 rows; verified by a test running each analytics aggregate over a deliberately mixed old/new row population and asserting correct, non-erroring results.
rationale: #160/#575 cover loading/rendering a SINGLE old row, but the MCP analytics surface (analytics_summary, portfolio_overview, signal_analytics) AGGREGATES across many rows — a SUM/AVG/filter touching a new NULL column over mixed-vintage rows is an untested failure mode during the long backfill/rollout window.

text: An auto-created monthly Postgres partition for a >6mo-old or future range (#137/#456) MUST follow the EXACT partition NAMING convention that migration-38 and any external retention/pg_partman tooling recognize, so an auto-created partition is not orphaned from (nor collides with) the retention tool's name-pattern matching; verified by a test asserting the auto-created partition's NAME matches the established convention, complementing the BOUNDS-alignment guarantee (#456).
rationale: #456 pins partition BOUNDS but external retention/maintenance tooling typically keys on partition NAME patterns; an auto-created partition with a divergent name silently escapes retention or duplicates a managed partition — an operational hazard distinct from bound overlap.

text: When the seal-backfill SKIPS poison chunks (#932), the completion marker MUST distinguish 'complete-clean' from 'complete-with-skips' (carrying the skipped-(symbol,interval,day) remediation set as a queryable list), and the optimization-enable rollout gate (#811) MUST treat 'complete-with-skips' as NOT fully clean (warn/hold or require explicit operator ack) rather than enabling flags as if the cache were fully sealed; verified by a test that a backfill with a skipped chunk yields the distinct marker state and the rollout gate does not silently flip flags on.
rationale: #932 makes the backfill skip-and-continue on bad chunks, but the marker (#663) only has not_started/in_progress/complete — a partial-failure backfill would report 'complete' and let #811 enable optimizations over an incompletely-sealed cache, masking the skipped days.

text: Re-ENABLING an optimization at runtime via flag flip after a prior kill-switch revert (#673) — especially numba/columnar that may have been disabled due to a crash/health failure — MUST re-run the accel-path boot/health self-check (#155/#271/#422/#615/#861) BEFORE serving on the re-enabled path, and fall back again (logging) if still unhealthy, rather than blindly trusting the flag; verified by a test that disables numba via injected failure, re-enables the flag, and asserts the self-check re-runs and re-degrades if the fault persists.
rationale: #673 makes flags re-readable per run and #271/#422 cover BOOT-time health, but nothing re-validates accel health when a flag is flipped back ON mid-process-life — re-enabling a still-broken JIT/columnar path at runtime must re-gate on health, not assume boot-time state still holds.

text: The advisory-lock KEY used by the v58 migration (#413/#665), the key used for deferred-backfill RUNNER ELECTION (#580), and any advisory-lock key used by the live scanner/auto-trade path MUST be in DISJOINT key namespaces, so the migration lock, the backfill-election lock, and app locks cannot collide and self-deadlock during a concurrent boot/deploy; verified by a test asserting the three advisory-lock keys are distinct and that holding any one does not block acquisition of the others.
rationale: #413/#665 pin the migration lock and #580 introduces a separate election lock, but nothing asserts these (plus any pre-existing app advisory lock) occupy DISJOINT key spaces — a key collision between the migration loop and the backfill election would deadlock boot in a multi-pod deploy.

text: The deferred-backfill resumable CHECKPOINT (#775) MUST advance ONLY after a chunk's seal-write transaction COMMITS, so a runner hard-killed (SIGKILL/OOM, not a clean exception) mid-chunk leaves the checkpoint at the last committed boundary and the in-flight chunk is safely RE-DONE (idempotently) on resume — never recorded as done-when-it-wasn't; verified by a test that kills the backfill mid-chunk before commit and asserts the resumed runner re-processes exactly that chunk and the final manifest is correct.
rationale: #775 covers resume EFFICIENCY (skip done chunks) and #132 covers idempotency, but neither pins the crash-consistency invariant that the checkpoint is commit-gated — a checkpoint advanced before commit under SIGKILL would skip an un-sealed chunk, leaving a permanent gap the marker reports as complete.

text: When a sealed day is later corrected (hash-mismatch refetch #296 or manual force-reverify #397) and dependent stored results are enumerated by data-provenance (#795), the affected backtest_results/sweep_results MUST be FLAGGED to consumers (GET/API/UI and the MCP backtest_get/sweep_results outputs) as 'input data changed — result may be stale' until re-run, rather than silently serving the pre-correction numbers as authoritative; verified by a test that corrects a day, then GETs an affected result and asserts the staleness flag is surfaced while an unaffected result is not flagged.
rationale: #795 ENUMERATES results whose input data changed and #778 enables bulk re-run, but nothing surfaces staleness to the consumer in the interim — a user/agent viewing an affected result between correction and re-run currently sees stale numbers with no indication, a downstream observability gap.

text: The Postgres kline_cache OHLCV column type MUST be documented and pinned, and the Bybit-string -> Postgres-stored-value -> float64 SoA conversion MUST be lossless and deterministic such that the float64 reproduced from the Postgres value is bit-identical to the float64 stored in the Parquet/Feather/Arrow tier; verified by a test that ingests a candle whose OHLC strings carry >15 significant digits and asserts the float64 obtained via the Postgres read equals the float64 obtained via the columnar read bit-for-bit, so the byte-identity guarantee (#115) is well-defined at the numeric-type level (which #437 leaves unpinned for OHLCV values, covering only open_time epoch round-trip and the Arrow logical type).
rationale: Byte-identity across tiers (#101/#115/#437) is asserted but the underlying Postgres OHLCV storage type (NUMERIC vs DOUBLE vs TEXT) and the string->storage->float64 rounding path are never pinned; if Postgres stores DOUBLE it rounds on write while Parquet rounds from the original string, so the two tiers could differ in the last bits and silently fail the byte-identity gate the whole parity story depends on.

text: A single documented symbol-string canonicalization (case, separator, and contract-suffix normalization, e.g. 'BTC/USDT' vs 'BTCUSDT' vs 'BTCUSDT.P') MUST be applied uniformly so the same logical symbol keys identically across scan_source signals, kline_cache, the sealed manifest, symbol_lifecycle, the Bybit fetch ticker, the Parquet hive partition value, the in-process LRU/hot-cache key, and the symbol->contiguous-int-code map; verified by a test feeding a signal whose symbol string differs in format from the kline-store form and asserting it resolves to ONE cache/manifest/partition key (never split into two entries, never silently demoted to signals_no_kline), distinct from the reserved-character path ENCODING of #438/#646.
rationale: Reqs #438/#646 cover encoding filesystem-reserved characters into the partition PATH but nothing pins that the canonical symbol FORM matches across the signal source and the kline store; a format mismatch between scan_source and kline_cache would split one logical symbol across two cache keys (doubling fetches/RAM) or turn every signal for it into signals_no_kline, silently changing the trade set.

text: kline rows persisted before the turnover (quote-volume) column existed (NULL/absent turnover, or NULL volume) MUST be handled by ONE documented policy on the read path, in the fine->coarse turnover/volume aggregation (#404/#1027), and in the content_sha256 / count-shortfall integrity checks (#254/#295/#602), so a sealed day whose stored rows predate the turnover column neither raises, nor produces a NULL-poisoned derived-coarse bar, nor spuriously trips a hash-mismatch/short-read refetch; verified by a test that seals and reads a day of legacy rows carrying NULL turnover and asserts a clean read, a correct coarse aggregation, and zero spurious integrity refetch.
rationale: #1027 mandates volume/turnover be persisted and aggregated going forward but the brownfield reality is a large body of already-cached rows written before that column existed; reading or coarse-deriving over NULL turnover, or folding it into content_sha256, can crash aggregation or flip the canonical hash and trigger a perpetual refetch of otherwise-immutable sealed days.

text: Every in-process memo whose CONTENTS depend on a data-path flag (derive-coarse-from-fine #940, columnar-vs-Postgres read #149, sealed-manifest gate #150) MUST incorporate the resolved data-path flag generation into its key OR be invalidated when that flag changes between runs on a long-lived process, so flipping a data-path flag never serves a frame/series built under the other path; verified by a test that warms the derived-coarse (or columnar) memo, flips the corresponding flag off for the next run on the same process, and asserts the second run does NOT serve the previously-built memo (it re-derives via the now-active path) while #424's within-run flag freeze still holds.
rationale: #673 re-reads flags per run and #424 freezes them within a run, but the process-global memos (derived-coarse #405, raw->SoA, columnar handles) are keyed only by (symbol,interval,range); after an operator flips derive-coarse or columnar-read off mid-incident, a stale memo built under the old path would still be served, splicing two data paths across runs and defeating the kill-switch.

text: The Parquet writer MUST be explicitly configured to EMIT per-column row-group min/max statistics (at minimum for open_time) and write open_time sorted within each row group, and a test MUST inspect the written Parquet file METADATA directly (not merely observe query behavior) to assert open_time column statistics are present and non-trivial in every row group, since predicate/partition pushdown (#110) and sub-file row-group pruning (#739) silently degrade to full-file scans when a writer ships with statistics disabled.
rationale: #110/#739 assume row-group min/max stats exist and tune row-group SIZE, but several Parquet writers default to statistics-off or drop them for some encodings; without a direct metadata assertion that stats are actually written, a config regression would silently turn every date-range predicate into a full-file scan, blowing the bytes-read gate (#446) and the cold/warm latency budgets while passing behavioral tests by luck.

text: Atomic columnar writes (temp-write + rename, #109) MUST create the temp file WITHIN the target partition's own directory/filesystem so the finalizing rename/replace is a same-filesystem atomic operation and never fails with EXDEV (cross-device link) when the columnar cache dir is on a different mount than the system temp dir; verified by a test that points the columnar cache dir at a filesystem distinct from TMPDIR and asserts the materialization still completes atomically (temp created beside the final file, single atomic replace), complementing the Windows rename-over-open-file rule (#440).
rationale: #109/#652/#440 cover atomicity, fsync ordering, and Windows sharing-violation but never the EXDEV case; a common deploy layout puts the durable cache volume on a separate mount from the ephemeral temp dir, and a naive tempfile.mkstemp()+os.replace() across mounts raises EXDEV mid-materialization, aborting cold columnar builds in production while passing every same-mount CI test.

text: When a month partition is materialized as MULTIPLE atomic part-files (incremental day-by-day sealing per #253/#791), a range read spanning several part-files of that one month MUST return globally open_time-ASCENDING, de-duplicated rows (part-files ordered by their covered day-range and merged), byte-identical to a single-file or Postgres read of the same span; verified by a test that seals days into one month across several separate runs (producing multiple part-files) and asserts a cross-part-file read of that month is correctly ordered with no intra-month seam gap, duplicate, or misorder, distinct from the cross-MONTH seam (#359) and cross-TIER seam (#439).
rationale: #253 only asserts the month's row SET equals the SOR (set, not order) and #359/#439/#598 cover cross-month, cross-tier, and in-process-overlap seams but never the ORDERING when one month is physically split into several part-files; since path-dependent metrics (max-drawdown/Sharpe) and the merge-walk pointers assume strictly-ascending per-symbol order, an unordered intra-month concatenation would silently corrupt results.

text: The SEAL operation MUST be deterministic across independent cold runs: two fresh-process cold runs over the same (symbols, interval, window) against identical exchange data MUST seal the IDENTICAL set of (symbol,interval,day) tuples with identical day-class (#1032) and identical content_sha256, regardless of fetch ordering, concurrency, or pagination-seam placement; verified by a test that runs two independent cold loads of the same range against the same mocked client and diffs the resulting sealed-manifest rows (sealed set, gap_count, sha) for byte-equality, complementing lifecycle-discovery determinism (#941), seam-invariant sha (#965), and concurrent same-day convergence (#252).
rationale: #941 pins lifecycle-discovery determinism and #252 pins concurrent same-day upsert convergence, but nothing asserts that two SEPARATE cold runs seal the same SET of days (which days cross the frontier, which are sealed-short vs sealed-with-gap) deterministically; a frontier/ordering-sensitive seal decision could seal a day in one run and leave it forming in another, producing divergent immutable manifests and non-reproducible downstream results.

text: A DURABLE negative-cached / sealed-empty day (pre-listing or post-delist, #90) MUST be invalidated and made refetchable when symbol_lifecycle is subsequently corrected (operator override #779, background-refresh relist/transition #931/#411) to place that day inside [listing,delist]; the invalidation MUST be durable (manifest-level, surviving process restart) — not only the in-process negative-cache memo of #1034 — so a cross-process or post-restart run re-discovers the now-in-life day instead of perpetually serving it empty; verified by a test that negative-caches a pre-listing day, corrects listing_time earlier, and asserts a FRESH-process run refetches/serves that day (no longer known-empty) while genuinely out-of-life days stay zero-fetch.
rationale: #1034 only invalidates the IN-PROCESS negative-cache memo on a mid-session lifecycle change; a durable sealed-empty manifest row for a day later corrected to in-life issues 0 fetch (#90/#448) forever across processes, so #411's 'detect unexpected candles in a known-empty range' can never trigger because the day is never fetched — the durable negative cache must be invalidated on the lifecycle correction itself.

text: Because backtests consume stored scan_source results as their signal source, the system MUST guarantee reproducibility against signal-data churn: either the scan_source signals (and per-scan ScanContexts) a run consumed are snapshotted/retained keyed to the run, or the reproduce-from-fingerprint path (#371) and any re-run/retry path MUST detect that a run's referenced scan_source rows were pruned/changed (via the signal-provenance fingerprint #794) and report 'signal inputs unavailable/changed' rather than silently producing a different or empty result; verified by a test that records a run, prunes its scan_source rows, and asserts the re-run/reproduce path reports unavailable instead of diverging, analogous to the foreign-provenance classification (#1048).
rationale: Reqs #371/#794 fingerprint and claim to reproduce a run by re-executing the engine, but the engine needs the original scan_source signals; if scan-result retention prunes those rows (a normal operational policy), a reproduce or retry silently re-runs over absent/changed signals and emits different numbers presented as authoritative — the signal-side analog of the kline data-immutability guarantees, currently unaddressed.

text: v58 ADD COLUMN and CHECK-constraint changes on the LIVE backtest_runs / backtest_results / sweep_results tables (the fingerprint/stage-timing/provenance columns of #430/#661 and the status-enum relaxation of #918) MUST be lock-bounded against in-flight run persistence exactly like the kline_cache_coverage DDL: ADD COLUMN uses constant defaults (no rewrite), and the status CHECK relaxation MUST be applied as ADD CONSTRAINT ... NOT VALID followed by VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (or drop-and-readd) so it never takes a long ACCESS EXCLUSIVE validation scan on a large backtest_runs; verified by a test holding an in-flight result-persist transaction during v58 and asserting the DDL on these tables blocks no longer than the bounded lock_timeout budget (existing lock reqs #431/#579/#971 target only kline_cache_coverage).
rationale: v58 demonstrably touches backtest_runs/results/sweep_results (#430/#661/#918) but every lock-safety requirement is scoped to kline_cache_coverage; relaxing a status CHECK constraint forces a full-table validation scan by default, a hidden long-lock hazard on a large run-history table during deploy.

text: The v58 migration (ADD COLUMN propagation to children, the >6mo/future monthly-partition auto-create of #137/#456, and the set-based seal-backfill UPDATE) MUST be tested against the ACTUAL production PARTITIONING TECHNOLOGY of kline_cache (native declarative partitioning vs a TimescaleDB hypertable vs pg_partman-managed partitions), since ADD COLUMN propagation, CREATE PARTITION DDL, chunk targeting, and bulk-UPDATE locking differ materially across them; verified by a CI lane that applies v58 against the same partitioning extension/setup used in prod and asserts column propagation to all partitions/chunks and a correct backfill (req #785 pins only the PG MAJOR VERSION, not the partitioning technology).
rationale: Crypto-futures OHLCV stores are commonly TimescaleDB hypertables or pg_partman-managed; the entire partition/seal model behaves differently there, and a prod-only failure of ADD COLUMN-to-chunks or partition creation would break the deploy despite all generic-PG tests passing.

text: Every completion-frontier / seal-backfill query that derives now_utc from the database clock (the coordinated source of #922, e.g. Postgres NOW()) MUST force a UTC basis (e.g. timezone('UTC', now()) or epoch math) so the per-(symbol,interval) floor((now-skew)/T)*T frontier is invariant to the Postgres server's TimeZone GUC; verified by a test that runs the frontier/seal decision with the server session TimeZone set to a non-UTC zone (e.g. America/New_York) and asserts the sealed-vs-forming boundary day is identical to the UTC-session result (distinct from #250/#406 app-side UTC normalization and #922's single-source rule, neither of which pins the GUC-independence of the DB clock read).
rationale: #922 mandates a single DB-derived clock but not that its read is UTC-normalized; a server with a local TimeZone GUC would skew NOW()-based frontier arithmetic by the local offset and prematurely seal or under-refresh the live-edge day, a classic Windows-dev/Linux-prod and managed-PG footgun.

text: For the deploy model where v58 runs as a SEPARATE pre-deploy migration Job / init-container (not app-boot auto-migrate), the app pods MUST gate readiness on a confirmed schema_version>=58 AND backfill-marker presence: a pod that boots before the migration Job has applied v58 MUST await (bounded) or serve the legacy/degraded path and report 'awaiting migration' on the status route, NEVER crash-loop on missing columns or attempt its own concurrent migrate; verified by a test booting an app pod against a still-pre-v58 DB (migration Job not yet complete) and asserting bounded-await-or-degrade plus an actionable status, distinct from #928 (mismatch alert) and #980 (CLI-vs-boot equivalence) which do not pin the separate-Job sequencing/await contract.
rationale: k8s deploys frequently split migrations into a Job/init-container; without an explicit await-or-degrade contract the app either crash-loops on missing v58 columns or races a second migration, and the existing reqs cover detection/alert and CLI equivalence but not the ordering handshake.

text: SWEEP run-state rows (the sweep/optimize orchestrator's own 'running'/'pending' record) left by a process killed during a deploy/restart MUST be reconciled to a terminal-or-resumable state on boot — marked failed/'interrupted by restart' or requeued, with all _MAX_CONCURRENT slots, ProcessPool workers, and shared-memory segments released — never stuck 'running' forever; verified by a test that seeds a 'running' sweep row, simulates a restart, and asserts reconciliation plus freed slots/workers, the sweep analog of #450 (which reconciles only backtest_runs) and distinct from the crash-RESUME combo test (#306).
rationale: #450 reconciles single-run backtest_runs but a sweep has its own run-state and pool/shared-memory footprint; a deploy mid-sweep would otherwise leave a phantom 'running' sweep and leaked workers/segments with no boot-time cleanup.

text: Engaging the master safe-mode kill-switch (#277) MUST also PAUSE the deferred seal-backfill (in addition to routing engine/cache/read/sweep to legacy #277 and halting new seal writes #1053), so a backfill suspected of causing a production incident (lock contention, replica lag, wrongful seals) stops immediately without a separate admin action; the backfill MUST resume idempotently from its checkpoint only after safe-mode is lifted; verified by a test that engages safe-mode mid-backfill and asserts the backfill halts within a bounded window and resumes correctly when safe-mode clears, distinct from the operator-only pause of #782.
rationale: The single-lever incident response (#277) must encompass the backfill, since the backfill is a prime suspect for deploy-time DB pressure; #1053 halts seal writes during fetch and #782 is a separate manual pause, leaving a gap where safe-mode does not stop the in-progress backfill.

text: The eventual CONTRACTION migration (v59+) that drops the legacy count-based coverage columns/paths deferred by #581 MUST carry a tested PRECONDITION GATE that refuses to execute its destructive DDL unless it can confirm no pre-v58 (legacy) code path could still be writing the table — e.g. a minimum drain-age since the last legacy-shaped upsert, an explicit operator/all-pods-drained confirmation token, or a fleet-version check — so contraction cannot run while a lingering pre-v58 instance still depends on the legacy columns; verified by a test that the contraction migration aborts when the drain-age/confirmation precondition is unmet and proceeds only when satisfied.
rationale: #581 mandates expand-then-later-contract but never pins a safety gate on the contraction step itself; without it, a contraction applied during a slow/stuck rollover (or an autoscaled-back old pod) would drop columns a still-running pre-v58 instance reads, causing exactly the breakage expand-only was meant to prevent.

text: The migration runner MUST bootstrap and self-repair its own ledger: create the schema_version / migration-metadata table if absent, and detect+surface (with an actionable, gated error and a documented manual-repair escape hatch) a missing, duplicated, or corrupt schema_version row rather than crashing or silently assuming version 0/57; verified by tests that (a) a DB with no ledger table is bootstrapped and migrated cleanly and (b) a DB with a corrupt/absent version row raises an actionable error and the documented repair path restores a consistent ledger without data loss.
rationale: No requirement covers the integrity of the migration ledger itself; a restore from a pre-ledger backup, a partial apply, or accidental ledger corruption would otherwise crash boot or trigger an incorrect re-apply, with no defined recovery path.

text: A forward redeploy / re-run of the idempotent flag-and-config seed migration (#968) and the boot resolver MUST preserve operator-MODIFIED flag/config values written during an incident (#925 audit / #981 durable store): the seed uses INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (never overwrite) and the resolver never resets a persisted operator value to its documented default on restart; verified by a test that sets a flag/numeric-config to a non-default value, re-runs the seed migration and restarts the process, and asserts the operator value survives (distinct from #667 which proves DDL/backfill no-op but not config-row VALUE preservation).
rationale: Operators flip kill-switches/budgets during incidents (#925/#981); a roll-forward that re-seeds the config table or a resolver that re-reads defaults could silently revert a deliberate emergency override, re-enabling the misbehaving optimization, and no existing req tests value preservation across re-seed/redeploy.

text: If the durable columnar tier (Parquet/Feather/Arrow) may reside on object storage or a network filesystem WITHOUT POSIX atomic rename (S3-compatible, some EFS/SMB/NFS configs), the writer MUST use a backend-appropriate atomic-commit primitive (conditional/if-none-match put + atomic manifest-pointer swap, or write-new-object-then-repoint) instead of temp-write+rename, and the boot/deploy validation MUST detect the configured backend's atomicity capability and degrade the read path to Postgres (logged) when the required primitive is unavailable; verified by a test simulating a no-atomic-rename backend asserting no reader ever observes a partial/duplicated object and an unsupported backend degrades to Postgres rather than corrupting the cache (reqs #109/#440/#652 assume POSIX/Windows local rename only).
rationale: Atomic-write requirements assume a local POSIX/Windows filesystem with rename semantics; on object storage there is no atomic rename, so the same durability guarantees silently break, and nothing pins backend-capability detection or an object-store-safe commit strategy.

text: The running service MUST expose a continuous sealed/materialized COVERAGE-RATIO gauge (sealed days ÷ in-life closed days, and a lazy-seal-backlog count) per the loaded universe, and an alertable signal MUST fire if this ratio REGRESSES after the backfill marker reports 'complete' (indicating un-sealing, manifest-row loss, or a runaway lazy-seal storm); verified by a test that drops/clears some sealed rows post-complete and asserts the ratio gauge falls and the regression alert fires, distinct from the one-time backfill PROGRESS (#669) and per-run lazy-seal COUNTERS (#764), neither of which provides a standing post-complete coverage-health signal.
rationale: After the backfill completes, nothing continuously watches whether the sealed surface STAYS sealed; a manifest-rebuild bug, an accidental reset (#452), or a lazy-seal storm could silently erode coverage and re-introduce re-download pressure with no standing alert.

text: The deploy MUST support a tested DRAIN/QUIESCE mode that, for the brief v58 DDL/lock window, stops admitting NEW backtest/sweep submissions (returning a retriable 'maintenance/busy' signal) while allowing in-flight runs to finish or be cooperatively cancelled, so no run's persist (delete-before-insert / results upsert) collides with the ADD COLUMN / CHECK-constraint change on backtest_runs/results; verified by a test asserting that during drain new submissions are rejected-retriable, in-flight runs reach clean terminal state, and the DDL applies without contending with an active persist (distinct from capacity queueing #536 and SIGTERM handling #451).
rationale: v58 mutates the live backtest_runs/results tables; without an admission-drain during the lock window a run persisting concurrently with the ADD COLUMN/CHECK change can block the DDL or fail mid-write, and no requirement provides an operational quiesce for the cutover.

text: v58 MUST run a PRE-FLIGHT validation of the existing kline_cache partition tree before any backfill or partition auto-create — asserting the partition parent exists, the migration-38 monthly RANGE scheme is intact (no missing or overlapping bounds, default partition present), and target partitions for the sealed ranges either exist or can be created — failing fast with an actionable message rather than backfilling into or auto-creating against a broken/overlapping partition layout; verified by a test that seeds a partition tree with a gap/overlap and asserts v58 refuses to proceed with a specific diagnostic (distinct from the read-time >6mo/default handling of #135/#136 and bounds-alignment of #456).
rationale: The backfill UPDATE and >6mo/future partition auto-create (#137/#456) assume a well-formed partition tree; a pre-existing gap/overlap (legacy migration-38 drift) would cause a mid-backfill failure or rows silently landing in kline_cache_default, and nothing validates partition-tree integrity before applying.

text: The deploy pre-flight MUST detect conditions that would stall or bloat v58 — a blocking long-running transaction holding a conflicting lock on kline_cache_coverage/backtest_runs, or an INACTIVE replication slot / old transaction pinning xmin (which prevents autovacuum/HOT cleanup of the backfill's churn per #942) — and warn or abort with an actionable message before starting, rather than blocking on lock acquisition or generating unvacuumable bloat; verified by a test that, with a simulated blocking long transaction / inactive slot present, the migration pre-flight surfaces it and refuses-or-warns rather than stalling indefinitely.
rationale: A common production migration failure is a stuck old transaction or an inactive logical-replication slot that blocks ALTER/VALIDATE and defeats autovacuum on the large backfill UPDATE; the lock-timeout reqs (#579) handle a single statement but nothing checks for the xmin-pinning / blocking-transaction condition before committing to the migration.

text: Columnar (Parquet/Feather/Arrow) partitions written by a phase later found defective MUST be invalidatable on rollback even when the FORMAT-version stamp is unchanged (a same-format but buggy WRITER): each artifact MUST embed the engine/columnar-builder BUILD-ID (git SHA) in addition to the format-version (#256/#287/#771) and SOR data-generation (#755), and a rollback to a known-good build MUST be able to quarantine+rebuild artifacts stamped with a denylisted/defective build-id rather than trusting them; verified by a test that marks a build-id defective and asserts artifacts it wrote are rebuilt-from-Postgres while format/SOR-generation-identical good-build artifacts are reused.
rationale: #978/#256/#771 only handle FORMAT-version mismatches and #755 handles DATA changes; a writer bug that produces content-wrong-but-format-valid Parquet within one format generation cannot be invalidated by either lever, leaving silently-corrupt cached partitions trusted after rollback.

text: The post-deploy canary/promotion suite MUST include exactly ONE real, non-mocked exchange fetch (a tiny bounded Bybit kline request for a known symbol) that asserts live connectivity, credentials/category mapping (#1039), and a parse of a real v5 payload, gating promotion on its success — because every golden/cache/canary test mocks the Bybit client (#293) and would stay green through a broken API key, a changed egress/proxy, a category/endpoint change, or a v5 wire-format change; verified by the deploy pipeline including the real-fetch smoke and failing the promotion when it errors.
rationale: All correctness/canary gates run with the exchange mocked and network blocked, so the very first REAL fetch after deploy is unverified; a credential/egress/category/wire-format regression would pass 100% of tests and only surface on a user's first production run.

text: The per-run data-provenance fingerprint (#395/#805/#966) and the 'input data changed — result may be stale' flag (#1061) MUST be invariant to a manifest REBUILD-from-SOR (#675/#777) and to a content_sha256 presence change (computed↔NULL) over UNCHANGED klines: the fingerprint MUST derive from a sha-presence-independent canonical token (e.g. per-day last_open_ts+candle_count+gap-range digest) so a manifest rebuild or a tier-warmth change never makes historic results appear stale/changed; verified by a test that rebuilds the manifest (flipping shas to NULL) over byte-identical klines and asserts the fingerprint and staleness flag of dependent results are unchanged, while an actual kline CONTENT change still flips them.
rationale: A manifest rebuild (#675/#777, used by copy-prod-scans and corruption recovery) legitimately changes sha presence without changing klines; if the fingerprint includes sha bytes, every historic result would falsely flag as 'input changed' (#1061), eroding trust in the staleness signal — #805/#966 imply but never pin rebuild-invariance.

text: Applying the v58 schema migration (DDL + seal-backfill, which are pure SQL) MUST NOT require numba/pyarrow/duckdb to be importable: the migration tooling MUST run successfully on the pure-Python deploy image (accel extra absent, #267), so an environment that ships without the accel stack can still bring its DB to v58 and serve the legacy-equivalent path; verified by a test that runs the full v58 migration with numba/pyarrow/duckdb force-absent and asserts the schema reaches 58 and the backfill completes with no import error.
rationale: The schema migration and the accel runtime must be independently deployable (schema-first/behavior-later #672, separable accel layer #433); if any migration code path imported the accel stack, a pure-Python image could not even apply the schema, coupling an irreversible-ish migration to an optional dependency.

text: A monthly Postgres partition AUTO-CREATED by store_klines for a >6mo-old or future range (#137/#456/#1056) MUST inherit the SAME storage parameters as the migration-38 partitions — fillfactor, autovacuum settings, and (for a TimescaleDB/compressed scheme) the compression/chunk policy — not just the name (#1056) and bounds (#456), so retention tooling, autovacuum behavior, and query plans treat auto-created and migration-created partitions uniformly; verified by a test asserting an auto-created partition's reloptions/storage parameters match an existing migration-38 partition.
rationale: #456/#1056 pin bounds and naming but not storage parameters; an auto-created partition lacking the established fillfactor/autovacuum/compression settings would bloat differently, evade retention tooling expectations, or read slower, producing subtle long-term operational drift that only manifests on historical/backfilled ranges.

text: The batched symbol=ANY($1) kline loader, the batched signal/ScanContext loader, the manifest seal-check, and all persist writes MUST use a FIXED parameterized SQL text whose statement string is INVARIANT to symbol count, row count, and combo count (array binding + executemany, never a string-interpolated IN-list), so asyncpg's per-connection prepared-statement cache holds a small bounded number of distinct prepared statements across an entire sweep/session and never thrashes or re-plans per symbol-count; a test MUST assert the SQL text is byte-identical across an N-symbol and an M-symbol load and that the distinct-prepared-statement count stays <= a documented small bound across a K-combo sweep.
rationale: Heavily covered are query COUNT (O(1) round-trips #16/#851) and partition pruning (#644), but nothing pins prepared-statement/plan-cache STABILITY. A loader that builds a different SQL string per symbol-set silently evicts asyncpg's statement cache (default 100) and re-plans every load, adding hidden per-load latency that scales with sweep size — a real throughput regression invisible to the round-trip-count gates.

text: The sweep/optimize_config orchestrator MUST DEDUPLICATE combos by config_hash BEFORE dispatch — executing each DISTINCT strategy config through the engine exactly once and fanning that single result back to every duplicate rank/combo slot — so a search space (grid/random) that emits identical or logically-equivalent configs does not multiply engine compute; a test feeding a space containing duplicate configs MUST assert the number of engine executions == the distinct-config count (< K) while all K rank slots still receive a correct result and the final 1..K ranking (#721) stays valid.
rationale: No requirement dedupes identical combos. Grid/random sweep spaces routinely generate duplicate configs (same config_hash); running each duplicate independently wastes proportional compute and DB writes. Dedupe-before-dispatch is a concrete, testable throughput win, complementing crash-resume idempotency (#306) and ON CONFLICT(config_hash) (#76) which only prevent duplicate STORAGE, not duplicate COMPUTE.

text: The cold-fetch kline INGEST PARSE/NORMALIZE step (Bybit string-typed rows -> int64 epoch-ms + float64 OHLCV, ascending-sort + de-dupe per #640) MUST be O(candles), allocate no retained per-candle Python object beyond the transient parse, and stay within a documented bounded share of cold-ingest wall-time; a candle-doubling test on a mocked paginating client MUST assert parse+normalize time scales ~linearly and stays under the documented share, distinct from the content_sha256 single-pass bound (#740) which gates only hashing, not the full string->float parse and ordering normalize.
rationale: Cold wall-time is asserted network-bound (#5), but the per-page parse of thousands of string-numeric rows + the ascending normalize is pure-Python CPU that can silently dominate a wide cold fetch. #31 bounds the columnar READ path's per-row dicts and #740 bounds the sha pass, but the cold INGEST parse/normalize throughput is ungated.

text: Single-core candle-evaluations/sec MUST stay within a documented band as the resident SoA working set GROWS (window-doubling and symbol-doubling toward the wide point), proving no super-linear per-candle throughput COLLAPSE when the int64-epoch+float64-OHLC arrays exceed CPU cache; a test MUST assert candle-evals/sec at the wide capacity point stays >= a documented fraction of the canonical-point rate, distinct from the linear total-WALL-TIME gate (#12) and the fixed throughput FLOORS (#14/#15/#554) which are measured only at canonical/heavy size.
rationale: #12 bounds total time linearly and #14/#15 set throughput floors at one size, but neither catches a cache-locality cliff where per-candle throughput degrades as the SoA outgrows L2/L3 (e.g. a random-access pattern, pointer-chasing, or a non-streaming column layout). A throughput-stability-vs-size gate catches a regression that keeps total time 'linear-ish' while quietly halving per-candle rate at scale.

text: Aggregate result PERSISTENCE across a K-combo sweep (K trade-list inserts + K equity_curve JSONB serializations + the set-based rank UPDATE) MUST scale with Sigma(trades + curve_points) across combos and issue O(K + Sigma pages) DB round-trips (batched/pipelined COPY/executemany), NOT O(Sigma trades), and MUST stay a bounded share of sweep wall-time; a test doubling combo count at fixed per-combo trade count MUST assert aggregate persist time grows ~linearly with K and never dominates the sweep, distinct from the single-run persist bound (#220/#508) and the sweep persist-MEMORY bounds (#738/#855).
rationale: Single-run persist scaling (#220/#508) and sweep persist MEMORY/backpressure (#738/#855) are covered, but the aggregate persist-TIME scaling of a large sweep (could be hundreds of full equity_curves) is ungated. Progressive per-combo persistence (#547) could still serialize on un-batched writes and dominate a fast-engine sweep's wall-time.

text: The single-run pre-flight cost/feasibility estimator (#224/#408/#517) MUST include a COLD-COLUMNAR-BUILD term when the durable Parquet/Feather volume is empty/wiped (post-deploy or ephemeral-volume per #802), so a first run whose latency is dominated by one-time columnar materialization is not under-estimated and timeout-killed; a calibration test with an empty columnar volume MUST assert the estimate incorporates the build term and brackets actual within the documented factor, distinct from the drill-cost term (#745), host-calibration (#806), and cold-fetch term (#408).
rationale: The estimator models compute (candles x scans), cold fetch (#408), drill (#745), and host speed (#806), but not the one-time Parquet/Feather BUILD cost that #233/#802 acknowledge is real after a volume wipe. A post-deploy first run on an ephemeral cache could be dominated by columnar materialization and hit the 120s kill despite passing the compute/fetch estimate.

text: For a SINGLE backtest run the recorded engine-CPU-time MUST stay within a documented band of engine WALL-time (no busy-wait/lock-spin inflating CPU, no accidental thread oversubscription on the single-run path), and on the GIL-bound pure-Python fallback the CPU/wall ratio MUST be ~1.0; a per-phase gate MUST assert the ratio stays in-band and FAIL on a regression that pushes CPU far above wall (spin) or far below (unexpected blocking/idle), complementing the realized-sweep-parallelism gate (#894) which only covers multi-combo sweeps.
rationale: #894 gates realized parallelism for SWEEPS, but a single-run CPU-efficiency invariant is missing. A regression that introduces a spin-loop, a needlessly-held lock, or nested BLAS/numba threads on the single-run path inflates CPU and host load without improving (or while degrading) throughput — invisible to a pure wall-time gate yet harmful to the shared host and to concurrent live-path latency.

text: The asyncpg pool max_size MUST be explicitly provisioned for the documented WORST-CASE concurrent steady-state demand = live-scanner/auto-trade baseline + (_MAX_CONCURRENT backtest runs x each run's bounded per-run DB concurrency, #376) + the sweep DB-read semaphore cap, so the combined live+backtest+sweep workload never blocks on pool acquisition; a test MUST assert the configured max_size >= the documented worst-case sum and that saturating _MAX_CONCURRENT backtests + a wide sweep never makes a live-path acquisition wait beyond a bounded budget (distinct from #373 'not deadlocked' and #886 min_size pre-warm).
rationale: #373 asserts the pool isn't exhausted under _MAX_CONCURRENT and #886 pre-warms min_size, but neither pins max_size to a SIZING FORMULA covering the simultaneous live + backtest + sweep demand. Under-provisioning silently serializes the live trading path behind backtest/sweep DB reads — a scalability hazard the existing gates can pass while still starving live acquisitions.

text: A single STANDING regression guard MUST assert the asyncio event-loop lag stays under a documented bound for the ENTIRE duration of both a backtest and a sweep (spanning kline load, SoA build, columnar materialization, persist, sha/integrity recompute, config re-read #769, and HTTP fetch), so ANY incidental synchronous/CPU-heavy/blocking call newly introduced on the loop thread by a later phase is caught holistically — consolidating the per-path probes (#375 engine, #557 SoA build, #900 columnar, #596 connection-hold) into one always-on guard rather than path-specific spot checks.
rationale: The per-path event-loop probes each cover ONE code path; a future phase could add a blocking call on a path none of them probes (a synchronous metrics flush, a blocking watched-file config re-read per #769, a sync sha recompute, a sync file stat in the read tier). A single whole-pipeline loop-lag guard makes 'never block the shared FastAPI loop / live coroutines' a standing invariant rather than a collection of point assertions.

text: The backtest run-HISTORY enumeration/list endpoint (and the run-selection path feeding /backtest/compare) MUST be index-backed and cursor/limit-paginated so its latency is independent of the total number of stored backtest_runs/results rows (no full-table scan per call), bounded as history grows; a test MUST assert list/enumeration latency stays flat as stored run count grows large, distinct from the single-run equity-curve GET (#515), single-run trades pagination (#853), and the readiness/status endpoint (#514).
rationale: All GET-latency gates target a SINGLE run's curve/trades/status. The history-list endpoint that the UI and compare-selection read — which grows monotonically with every run and sweep combo persisted — has no scaling gate. As thousands of runs accumulate, an unindexed ORDER BY/scan on the list endpoint degrades the whole dashboard even though every single-run GET stays fast.

text: After the v58 seal-backfill's bulk UPDATE on kline_cache_coverage (and after creating the manifest seal-check index), the migration MUST run ANALYZE (or rely on a documented auto-analyze wait) on kline_cache_coverage / the manifest before declaring success, so the planner's stale statistics from the mass column-add+UPDATE do not cause the seal-check query (#512/#514) to fall back to a sequential scan; verified by a test asserting ANALYZE is issued post-backfill and that an EXPLAIN of the seal-check uses the index immediately after migration (not after an unbounded autovacuum delay).
rationale: A large ADD COLUMN + set-based UPDATE leaves planner stats stale; until autovacuum re-analyzes, the index-backed seal-check (#512/#514) silently degrades to a full scan on the hot shared table — no requirement forces a stats refresh as part of the migration.

text: The master kill-switch / per-path optimization flag READ at run start (the DB-backed/persisted source of #769/#981) MUST observe read-your-writes for a just-flipped value fleet-wide — read from the primary or via a staleness-guarded path — so a safe-mode flip during an incident is honored on the very next run on every pod even under Postgres read-replica lag; verified by a test that flips safe-mode on the primary and asserts a pod resolving flags against a lagging replica still routes to the legacy path on its next run (never serves the stale pre-flip value).
rationale: #772 only makes the MANIFEST tolerate replica lag (treat-as-unsealed); #769 proves next-run-on-same-process; nothing guarantees the incident kill-switch VALUE itself isn't served stale from a lagging replica, which would silently delay the revert across the fleet.

text: Because OMP_NUM_THREADS / OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS / NUMBA_NUM_THREADS (#212/#577) are PROCESS-global, pinning them for the backtest accel path MUST be validated to not regress the in-process LIVE scanner/auto-trade numpy/BLAS indicator-compute latency (which shares the same process), or the caps MUST be applied scoped to the backtest execution context; a test MUST assert the live-path indicator-compute latency stays within a documented bound before vs after the thread caps are applied at import.
rationale: #857 confines the numba IMPORT to the backtest path, but BLAS/OMP env caps are process-wide and would throttle the live scanner's own numpy work — no requirement checks the live-path side effect of the global thread-cap pinning.

text: The v58 migration advisory lock(s) (#413/#665) MUST be explicitly released on NORMAL successful completion — not only auto-released on connection death — verified by a test that immediately after a successful migrate the advisory lock is free (a fresh acquisition succeeds without waiting), so a long-lived migrating connection that stays open in the pool cannot leave the fleet-wide migration lock held and block a subsequent boot/CLI migrate.
rationale: #665 only covers release on a DEAD backend; if the migrating connection survives (pooled/long-lived) and the code forgets to unlock on the success path, the next runner blocks until lock_timeout — release-on-success is unasserted.

text: The post-migration deploy canary (#288/#427/#809) MUST assert the new ADDITIVE columns (optimization fingerprint #430, per-stage timings #549, data/signal provenance #395/#794, recorded kernel #496, sweep fingerprint #661, backfill-marker-state #674) are actually POPULATED (written non-null with sane values) on the canary run — not merely present in the schema — gating promotion, so a deploy where the persist path silently fails to write the new columns is caught before traffic.
rationale: #288 asserts reconciliation+total_trades and #430 asserts schema PRESENCE, but nothing gates the deploy on the new columns being WRITTEN by the live persist path; a broken write would leave every new row's provenance NULL and silently un-attributable.

text: During the transient window after v58 DDL but BEFORE the idempotent flag/config seed migration (#968) has inserted rows (or if a seed row is later deleted), a run that reads a flag or numeric operational config whose backing DB row is ABSENT MUST resolve to that key's documented SAFE default (legacy path for kill-switches; staged default otherwise) without crashing or NULL-dereferencing — distinct from the malformed-ENV case (#425); verified by a test that deletes a seeded config row and asserts the resolver returns the safe default plus a structured warning.
rationale: #968 only proves a CLEAN migrated DB has every row; it does not pin resolver behavior when a DB-backed flag row is missing at runtime (pre-seed window or post-delete), which must degrade safe rather than error.

text: The deferred seal-backfill MUST carry a configured MAX-TOTAL-WALL-CLOCK budget after which it AUTO-SUSPENDS (leaving already-sealed days sealed, the completion marker 'in_progress', and a resumable checkpoint #775) and emits an alertable signal, distinct from the per-chunk statement/idle timeout (#1042), the replica-lag throttle (#921), and the stall ALERT-only signal (#669); verified by a test that a backfill exceeding its total budget self-suspends to a consistent resumable state rather than running unbounded, and resumes cleanly on the next election.
rationale: #669 alerts on exceeding budget and #782 allows MANUAL pause, but nothing makes the backfill self-bound its TOTAL runtime and auto-suspend — a pathologically slow backfill on a huge coverage table could run for hours degrading the DB with no automatic stop.

text: The equity_curve MANIFEST-HASH algorithm (hash over the full stored JSONB, #78) MUST be frozen and version-tagged so hashes stamped on PRE-deploy results remain verifiable after any phase ships; if the hashing scheme ever changes it MUST bump a manifest-hash-version tag and old-version stored hashes MUST be re-validated by recompute-under-old-scheme (never reported as a spurious integrity mismatch); verified by a test that a result hashed under the prior scheme still validates post-deploy and that a scheme change is version-gated.
rationale: #641/#959 version the KLINE content_sha256 and #1051 proves invariance to additive sibling columns, but no requirement freezes/versions the EQUITY_CURVE manifest-hash ALGORITHM itself — a phase altering how the curve is serialized/hashed would silently invalidate every historic stored manifest hash.

text: The durable flag/config STORE (#769/#981) AND the flag-change AUDIT table (#925) MUST each be created by the SAME idempotent v58 DDL (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS) and enumerated in the migration-completeness meta-test (#927), so the kill-switch/flag infrastructure and its audit trail are provisioned by migration (present on a freshly-migrated DB) rather than lazily created at first write; verified by a post-migration schema test asserting both tables exist after one and after two migration runs.
rationale: #968 covers SEEDING the config store but neither it nor #925/#927 ties the audit-table and flag-store TABLE CREATION to the idempotent migration DDL — ad-hoc runtime creation would mean the kill-switch store may not exist when first needed during an incident.

text: On boot the process MUST PROACTIVELY scan the durable columnar cache directory and classify artifacts whose embedded format-version (#256/#287/#771) or builder build-id (#1086) is incompatible/denylisted, reporting the columnar tier as 'degraded — N partitions pending rebuild' on the status route (#428) and scheduling lazy rebuild, rather than discovering each incompatibility only on a per-read fault mid-run; verified by a boot test that planting newer-format/denylisted-build artifacts yields a degraded-tier status report and those files are quarantined+rebuilt on first access.
rationale: #978/#1086 handle incompatibility lazily per-read and #983 alerts on an empty expected-persistent volume, but nothing proactively surfaces 'this deploy inherited incompatible columnar artifacts' at boot so operators see the rebuild cost before runs hit it.

text: Rolling back ONLY the accel-dependency layer (numba/llvmlite/pyarrow/duckdb) to a prior pinned set — or to ABSENT — WITHOUT reverting the application code or the v58 schema MUST be a supported, tested operation: the current app code MUST run pure-Python/Postgres-correct (oracle-identical on the canonical 5m no-drill path) against the older/absent accel wheels and detect any ABI incompatibility and disable the JIT/columnar path (#776) rather than crash; verified by a test pinning a prior/absent accel set under current code and asserting oracle-identical output plus graceful degradation.
rationale: #433 makes the layer separable and #1050 verifies image-profile/declared-vs-actual, but nothing tests the ASYMMETRIC rollback of just the accel wheels under unchanged app code — a common incident response when a numba/llvmlite bump misbehaves in prod.

text: The migration runner MUST guard against applying v58 to an unintended database: before any DDL it MUST verify a configured target-DB identity marker (expected database name / environment tag / an opt-in confirmation token for non-dev environments) and refuse with an actionable error if the connected DB does not match, so a misconfigured DATABASE_URL or a copy-prod-scans workflow cannot accidentally run the destructive seal-backfill against the wrong/prod DB; verified by a test that a mismatched target-identity aborts v58 with a clear message and zero schema change.
rationale: #474 guards the TEST SUITE against a prod DSN, but the MIGRATION RUNNER itself has no wrong-target guard — an operational mis-set DATABASE_URL during deploy or scan-copy could apply v58 + backfill to an unintended database.

text: A previously-FAILED v58 attempt that left PARTIAL artifacts — an orphaned auto-created monthly partition (from a mid-DDL >6mo/future partition create per #137/#456) or a half-populated manifest column/marker — MUST be cleanly reconciled on re-run: re-creating an existing partition must not error, partially-set fact columns must converge, and the backfill resumes from its checkpoint (#775); verified by a test that injects a partial-failure state (orphan partition + some sealed rows + marker in_progress) and asserts a clean idempotent re-run with no duplicate-partition error and a consistent final manifest.
rationale: #134 only asserts a mid-migration failure leaves schema_version at 57 with DDL rolled back; it does not cover reconciling PARTIAL ARTIFACTS (esp. orphan partitions auto-created outside the DDL transaction, or a half-run deferred backfill) on the subsequent retry.

text: A data-integrity guard MUST diff kline_cache_coverage BEFORE and AFTER the v58 seal-backfill and assert the backfill only ADDED seal/fact columns — no coverage row was deleted, no existing candle_count was reduced, and no last_covered high-water-mark regressed — complementing #967 (kline candle rows byte-identical) which does not cover the coverage rows the backfill legitimately UPDATEs; verified by a test snapshotting coverage pre/post backfill and asserting row count is non-decreasing and every pre-existing candle_count/last_covered value is preserved.
rationale: #967 proves kline_cache ROWS are untouched, but the backfill DOES update kline_cache_coverage — and no requirement asserts that update is strictly additive (a buggy UPDATE could null/reduce candle_count or drop coverage rows, silently corrupting the legacy GREATEST high-water-mark #935).

text: Once the seal-backfill completion marker reports 'complete', any RUNTIME lazy-seal (#592) of a CLOSED day whose day.end predates the marker-complete timestamp MUST increment a distinct 'post-complete backfill-miss' counter with a documented alert threshold, since such a lazy-seal indicates the backfill omitted a day it should have sealed (a backfill-completeness bug); verified by a test that seeds a sealable-but-unsealed closed day, marks the backfill complete, runs a backtest, and asserts the backfill-miss counter increments and alerts.
rationale: #592 silently self-heals such days and #1082 tracks a coarse coverage-ratio regression, but neither raises a sharp, attributable signal that the COMPLETED backfill missed a day — masking a backfill bug behind the lazy-seal safety net.

text: The aggregate analytics endpoints (analytics_summary, portfolio_overview, signal_analytics) and POST /backtest/compare MUST tolerate NEW enum values introduced by the optimization — additional close_reason values (e.g. mr_target #840) and new backtest_runs.status values ('cancelled'/'queued'/'interrupted by restart' #450/#451/#536) — flowing into their group-bys/filters without error or skew, defaulting unknown buckets safely; verified by a test running each analytics aggregate over a population mixing legacy rows and new-engine rows carrying the new close_reason/status values.
rationale: #1055 covers NULL new FIELDS in analytics aggregation, but new ENUM VALUES (new close_reason labels and new run statuses) entering analytics group-bys/filters is a distinct compatibility surface — an unhandled enum could break a count or mis-bucket trades in dashboards during the rollout.

text: Immediately after the v58 DDL is applied in PRODUCTION, the deploy automation MUST re-invoke the migration in dry-run/idempotency-verify mode and assert it reports ZERO pending changes, so a statement that 'succeeded' but is not truly idempotent (would re-apply on the next boot) is detected on the real target — not only in CI (#126); verified by a test asserting the post-apply dry-run reports no-op and fails the gate if any v58 statement would re-execute.
rationale: #126 proves idempotency in CI against a synthetic schema, but production schema drift (pre-existing columns of a different type #770, vendor partitioning quirks #1073) can make a statement non-idempotent only on the real target — a post-apply dry-run is the deploy-side teeth that CI cannot provide.

text: A single documented INTERVAL canonicalization (the engine forms '1m/5m/15m/1h/4h' as the one internal key) MUST be applied uniformly so the same logical interval keys identically across the kline_cache PK, the sealed-manifest PK (symbol,interval,day_utc), symbol_lifecycle, the in-process LRU/hot/1m-drill cache keys, the columnar interval partition value (#762), and the Bybit fetch param (#1039 maps to '5/15/60/240/1'); any alternate stored/requested form ('60' vs '1h', '5' vs '5m', case/whitespace variants) MUST resolve to ONE key (never splitting a logical interval into two coverage/manifest rows or two cache entries), and an unsupported interval MUST be rejected at the cache boundary with an actionable error rather than creating a bogus manifest key or fetching wrong-interval klines; verified by a test feeding alternate-form and unsupported interval strings asserting a single canonical key everywhere and a clean rejection — the interval analog of the symbol-string canonicalization (#1063), which #1039 leaves unpinned for STORAGE/key identity (it covers only the fetch-param mapping).
rationale: Symbol canonicalization is pinned (#1063) and the fetch-param interval mapping is pinned (#1039), but nothing pins that the interval STRING keys identically across kline_cache/manifest/lifecycle/cache/partition keys; a '60' vs '1h' skew would silently split a logical interval into two coverage/manifest rows and re-download, exactly the bug class this whole effort exists to prevent.

text: A manifest-vs-store integrity guard MUST detect the INVERSE of #295: a (symbol,interval,day) recorded sealed-known-empty / negative-cached (candle_count==0 or a covering gap-range #753) that nevertheless has kline_cache rows PRESENT on read (store/manifest skew, a stale negative-cache racing a real backfill, a relist #411, or a legacy-instance store during overlap) MUST raise an integrity error or trigger one-shot re-verification + reseal, rather than silently serving an empty set and DROPPING the real candles (which would turn legitimate signals into signals_no_kline and change trades); verified by a test that seeds rows behind a known-empty manifest entry and asserts the loader fails-loud or reseals to the true non-empty content instead of returning empty.
rationale: #295 only guards sealed-with-FEWER-rows-than-count (data lost behind a seal). The opposite skew — manifest says empty but rows exist — silently drops real klines and is uncovered; it is a realistic outcome of a relist (#411) or a negative-cache write racing a real fetch, and would corrupt results without any loud failure.

text: The system MUST DETECT a Postgres-SOR content drift that the #755 data-generation token cannot catch on its own — specifically a klines content change applied WITHOUT a generation bump (manual delete+reinsert, an out-of-band restatement, or a PITR that rewinds the token to an old value while later-built artifacts carry a higher token) — via a sampled/triggered integrity check (a sealed day's row count vs manifest candle_count, or a recomputed content_sha256 vs stored) and MUST then bump the generation token, invalidate dependent columnar/in-process artifacts, and emit an alertable signal, rather than assuming every SOR change is accompanied by a correct token increment; verified by a test that mutates sealed klines in place without bumping the token and asserts the drift is detected, the generation advances, and caches self-invalidate.
rationale: #755 establishes the generation token and the invalidate-on-mismatch behavior but ASSUMES the token is correctly bumped whenever the SOR changes; an in-place content change at an unchanged token (or a PITR that rewinds the token itself) is invisible to a pure token-compare and would serve stale derived data with no detection — a defense-in-depth gap distinct from #755/#1088.

text: Because the sealed manifest REUSES the existing kline_cache_coverage.candle_count column (#401) and the integrity checks (#295/#602) compare stored rows against candle_count, a pre-v58 (count-based) instance's legitimate UPDATE of candle_count on a row the new code has already SEALED during the rolling-deploy overlap (#807) MUST NOT desync integrity: either the seal MUST snapshot the immutable expected count into a SEPARATE manifest fact field used by the integrity check (leaving candle_count as the legacy live counter), or sealed rows MUST be protected from legacy count rewrites; verified by a test that seals a day, applies a legacy-shaped candle_count UPDATE to it, and asserts the integrity check still passes (no false hash-mismatch/short-read refetch) and the sealed content is unchanged — extending #418 (which protects only the NEW additive columns, explicitly NOT the shared candle_count column).
rationale: #418 protects the new manifest columns from legacy clobber but candle_count is a SHARED EXISTING column the legacy count-based path legitimately writes; if the integrity check keys off the same candle_count a legacy instance updates during overlap, a sealed day can false-trigger a refetch — a subtle column-reuse hazard nobody else pins.

text: A range read of a SINGLE month whose days are split between MATERIALIZED Parquet/Feather days and sealed-but-Postgres-only days (#964 materialized flag — e.g. a Phase-1 seal that predates lazy columnar build) MUST route per-day to the correct tier and stitch into one strictly-ascending, de-duplicated series byte-identical to a pure-Postgres read of that month, with no intra-month seam gap/duplicate/misorder; verified by a test that materializes only SOME days of a month and asserts a whole-month read correctly stitches Parquet+Postgres — distinct from the seal/forming boundary stitch (#439), the cross-MONTH seam (#359), the cross-TIER per-symbol routing (#597), and the multi-part-file merge (#1068), none of which exercises a partially-materialized single month.
rationale: #964 routes a SINGLE sealed-Postgres-only day to Postgres, but a real month will be partially materialized during the lazy-build window; reading it requires per-day tier interleaving WITHIN one month, a stitch seam none of the existing seam tests (#439/#359/#597/#1068) covers.

text: Because incremental day-by-day sealing appends multiple atomic part-files per month (#253/#791), an idempotent, refcount/in-use-guarded (#403) COMPACTION pass MUST periodically merge a month's accumulated part-files into a single sorted month file (or a bounded count) to cap per-read file-opens (#760) and file-descriptor pressure (#790) as a month fills, NEVER dropping/duplicating/misordering rows and never being observed partial by a concurrent reader (atomic pointer/manifest swap per #440/#1067); verified by a test that seals many days into one month across separate runs (producing many part-files), runs compaction concurrently with an open reader, and asserts the compacted month equals the Postgres SOR with a reduced file count and no reader disruption.
rationale: #791 caps write amplification by APPENDING part-files and #1068 reads them, but nothing MERGES them; a month sealed one day at a time accumulates ~30 part-files, steadily inflating the file-open count (#760) and fd usage (#790) until a wide read hits EMFILE — a compaction step is an unaddressed operational need.

text: When the copy-prod-scans workflow copies the sealed-manifest ROWS themselves (not only klines), the target environment MUST treat copied seal rows whose content_sha256 / MATERIALIZED state references source-environment columnar artifacts absent locally as Postgres-only-sealed: it MUST reset/ignore the materialized flag (no local Parquet to open), serve from the copied Postgres rows, and lazily re-materialize locally with ZERO Bybit refetch — never attempt to open a non-existent source-env Parquet partition (which would error or fall through to a refetch); verified by a test that copies klines+manifest into a fresh env lacking columnar files and asserts reads serve from Postgres, materialized flags reconcile to false, and a subsequent run issues 0 sealed-day refetches — distinct from #777 which rebuilds the manifest from SOR rather than consuming a COPIED manifest.
rationale: #777 assumes the target REBUILDS the manifest from copied klines; but the copy-prod-scans skill may copy the manifest table directly, in which case its materialized flags / shas point at source-env Parquet files that do not exist in the target — an unhandled path that would either crash on a missing-file open or trigger spurious refetches.

text: The in-place manifest rebuild-from-SOR (#675/#777) MUST set each day's MATERIALIZED flag (#964) to reflect ACTUAL on-disk columnar presence AND validity (content_sha256 / SOR-verified), not blindly true or false, so a rebuild over an environment with some partitions present, some missing, and some corrupt yields a flag set that routes each day to the correct tier; verified by a test that rebuilds the manifest against a mix of present-valid, missing, and bit-corrupt Parquet partitions and asserts materialized flags match real on-disk validity and reads route each day correctly (Postgres fallback for missing/corrupt).
rationale: #675 rebuilds seal flags and fact columns from the SOR and #964 introduces the materialized flag, but no requirement ties the rebuild to RECONCILING materialized against real on-disk columnar state; a rebuild that leaves materialized=true for a missing partition would crash reads, and materialized=false for a present-valid partition needlessly re-materializes.

text: Every run that materializes columnar data MUST emit structured counters for BYTES WRITTEN and partitions/part-files materialized to the Parquet/Feather tier (the write-side analog of the bytes-READ counter #446), pinned by the observability schema-snapshot contract (#534); the cache-efficiency regression gate (#263/#446/#760) MUST additionally gate columnar bytes-written per run on the canonical fixture and MUST assert a WARM rerun materializes 0 bytes, so a write-amplification regression (month rewrite-on-every-seal #791, or re-materializing already-materialized days) is caught phase-over-phase; verified by a test asserting the counters reflect a seeded cold materialization, are 0 on warm rerun, and the gate fails on an injected write-amplification regression.
rationale: The bytes-READ side is observed and gated (#446) but the write/materialization side is not; a regression that re-writes a whole month on every daily seal (#791's failure mode) or re-materializes already-built days would inflate disk/IO invisibly to every existing counter.

text: The shared process-level Bybit CIRCUIT-BREAKER (#756) state — closed/open/half-open, consecutive-failure count, and next-probe time — MUST be exposed on the queryable runtime status route (#428) and emitted as a structured metric, so an operator/deploy can see WHY cold runs are slow or failing (exchange degraded) and the fleet-consistency check (#463/#800) can surface a pod stuck open; verified by a contract test asserting the breaker fields appear in the status payload and reflect a tripped breaker after seeded failures, and stay closed/healthy on a nominal run.
rationale: #756 introduces a shared breaker and #428 a status route, but nothing exposes the breaker's state for ops; a tripped breaker silently degrading every cold run fleet-wide would be undiagnosable without scraping logs.

text: The ingest path MUST CLIP Bybit-returned candles to the requested in-life window before store_klines and before content_sha256 (#254) — discarding any row with open_time < the requested/sealed-day start, open_time >= the completion frontier (forming, #435), or open_time outside confirmed [listing,delist] — so an exchange page that over-returns rows beyond the requested bounds can never inflate a sealed day's stored set, perturb its canonical sha, leak a forming candle into a sealed day, or seal candles belonging to an adjacent day; verified by a test feeding a mocked page carrying extra out-of-window rows (a day before, a forming day after) and asserting only in-window candles are stored/sealed and the resulting content_sha256 is invariant to the over-fetch (consistent with the seam-invariant sha of #965).
rationale: #435 clips the FORMING (after-frontier) edge and #965 pins boundary-exact pagination, but neither clips rows the exchange may return BEFORE the requested start or for an adjacent day; an over-returning page would land foreign candles into the wrong sealed day and change its immutable sha — a determinism/immutability hazard.

text: The one-time v58 seal-backfill UPDATEs every existing kline_cache_coverage row once, producing a large dead-tuple footprint and stale planner statistics; the migration MUST trigger (or document + schedule) a VACUUM (ANALYZE) of kline_cache_coverage / the manifest after the backfill so the index-backed seal-check (#512) is not degraded by bloat and the planner actually adopts the new manifest/seal index, distinct from the ongoing-churn autovacuum-friendliness policy (#942) and the migration time/lock budgets (#133/#266/#670); verified by a test asserting that after the backfill the table is ANALYZEd (the planner uses the manifest index for the seal-check) and the dead-tuple ratio is bounded by the migration's maintenance step rather than left to drift until the next autovacuum.
rationale: A backfill that rewrites every coverage row leaves a one-time bloat spike and stale stats that can make the brand-new seal-check index go unused (full scans) right after deploy — the exact path the perf-critical seal gate depends on; #942 covers ONGOING churn, not the one-shot backfill's bloat/ANALYZE.
