text: Recording a known-empty / confirmed-structural-gap sub-range (#753) MUST be a race-safe idempotent upsert (ON CONFLICT) exactly like the seal-row write (#252), so concurrent runs / sweep combos confirming the SAME gap-range for one (symbol,interval,day) converge to a single consistent gap-range record with no duplicate, divergent, or partially-written row and no error; verified by a concurrency test recording the identical gap-range from >=3 concurrent writers under _MAX_CONCURRENT and asserting exactly one consistent gap-range row, mirroring the per-day seal idempotency guarantee (#252).
rationale: #252 pins concurrency idempotency for the per-day SEAL upsert, but #753 introduces a SEPARATE gap-ranges structure whose concurrent writes are never pinned; two combos confirming the same interior hole simultaneously could produce duplicate/divergent gap rows that desync the count-free skip predicate.

text: The durable columnar tier (Parquet/Feather/Arrow) MUST persist ONLY the fine base interval (and any legacy exchange-fetched native-coarse rows per #455); DERIVED coarse bars (15m/1h/4h from the fine base, #116/#404/#849) MUST NOT be written as a durable derived artifact that could silently drift from the fine base — they are computed on read via the in-session memo (#405), OR, if a durable derived-coarse cache is introduced, each such artifact MUST carry the fine-base data-generation stamp (#755) and be auto-invalidated/regenerated whenever the fine base changes and MUST never become the authoritative source over fine (#258); verified by a test asserting a coarse-interval read always reflects the current fine base (a fine-base correction #436 changes the next coarse read) and that no durable coarse artifact is served stale after a fine-base content change.
rationale: #257/#258/#405/#645 cover whether derived-coarse is SEALED, AUTHORITATIVE, and in-session-memoized, but none pins whether it is DURABLY materialized to disk; if it were persisted without a generation stamp, a later fine-base correction (#436/#296) would leave a stale durable coarse artifact that silently diverges from its own fine base.

text: The negative-cache / transient-structural-gap TTL (#93/#248/#1040) MUST be DURABLE (persisted in the manifest, surviving process restart so it neither re-fetches every restart nor heals only in-process) and its expiry MUST be evaluated against the SAME coordinated UTC clock source used for the completion frontier (#922) — never the local host wall clock — so two instances with skewed clocks cannot heal/seal a gap day early or late and a restart cannot reset the heal timer; verified by a test that records a transient-gap TTL, restarts the process and advances the coordinated clock, and asserts the TTL heals/re-verifies exactly once at the coordinated expiry (not on restart, not on a skewed local clock).
rationale: #93/#248/#1040 define the TTL-bearing transient-gap behavior and #922 pins a coordinated clock for the FRONTIER, but the TTL's own clock-source and durability are unpinned; an in-process-only or host-clock TTL would either re-fetch every restart or let a skewed pod heal a real day to empty at the wrong time.

text: ALL side_sign-dependent ACCOUNTING quantities under direction='reverse' MUST be computed on the POST-reverse EXECUTED position side, not the raw signal side: funding direction (long-pays / short-receives), the uPnL sign in qty x (mark - equity_ref_entry) x side_sign, the favorable/adverse high-vs-low selection for MFE/MAE, and the TP/SL/liquidation barrier sign — while #235's FILTER-side quantities (signal_sides, signed max_price_drift) stay on the RAW pre-reverse direction. Frozen by a reverse fixture (raw Buy -> executed Short) asserting the short RECEIVES funding, its uPnL/MFE/MAE/barriers carry the short-side sign, and every such field is byte-identical to the oracle, so the SoA/numba refactor cannot compute any accounting quantity on the wrong (pre-reverse) side.
rationale: #235 enumerates ONLY the pre/post-reverse split for the filter chain (signal_sides, drift, max_same_direction) and #55 states 'longs pay, shorts receive' without ever tying the sign of funding/uPnL/MFE-MAE/barriers to the executed (flipped) side — leaving every reverse-direction accounting sign unpinned and at risk of being computed on the raw signal side by a kernel reimplementation.

text: BREAKEVEN_TIMEOUT MUST mutate ONLY the position's TP to the breakeven level; the position's SL and its derived LIQUIDATION price MUST remain at their original open-time values (consistent with #605 'only breakeven-mutation rewrites TP'), so after the timeout a continued adverse move still stops at the original SL or liquidates at the original liq price. Frozen by a fixture that triggers BREAKEVEN_TIMEOUT and then drives price adversely, asserting the post-timeout SL fill price and the liq trigger are bit-identical to the un-mutated position's values and that only the TP barrier changed.
rationale: #57/#169/#702 pin the breakeven TP-mutation basis and #605 implies SL/liq are cached-once, but no fixture asserts that the SL and liquidation barriers SURVIVE the TP mutation unchanged — a kernel that recomputes all barriers on mutation could silently shift SL/liq.

text: The skip_if_positions_open decision MUST be evaluated against the open-book state as it exists at SCAN START (before that scan's own carried-book close-rule pass): a scan that is skipped because the book is non-empty, and whose close-rule pass then EMPTIES the book, MUST NOT subsequently admit that scan's own signals — the skip is latched at scan entry. Frozen by a fixture where the skipped scan's close rules flatten all positions mid-scan, asserting no signal from that scan is admitted and the anchor-preservation (#71) still applies.
rationale: #695 pins the EMPTY-book trading-scan case and #71/#623 pin the skip branch's anchor preservation, but none pins the ORDER edge where a skip scan empties the book via its own closes — leaving ambiguous whether it then flips into an admitting scan, a path-dependent divergence the SoA refactor could introduce.

text: In a MIXED-cohort scan (some signals route trend, some mean_reversion) the concurrent-position caps MUST compose bit-identically to legacy: pin whether the trend max_trades counts currently-open MR positions (and whether mr_max_trades counting 'ALL open positions' per #243 includes open trend positions), and how the per-scan scan_entered / max_trades admission budget is shared between the two cohorts' admissions. Frozen by a mixed-cohort fixture sitting at both caps' boundaries asserting exactly which signals are admitted and the counter increments match the oracle.
rationale: #243 pins ONLY the MR-side (mr_max_trades vs ALL open) and #904 pins cross-cohort dedup, but the TREND-side cap behavior toward open MR positions and the shared per-scan admission budget in a mixed scan are unpinned — a refactor could count caps per-cohort-only or globally and change admissions.

text: A position force-closed at end-of-backtest (close_reason=backtest_end) on a bar that COINCIDES with a 0/8/16h funding boundary MUST charge/credit that boundary's funding via the (date,hour) dedupe exactly as a normal close, record it in trade.funding_paid, and still satisfy the three-way reconciliation with the funding term. Frozen by a fixture whose terminal force-close bar lands on a funding boundary, asserting funding_paid is non-zero with correct sign and Sum trade.pnl == final_equity - starting_capital holds.
rationale: #905 pins liquidation-on-boundary and #820 pins the no-boundary-crossed zero case, but the backtest_end force-close path (#68/#325) is never tested for funding on its terminal bar — the force-close branch could omit the boundary charge that a normal close would apply.

text: Funding charged/credited at a 0/8/16h boundary MUST be counted EXACTLY ONCE across the running wallet and the open position's carried uPnL: pin whether a boundary charge is settled into the realized wallet at the boundary (and thereby REMOVED from the position's forward carried-uPnL term) or accrued only in carried uPnL until close — so funding can never be double-counted in sizing_capital (#61) NOR in the basket equity E (#1005), and a NEW entry admitted in a LATER scan while the funded position is still open is sized bit-identically. Frozen by a fixture holding a position across a boundary then admitting a next-scan entry, asserting that entry's floored qty reflects the funding term exactly once.
rationale: #558 pins the funding magnitude/basis and #1000 pins that the equity cascade reads the post-funding wallet on the boundary bar, but the SETTLEMENT TIMING (wallet-at-boundary vs accrued-in-uPnL) is unpinned; since sizing_capital and basket-E both combine wallet + uPnL, a wrong split risks double-counting or dropping funding in cross-scan sizing.

text: When a single position is eligible on one bar for BOTH its own per-position TP/SL exit AND a basket equity-rule flatten (EQUITY_DROP/SMART/close_on_profit/EQUITY_RISE), the per-position liq/TP/SL exit MUST win and the recorded close_reason MUST be take_profit/stop_loss/liquidation (NOT equity_*), because per-position checks run BEFORE the equity cascade in the within-bar order (#42) and that position is no longer in the book when the cascade evaluates the remaining book. Frozen by a fixture where one position's TP touches on the same bar an equity threshold is crossed, asserting that position records take_profit while only the surviving positions flatten via the equity rule.
rationale: #42 pins the generic within-bar rule-TYPE order but never exercises a single position eligible for BOTH a per-position barrier and a basket flatten on the same bar — leaving the recorded close_reason ATTRIBUTION for that position unpinned, a field a refactor could mislabel as equity_*.

text: On a bar where TRAILING_PROFIT DEACTIVATES (uPnL <= 0 clears the peak per #56, so trailing is no longer active), the BREAKEVEN_TIMEOUT eligibility MUST be reproduced bit-identically: pin whether BREAKEVEN_TIMEOUT (skipped while trailing active per #57) becomes eligible to mutate TP in that SAME bar's time-rule phase (which runs after trailing in the within-bar order #42) or only on the next bar. Frozen by a fixture where trailing deactivates exactly on the bar the breakeven timeout matures, asserting the breakeven mutation fires (or defers) on the legacy bar.
rationale: #57 pins that breakeven is skipped WHILE trailing is active but never pins the re-eligibility micro-edge on the exact bar trailing deactivates — the within-bar order (#42 trailing -> time rules) means same-bar re-eligibility is possible and the firing bar is path-dependent.

text: An open position whose symbol's kline series ENDS before the global window end (mid-window delist or data stop, #654) MUST be force-closed (close_reason=backtest_end) at THAT symbol's LAST AVAILABLE candle close — never carried to, or closed at, the global window-end timestamp/price — applying that bar's funding/fee/slippage treatment (#325) and setting the equity_curve point ts to the symbol's last-candle time (consistent with #68/#633). Frozen by a fixture where a symbol delists mid-window while still holding an open position, asserting the force-close bar, price, fees/funding, equity-point ts, and reconciliation are bit-identical to the oracle.
rationale: #68 states force-close happens 'at each symbol's last close' and #654 covers post-delist signals as signals_no_kline, but no fixture pins the OPEN-position case for a symbol that ends mid-window — a refactor with a single global terminal bar could close it at the wrong (global) time/price and shift its funding/fees and equity-point ts.

text: A content-changing sealed-day correction (content_sha256-mismatch refetch #296 or manual force-reverify #397) MUST invalidate the raw->SoA conversion memo (#218) AND the global-timeline/scan-anchor memo (#854) for any (symbol,interval,range) overlapping that day, NOT only the LRU/hot-frame/mmap/derived-coarse memo that #436 enumerates; verified by a test that warms the SoA-conversion and timeline/anchor memos, forces a content-changing refetch of an underlying day, and asserts the next run rebuilds the SoA arrays and timeline from corrected data (the prior memo is not served) while #424's within-run freeze still holds.
rationale: #436 says 'EVERY in-process artifact' but enumerates a list that omits the raw->SoA conversion memo (#218) and the timeline/anchor memo (#854); a long-lived process would keep serving stale SoA arrays/timeline built from the pre-correction klines, silently producing wrong-but-cached results after an integrity correction.

text: The DELIST day itself (a symbol that stops trading mid-day, leaving partial candles up to delist_time then nothing) MUST be sealable count-free as a complete-up-to-delist day via an explicit delist_snapped marker (the delist analog of listing_snapped #94), so once day.end <= frontier it is sealed and never refetched; verified by a test seeding a symbol delisting mid-day and asserting that day seals delist_snapped with its partial count accepted and reruns issue 0 Bybit calls for it.
rationale: The count-free predicate #91 (need_fetch = not sealed AND day_overlaps[listing,delist] AND day.end > last_covered) makes the delist day a PERPETUAL refetch candidate (day.end > last_covered forever, since the exchange will never return candles past delist_time); #94 covers only the listing-day boundary and #654 covers only the days AFTER the last candle, leaving the partial delist day itself uncovered.

text: A manifest internal-consistency invariant MUST hold at seal-write and read time: Sum(gap-range candle slots from #753) == gap_count, and candle_count + gap_count + boundary-clip(listing/delist/forming) == the interval-derived expected in-life day-slot count; a sealed day whose aggregate gap_count disagrees with its detailed gap-ranges (or whose counts do not reconcile to the expected slots) MUST raise an integrity error or trigger one-shot re-verification rather than be silently trusted; verified by a test seeding a day whose gap_count and gap-ranges disagree and asserting detection.
rationale: #260/#753 store both an aggregate gap_count and detailed gap-ranges and #602 uses 'candle_count minus gap_count', but nothing asserts the aggregate and the detailed ranges are mutually consistent; a corrupt/half-written manifest where count says full but ranges say holes (or vice versa) would silently drive wrong fetch-skip or short-read decisions.

text: The standing per-phase cache-efficiency regression gate MUST additionally track and fail phase-over-phase on the 1m DRILL Bybit fetch count on a drill-enabled canonical fixture (warm in-process rerun == 0 per #23/#380; cold ~ needed bars per #21/#106, not the full min->max span), since the existing standing gates (#263 Bybit-5m-calls/#446 bytes-read/#760 file-opens/#798 seal-writes) are all 5m-path-only and a regression that re-fetches 1m sub-bars on a warm rerun (broken 1m cache) would slip through them.
rationale: All existing standing cache-efficiency gates measure the 5m loader path; the in-memory-only 1m drill cache (#104/#380) has correctness tests but no phase-over-phase regression GATE, so a lost-1m-cache regression is undetectable until a benchmark re-run.

text: A Postgres WRITE failure during cold gap-fill store_klines (disk-full, statement_timeout, constraint violation, or mid-batch connection loss) MUST leave the affected day UNSEALED and refetchable, write no torn/partial manifest seal row, and either fail the run loud with an actionable (symbol+range) message or surface a documented explicit partial-data warning - it MUST NEVER seal a day whose rows failed to persist nor let the engine proceed silently on a truncated kline set; verified by a fault-injection test that makes store_klines raise mid cold gap-fill and asserts the day stays unsealed, no partial manifest row exists, and a subsequent run refetches and seals it.
rationale: #247 covers the EXCHANGE fetch failing (and assumes the store of whatever was fetched succeeds), and #939 covers a Postgres failure during kline-LOAD or persist-RESULTS - but the store_klines WRITE failing during cold gap-fill (the ingest write itself) is an uncovered third failure mode that could seal-on-partial-store or proceed on truncated data.

text: A single (symbol,interval,range) cache entry whose serialized klines exceed the ENTIRE configured LRU/Arrow-hot-cache byte budget (plausible under a constrained per-process budget in a multi-worker host #445, or a wide long single-symbol span #374) MUST be served correctly by bypassing the cache (or serve-then-immediately-evict) without thrashing, without refusing to serve, and without evicting the whole cache to (fail to) make room; verified by a test under a deliberately tiny byte budget asserting the oversized range still returns byte-identical klines and other entries are not catastrophically evicted.
rationale: #102/#388 bound the LRU and require whole-entry LRU eviction but implicitly assume each entry fits within the budget; the degenerate 'one entry larger than the whole budget' case is unspecified and could deadlock eviction or refuse the load.

text: The admin in-place manifest REBUILD-from-SOR path (#675/#777) MUST be interruptible, resumable from its OWN persisted checkpoint, and idempotent, so an interrupted rebuild resumes without re-scanning already-rebuilt (symbol,interval,day) chunks and never leaves a half-rebuilt/internally-inconsistent manifest; verified by a test that kills the rebuild mid-way, restarts it, and asserts it completes from the checkpoint with a final manifest byte-identical (modulo NULL sha) to a single uninterrupted rebuild.
rationale: The v58 backfill has explicit checkpoint/resume/idempotency reqs (#132/#775), but the separately-invoked admin rebuild-from-SOR (#675/#777) - used to repair a corrupted manifest or seed a copied environment - has no resumability/interruption guarantee of its own, so an interrupted operator rebuild could strand the manifest in an inconsistent state.

text: Columnar GC/eviction (#261/#403/#957) and rollback quarantine (#978/#1110) MUST clear/flip the per-day MATERIALIZED flag (#964) to false ATOMICALLY with deleting/moving a Parquet/Feather partition, so subsequent reads route deterministically to the Postgres SOR rather than incurring a per-read missing-file open fault + fallback; verified by a GC-then-read test asserting the evicted day reports materialized=false, reads serve byte-identical from Postgres, and no missing-file fault/error is logged on the read path.
rationale: #964 makes the materialized flag the deterministic tier router and #403 guards eviction by refcount, but no requirement ties flag-clearing to the eviction/quarantine so reads route cleanly; leaving materialized=true after a file is deleted degrades every subsequent read of that day to a fault+fallback instead of a clean Postgres route.

text: The running service MUST emit a continuous alertable structured signal when kline_cache_default (the unpruned catch-all partition) becomes NON-EMPTY in production - indicating store_klines silently failed to auto-create or attach the needed monthly partition (the partition-create-miss of #137/#284/#399/#1046) - so rows accumulating in the default partition are detected and remediated without re-running the benchmark; verified by a test seeding a row into kline_cache_default and asserting the signal fires while an all-partitioned state stays silent.
rationale: #399 asserts kline_cache_default stays empty only as a REGRESSION TEST against the canonical fixture; there is no production-runtime observability signal, so a real-world partition-create race/failure (which silently disables pruning and degrades query plans) would accumulate undetected in prod.

text: The Postgres-SOR monotonic data-generation/epoch token (#755) MUST be globally-unique-enough or environment-namespaced so the copy-prod-scans workflow cannot copy a SOR token that COLLIDES with a pre-existing target-environment columnar/in-process artifact's embedded generation (which would cause false reuse of a stale dev artifact as if it matched the freshly-copied data); verified by a test that copies prod klines+token into a dev env holding pre-existing artifacts and asserts every copied (symbol,interval,range) is rebuilt locally and no pre-existing same-token artifact is served as a match.
rationale: #755 invalidates artifacts whose embedded generation != current SOR generation, and #1124 resets materialized flags on copied manifests - but neither prevents the copied SOR token from numerically COLLIDING with a target-env artifact's pre-existing token, which would silently satisfy the generation-match check and serve stale dev data after a prod-scan copy.

text: A RUNTIME store_klines monthly-partition auto-create (#137/#284/#1046) that races the v58 migration's own partition pre-creation/backfill (#122) - or the eventual contraction/retention tool - during the deploy window MUST be mutually idempotent and non-colliding (CREATE ... IF NOT EXISTS + catch duplicate/overlap, or coordinate via advisory lock), so a live backtest/scanner writing a >6mo-old or future month while v58 is mid-flight cannot raise a partition-collision or leave a half-attached partition; verified by a test that triggers a runtime partition-create for the same month the migration is concurrently creating and asserts a single clean attach with the row in its dedicated partition.
rationale: #284 covers two concurrent RUNTIME writers creating the same partition and #265 serializes the migration via advisory lock, but the runtime store_klines path is NOT under the migration's lock, so a runtime partition-create racing the v58 migration's partition-create is an uncovered deploy-window collision vector.

text: v58 MUST validate the EXISTING kline_cache_coverage PRIMARY KEY / unique constraint is actually (symbol, interval, day/date) BEFORE reusing it as the manifest seal key (#401): if the live PK differs (e.g. legacy PK is (symbol, date) lacking interval, or carries an extra column), v58 MUST fail-fast with an actionable diagnostic naming the actual vs expected key rather than creating a divergent/duplicate manifest keyspace or silently sealing under the wrong granularity; verified by a test that a coverage table whose PK lacks interval causes v58 to abort with the specific key-mismatch message and zero schema change.
rationale: #401 ASSUMES the coverage PK is (symbol,interval,date) and reuses it for the manifest, but no requirement verifies that precondition holds on the real production table; a mismatched legacy PK would silently break seal idempotency or split the manifest.

text: The v58 seal-backfill MUST derive candle_count/first_open_ts/last_open_ts/gap_count from the ACTUAL distinct kline_cache rows (set-based aggregate per #655), NOT trust the pre-existing kline_cache_coverage.candle_count column, because that legacy counter may be inflated or stale from the very count-based/re-download bug being fixed; when the stored coverage.candle_count disagrees with the true row-derived count the backfill MUST seal with the row-derived fact and surface the discrepancy; verified by a test seeding a coverage row whose stored candle_count disagrees with its actual kline rows and asserting the sealed fact equals the recomputed row count.
rationale: #275/#655 populate facts 'from existing data' but never pin that a STALE/incorrect legacy candle_count is ignored in favor of the recomputed row count — sealing a wrong fact would poison every downstream integrity check (#295/#602).

text: At backfill time an interior day inside [listing,delist] that is missing candles but CANNOT be confirmed structural (it may be a hole created by the legacy incomplete-fetch/re-download bug, since the backfill only sees stored rows and never the exchange) MUST be sealed-with-gap_count but flagged refetch-pending-reverify (a one-shot post-frontier re-verification per #248) rather than permanently sealed-known-empty (#260) — so a real hole introduced by the bug being fixed is healed on a later run, not frozen empty forever; verified by a test that an ambiguous backfill-time interior hole is reverified exactly once against the exchange before being permanently sealed known-empty, while a confirmed pre-listing/post-delist range is permanently negative-cached with no reverify.
rationale: #260 unconditionally records interior holes as permanent count-free known-empty, but at backfill time the system cannot distinguish a genuine structural hole from missing rows caused by the legacy re-download/incomplete-fetch bug — permanently freezing such a hole would silently drop real candles.

text: Each sealed day MUST record a SEAL-LOGIC/manifest-semantics version stamp (covering the completion-frontier formula, skew_margin #249, and the day-class taxonomy #1032) DISTINCT from the content_sha256 canonicalization-scheme version (#641/#974); a later code version that changes the seal DECISION rules MUST be able to SELECT and selectively re-evaluate only days sealed under the prior semantics (bounded migration), never silently trusting them under the new rules; verified by a test asserting the seal-version column is present and set on every seal and that bumping it targets re-evaluation of exactly the old-version sealed days.
rationale: #641/#974 version the HASH canonicalization but nothing versions the SEAL-DECISION logic itself; a future change to frontier/skew/day-class rules would leave historic seals computed under different semantics with no way to identify or re-evaluate them.

text: Rows that landed in the unpruned kline_cache_default partition because a pre-v58/legacy-code instance wrote a >6mo-old or future range during the rolling-deploy overlap window (legacy code lacks the runtime monthly-partition auto-create of #137) MUST be detected and reconciled — migrated into their dedicated monthly partition (or proven correctly readable) — so the steady-state 'kline_cache_default is empty' invariant (#399) is restored AFTER the overlap window, not just asserted under new code; verified by a test that seeds default-partition rows simulating a legacy write, runs the reconciliation pass, and asserts row_count(kline_cache_default)==0 with each row landed in its proper partition and byte-identical on read.
rationale: #399 asserts default-partition-empty only under new code; the rolling-deploy overlap (#807) lets legacy instances write into the default partition, and no requirement reconciles those rows back out afterward.

text: During a rolling deploy where two instances run DIFFERENT columnar FORMAT versions, concurrent writers sealing disjoint days into the SAME month partition MUST NOT produce an unreadable mixed-format month: either every part-file carries its own independently-checkable format stamp and the reader handles a heterogeneous-format month (per-part-file version check, rebuilding/quarantining stale-format parts per #771/#978), or columnar writes are format-version-gated so only one format writes per month; verified by a test that writes two different-format-version part-files into one month and asserts a correct, stitched, SOR-identical read (no garble, no whole-month rebuild required for the valid parts).
rationale: #256/#287/#771/#978 handle a single reader meeting an older/newer FILE, but the rolling-deploy case of two format generations concurrently WRITING into the same incrementally-materialized month (#253/#791) is unaddressed.

text: Pre-building the durable columnar (Parquet/Feather) tier for the universe of already-sealed days MUST be EITHER a bounded, resumable, throttled background job with its OWN completion marker/checkpoint (distinct from the seal-backfill marker of #412/#663, never blocking readiness, replica-lag/WAL-throttled like #921/#1047), OR documented and asserted as strictly lazy-on-first-touch with the cold-build penalty bounded (#233); the chosen model MUST be explicit so a Phase-5 deploy does not silently trigger an unbounded synchronous mass-materialization; verified by a test asserting the bulk-materialize job is resumable+throttled with its own marker, or that lazy-only is enforced and first-touch build stays within budget with no mass build at deploy.
rationale: #444 establishes Postgres-only seal then lazy materialize, but there is no requirement governing a BULK columnar materialization of the historic sealed universe (its own marker/throttle/resume) versus an explicit lazy-only contract — a Phase-5 deploy could otherwise kick off an unbounded build.

text: A holistic DISASTER-RECOVERY test MUST simulate total loss of the sealed manifest AND the entire durable columnar tier AND all in-process caches (only Postgres SOR kline_cache + symbol_lifecycle intact) and assert the documented DR procedure rebuilds the manifest from SOR (#675), lazily re-materializes columnar, issues ZERO Bybit refetches, and produces golden-master-byte-identical backtest results on the canonical fixture — proving Postgres-as-system-of-record (#113) is sufficient to fully reconstruct the derived stack; distinct from the manifest-only rebuild (#675) and copy-prod-scans (#777) which each lose only one tier.
rationale: #675 rebuilds only the manifest and #777 only copies klines; no single test proves the WHOLE derived stack (manifest+columnar+caches) is regenerable from Postgres alone with zero refetch and full parity — the core DR guarantee.

text: The boot sequence MUST bound the AGGREGATE warm-up wall-time across all enabled startup tasks — numba JIT warm (#271/#422), DuckDB/Polars columnar-engine warm (#861), and asyncpg pool pre-warm (#886) — so that even though each is individually watchdog-bounded they cannot COLLECTIVELY exceed the readiness/liveness probe deadline; the tasks MUST run concurrently and/or deferred under a single aggregate boot-budget, verified by a test asserting total boot warm-up time with all accel tiers enabled stays under the documented readiness deadline (not merely each sub-warm-up individually).
rationale: #271/#422/#861/#886 each bound their OWN warm-up but nothing caps their SUM against the single readiness deadline — three individually-fine warm-ups can collectively trip a pod-kill timeout.

text: Beyond the one-time boot self-check (#164) and per-run degradation record (#496/#691), the running service MUST track the RATE of runs degrading to the pure-Python/Postgres fallback (numba/duckdb/pyarrow unavailable or an accel-path fault per #155/#301) over a rolling window and fire an alertable structured signal when that rate exceeds a documented threshold, so a partial production breakage where the accel stack silently fails for many runs (masked by correct-but-slow fallback) is detected without inspecting individual runs; verified by a test seeding repeated fallbacks and asserting the fallback-rate alert fires while an all-accel-healthy window stays silent.
rationale: The fallback is intentionally transparent for CORRECTNESS, which means a broadly-broken accel deploy stays invisible at the result level; only a standing fallback-RATE alert surfaces it as a PERFORMANCE/health incident.

text: An AUTOMATED kill-switch revert triggered by a failing canary/smoke or non-zero sealed-day-refetch promotion check (#924) MUST itself append to the persisted flag-change audit trail (#925) — recording timestamp, the triggering condition/metric, and the before/after flag state — and emit a distinct deploy/rollback EVENT, so an auto-revert is attributable and correlatable in the incident timeline exactly like a manual flip; verified by a test asserting an auto-revert writes an audit record naming the trigger and emits the rollback event.
rationale: #924 performs auto-revert and #925 audits MANUAL flag changes, but nothing requires the AUTOMATED revert path to write the audit/event — an unattributed automatic rollback is hard to triage.

text: The rollback DRILL (#432), the master-kill-switch golden-master parity check (#277/#432), and the new full disaster-recovery rebuild test MUST be WIRED to run on every phase PR (a CI-wiring meta-test analogous to #812/#465/#684), so the revert and recover procedures cannot silently rot as later phases edit async_persistence/manifest/columnar/engine code; verified by a meta-test asserting the rollback-drill and DR tests are attached to each phase's workflow and fail the build if removed.
rationale: #432 authors a rollback drill and a DR test is added here, but #812 wires only the migration-INTEGRITY gates per phase — the rollback/DR procedures themselves are not asserted to run every phase and would rot.

text: The v58 migration and its deferred seal-backfill connection(s) MUST set a distinctive application_name (and label long-running statements) in pg_stat_activity, so an operator can identify and, if it stalls during an incident, safely terminate the migration/backfill session without guessing which connection it is; verified by a test asserting the migration/backfill connection carries the documented application_name and that the backfill's statements are attributable to it.
rationale: Operational triage of a stuck migration/backfill (lock contention, runaway WAL) requires identifiability in pg_stat_activity; no requirement pins a migration application_name despite many lock/timeout/throttle requirements assuming operators can find and act on it.

text: The service MUST expose a gauge of the current kline_cache monthly-partition COUNT (and the count of part-files per recently-touched month) and fire an alertable signal on unexpected growth, so a partition-granularity regression (#108), a per-day small-file sprawl (#760/#1123), or a runtime auto-create/detach thrash loop (#1046) is detectable operationally rather than only via a one-off test; verified by a test asserting the gauge reflects partition count and the alert fires past a documented growth bound.
rationale: Many requirements forbid partition/file sprawl but only via point tests; a standing operational gauge+alert is needed to catch a sprawl regression that reaches production between releases.

text: The persistent flag/config store (#769/#981) MUST tag each row's ORIGIN (default-seeded vs operator-overridden), so that when a later code version changes a DOCUMENTED default the idempotent seed migration (#968/#1080) UPDATES only still-at-default (unmodified-origin) rows to the new default while leaving operator-overridden rows untouched; verified by a test that changes a documented default, re-runs the seed, and asserts unmodified rows adopt the new default while an operator-overridden row is preserved.
rationale: #1080 only proves overrides survive re-seed (ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING), which means a CHANGED documented default can never reach already-seeded-but-unmodified rows — an origin marker is required to propagate a new default without clobbering overrides.

text: During a rolling deploy where instances run DIFFERENT pinned accel-dependency versions (one image upgraded numba/llvmlite/pyarrow/duckdb), the float64/numba results for the same config across the two versions MUST stay within the defined continuous-field epsilon AND remain discrete-identical (or the per-arch oracle policy of #302/#723 applies), so a mid-upgrade fleet never returns visibly divergent numbers for one logical config; verified by a test diffing results produced under two pinned accel-version sets and asserting within-tolerance + discrete-identical, extending #302/#723 from OS/arch skew to dependency-VERSION skew.
rationale: #302/#723 pin tolerance across OS/arch but not across DIFFERENT accel-dependency VERSIONS coexisting during a rolling upgrade — a numba/llvmlite ABI/codegen change could shift last-bit results between two live pods.

text: The deferred-backfill completion marker MUST be validated for consistency with the actual manifest/SOR data-generation token (#755) on boot, so a Postgres point-in-time/partial RESTORE that leaves a 'complete' marker while the manifest/klines were rewound (or a cross-source restore) is DETECTED — the marker is invalidated and the backfill re-evaluated/resumed — rather than the system falsely reporting the cache fully sealed; verified by a test that injects a marker-ahead-of-data restore state and asserts the marker is re-validated to not-complete and the backfill resumes, distinct from the failover-resume case (#1044) where marker and data rewind together consistently.
rationale: #1044 covers consistent failover but not an INCONSISTENT restore where the in-DB marker and the manifest/klines diverge (partial/cross-source PITR) — a false 'complete' would gate optimization flags on (#811) over an unsealed cache.

text: Before the eventual CONTRACTION migration (v59+) drops the legacy count-based coverage columns/paths (#581/#1078), the deploy MUST take and verify a Postgres restore point as a GATING step (analogous to the v58 backup gate #920) AND the contraction MUST be dry-run-verified to touch ONLY the intended legacy columns (no manifest/result/lifecycle column), so the irreversible drop is both recoverable and provably scoped; verified by a test asserting the contraction captures a restore point and its dry-run reports exclusively legacy-column drops, refusing if any non-legacy object is in scope.
rationale: #920 protects the v58 EXPAND migration with a backup gate, but the later destructive CONTRACTION (#1078) — which actually drops columns — has only a drain-precondition gate, no recoverability/restore-point or scoped-dry-run guarantee.

text: The standalone migration entrypoint (boot auto-migrate AND the CLI/Job per #980/#1075) MUST import only the persistence/DDL layer and MUST NOT transitively import the backtest engine, SoA/numba kernel, scanner, or columnar modules, so a broken/slow engine or accel import can never block or delay applying the schema, and a lean pure-DDL migration Job can run independently of the application image's heavy deps; verified by a test asserting the migration entrypoint imports succeed with the engine/accel modules force-absent and that no engine/numba/duckdb module is loaded during a migration run.
rationale: #1089 covers accel-deps absent during migration, but does not isolate the migration entrypoint from the ENGINE/scanner modules themselves — a standalone migrate Job and a fast schema apply require the DDL path not to drag in the whole application.
